## Page 82

# COBIT® 2019 FRAMEWORK: GOVERNANCE AND MANAGEMENT OBJECTIVES

## A. Component: Process (cont.)

<table>
  <thead>
    <tr>
      <th>Management Practice</th>
      <th>Example Metrics</th>
    </tr>
  </thead>
  <tbody>
    <tr>
      <td><strong>APO04.02 Maintain an understanding of the enterprise environment.</strong><br>Work with relevant stakeholders to understand their challenges.<br>Maintain an adequate understanding of enterprise strategy, competitive environment and other constraints, so that opportunities enabled by new technologies can be identified.</td>
      <td>a. Percent of implemented initiatives with a clear linkage to an enterprise objective<br>b. Percent of opportunities enabled by new technologies identified</td>
    </tr>
  </tbody>
</table>

### Activities

<table>
  <thead>
    <tr>
      <th></th>
      <th>Capability Level</th>
    </tr>
  </thead>
  <tbody>
    <tr>
      <td>1. Maintain an understanding of industry and business drivers, enterprise and I&T strategy, and enterprise operations and current challenges. Apply the understanding to identify potential value-add technology and innovate I&T.</td>
      <td>2</td>
    </tr>
    <tr>
      <td>2. Conduct regular meetings with business units, divisions and/or other stakeholder entities to understand current business problems, process bottlenecks or other constraints where emerging technologies or I&T innovation can create opportunities.</td>
      <td>3</td>
    </tr>
    <tr>
      <td>3. Understand enterprise investment parameters for innovation and new technology so appropriate strategies are developed.</td>
      <td></td>
    </tr>
  </tbody>
</table>

### Related Guidance (Standards, Frameworks, Compliance Requirements)

No related guidance for this management practice

### Management Practice

**APO04.03 Monitor and scan the technology environment.**

Set up a technology watch process to perform systematic monitoring and scanning of the enterprise's external environment to identify emerging technologies that have the potential to create value (e.g., by realizing the enterprise strategy, optimizing costs, avoiding obsolescence, and better enabling enterprise and I&T processes). Monitor the marketplace, competitive landscape, industry sectors, and legal and regulatory trends to be able to analyze emerging technologies or innovation ideas in the enterprise context.

### Activities

<table>
  <thead>
    <tr>
      <th></th>
      <th>Capability Level</th>
    </tr>
  </thead>
  <tbody>
    <tr>
      <td>1. Understand enterprise appetite and potential for technology innovation. Focus awareness efforts on the most opportune technology innovations.</td>
      <td>2</td>
    </tr>
    <tr>
      <td>2. Set up a technology watch process and perform research and scanning of the external environment, including appropriate websites, journals and conferences, to identify emerging technologies and their potential value to the enterprise.</td>
      <td></td>
    </tr>
    <tr>
      <td>3. Consult third-party experts as necessary to confirm research or supply information on emerging technologies.</td>
      <td></td>
    </tr>
    <tr>
      <td>4. Capture I&T-innovation ideas from staff and review for potential implementation.</td>
      <td></td>
    </tr>
  </tbody>
</table>

### Related Guidance (Standards, Frameworks, Compliance Requirements)

No related guidance for this management practice

### Management Practice

**APO04.04 Assess the potential of emerging technologies and innovative ideas.**

Analyze identified emerging technologies and/or other I&T innovative suggestions to understand their business potential. Work with stakeholders to validate assumptions on the potential of new technologies and innovation.

### Activities

<table>
  <thead>
    <tr>
      <th></th>
      <th>Capability Level</th>
    </tr>
  </thead>
  <tbody>
    <tr>
      <td>1. Evaluate identified technologies, considering aspects such as time to reach maturity, inherent risk (including potential legal implications), fit with enterprise architecture and value potential, in line with enterprise and I&T strategy.</td>
      <td>2</td>
    </tr>
    <tr>
      <td>2. Identify issues that may need to be resolved or validated through a proof-of-concept initiative.</td>
      <td>3</td>
    </tr>
    <tr>
      <td>3. Scope the proof-of-concept initiative, including desired outcomes, required budget, time frames and responsibilities.</td>
      <td></td>
    </tr>
    <tr>
      <td>4. Obtain approval for the proof-of-concept initiative.</td>
      <td></td>
    </tr>
    <tr>
      <td>5. Conduct proof-of-concept initiatives to test emerging technologies or other innovation ideas. Identify issues and determine whether implementation or rollout should be considered based on feasibility and potential ROI.</td>
      <td></td>
    </tr>
  </tbody>
</table>

### Related Guidance (Standards, Frameworks, Compliance Requirements)

No related guidance for this management practice

&lt;page_number&gt;82&lt;/page_number&gt;

CHAPTER 4
COBIT GOVERNANCE AND MANAGEMENT OBJECTIVES—DETAILED GUIDANCE

A. Component: Process (cont.)

<tr>
<th>Management Practice</th>
<th>Example Metrics</th>

<tbody>
<tr>
<td><strong>APO04.05 Recommend appropriate further initiatives.</strong><br>Evaluate and monitor the results of proof-of-concept initiatives and, if favorable, generate recommendations for further initiatives. Gain stakeholder support.</td>
<td>a. Number of proof-of-concept initiatives evaluated and approved for further rollout<br>b. Number of proof-of-concept initiatives that have been leveraged in actual investment</td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td><strong>Activities</strong></td>
<td></td>
<td><strong>Capability Level</strong></td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td>1. Document proof-of-concept results, including guidance and recommendations for trends and innovation programs.</td>
<td></td>
<td rowspan="4">3</td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td>2. Communicate viable innovation opportunities into the I&T strategy and enterprise architecture processes.</td>
<td></td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td>3. Analyze and communicate reasons for rejected proof-of-concept initiatives.</td>
<td></td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td>4. Follow up on proof-of-concept initiatives to measure actual investment.</td>
<td></td>
</tr>
<tr>

<td></td>
</tr>

<td><strong>Management Practice</strong></td>
<td><strong>Example Metrics</strong></td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td><strong>APO04.06 Monitor the implementation and use of innovation.</strong><br>Monitor the implementation and use of emerging technologies and innovations during adoption, integration and for the full economic life cycle to ensure that the promised benefits are realized and to identify lessons learned.</td>
<td>a. Increase in market share or competitiveness due to innovations<br>b. Number of lessons learned and opportunities for improvement captured for future use</td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td><strong>Activities</strong></td>
<td></td>
<td><strong>Capability Level</strong></td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td>1. Capture lessons learned and opportunities for improvement.</td>
<td></td>
<td rowspan="4">3</td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td>2. Ensure that innovation initiatives align with enterprise and I&T strategy. Monitor alignment continuously. Adjust innovation plan, if required.</td>
<td></td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td>3. Assess new technology or I&T innovations implemented as part of I&T strategy and enterprise architecture development. Evaluate level of adoption during program management of initiatives.</td>
<td></td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td>4. Identify and assess potential value of innovation.</td>
<td></td>
</tr>

## Page 87

# CHAPTER 4
## COBIT GOVERNANCE AND MANAGEMENT OBJECTIVES—DETAILED GUIDANCE

<table>
  <tr>
    <td><strong>Domain:</strong> Align, Plan and Organize<br><strong>Management Objective:</strong> APO05 – Managed Portfolio</td>
    <td><strong>Focus Area:</strong> COBIT Core Model</td>
  </tr>
</table>

### Description
Execute the strategic direction set for investments in line with the enterprise architecture vision and I&T road map. Consider the different categories of investments and the resources and funding constraints. Evaluate, prioritize and balance programs and services, managing demand within resource and funding constraints, based on their alignment with strategic objectives, enterprise worth and risk. Move selected programs into the active products or services portfolio for execution. Monitor the performance of the overall portfolio of products and services and programs, proposing adjustments as necessary in response to program, product or service performance or changing enterprise priorities.

### Purpose
Optimize the performance of the overall portfolio of programs in response to individual program, product and service performance and changing enterprise priorities and demand.

The management objective supports the achievement of a set of primary enterprise and alignment goals:

<table>
  <tr>
    <td><strong>Enterprise Goals</strong></td>
    <td><strong>Alignment Goals</strong></td>
  </tr>
  <tr>
    <td>
      • EG01 Portfolio of competitive products and services<br>
      • EG08 Optimization of internal business process functionality<br>
      • EG12 Managed digital transformation programs
    </td>
    <td>
      • AG03 Realized benefits from I&T-enabled investments and services portfolio<br>
      • AG05 Delivery of I&T services in line with business requirements
    </td>
  </tr>
</table>

### Example Metrics for Enterprise Goals
<table>
  <tr>
    <td>EG01</td>
    <td>a. Percent of products and services that meet or exceed targets in revenues and/or market share</td>
    <td>AG03</td>
    <td>a. Percent of I&T-enabled investments for which claimed benefits in the business case are met or exceeded</td>
  </tr>
  <tr>
    <td></td>
    <td>b. Percent of products and services that meet or exceed customer satisfaction targets</td>
    <td></td>
    <td>b. Percent of I&T services for which expected benefits (as stated in service level agreements) are realized</td>
  </tr>
  <tr>
    <td></td>
    <td>c. Percent of products and services that provide competitive advantage</td>
    <td></td>
    <td></td>
  </tr>
  <tr>
    <td></td>
    <td>d. Time to market for new products and services</td>
    <td></td>
    <td></td>
  </tr>
  <tr>
    <td>EG08</td>
    <td>a. Satisfaction levels of board and executive management with business process capabilities</td>
    <td>AG05</td>
    <td>a. Percent of business stakeholders satisfied that I&T service delivery meets agreed service levels</td>
  </tr>
  <tr>
    <td></td>
    <td>b. Satisfaction levels of customers with service delivery capabilities</td>
    <td></td>
    <td>b. Number of business disruptions due to I&T service incidents</td>
  </tr>
  <tr>
    <td></td>
    <td>c. Satisfaction levels of suppliers with supply chain capabilities</td>
    <td></td>
    <td>c. Percent of users satisfied with the quality of I&T service delivery</td>
  </tr>
  <tr>
    <td>EG12</td>
    <td>a. Number of programs on time and within budget</td>
    <td></td>
    <td></td>
  </tr>
  <tr>
    <td></td>
    <td>b. Percent of stakeholders satisfied with program delivery</td>
    <td></td>
    <td></td>
  </tr>
  <tr>
    <td></td>
    <td>c. Percent of business transformation programs stopped</td>
    <td></td>
    <td></td>
  </tr>
  <tr>
    <td></td>
    <td>d. Percent of business transformation programs with regular reported status updates</td>
    <td></td>
    <td></td>
  </tr>
</table>

### A. Component: Process

<table>
  <tr>
    <td><strong>Management Practice</strong></td>
    <td><strong>Example Metrics</strong></td>
  </tr>
  <tr>
    <td>APO05.01 Determine the availability and sources of funds.<br>Determine potential sources of funds, different funding options and the implications of the funding source on the investment return expectations.</td>
    <td>a. Ratio between funds allocated and funds used<br>b. Ratio between retained earnings and funds allocated</td>
  </tr>
</table>

<table>
  <tr>
    <td><strong>Activities</strong></td>
    <td><strong>Capability Level</strong></td>
  </tr>
  <tr>
    <td>1. Understand current availability and commitment of funds, current approved spend and actual spend to date.</td>
    <td rowspan="3">2</td>
  </tr>
  <tr>
    <td>2. Identify options for additional funding of I&T-enabled investments, considering both internal and external sources.</td>
  </tr>
  <tr>
    <td>3. Determine the implications of the funding source on the investment return expectations.</td>
  </tr>
</table>

<table>
  <tr>
    <td><strong>Related Guidance (Standards, Frameworks, Compliance Requirements)</strong></td>
    <td><strong>Detailed Reference</strong></td>
  </tr>
  <tr>
    <td>No related guidance for this management practice</td>
    <td></td>
  </tr>
</table>

&lt;page_number&gt;87&lt;/page_number&gt;

## Page 88

# COBIT® 2019 FRAMEWORK: GOVERNANCE AND MANAGEMENT OBJECTIVES

## A. Component: Process (cont.)

<table>
  <thead>
    <tr>
      <th>Management Practice</th>
      <th>Example Metrics</th>
    </tr>
  </thead>
  <tbody>
    <tr>
      <td><strong>APO05.02 Evaluate and select programs to fund.</strong><br>Based on requirements for the overall investment portfolio mix and the I&T strategic plan and road map, evaluate and prioritize program business cases and decide on investment proposals. Allocate funds and initiate programs.</td>
      <td>a. Percent of projects in the I&T project portfolio that can be directly traced back to the I&T strategy<br>b. Percent of business units involved in the evaluation and prioritization process</td>
    </tr>
    <tr>
      <td colspan="2"><strong>Activities</strong></td>
    </tr>
    <tr>
      <td colspan="2">1. Identify and classify investment opportunities in line with investment portfolio categories. Specify expected enterprise outcome(s), initiatives required to achieve expected outcome(s), high-level costs, dependencies and risk. Specify methodology for measuring outcomes, cost and risk.</td>
    </tr>
    <tr>
      <td colspan="2">2. Perform detailed assessment of all program business cases. Evaluate strategic alignment, enterprise benefit, risk and availability of resources.</td>
    </tr>
    <tr>
      <td colspan="2">3. Assess impact of adding potential programs on overall investment portfolio, including changes that might be required to other programs.</td>
    </tr>
    <tr>
      <td colspan="2">4. Decide which candidate programs should be moved to the active investment portfolio. Decide whether rejected programs should be held for future consideration or provided with seed funding to determine if business case can be improved or discarded.</td>
    </tr>
    <tr>
      <td colspan="2">5. Determine required milestones for each selected program’s full economic life cycle. Allocate and reserve total program funding per milestone. Move the program into the active investment portfolio.</td>
    </tr>
    <tr>
      <td colspan="2">6. Establish procedures to communicate the cost, benefit and risk-related aspects of portfolios for consideration in budget prioritization, cost management and benefit management processes.</td>
    </tr>
    <tr>
      <td colspan="2"><strong>Related Guidance (Standards, Frameworks, Compliance Requirements)</strong></td>
    </tr>
    <tr>
      <td colspan="2">PMBOK Guide Sixth Edition, 2017</td>
    </tr>
    <tr>
      <td colspan="2"><strong>Management Practice</strong></td>
    </tr>
    <tr>
      <td><strong>APO05.03 Monitor, optimize and report on investment portfolio performance.</strong><br>On a regular basis, monitor and optimize the performance of the investment portfolio and individual programs throughout the entire investment life cycle. Ensure continuous follow-up on the alignment of the portfolio with I&T strategy.</td>
      <td>a. Trends in ROI of initiatives included in the I&T strategy<br>b. Level of satisfaction with the portfolio monitoring reports<br>c. Percent of programs aligned with enterprise business requirements</td>
    </tr>
    <tr>
      <td colspan="2"><strong>Activities</strong></td>
    </tr>
    <tr>
      <td colspan="2">1. Review portfolio regularly to identify and exploit synergies, eliminate duplication among programs, and identify and mitigate risk.</td>
    </tr>
    <tr>
      <td colspan="2">2. When changes occur, reevaluate and reprioritize portfolio to ensure alignment with business and I&T strategy. Maintain target mix of investments so that the portfolio optimizes overall value. Programs may be changed, deferred or retired, and new programs may be initiated, to rebalance and optimize portfolio.</td>
    </tr>
    <tr>
      <td colspan="2">3. Adjust enterprise targets, forecasts, budgets and, if required, degree of monitoring to reflect expenditures and enterprise benefits attributable to programs in the active investment portfolio. Charge back program expenditures. Establish flexible budgeting processes so that promising projects get resources to scale quickly.</td>
    </tr>
    <tr>
      <td colspan="2">4. Develop metrics to measure I&T contribution to the enterprise. Establish appropriate performance targets reflecting required I&T and enterprise capability targets. Use guidance from external experts and benchmark data to develop metrics.</td>
    </tr>
    <tr>
      <td colspan="2">5. Provide an accurate view of the performance of the investment portfolio to all stakeholders.</td>
    </tr>
    <tr>
      <td colspan="2">6. Provide reports for senior management’s review of enterprise progress towards identified goals, stating what still needs to be spent and accomplished over given time frames.</td>
    </tr>
    <tr>
      <td colspan="2">7. In regular performance monitoring, include information on the extent to which planned objectives have been achieved, risk mitigated, capabilities created, deliverables obtained and performance targets met.</td>
    </tr>
    <tr>
      <td colspan="2">8. Identify deviations for budget vs. actual spend and expected ROI on investments.</td>
    </tr>
    <tr>
      <td colspan="2"><strong>Related Guidance (Standards, Frameworks, Compliance Requirements)</strong></td>
    </tr>
    <tr>
      <td colspan="2">No related guidance for this management practice</td>
    </tr>
  </tbody>
</table>

&lt;page_number&gt;88&lt;/page_number&gt;

## Page 89

CHAPTER 4
COBIT GOVERNANCE AND MANAGEMENT OBJECTIVES—DETAILED GUIDANCE

A. Component: Process (cont.)

<table>
<thead>
<tr>
<th>Management Practice</th>
<th>Example Metrics</th>
</tr>
</thead>
<tbody>
<tr>
<td><strong>APO05.04 Maintain portfolios.</strong><br>Maintain portfolios of investment programs and projects, I&T products and services, and I&T assets.</td>
<td>a. Number of completed programs and projects<br>b. Time since last update of services portfolio</td>
</tr>
</tbody>
</table>

<table>
<thead>
<tr>
<th>Activities</th>
<th>Capability Level</th>
</tr>
</thead>
<tbody>
<tr>
<td>1. Create and maintain portfolios of I&T-enabled investment programs, I&T services and I&T assets, which form the basis for the current I&T budget and support the I&T tactical and strategic plans.</td>
<td rowspan="3">3</td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td>2. Work with service delivery managers to maintain the service portfolios. Work with operations managers, product managers and architects to maintain the asset portfolios. Prioritize portfolios to support investment decisions.</td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td>3. Remove a program from the active investment portfolio when the desired enterprise benefits have been achieved or when it is clear that benefits will not be achieved within the value criteria set for the program.</td>
</tr>
</tbody>
</table>

<table>
<thead>
<tr>
<th>Management Practice</th>
<th>Example Metrics</th>
</tr>
</thead>
<tbody>
<tr>
<td><strong>APO05.05 Manage benefits achievement.</strong><br>Monitor the benefits of providing and maintaining appropriate I&T products, services and capabilities, based on the agreed and current business case.</td>
<td>a. Percent of changes from the investment program reflected in the relevant I&T portfolios<br>b. Percent of stakeholders satisfied with efforts to monitor the benefits of providing and maintaining appropriate I&T services and capabilities, based on the agreed and current business case</td>
</tr>
</tbody>
</table>

<table>
<thead>
<tr>
<th>Activities</th>
<th>Capability Level</th>
</tr>
</thead>
<tbody>
<tr>
<td>1. Use the agreed metrics and track how benefits are achieved, how they evolve throughout the life cycle of programs and projects, how they are being delivered from I&T products and services, and how they compare to internal and industry benchmarks. Communicate results to stakeholders.</td>
<td>4</td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td>2. Implement corrective action when achieved benefits significantly deviate from expected benefits. Update the business case for new initiatives and implement business process and service improvements as required.</td>
<td>5</td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td>3. Consider obtaining guidance from external experts, industry leaders and comparative benchmarking data to test and improve the metrics and targets.</td>
<td></td>
</tr>
</tbody>
</table>

## Page 93

# CHAPTER 4
## COBIT GOVERNANCE AND MANAGEMENT OBJECTIVES—DETAILED GUIDANCE

<table>
  <tr>
    <td style="vertical-align: top;">
      **Domain:** Align, Plan and Organize<br>
      **Management Objective:** APO06 – Managed Budget and Costs
    </td>
    <td style="vertical-align: top;">
      **Focus Area:** COBIT Core Model
    </td>
  </tr>
</table>

### Description
Manage the I&T-related financial activities in both the business and IT functions, covering budget, cost and benefit management and prioritization of spending through the use of formal budgeting practices and a fair and equitable system of allocating costs to the enterprise. Consult stakeholders to identify and control the total costs and benefits within the context of the I&T strategic and tactical plans. Initiate corrective action where needed.

### Purpose
Foster a partnership between IT and enterprise stakeholders to enable the effective and efficient use of I&T-related resources and provide transparency and accountability of the cost and business value of solutions and services. Enable the enterprise to make informed decisions regarding the use of I&T solutions and services.

The management objective supports the achievement of a set of primary enterprise and alignment goals:

<table>
  <tr>
    <td style="vertical-align: top; width: 50%;">
      ### Enterprise Goals
      *   EG01 Portfolio of competitive products and services
      *   EG04 Quality of financial information
      *   EG07 Quality of management information
      *   EG08 Optimization of internal business process functionality
      *   EG09 Optimization of business process costs
      *   EG12 Managed digital transformation programs
    </td>
    <td style="vertical-align: top; width: 50%;">
      ### Alignment Goals
      *   AG04 Quality of technology-related financial information
      *   AG09 Delivering programs on time, on budget and meeting requirements and quality standards
    </td>
  </tr>
  <tr>
    <td style="vertical-align: top;">
      ### Example Metrics for Enterprise Goals
      EG01
      a. Percent of products and services that meet or exceed targets in revenues and/or market share
      b. Percent of products and services that meet or exceed customer satisfaction targets
      c. Percent of products and services that provide competitive advantage
      d. Time to market for new products and services
      EG04
      a. Satisfaction survey of key stakeholders regarding the transparency, understanding and accuracy of enterprise financial information
      b. Cost of noncompliance with finance-related regulations
      EG07
      a. Degree of board and executive management satisfaction with decision-making information
      b. Number of incidents caused by incorrect business decisions based on inaccurate information
      c. Time to provide information supporting effective business decisions
      d. Timeliness of management information
      EG08
      a. Satisfaction levels of board and executive management with business process capabilities
      b. Satisfaction levels of customers with service delivery capabilities
      c. Satisfaction levels of suppliers with supply chain capabilities
      EG09
      a. Ratio of cost vs. achieved service levels
      b. Satisfaction levels of board and executive management with business processing costs
      EG12
      a. Number of programs on time and within budget
      b. Percent of stakeholders satisfied with program delivery
      c. Percent of business transformation programs stopped
      d. Percent of business transformation programs with regular reported status updates
    </td>
    <td style="vertical-align: top;">
      ### Example Metrics for Alignment Goals
      AG04
      a. Satisfaction of key stakeholders regarding the level of transparency, understanding and accuracy of I&T financial information
      b. Percent of I&T services with defined and approved operational costs and expected benefits
      AG09
      a. Number of programs/projects on time and within budget
      b. Number of programs needing significant rework due to quality defects
      c. Percent of stakeholders satisfied with program/project quality
    </td>
  </tr>
</table>

Align, Plan and Organize

&lt;page_number&gt;93&lt;/page_number&gt;

## Page 94

# COBIT® 2019 FRAMEWORK: GOVERNANCE AND MANAGEMENT OBJECTIVES

<table>
  <thead>
    <tr>
      <th colspan="3">A. Component: Process</th>
    </tr>
    <tr>
      <th>Management Practice</th>
      <th>Example Metrics</th>
      <th></th>
    </tr>
  </thead>
  <tbody>
    <tr>
      <td>
        **APO06.01 Manage finance and accounting.**<br>
        Establish and maintain a method to manage and account for all I&T-related costs, investments and depreciation as an integral part of enterprise financial systems and accounts. Report using the enterprise's financial measurement systems.
      </td>
      <td>
        a. Numbers of deviations between expected and actual budget categories<br>
        b. Usefulness of financial information as input to business cases for new investment in I&T assets and services
      </td>
      <td></td>
    </tr>
    <tr>
      <td colspan="3">
        <strong>Activities</strong>
      </td>
    </tr>
    <tr>
      <td colspan="3">1. Define processes, inputs, outputs and responsibilities for the financial management and accounting of I&T in alignment with the enterprise budgeting and cost accounting policies and approach. Define how to analyze and report (to whom and how) on the I&T budget control process.</td>
    </tr>
    <tr>
      <td colspan="3">2. Define a classification scheme to identify all I&T-related cost elements (capital expenditures [capex] vs. operational expenses [opex], hardware, software, people, etc.). Identify how they are captured.</td>
    </tr>
    <tr>
      <td colspan="3">3. Use financial information to provide input to business cases for new investments in I&T assets and services.</td>
    </tr>
    <tr>
      <td colspan="3">4. Ensure that costs are maintained in the I&T assets and services portfolios.</td>
    </tr>
    <tr>
      <td colspan="3">5. Establish and maintain practices for financial planning and the optimization of recurring operational costs to deliver maximum value to the enterprise for the least expenditure.</td>
    </tr>
    <tr>
      <td colspan="3">
        <strong>Related Guidance (Standards, Frameworks, Compliance Requirements)</strong>
      </td>
    </tr>
    <tr>
      <td colspan="3">ITIL V3, 2011</td>
    </tr>
    <tr>
      <td colspan="3">
        <strong>Detailed Reference</strong><br>
        Service Strategy, 4.3 Financial management for IT services
      </td>
    </tr>
    <tr>
      <td colspan="3">
        <strong>Management Practice</strong>
      </td>
    </tr>
    <tr>
      <td>
        **APO06.02 Prioritize resource allocation.**<br>
        Implement a decision-making process to prioritize the allocation of resources and establish rules for discretionary investments by individual business units. Include the potential use of external service providers and consider the buy, develop and rent options.
      </td>
      <td>
        a. Number of resource-allocation issues escalated<br>
        b. Percent of alignment of I&T resources with high-priority initiatives
      </td>
      <td></td>
    </tr>
    <tr>
      <td colspan="3">
        <strong>Activities</strong>
      </td>
    </tr>
    <tr>
      <td colspan="3">1. Rank all I&T initiatives and budget requests based on business cases and strategic and tactical priorities. Establish procedures to determine budget allocations and cutoff.</td>
    </tr>
    <tr>
      <td colspan="3">2. Allocate business and IT resources (including external service providers) within the high-level budget allocations for I&T-enabled programs, services and assets. Consider the options for buying or developing capitalized assets and services vs. externally utilized assets and services on a pay-for-use basis.</td>
    </tr>
    <tr>
      <td colspan="3">3. Establish a procedure to communicate budget decisions and review them with the business unit budget holders.</td>
    </tr>
    <tr>
      <td colspan="3">4. Identify, communicate and resolve significant impacts of budget decisions on business cases, portfolios and strategy plans. For example, this may include when budgets require revision due to changing enterprise circumstances or when they are not sufficient to support strategic objectives or business case objectives).</td>
    </tr>
    <tr>
      <td colspan="3">5. Obtain ratification from the executive committee for the I&T budget implications that negatively impact the entity's strategic or tactical plans. Suggest actions to resolve these impacts.</td>
    </tr>
    <tr>
      <td colspan="3">
        <strong>Related Guidance (Standards, Frameworks, Compliance Requirements)</strong>
      </td>
    </tr>
    <tr>
      <td colspan="3">No related guidance for this management practice</td>
    </tr>
    <tr>
      <td colspan="3">
        <strong>Management Practice</strong>
      </td>
    </tr>
    <tr>
      <td>
        **APO06.03 Create and maintain budgets.**<br>
        Prepare a budget reflecting investment priorities based on the portfolio of I&T-enabled programs and I&T services.
      </td>
      <td>
        a. Number of budget changes due to omissions and errors<br>
        b. Usefulness of I&T budget in identifying all expected I&T costs of I&T-enabled programs, services and assets
      </td>
      <td></td>
    </tr>
  </tbody>
</table>

&lt;page_number&gt;94&lt;/page_number&gt;

## Page 95

CHAPTER 4
COBIT GOVERNANCE AND MANAGEMENT OBJECTIVES—DETAILED GUIDANCE

A. Component: Process (cont.)

<table>
<thead>
<tr>
<th>Activities</th>
<th>Capability Level</th>
</tr>
</thead>
<tbody>
<tr>
<td>1. Implement a formal I&T budget, including all expected I&T costs of I&T-enabled programs, services and assets.</td>
<td>2</td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td>2. When creating the budget, consider the following components: alignment with the business; alignment with the sourcing strategy; authorized sources of funding; internal resource costs, including personnel, information assets and accommodations; third-party costs, including outsourcing contracts, consultants and service providers; capital and operational expenses; and cost elements that depend on the workload.</td>
<td></td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td>3. Document the rationale to justify contingencies and review them regularly.</td>
<td></td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td>4. Instruct process, service and program owners, as well as project and asset managers, to plan budgets.</td>
<td></td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td>5. Review the budget plans and make decisions about budget allocations. Compile and adjust the budget based on changing enterprise needs and financial considerations.</td>
<td>3</td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td>6. Record, maintain and communicate the current I&T budget, including committed expenditures and current expenditures, considering I&T projects recorded in the I&T-enabled investment portfolios and operation and maintenance of asset and service portfolios.</td>
<td></td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td>7. Monitor the effectiveness of the different aspects of budgeting.</td>
<td>4</td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td>8. Use the monitoring results to implement improvements and ensure that future budgets are more accurate, reliable and cost-effective.</td>
<td>5</td>
</tr>
</tbody>
</table>

<table>
<thead>
<tr>
<th>Related Guidance (Standards, Frameworks, Compliance Requirements)</th>
<th>Detailed Reference</th>
</tr>
</thead>
<tbody>
<tr>
<td>ISO/IEC 20000-1:2011(E)</td>
<td>6.4 Budgeting and accounting for services</td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td>PMBOK Guide Sixth Edition, 2017</td>
<td>Part 1: 7. Project cost management</td>
</tr>
</tbody>
</table>

Align, Plan and Organize

<table>
<thead>
<tr>
<th>Management Practice</th>
<th>Example Metrics</th>
</tr>
</thead>
<tbody>
<tr>
<td>APO06.04 Model and allocate costs.<br>Establish and use an I&T costing model based, for example, on the service definition. This approach ensures that allocation of costs for services is identifiable, measurable and predictable, and encourages the responsible use of resources, including those provided by service providers. Regularly review and benchmark the cost/chargeback model to maintain its relevance and appropriateness for evolving business and IT activities.</td>
<td>a. Percent of overall I&T costs that are allocated according to the agreed cost models<br>b. Number of reviews and benchmarks of the cost/chargeback model and its appropriateness to evolving business and I&T activities</td>
</tr>
</tbody>
</table>

<table>
<thead>
<tr>
<th>Activities</th>
<th>Capability Level</th>
</tr>
</thead>
<tbody>
<tr>
<td>1. Decide on a cost allocation model that enables fair, transparent, repeatable and comparable allocation of I&T-related costs to users. A basic allocation model example is the even spread of shared I&T-related costs. This is a very simple allocation model that is easy to apply; however, depending on the context of the enterprise, it is often viewed as unfair and it does not encourage responsible use of resources. An activity-based costing scheme, in which costs are allocated to IT services and charged to users of these services, enables a more transparent and comparable allocation of cost.</td>
<td>3</td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td>2. Inspect service definition catalogs to identify services subject to user chargeback and those that are shared services.</td>
<td></td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td>3. Design the cost model to be transparent enough to allow users to identify their actual usage and charges by using categories and cost drivers that make sense for the user (e.g., cost per help desk call, cost per software license) and to better enable predictability of I&T costs and efficient and effective utilization of I&T resources. Analyze cost drivers (time spent per activity, expenses, portion of fixed vs. variable costs, etc.). Decide on appropriate differentiation (e.g., different categories of users with different weights) and use cost approximations or averages when actual costs are highly variable in nature.</td>
<td></td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td>4. Explain the cost model principles and outcome to key stakeholders. Obtain their feedback for further fine-tuning toward a transparent and comprehensive model.</td>
<td></td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td>5. Obtain approval of key stakeholders and communicate the I&T costing model to the management of user departments.</td>
<td></td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td>6. Communicate important changes in the cost/chargeback model principles to key stakeholders and management of user departments.</td>
<td></td>
</tr>
</tbody>
</table>

<table>
<thead>
<tr>
<th>Related Guidance (Standards, Frameworks, Compliance Requirements)</th>
<th>Detailed Reference</th>
</tr>
</thead>
<tbody>
<tr>
<td>No related guidance for this management practice</td>
<td></td>
</tr>
</tbody>
</table>

&lt;page_number&gt;95&lt;/page_number&gt;

## Page 96

# COBIT® 2019 FRAMEWORK: GOVERNANCE AND MANAGEMENT OBJECTIVES

## A. Component: Process (cont.)

<table>
  <thead>
    <tr>
      <th>Management Practice</th>
      <th>Example Metrics</th>
    </tr>
  </thead>
  <tbody>
    <tr>
      <td><strong>APO06.05 Manage costs.</strong><br>Implement a cost management process that compares actual costs against budget. Costs should be monitored and reported. Deviations from budget should be identified in a timely manner and their impact on enterprise processes and services assessed.</td>
      <td>a. Percent of variance among budgets, forecasts and actual costs<br>b. Timeliness of monitoring and reporting in the case of deviations and the impact of deviations on enterprise processes and services assessed</td>
    </tr>
    <tr>
      <td colspan="2"><strong>Activities</strong></td>
    </tr>
    <tr>
      <td>1. Obtain approval of key stakeholders and communicate the I&T costing model to the management of user departments.</td>
      <td>Capability Level<br>2</td>
    </tr>
    <tr>
      <td>2. Establish time scales for the operation of the cost management process in line with budgeting and accounting requirements and timeline.</td>
      <td></td>
    </tr>
    <tr>
      <td>3. Define a method for the collection of relevant data to identify deviations in budget vs. actuals, investment ROI, service cost trends, etc.</td>
      <td></td>
    </tr>
    <tr>
      <td>4. Define how costs are consolidated for the appropriate levels in the enterprise (central IT vs. IT budget within business departments) and how they will be presented to the stakeholders. The reports provide information on costs per cost category, budget vs. actuals status, top spending, etc., to enable the timely identification of required corrective actions.</td>
      <td>Capability Level<br>3</td>
    </tr>
    <tr>
      <td>5. Instruct those responsible for cost management to capture, collect and consolidate the data, and present and report the data to the appropriate budget owners. Budget analysts and owners jointly analyze deviations and compare performance to internal and industry benchmarks. They should establish and maintain the overheads allocation method. The result of the analysis provides an explanation of significant deviations and the suggested corrective actions.</td>
      <td></td>
    </tr>
    <tr>
      <td>6. Ensure that the appropriate levels of management review the results of the analysis and approve suggested corrective actions.</td>
      <td></td>
    </tr>
    <tr>
      <td>7. Ensure that changes in cost structures and enterprise needs are identified and budgets and forecasts are revised as required.</td>
      <td>Capability Level<br>4</td>
    </tr>
    <tr>
      <td>8. At regular intervals, and especially when budgets are cut due to financial constraints, identify ways to optimize costs and introduce efficiencies without jeopardizing services.</td>
      <td>Capability Level<br>5</td>
    </tr>
    <tr>
      <td><strong>Related Guidance (Standards, Frameworks, Compliance Requirements)</strong></td>
      <td><strong>Detailed Reference</strong></td>
    </tr>

<td>Effective and efficient management of I&T is supported by a culture of transparency on budget, costs and benefits throughout the organization. ## Page 99

# CHAPTER 4
## COBIT GOVERNANCE AND MANAGEMENT OBJECTIVES—DETAILED GUIDANCE

<table>
  <tr>
    <td style="vertical-align: top;">
      **Domain:** Align, Plan and Organize<br>
      **Management Objective:** APO07 – Managed Human Resources
    </td>
    <td style="vertical-align: top;">
      **Focus Area:** COBIT Core Model
    </td>
  </tr>
</table>

### Description
Provide a structured approach to ensure optimal recruitment/acquisition, planning, evaluation and development of human resources (both internal and external).

### Purpose
Optimize human resources capabilities to meet enterprise objectives.

The management objective supports the achievement of a set of primary enterprise and alignment goals:

<table>
  <tr>
    <td style="vertical-align: top; width: 50%;">
      **Enterprise Goals**
      <ul>
        <li>• EG01 Portfolio of competitive products and services</li>
        <li>• EG10 Staff skills, motivation and productivity</li>
        <li>• EG13 Product and business innovation</li>
      </ul>
      **Example Metrics for Enterprise Goals**
      <table>
        <tr>
          <td>EG01</td>
          <td>
            a. Percent of products and services that meet or exceed targets in revenues and/or market share<br>
            b. Percent of products and services that meet or exceed customer satisfaction targets<br>
            c. Percent of products and services that provide competitive advantage<br>
            d. Time to market for new products and services
          </td>
        </tr>
        <tr>
          <td>EG10</td>
          <td>
            a. Staff productivity compared to benchmarks<br>
            b. Level of stakeholder satisfaction with staff expertise and skills<br>
            c. Percent of staff whose skills are insufficient for competency in their role<br>
            d. Percent of satisfied staff
          </td>
        </tr>
        <tr>
          <td>EG13</td>
          <td>
            a. Level of awareness and understanding of business innovation opportunities<br>
            b. Stakeholder satisfaction with levels of product and innovation expertise and ideas<br>
            c. Number of approved product and service initiatives resulting from innovative ideas
          </td>
        </tr>
      </table>
    </td>
    <td style="vertical-align: top; width: 50%;">
      **Alignment Goals**
      <ul>
        <li>• AG12 Competent and motivated staff with mutual understanding of technology and business</li>
        <li>• AG13 Knowledge, expertise and initiatives for business innovation</li>
      </ul>
      **Example Metrics for Alignment Goals**
      <table>
        <tr>
          <td>AG12</td>
          <td>
            a. Percent of I&T-savvy business people (i.e., those having the required knowledge and understanding of I&T to guide, direct, innovate and see I&T opportunities in their domain of business expertise)<br>
            b. Percent of business-savvy I&T people (i.e., those having the required knowledge and understanding of relevant business domains to guide, direct, innovate and see I&T opportunities for the business domain)<br>
            c. Number or percentage of business people with technology management experience
          </td>
        </tr>
        <tr>
          <td>AG13</td>
          <td>
            a. Level of business executive awareness and understanding of I&T innovation possibilities<br>
            b. Number of approved initiatives resulting from innovative I&T ideas<br>
            c. Number of innovation champions recognized/awarded
          </td>
        </tr>
      </table>
    </td>
  </tr>
</table>

### A. Component: Process

<table>
  <tr>
    <td style="vertical-align: top;">
      **Management Practice**
      <table>
        <tr>
          <td>APO07.01 Acquire and maintain adequate and appropriate staffing.<br>
            Establish and maintain a method to manage and account for all I&T-related costs, investments and depreciation as an integral part of the enterprise financial systems and accounts. Report using the enterprise’s financial measurement systems.
          </td>
        </tr>
      </table>
      **Activities**
      <ol>
        <li>Evaluate staffing requirements on a regular basis or upon major changes. Ensure that both the enterprise and the IT function have sufficient resources to support enterprise goals and objectives, business processes and controls, and I&T-enabled initiatives adequately and appropriately.</li>
        <li>Maintain business and IT personnel recruitment and retention processes in line with the overall enterprise’s personnel policies and procedures.</li>
        <li>Establish flexible resource arrangements, such as the use of transfers, external contractors and third-party service arrangements, to support changing business needs.</li>
        <li>Include background checks in the IT recruitment process for employees, contractors and vendors. The extent and frequency of these checks should depend on the sensitivity and/or criticality of the function.</li>
      </ol>
    </td>
    <td style="vertical-align: top;">
      **Example Metrics**
      <table>
        <tr>
          <td>a. Average duration of vacancies</td>
        </tr>
        <tr>
          <td>b. Percent of IT posts vacant</td>
        </tr>
        <tr>
          <td>c. Percent of staff turnover</td>
        </tr>
      </table>
    </td>
    <td style="vertical-align: top; text-align: center;">
      **Capability Level**
      <table>
        <tr>
          <td>2</td>
        </tr>
        <tr>
          <td>3</td>
        </tr>
      </table>
    </td>
  </tr>
</table>

&lt;page_number&gt;99&lt;/page_number&gt;

# COBIT® 2019 FRAMEWORK: GOVERNANCE AND MANAGEMENT OBJECTIVES

## A. Component: Process (cont.)

      <td>SFIA and skills management—Acquire</td>
    </tr>
  </tbody>
</table>

### Management Practice

**APO07.02 Identify key IT personnel.**
Identify key IT personnel. Use knowledge capture (documentation), knowledge sharing, succession planning and staff backup to minimize reliance on a single individual performing a critical job function.

#### Activities

1. As a security precaution, provide guidelines on a minimum time of annual vacation to be taken by key individuals.
2. Take appropriate actions regarding job changes, especially job terminations.
3. Use knowledge capture (documentation), knowledge sharing, succession planning, staff backup, cross-training and job rotation initiatives to minimize reliance on a single individual performing a critical job function.
4. Regularly test staff backup plans.

<table>
  <thead>
    <tr>
      <th>Example Metrics</th>
      <th>Capability Level</th>
    </tr>
  </thead>
  <tbody>
    <tr>
      <td>a. Percent of critical jobs where the enterprise relies on a single individual<br>b. Number of staff backup plans performed</td>
      <td>2</td>
    </tr>
    <tr>
      <td></td>
      <td>3</td>
    </tr>
  </tbody>
</table>

### Related Guidance (Standards, Frameworks, Compliance Requirements)

<table>
  <thead>
    <tr>
      <th>Related Guidance (Standards, Frameworks, Compliance Requirements)</th>
      <th>Detailed Reference</th>
    </tr>
  </thead>
  <tbody>
    <tr>
      <td>CMMI Cybermaturity Platform, 2018</td>
      <td>RI.RR Identification of Roles and Responsibilities</td>
    </tr>
    <tr>
      <td>Skills Framework for the Information Age V6, 2015</td>
      <td>SFIA and skills management—Acquire</td>
    </tr>
  </tbody>
</table>

### Management Practice

**APO07.03 Maintain the skills and competencies of personnel.**
Define and manage the skills and competencies required of personnel. Regularly verify that personnel have the competencies to fulfill their roles on the basis of their education, training and/or experience. Verify that these competencies are being maintained, using qualification and certification programs where appropriate. Provide employees with ongoing learning and opportunities to maintain their knowledge, skills and competencies at a level required to achieve enterprise goals.

#### Activities

1. Identify currently available skills and competencies of internal and external resources.
2. Identify gaps between required and available skills. Develop action plans, such as training (technical and behavioral skills), recruitment, redeployment and changed sourcing strategies, to address the gaps on an individual and collective basis.
3. Review training materials and programs on a regular basis. Ensure adequacy with respect to changing enterprise requirements and their impact on necessary knowledge, skills and abilities.
4. Provide access to knowledge repositories to support the development of skills and competencies.
5. Develop and deliver training programs based on organizational and process requirements, including requirements for enterprise knowledge, internal control, ethical conduct, security and privacy.
6. Conduct regular reviews to assess the evolution of the skills and competencies of the internal and external resources. Review succession planning.

<table>
  <thead>
    <tr>
      <th>Example Metrics</th>
      <th>Capability Level</th>
    </tr>
  </thead>
  <tbody>
    <tr>
      <td>a. Identified key skills and competencies missing in the resource matrix<br>b. Number of identified gaps between required and available skills<br>c. Number of training programs provided</td>
      <td>2</td>
    </tr>
    <tr>
      <td></td>
      <td>3</td>
    </tr>
    <tr>
      <td></td>
      <td>4</td>
    </tr>
  </tbody>
</table>

### Related Guidance (Standards, Frameworks, Compliance Requirements)

  <thead>
    <tr>
      <th>Related Guidance (Standards, Frameworks, Compliance Requirements)</th>
      <th>Detailed Reference</th>
    </tr>
  </thead>

## Page 101

CHAPTER 4
COBIT GOVERNANCE AND MANAGEMENT OBJECTIVES—DETAILED GUIDANCE

<table>
  <thead>
    <tr>
      <th colspan="3">A. Component: Process (cont.)</th>
    </tr>
  </thead>
  <tbody>
    <tr>
      <td><strong>Management Practice</strong></td>
      <td><strong>Example Metrics</strong></td>
      <td></td>
    </tr>
    <tr>
      <td><strong>APO07.04 Assess and recognize/reward employee job performance.</strong> Conduct timely, regular performance evaluations against individual objectives derived from enterprise goals, established standards, specific job responsibilities, and the skills and competency framework. Implement a remuneration/recognition process that rewards successful attainment of performance goals.</td>
      <td>a. Number of official feedback moments and 360-degree evaluations performed<br>b. Number and value of rewards given to staff</td>
      <td></td>
    </tr>
    <tr>
      <td><strong>Activities</strong></td>
      <td></td>
      <td><strong>Capability Level</strong></td>
    </tr>
    <tr>
      <td>1. Consider functional/enterprise goals as the context for setting individual goals.</td>
      <td></td>
      <td>2</td>
    </tr>
    <tr>
      <td>2. Set individual goals aligned with the relevant I&T and enterprise goals. Base goals on specific, measurable, achievable, relevant and time-bound (SMART) objectives that reflect core competencies, enterprise values and skills required for the role(s).</td>
      <td></td>
      <td></td>
    </tr>
    <tr>
      <td>3. Provide timely feedback regarding performance against the individual's goals.</td>
      <td></td>
      <td></td>
    </tr>
    <tr>
      <td>4. Provide specific instructions for the use and storage of personal information in the evaluation process, in compliance with applicable personal data and employment legislation.</td>
      <td></td>
      <td></td>
    </tr>
    <tr>
      <td>5. Compile 360-degree performance evaluation results.</td>
      <td></td>
      <td>3</td>
    </tr>
    <tr>
      <td>6. Provide formal career planning and professional development plans based on the results of the evaluation process to encourage competency development and opportunities for personal advancement and to reduce dependence on key individuals. Provide employee coaching on performance and conduct whenever appropriate.</td>
      <td></td>
      <td></td>
    </tr>
    <tr>
      <td>7. Implement a remuneration/recognition process that rewards appropriate commitment, competency development and successful attainment of performance goals. Ensure that the process is applied consistently and in line with organizational policies.</td>
      <td></td>
      <td></td>
    </tr>
    <tr>
      <td>8. Implement and communicate a disciplinary process.</td>
      <td></td>
      <td></td>
    </tr>
    <tr>
      <td><strong>Related Guidance (Standards, Frameworks, Compliance Requirements)</strong></td>
      <td><strong>Detailed Reference</strong></td>
      <td></td>
    </tr>
    <tr>
      <td>Skills Framework for the Information Age V6, 2015</td>
      <td>SFIA and skills management—Develop</td>
      <td></td>
    </tr>
    <tr>
      <td><strong>Management Practice</strong></td>
      <td><strong>Example Metrics</strong></td>
      <td></td>
    </tr>
    <tr>
      <td><strong>APO07.05 Plan and track the usage of IT and business human resources.</strong> Understand and track the current and future demand for business and IT human resources with responsibilities for enterprise I&T. Identify shortfalls and provide input into sourcing plans, enterprise and IT recruitment processes, and business and IT recruitment processes.</td>
      <td>a. Number of identified shortfalls and missing skills in planning for staffing<br>b. Time spent per full-time equivalent (FTE) on assignments and projects</td>
      <td></td>
    </tr>
    <tr>
      <td><strong>Activities</strong></td>
      <td></td>
      <td><strong>Capability Level</strong></td>
    </tr>
    <tr>
      <td>1. Create and maintain an inventory of business and IT human resources.</td>
      <td></td>
      <td>2</td>
    </tr>
    <tr>
      <td>2. Understand the current and future demand for human resources to support the achievement of I&T objectives and to deliver services and solutions based on the portfolio of current I&T-related initiatives, the future investment portfolio and day-to-day operational needs.</td>
      <td></td>
      <td>3</td>
    </tr>
    <tr>
      <td>3. Identify shortfalls and provide input into sourcing plans as well as enterprise and IT recruitment processes. Create and review the staffing plan, keeping track of actual usage.</td>
      <td></td>
      <td></td>
    </tr>
    <tr>
      <td>4. Maintain adequate information on the time spent on different tasks, assignments, services or projects.</td>
      <td></td>
      <td>4</td>
    </tr>
    <tr>
      <td><strong>Related Guidance (Standards, Frameworks, Compliance Requirements)</strong></td>
      <td><strong>Detailed Reference</strong></td>
      <td></td>
    </tr>
    <tr>
      <td>Skills Framework for the Information Age V6, 2015</td>
      <td>SFIA and skills management—Assess; Reward</td>
      <td></td>
    </tr>
    <tr>
      <td><strong>Management Practice</strong></td>
      <td><strong>Example Metrics</strong></td>
      <td></td>
    </tr>
    <tr>
      <td><strong>APO07.06 Manage contract staff.</strong> Ensure that consultants and contract personnel who support the enterprise with I&T skills know and comply with the organization’s policies and meet agreed contractual requirements.</td>
      <td>a. Percent of contractors signing off on the enterprise control framework<br>b. Frequency of periodic reviews conducted to ensure correctness and compliance of contractor’s staff</td>
      <td></td>
    </tr>

## Page 102

# COBIT® 2019 FRAMEWORK: GOVERNANCE AND MANAGEMENT OBJECTIVES

## A. Component: Process (cont.)

<table>
<thead>
<tr>
<th>Activities</th>
<th>Capability Level</th>
</tr>
</thead>
<tbody>
<tr>
<td>1. Implement contract staff policies and procedures.</td>
<td rowspan="8">2</td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td>2. At the commencement of the contract, obtain formal agreement from contractors that they are required to comply with the enterprise's I&T control framework, such as policies for security clearance, physical and logical access control, use of facilities, information confidentiality requirements, and nondisclosure agreements.</td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td>3. Advise contractors that management reserves the right to monitor and inspect all usage of IT resources, including email, voice communications, and all programs and data files.</td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td>4. As part of their contracts, provide contractors with a clear definition of their roles and responsibilities, including explicit requirements to document their work to agreed standards and formats.</td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td>5. Review contractors' work and base the approval of payments on the results.</td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td>6. In formal and unambiguous contracts, define all work performed by external parties.</td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td>7. Conduct periodic reviews to ensure that contract staff have signed and agreed on all necessary agreements.</td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td>8. Conduct periodic reviews to ensure that contractors' roles and access rights are appropriate and in line with agreements.</td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td><strong>Related Guidance (Standards, Frameworks, Compliance Requirements)</strong></td>
<td><strong>Detailed Reference</strong></td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td>Skills Framework for the Information Age V6, 2015</td>
<td>SFIA and skills management—Deploy</td>
</tr>
</tbody>
</table>

<tr>
<td>Contract staff policy</td>
<td>Enumerates criteria for augmenting staff with third-party consultants and/or contractors in accordance with enterprise IT procurement policy and the I&T control framework. Specifies what type of work can be performed or augmented by third parties, under what conditions, and when.</td>
<td>National Institute of Standards and Technology Special Publication 800-53, Revision 5 (Draft), August 2017</td>
<td>3.16 Personnel security (PS-1)</td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td>Human resources (HR) policies</td>
<td>Outlines mutual expectations of the enterprise and its employees. Enumerates acceptable and unacceptable employee behaviors in a code of conduct to help manage risk related to human behavior.</td>
<td></td>
<td></td>
</tr>
</tbody>
</table>

<td>National Institute of Standards and Technology Special Publication 800-53, Revision 5, August 2017</td>

## Page 107

# CHAPTER 4
## COBIT GOVERNANCE AND MANAGEMENT OBJECTIVES—DETAILED GUIDANCE

<table>
  <tr>
    <td style="vertical-align: top;">
      **Domain:** Align, Plan and Organize<br>
      **Management Objective:** APO08 – Managed Relationships
    </td>
    <td style="vertical-align: top;">
      **Focus Area:** COBIT Core Model
    </td>
  </tr>
</table>

### Description
Manage relationships with business stakeholders in a formalized and transparent way that ensures mutual trust and a combined focus on achieving the strategic goals within the constraints of budgets and risk tolerance. Base relationships on open and transparent communication, a common language, and the willingness to take ownership and accountability for key decisions on both sides. Business and IT must work together to create successful enterprise outcomes in support of the enterprise objectives.

### Purpose
Enable the right knowledge, skills and behaviors to create improved outcomes, increased confidence, mutual trust and effective use of resources that stimulate a productive relationship with business stakeholders.

The management objective supports the achievement of a set of primary enterprise and alignment goals:

<table>
  <tr>
    <td style="vertical-align: top; width: 50%;">
      ### Enterprise Goals
      <ul>
        <li>• EG01 Portfolio of competitive products and services</li>
        <li>• EG08 Optimization of internal business process functionality</li>
        <li>• EG10 Staff skills, motivation and productivity</li>
        <li>• EG13 Product and business innovation</li>
      </ul>
      <br>
      ### Example Metrics for Enterprise Goals
      <table>
        <tr>
          <td>EG01</td>
          <td>
            <ul>
              <li>a. Percent of products and services that meet or exceed targets in revenues and/or market share</li>
              <li>b. Percent of products and services that meet or exceed customer satisfaction targets</li>
              <li>c. Percent of products and services that provide competitive advantage</li>
              <li>d. Time to market for new products and services</li>
            </ul>
          </td>
        </tr>
        <tr>
          <td>EG08</td>
          <td>
            <ul>
              <li>a. Satisfaction levels of board and executive management with business process capabilities</li>
              <li>b. Satisfaction levels of customers with service delivery capabilities</li>
              <li>c. Satisfaction levels of suppliers with supply chain capabilities</li>
            </ul>
          </td>
        </tr>
        <tr>
          <td>EG10</td>
          <td>
            <ul>
              <li>a. Staff productivity compared to benchmarks</li>
              <li>b. Level of stakeholder satisfaction with staff expertise and skills</li>
              <li>c. Percent of staff whose skills are insufficient for competency in their role</li>
              <li>d. Percent of satisfied staff</li>
            </ul>
          </td>
        </tr>
        <tr>
          <td>EG13</td>
          <td>
            <ul>
              <li>a. Level of awareness and understanding of business innovation opportunities</li>
              <li>b. Stakeholder satisfaction with levels of product and innovation expertise and ideas</li>
              <li>c. Number of approved product and service initiatives resulting from innovative ideas</li>
            </ul>
          </td>
        </tr>
      </table>
    </td>
    <td style="vertical-align: top; width: 50%;">
      ### Alignment Goals
      <ul>
        <li>• AG05 Delivery of I&T services in line with business requirements</li>
        <li>• AG06 Agility to turn business requirements into operational solutions</li>
        <li>• AG12 Competent and motivated staff with mutual understanding of technology and business</li>
        <li>• AG13 Knowledge, expertise and initiatives for business innovation</li>
      </ul>
      <br>
      ### Example Metrics for Alignment Goals
      <table>
        <tr>
          <td>AG05</td>
          <td>
            <ul>
              <li>a. Percent of business stakeholders satisfied that I&T service delivery meets agreed service levels</li>
              <li>b. Number of business disruptions due to I&T service incidents</li>
              <li>c. Percent of users satisfied with the quality of I&T service delivery</li>
            </ul>
          </td>
        </tr>
        <tr>
          <td>AG06</td>
          <td>
            <ul>
              <li>a. Level of satisfaction of business executives with I&T responsiveness to new requirements</li>
              <li>b. Average time to market for new I&T-related services and applications</li>
              <li>c. Average time to turn strategic I&T objectives into agreed and approved initiatives</li>
              <li>d. Number of critical business processes supported by up-to-date infrastructure and applications</li>
            </ul>
          </td>
        </tr>
        <tr>
          <td>AG12</td>
          <td>
            <ul>
              <li>a. Percent of I&T-savvy business people (i.e., those having the required knowledge and understanding of I&T to guide, direct, innovate and see I&T opportunities in their domain of business expertise)</li>
              <li>b. Percent of business-savvy I&T people (i.e., those having the required knowledge and understanding of relevant business domains to guide, direct, innovate and see I&T opportunities for the business domain)</li>
              <li>c. Number or percentage of business people with technology management experience</li>
            </ul>
          </td>
        </tr>
        <tr>
          <td>AG13</td>
          <td>
            <ul>
              <li>a. Level of business executive awareness and understanding of I&T innovation possibilities</li>
              <li>b. Number of approved initiatives resulting from innovative I&T ideas</li>
              <li>c. Number of innovation champions recognized/awarded</li>
            </ul>
          </td>
        </tr>
      </table>
    </td>
  </tr>
</table>

&lt;page_number&gt;107&lt;/page_number&gt;

---


## Page 108

# COBIT® 2019 FRAMEWORK: GOVERNANCE AND MANAGEMENT OBJECTIVES

<table>
  <thead>
    <tr>
      <th colspan="3">A. Component: Process</th>
    </tr>
  </thead>
  <tbody>
    <tr>
      <td rowspan="15" style="vertical-align: top;">Align, Plan and Organize</td>
      <td>Management Practice</td>
      <td>Example Metrics</td>
    </tr>
    <tr>
      <td>APO08.01 Understand business expectations.</td>
      <td>a. Number of identified current business issues<br>b. Number of defined business requirements for I&T-enabled services</td>
    </tr>
    <tr>
      <td colspan="2">Understand current business issues, objectives and expectations for I&T. Ensure that requirements are understood, managed and communicated, and their status agreed and approved.</td>
    </tr>
    <tr>
      <td>Activities</td>
      <td>Capability Level</td>
    </tr>
    <tr>
      <td>1. Identify business stakeholders, their interests and their areas of responsibilities.</td>
      <td>2</td>
    </tr>
    <tr>
      <td>2. Review current enterprise direction, issues, strategic objectives, and alignment with enterprise architecture.</td>
      <td></td>
    </tr>
    <tr>
      <td>3. Understand the current business environment, process constraints or issues, geographical expansion or contraction, and industry/regulatory drivers.</td>
      <td></td>
    </tr>
    <tr>
      <td>4. Maintain an awareness of business processes and associated activities. Understand demand patterns that relate to service volumes and use.</td>
      <td></td>
    </tr>
    <tr>
      <td>5. Manage expectations by ensuring that business units understand priorities, dependencies, financial constraints and the need to schedule requests.</td>
      <td>3</td>
    </tr>
    <tr>
      <td>6. Clarify business expectations for I&T-enabled services and solutions. Ensure that requirements are defined with associated business acceptance criteria and metrics.</td>
      <td>4</td>
    </tr>
    <tr>
      <td>7. Confirm that there is agreement between IT and all business departments on expectations and how they will be measured. Ensure that this agreement is confirmed by all stakeholders.</td>
      <td></td>
    </tr>
    <tr>
      <td>Related Guidance (Standards, Frameworks, Compliance Requirements)</td>
      <td>Detailed Reference</td>
    </tr>
    <tr>
      <td>No related guidance for this management practice</td>
      <td></td>
    </tr>
    <tr>
      <td>Management Practice</td>
      <td>Example Metrics</td>
    </tr>
    <tr>
      <td>APO08.02 Align I&T strategy with business expectations and identify opportunities for IT to enhance the business.</td>
      <td>a. Inclusion rate of technology opportunities in investment proposals<br>b. Survey of business stakeholders regarding their level of technological awareness</td>
    </tr>
    <tr>
      <td colspan="2">Align I&T strategies with current business objectives and expectations to enable IT to be a value-add partner for the business and a governance component for enhanced enterprise performance.</td>
    </tr>
    <tr>
      <td>Activities</td>
      <td>Capability Level</td>
    </tr>
    <tr>
      <td>1. Position IT as a partner to the business. Play a proactive role in identifying and communicating with key stakeholders on opportunities, risk and constraints. This includes current and emerging technologies, services and business process models.</td>
      <td>3</td>
    </tr>
    <tr>
      <td>2. Collaborate on major new initiatives with portfolio, program and project management. Ensure the involvement of the IT organization from the start of a new initiative by providing value-add advice and recommendations (e.g., for business case development, requirements definition, solution design) and by taking ownership for I&T work streams.</td>
      <td></td>
    </tr>
    <tr>
      <td>Related Guidance (Standards, Frameworks, Compliance Requirements)</td>
      <td>Detailed Reference</td>
    </tr>
    <tr>
      <td>ITIL V3, 2011</td>
      <td>Service Strategy, 4.4 Demand management</td>
    </tr>
    <tr>
      <td>Management Practice</td>
      <td>Example Metrics</td>
    </tr>
    <tr>
      <td>APO08.03 Manage the business relationship.</td>
      <td>a. Ratings of user and IT personnel satisfaction surveys<br>b. Percent of relationship roles and responsibilities defined, assigned, and communicated</td>
    </tr>
    <tr>
      <td colspan="2">Manage the relationship between the IT service organization and its business partners. Ensure that relationship roles and responsibilities are defined and assigned, and communication is facilitated.</td>
    </tr>
    <tr>
      <td>Activities</td>
      <td>Capability Level</td>
    </tr>
    <tr>
      <td>1. Assign a relationship manager as a single point of contact for each significant business unit. Ensure that a single counterpart is identified in the business organization and the counterpart has business understanding, sufficient technology awareness and the appropriate level of authority.</td>
      <td>3</td>
    </tr>
    <tr>
      <td>2. Manage the relationship in a formalized and transparent way that ensures a focus on achieving a common and shared goal of successful enterprise outcomes in support of strategic goals and within the constraint of budgets and risk tolerance.</td>
      <td></td>
    </tr>
    <tr>
      <td>3. Define and communicate a complaints and escalation procedure to resolve any relationship issues.</td>
      <td></td>
    </tr>
    <tr>
      <td>4. Ensure that key decisions are agreed and approved by relevant accountable stakeholders.</td>
      <td></td>
    </tr>
    <tr>
      <td>5. Plan specific interactions and schedules based on mutually agreed objectives and common language (service and performance review meetings, review of new strategies or plans, etc.).</td>
      <td>4</td>
    </tr>
  </tbody>
</table>

<td>a. Time since last update of end-to-end communication plan to business<br/>b. Percent of business owner satisfaction with coordination of the end to end delivery of I&T services and solutions</td>

<table>
<thead>
<tr>
<th>Activities</th>
<th>Capability Level</th>
</tr>
</thead>
<tbody>
<tr>
<td>1. Coordinate and communicate changes and transition activities such as project or change plans, schedules, release policies, release known errors, and training awareness.</td>
<td>2</td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td>2. Coordinate and communicate operational activities, roles and responsibilities, including the definition of request types, hierarchical escalation, major outages (planned and unplanned), and content and frequency of service reports.</td>
<td></td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td>3. Take ownership of the response to the business for major events that may influence the relationship with the business. Provide direct support if required.</td>
<td></td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td>4. Maintain an end-to-end communication plan that defines the content, frequency and recipients of service delivery information, including status of value delivered and any risk identified.</td>
<td>3</td>
</tr>
</tbody>
</table>

</tr>

</table>

<thead>
<tr>
<th>Activities</th>
<th>Capability Level</th>
</tr>
</thead>

<td>4</td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td>2. Work together to identify, communicate and implement improvement initiatives.</td>
<td>5</td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td>3. Work with service management and process owners to ensure that I&T-enabled services and service management processes are continually improved and the root causes of any issues are identified and resolved.</td>
<td></td>
</tr>
</tbody>
</table>

## Page 113

# CHAPTER 4
## COBIT GOVERNANCE AND MANAGEMENT OBJECTIVES—DETAILED GUIDANCE

<table>
  <tr>
    <td>
      **Domain:** Align, Plan and Organize<br>
      **Management Objective:** APO09 – Managed Service Agreements
    </td>
    <td>
      **Focus Area:** COBIT Core Model
    </td>
  </tr>
</table>

### Description
Align I&T-enabled products and services and service levels with enterprise needs and expectations, including identification, specification, design, publishing, agreement, and monitoring of I&T products and services, service levels and performance indicators.

### Purpose
Ensure that I&T products, services and service levels meet current and future enterprise needs.

The management objective supports the achievement of a set of primary enterprise and alignment goals:

<table>
  <tr>
    <td>
      **Enterprise Goals**
    </td>
    <td>
      &lt;img&gt;Right arrow icon&lt;/img&gt;
    </td>
    <td>
      **Alignment Goals**
    </td>
  </tr>
  <tr>
    <td>
      • EG01 Portfolio of competitive products and services<br>
      • EG08 Optimization of internal business process functionality
    </td>
    <td>
    </td>
    <td>
      AG05 Delivery of I&T services in line with business requirements
    </td>
  </tr>
  <tr>
    <td>
      **Example Metrics for Enterprise Goals**
    </td>
    <td>
    </td>
    <td>
      **Example Metrics for Alignment Goals**
    </td>
  </tr>
  <tr>
    <td>
      EG01
      <ul>
        <li>a. Percent of products and services that meet or exceed targets in revenues and/or market share</li>
        <li>b. Percent of products and services that meet or exceed customer satisfaction targets</li>
        <li>c. Percent of products and services that provide competitive advantage</li>
        <li>d. Time to market for new products and services</li>
      </ul>
    </td>
    <td>
    </td>
    <td>
      AG05
      <ul>
        <li>a. Percent of business stakeholders satisfied that I&T service delivery meets agreed service levels</li>
        <li>b. Number of business disruptions due to I&T service incidents</li>
        <li>c. Percent of users satisfied with the quality of I&T service delivery</li>
      </ul>
    </td>
  </tr>
  <tr>
    <td>
      EG08
      <ul>
        <li>a. Satisfaction levels of board and executive management with business process capabilities</li>
        <li>b. Satisfaction levels of customers with service delivery capabilities</li>
        <li>c. Satisfaction levels of suppliers with supply chain capabilities</li>
      </ul>
    </td>
    <td>
    </td>
    <td>
    </td>
  </tr>
</table>

### A. Component: Process

<table>
  <tr>
    <td>
      **Management Practice**
    </td>
    <td>
      **Example Metrics**
    </td>
  </tr>
  <tr>
    <td>
      **APO09.01 Identify I&T services.**<br>
      Analyze business requirements and the degree to which I&T-enabled services and service levels support business processes. Discuss and agree with the business on potential services and service levels. Compare potential service levels against the current service portfolio; identify new or changed services or service level options.
    </td>
    <td>
      a. Number of business activities that are not supported by any I&T service<br>
      b. Number of obsolete services identified
    </td>
  </tr>
  <tr>
    <td>
      **Activities**
    </td>
    <td>
      **Capability Level**
    </td>
  </tr>
  <tr>
    <td>
      1. Assess current I&T services and service levels to identify gaps between existing services and the business activities they support. Identify areas for improvement of existing services and service level options.<br><br>
      2. Analyze, study and estimate future demand and confirm capacity of existing I&T-enabled services.<br><br>
      3. Analyze business process activities to identify the need for new or redesigned I&T services.<br><br>
      4. Compare identified requirements to existing service components in the portfolio. If possible, package existing service components (I&T services, service level options and service packages) into new service packages to meet identified business requirements.<br><br>
      5. Regularly review the portfolio of I&T services with portfolio management and business relationship management to identify obsolete services. Agree on retirement and propose change.<br><br>
      6. Where possible, match demands to service packages and create standardized services to obtain overall efficiencies.
    </td>
    <td>
      2<br><br>
      3<br><br>
      4
    </td>
  </tr>
  <tr>
    <td>
      **Related Guidance (Standards, Frameworks, Compliance Requirements)**
    </td>
    <td>
      **Detailed Reference**
    </td>
  </tr>
  <tr>
    <td>
      ITIL V3, 2011
    </td>
    <td>
      Service Strategy, 4.4 Demand management
    </td>
  </tr>
</table>

&lt;page_number&gt;113&lt;/page_number&gt;
