## Page 114

# COBIT® 2019 FRAMEWORK: GOVERNANCE AND MANAGEMENT OBJECTIVES

## A. Component: Process (cont.)

<table>
  <thead>
    <tr>
      <th>Management Practice</th>
      <th>Example Metrics</th>
    </tr>
  </thead>
  <tbody>
    <tr>
      <td><strong>APO09.02 Catalog I&amp;T-enabled services.</strong><br>Define and maintain one or more service catalogues for relevant target groups. Publish and maintain live I&amp;T-enabled services in the service catalogs.</td>
      <td>a. Percent of live I&amp;T-enabled services and service packages offered in comparison to the portfolio<br>b. Time since last service portfolio update</td>
    </tr>
    <tr>
      <td colspan="2"><strong>Activities</strong></td>
    </tr>
    <tr>
      <td colspan="2">1. Publish in catalogues relevant live I&amp;T-enabled services, service packages and service level options from the portfolio.</td>
      <td rowspan="3">Capability Level<br>2</td>
    </tr>
    <tr>
      <td colspan="2">2. Continually ensure that the service components in the portfolio and the related service catalogues are complete and up to date.</td>
    </tr>
    <tr>
      <td colspan="2">3. Inform business relationship management of any updates to the service catalogues.</td>
    </tr>
    <tr>
      <td colspan="2"><strong>Related Guidance (Standards, Frameworks, Compliance Requirements)</strong></td>
      <td><strong>Detailed Reference</strong></td>
    </tr>
    <tr>
      <td colspan="2">ITIL V3, 2011</td>
      <td>Service Design, 4.2 Service Catalogue Management</td>
    </tr>
    <tr>
      <td colspan="2"><strong>Management Practice</strong></td>
      <td><strong>Example Metrics</strong></td>
    </tr>
    <tr>
      <td><strong>APO09.03 Define and prepare service agreements.</strong><br>Define and prepare service agreements based on options in the service catalogues. Include internal operational agreements.</td>
      <td>a. Number of business processes with undefined service agreements<br>b. Percent of live IT services covered by service agreements</td>
    </tr>
    <tr>
      <td colspan="2"><strong>Activities</strong></td>
    </tr>
    <tr>
      <td colspan="2">1. Analyze requirements for new or changed service agreements received from business relationship management to ensure that the requirements can be matched. Consider aspects such as service times, availability, performance, capacity, security, privacy, continuity, compliance and regulatory issues, usability, demand constraints, and data quality.</td>
      <td rowspan="5">Capability Level<br>2</td>
    </tr>
    <tr>
      <td colspan="2">2. Draft customer service agreements based on the services, service packages and service level options in the relevant service catalogues.</td>
    </tr>
    <tr>
      <td colspan="2">3. Finalize customer service agreements with business relationship management.</td>
    </tr>
    <tr>
      <td colspan="2">4. Determine, agree on and document internal operational agreements to underpin the customer service agreements, if applicable.</td>
    </tr>
    <tr>
      <td colspan="2">5. Liaise with supplier management to ensure that appropriate commercial contracts with external service providers underpin the customer service agreements, if applicable.</td>
    </tr>
    <tr>
      <td colspan="2"><strong>Related Guidance (Standards, Frameworks, Compliance Requirements)</strong></td>
      <td><strong>Detailed Reference</strong></td>
    </tr>
    <tr>
      <td colspan="2">ISF, The Standard of Good Practice for Information Security 2016</td>
      <td>SY2.1 Service Level Agreements</td>
    </tr>
    <tr>
      <td colspan="2">ISO/IEC 20000-1:2011(E)</td>
      <td>4 4.5 Establish and improve the SMS; 6.1 Service level management</td>
    </tr>
    <tr>
      <td colspan="2">ITIL V3, 2011</td>
      <td>in Service Design, 4.3 Service Level Management</td>
    </tr>
    <tr>
      <td colspan="2">National Institute of Standards and Technology Special Publication 800-53, Revision 5 (Draft), August 2017</td>
      <td>in 3.18 System and services acquisition (SA-9)</td>
    </tr>
    <tr>
      <td colspan="2"><strong>Management Practice</strong></td>
      <td><strong>Example Metrics</strong></td>
    </tr>
    <tr>
      <td><strong>APO09.04 Monitor and report service levels.</strong><br>Monitor service levels, report on achievements and identify trends. Provide the appropriate management information to aid performance management.</td>
      <td>a. Number and severity of service breaches<br>b. Percent of customers satisfied that service delivery meets agreed levels<br>c. Percent of service targets being met<br>d. Percent of services being monitored to service levels</td>
    </tr>
    <tr>
      <td colspan="2"><strong>Activities</strong></td>
    </tr>
    <tr>
      <td colspan="2">1. Establish and maintain measures to monitor and collect service level data.</td>
      <td rowspan="5">Capability Level<br>4</td>
    </tr>
    <tr>
      <td colspan="2">2. Evaluate performance and provide regular and formal reporting of service agreement performance, including deviations from the agreed values. Distribute this report to business relationship management.</td>
    </tr>
    <tr>
      <td colspan="2">3. Perform regular reviews to forecast and identify trends in service level performance. Incorporate quality management practices in the service monitoring.</td>
    </tr>
    <tr>
      <td colspan="2">4. Provide the appropriate management information to aid performance management.</td>
    </tr>
    <tr>
      <td colspan="2">5. Agree on action plans and remediations for any performance issues or negative trends.</td>
    </tr>
    <tr>
      <td colspan="2"><strong>Related Guidance (Standards, Frameworks, Compliance Requirements)</strong></td>
      <td><strong>Detailed Reference</strong></td>
    </tr>
    <tr>
      <td colspan="2">HITRUST CSF version 9, September 2017</td>
      <td>in 09.02 Control Third Party Service Delivery</td>
    </tr>
    <tr>
      <td colspan="2">ISO/IEC 20000-1:2011(E)</td>
      <td>in 6.2 Service reporting</td>
    </tr>
  </tbody>
</table>

## Page 119

# CHAPTER 4
## COBIT GOVERNANCE AND MANAGEMENT OBJECTIVES—DETAILED GUIDANCE

<table>
  <tr>
    <td><strong>Domain:</strong> Align, Plan and Organize<br><strong>Management Objective:</strong> APO10 – Managed Vendors</td>
    <td><strong>Focus Area:</strong> COBIT Core Model</td>
  </tr>
</table>

### Description
Manage I&T-related products and services provided by all types of vendors to meet enterprise requirements. This includes the search for and selection of vendors, management of relationships, management of contracts, and reviewing and monitoring of vendor performance and vendor ecosystem (including upstream supply chain) for effectiveness and compliance.

### Purpose
Optimize available I&T capabilities to support the I&T strategy and road map, minimize the risk associated with nonperforming or noncompliant vendors, and ensure competitive pricing.

The management objective supports the achievement of a set of primary enterprise and alignment goals:

<table>
  <tr>
    <td><strong>Enterprise Goals</strong></td>
    <td><strong>Alignment Goals</strong></td>
  </tr>
  <tr>
    <td>
      • EG01 Portfolio of competitive products and services<br>
      • EG08 Optimization of internal business process functionality
    </td>
    <td>
      AG05 Delivery of I&T services in line with business requirements
    </td>
  </tr>
  <tr>
    <td><strong>Example Metrics for Enterprise Goals</strong></td>
    <td><strong>Example Metrics for Alignment Goals</strong></td>
  </tr>
  <tr>
    <td>
      EG01
      <ul>
        <li>a. Percent of products and services that meet or exceed targets in revenues and/or market share</li>
        <li>b. Percent of products and services that meet or exceed customer satisfaction targets</li>
        <li>c. Percent of products and services that provide competitive advantage</li>
        <li>d. Time to market for new products and services</li>
      </ul>
    </td>
    <td>
      AG05
      <ul>
        <li>a. Percent of business stakeholders satisfied that I&T service delivery meets agreed service levels</li>
        <li>b. Number of business disruptions due to I&T service incidents</li>
        <li>c. Percent of users satisfied with the quality of I&T service delivery</li>
      </ul>
    </td>
  </tr>
  <tr>
    <td>
      EG08
      <ul>
        <li>a. Satisfaction levels of board and executive management with business process capabilities</li>
        <li>b. Satisfaction levels of customers with service delivery capabilities</li>
        <li>c. Satisfaction levels of suppliers with supply chain capabilities</li>
      </ul>
    </td>
    <td></td>
  </tr>
</table>

### A. Component: Process

<table>
  <tr>
    <td><strong>Management Practice</strong></td>
    <td><strong>Example Metrics</strong></td>
  </tr>
  <tr>
    <td>
      **APO10.01 Identify and evaluate vendor relationships and contracts.**<br>
      Continuously search for and identify vendors and categorize them into type, significance and criticality. Establish criteria to evaluate vendors and contracts. Review the overall portfolio of existing and alternative vendors and contracts.
    </td>
    <td>
      a. Percent of defined evaluation criteria achieved for existing suppliers and contracts<br>
      b. Percent of alternative suppliers providing equivalent services of existing supplier contracts
    </td>
  </tr>
  <tr>
    <td><strong>Activities</strong></td>
    <td><strong>Capability Level</strong></td>
  </tr>
  <tr>
    <td>
      1. Continuously scan the enterprise landscape in search for new partners and vendors that can provide complementary capabilities and support the realization of the I&T strategy, road map and enterprise objectives.<br>
      2. Establish and maintain criteria relating to type, significance and criticality of vendors and vendor contracts, enabling a focus on preferred and important vendors.<br>
      3. Identify, record and categorize existing vendors and contracts according to defined criteria to maintain a detailed register of preferred vendors that need to be managed carefully.<br>
      4. Establish and maintain vendor and contract evaluation criteria to enable overall review and comparison of vendor performance in a consistent way.<br>
      5. Periodically evaluate and compare the performance of existing and alternative vendors to identify opportunities or a compelling need to reconsider current vendor contracts.
    </td>
    <td>3</td>
  </tr>
  <tr>
    <td></td>
    <td>4</td>
  </tr>
  <tr>
    <td></td>
    <td>5</td>
  </tr>
  <tr>
    <td><strong>Related Guidance (Standards, Frameworks, Compliance Requirements)</strong></td>
    <td><strong>Detailed Reference</strong></td>
  </tr>
  <tr>
    <td>No related guidance for this management practice</td>
    <td></td>
  </tr>
</table>

&lt;page_number&gt;119&lt;/page_number&gt;

## Page 120

# COBIT® 2019 FRAMEWORK: GOVERNANCE AND MANAGEMENT OBJECTIVES

## A. Component: Process (cont.)

<table>
  <thead>
    <tr>
      <th>Management Practice</th>
      <th>Example Metrics</th>
    </tr>
  </thead>
  <tbody>
    <tr>
      <td><strong>APO10.02 Select vendors.</strong><br>Select suppliers according to a fair and formal practice to ensure a viable best fit based on specified requirements. Requirements should be optimized with input from potential suppliers.</td>
      <td>a. Number of identified gaps between the selected supplier’s offerings and the needs specified in the request for proposal (RFP)<br>b. Percent of stakeholders satisfied with suppliers</td>
    </tr>
    <tr>
      <td colspan="2"><strong>Activities</strong></td>
    </tr>
    <tr>
      <td colspan="2">1. Review all requests for information (RFIs) and requests for proposals (RFPs) to ensure that they clearly define requirements (e.g., enterprise requirements for security and privacy of information, operational business and I&T processing requirements, priorities for service delivery) and include a procedure to clarify requirements. The RFIs and RFPs should allow vendors sufficient time to prepare their proposals and should clearly define award criteria and the decision process.</td>
      <td rowspan="7">Capability Level<br>2</td>
    </tr>
    <tr>
      <td colspan="2">2. Evaluate RFIs and RFPs in accordance with the approved evaluation process/criteria and maintain documentary evidence of the evaluations. Verify the references of candidate vendors.</td>
    </tr>
    <tr>
      <td colspan="2">3. Select the vendor that best fits the RFP. Document and communicate the decision, and sign the contract.</td>
    </tr>
    <tr>
      <td colspan="2">4. In the specific case of software acquisition, include and enforce the rights and obligations of all parties in the contractual terms. These rights and obligations may include ownership and licensing of IP; maintenance; warranties; arbitration procedures; upgrade terms; and fit for purpose, including security, privacy, escrow and access rights.</td>
    </tr>
    <tr>
      <td colspan="2">5. In the specific case of acquisition of development resources, include and enforce the rights and obligations of all parties in the contractual terms. These rights and obligations may include ownership and licensing of IP; fit for purpose, including development methodologies; testing; quality management processes, including required performance criteria; performance reviews; basis for payment; warranties; arbitration procedures; human resource management; and compliance with the enterprise’s policies.</td>
    </tr>
    <tr>
      <td colspan="2">6. Obtain legal advice on resource development acquisition agreements regarding ownership and licensing of IP.</td>
    </tr>
    <tr>
      <td colspan="2">7. In the specific case of acquisition of infrastructure, facilities and related services, include and enforce the rights and obligations of all parties in the contractual terms. These rights and obligations may include service levels, maintenance procedures, access controls, security, privacy, performance review, basis for payment and arbitration procedures.</td>
    </tr>
    <tr>
      <td colspan="2"><strong>Related Guidance (Standards, Frameworks, Compliance Requirements)</strong></td>
      <td><strong>Detailed Reference</strong></td>
    </tr>
    <tr>
      <td colspan="2">No related guidance for this management practice</td>
      <td></td>
    </tr>
  </tbody>
</table>

## A. Component: Process (cont.)

<table>
  <thead>
    <tr>
      <th>Management Practice</th>
      <th>Example Metrics</th>
    </tr>
  </thead>
  <tbody>
    <tr>
      <td><strong>APO10.03 Manage vendor relationships and contracts.</strong><br>Formalize and manage the supplier relationship for each supplier. Manage, maintain and monitor contracts and service delivery. Ensure that new or changed contracts conform to enterprise standards and legal and regulatory requirements. Deal with contractual disputes.</td>
      <td>a. Percent of third-party suppliers who have contracts defining control requirements<br>b. Number of formal disputes with suppliers<br>c. Number of supplier review meetings<br>d. Percent of disputes resolved amicably in a reasonable time frame</td>
    </tr>
    <tr>
      <td colspan="2"><strong>Activities</strong></td>
    </tr>
    <tr>
      <td colspan="2">1. Assign relationship owners for all vendors and make them accountable for the quality of service(s) provided.</td>
      <td rowspan="8">Capability Level<br>3</td>
    </tr>
    <tr>
      <td colspan="2">2. Specify a formal communication and review process, including vendor interactions and schedules.</td>
    </tr>
    <tr>
      <td colspan="2">3. Agree on, manage, maintain and renew formal contracts with the vendor. Ensure that contracts conform to enterprise standards and legal and regulatory requirements.</td>
    </tr>
    <tr>
      <td colspan="2">4. Include provisions in contracts with key service vendors for review of the vendor site and internal practices and controls by management or independent third parties. Agree on independent audit and assurance controls of the operational environments of vendors providing outsourced services to confirm that agreed requirements are being adequately addressed.</td>
    </tr>
    <tr>
      <td colspan="2">5. Use established procedures to deal with contract disputes. Whenever possible, first use effective relationships and communications to overcome service problems.</td>
    </tr>
    <tr>
      <td colspan="2">6. Define and formalize roles and responsibilities for each service vendor. Where several vendors combine to provide a service, consider allocating a lead contractor role to one of the vendors to take responsibility for an overall contract.</td>
    </tr>
    <tr>
      <td colspan="2">7. Evaluate the effectiveness of the relationship and identify necessary improvements.</td>
    </tr>
    <tr>
      <td colspan="2">8. Define, communicate and agree on ways to implement required improvements to the relationship.</td>
    </tr>
    <tr>
      <td colspan="2"><strong>Related Guidance (Standards, Frameworks, Compliance Requirements)</strong></td>
      <td><strong>Detailed Reference</strong></td>
    </tr>
    <tr>
      <td colspan="2">ISO/IEC 20000-1:2011(E)</td>
      <td>7.2 Supplier management</td>
    </tr>
    <tr>
      <td colspan="2">ITIL V3, 2011</td>
      <td>Service Design, 4.8 Supplier Management</td>
    </tr>
  </tbody>
</table>

&lt;page_number&gt;120&lt;/page_number&gt;

## Page 121

CHAPTER 4
COBIT GOVERNANCE AND MANAGEMENT OBJECTIVES—DETAILED GUIDANCE

A. Component: Process (cont.)

<table>
<thead>
<tr>
<th>Management Practice</th>
<th>Example Metrics</th>
</tr>
</thead>
<tbody>
<tr>
<td><strong>APO10.04 Manage vendor risk.</strong><br>Identify and manage risk relating to vendors’ ability to continually provide secure, efficient and effective service delivery. This also includes the subcontractors or upstream vendors that are relevant in the service delivery of the direct vendor.</td>
<td>a. Frequency of risk management sessions with supplier<br>b. Number of risk-related events leading to service incidents<br>c. Percent of risk-related incidents resolved acceptably (time and cost)</td>
</tr>
</tbody>
</table>

<table>
<thead>
<tr>
<th>Activities</th>
<th>Capability Level</th>
</tr>
</thead>
<tbody>
<tr>
<td>1. When preparing the contract, provide for potential service risk by clearly defining service requirements, including software escrow agreements, alternative vendors or standby agreements to mitigate possible vendor failure; security and protection of IP; privacy; and any legal or regulatory requirements.</td>
<td>3</td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td>2. Identify, monitor and, where appropriate, manage risk relating to the vendor’s ability to deliver service efficiently, effectively, securely, confidentially, reliably and continually. Integrate critical internal IT management processes with those of the outsourced service providers, covering, for example, performance and capacity planning, change management, and configuration management.</td>
<td>4</td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td>3. Assess the larger ecosystem of the vendor and identify, monitor, and, where appropriate, manage risk related to the subcontractors and upstream vendors influencing the vendor’s ability to deliver service efficiently, effectively, securely, reliably and continually.</td>
<td></td>
</tr>
</tbody>
</table>

<table>
<thead>
<tr>
<th>Related Guidance (Standards, Frameworks, Compliance Requirements)</th>
<th>Detailed Reference</th>
</tr>
</thead>
<tbody>
<tr>
<td>CMMI Cybermaturity Platform, 2018</td>
<td>RM.MP Manage External Participation</td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td>ISF, The Standard of Good Practice for Information Security 2016</td>
<td>SC1.1 External Supplier Management Process</td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td>ISO/IEC 27002:2013/Cor.2:2015(E)</td>
<td>15. Supplier relationships</td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td>National Institute of Standards and Technology Framework for Improving Critical Infrastructure Cybersecurity v1.1, April 2018</td>
<td>D.SC Supply Chain Risk Management</td>
</tr>
</tbody>
</table>

<table>
<thead>
<tr>
<th>Management Practice</th>
<th>Example Metrics</th>
</tr>
</thead>
<tbody>
<tr>
<td><strong>APO10.05 Monitor vendor performance and compliance.</strong><br>Periodically review overall vendor performance, compliance to contract requirements and value for money. Address identified issues.</td>
<td>a. Number of service breaches to I&T-related services caused by suppliers<br>b. Percent of suppliers meeting agreed requirements</td>
</tr>
</tbody>
</table>

<table>
<thead>
<tr>
<th>Activities</th>
<th>Capability Level</th>
</tr>
</thead>
<tbody>
<tr>
<td>1. Request independent reviews of vendor internal practices and controls, if necessary.</td>
<td>3</td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td>2. Define and document criteria to monitor vendor performance aligned with service level agreements. Ensure that the vendor regularly and transparently reports on agreed criteria.</td>
<td>4</td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td>3. Monitor and review service delivery to ensure that the vendor is providing an acceptable quality of service, meeting requirements and adhering to contract conditions.</td>
<td></td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td>4. Review vendor performance and value for money. Ensure that the vendor is reliable and competitive, compared with alternative vendors and market conditions.</td>
<td></td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td>5. Monitor and evaluate externally available information about the vendor and the vendor’s supply chain.</td>
<td></td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td>6. Record and assess review results periodically and discuss them with the vendor to identify needs and opportunities for improvement.</td>
<td>5</td>
</tr>
</tbody>
</table>

<table>
<thead>
<tr>
<th>Related Guidance (Standards, Frameworks, Compliance Requirements)</th>
<th>Detailed Reference</th>
</tr>
</thead>
<tbody>
<tr>
<td>No related guidance for this management practice</td>
<td></td>
</tr>
</tbody>
</table>

## Page 125

# CHAPTER 4
## COBIT GOVERNANCE AND MANAGEMENT OBJECTIVES—DETAILED GUIDANCE

<table>
  <tr>
    <td style="vertical-align: top;">
      **Domain:** Align, Plan and Organize<br>
      **Management Objective:** APO11 – Managed Quality
    </td>
    <td style="vertical-align: top;">
      **Focus Area:** COBIT Core Model
    </td>
  </tr>
</table>

### Description
Define and communicate quality requirements in all processes, procedures and related enterprise outcomes. Enable controls, ongoing monitoring, and the use of proven practices and standards in continuous improvement and efficiency efforts.

### Purpose
Ensure consistent delivery of technology solutions and services to meet the quality requirements of the enterprise and satisfy stakeholder needs.

The management objective supports the achievement of a set of primary enterprise and alignment goals:

<table>
  <tr>
    <td style="vertical-align: top; width: 50%;">
      ### Enterprise Goals
      <ul>
        <li>• EG01 Portfolio of competitive products and services</li>
        <li>• EG04 Quality of financial information</li>
        <li>• EG07 Quality of management information</li>
        <li>• EG08 Optimization of internal business process functionality</li>
        <li>• EG12 Managed digital transformation programs</li>
      </ul>
      ### Example Metrics for Enterprise Goals
      <table>
        <tr>
          <td>EG01</td>
          <td>
            a. Percent of products and services that meet or exceed targets in revenues and/or market share<br>
            b. Percent of products and services that meet or exceed customer satisfaction targets<br>
            c. Percent of products and services that provide competitive advantage<br>
            d. Time to market for new products and services
          </td>
        </tr>
        <tr>
          <td>EG04</td>
          <td>
            a. Satisfaction survey of key stakeholders regarding the transparency, understanding and accuracy of enterprise financial information<br>
            b. Cost of noncompliance with finance-related regulations
          </td>
        </tr>
        <tr>
          <td>EG07</td>
          <td>
            a. Degree of board and executive management satisfaction with decision-making information<br>
            b. Number of incidents caused by incorrect business decisions based on inaccurate information<br>
            c. Time to provide information supporting effective business decisions<br>
            d. Timeliness of management information
          </td>
        </tr>
        <tr>
          <td>EG08</td>
          <td>
            a. Satisfaction levels of board and executive management with business process capabilities<br>
            b. Satisfaction levels of customers with service delivery capabilities<br>
            c. Satisfaction levels of suppliers with supply chain capabilities
          </td>
        </tr>
        <tr>
          <td>EG12</td>
          <td>
            a. Number of programs on time and within budget<br>
            b. Percent of stakeholders satisfied with program delivery<br>
            c. Percent of business transformation programs stopped<br>
            d. Percent of business transformation programs with regular reported status updates
          </td>
        </tr>
      </table>
    </td>
    <td style="vertical-align: top; width: 50%;">
      ### Alignment Goals
      <ul>
        <li>• AG09 Delivering programs on time, on budget and meeting requirements and quality standards</li>
        <li>• AG10 Quality of I&T management information</li>
      </ul>
      ### Example Metrics for Alignment Goals
      <table>
        <tr>
          <td>AG09</td>
          <td>
            a. Number of programs/projects on time and within budget<br>
            b. Number of programs needing significant rework due to quality defects<br>
            c. Percent of stakeholders satisfied with program/project quality
          </td>
        </tr>
        <tr>
          <td>AG10</td>
          <td>
            a. Level of user satisfaction with quality, timeliness and availability of I&T-related management information, taking into account available resources<br>
            b. Ratio and extent of erroneous business decisions in which erroneous or unavailable I&T-related information was a key factor<br>
            c. Percentage of information meeting quality criteria
          </td>
        </tr>
      </table>
    </td>
  </tr>
</table>

&lt;page_number&gt;125&lt;/page_number&gt;

## Page 126

# COBIT® 2019 FRAMEWORK: GOVERNANCE AND MANAGEMENT OBJECTIVES

<table>
  <thead>
    <tr>
      <th colspan="3">A. Component: Process</th>
    </tr>
  </thead>
  <tbody>
    <tr>
      <td rowspan="15" style="writing-mode: vertical-rl; text-orientation: mixed; transform: rotate(180deg); padding-left: 10px;">Align, Plan and Organize</td>
      <td>Management Practice</td>
      <td>Example Metrics</td>
    </tr>
    <tr>
      <td>APO11.01 Establish a quality management system (QMS).</td>
      <td>a. Percent of effectiveness of quality management reviews<br>b. Percent of key stakeholder satisfaction with quality management review program</td>
    </tr>
    <tr>
      <td colspan="2">Establish and maintain a quality management system (QMS) that provides a standard, formal and continuous approach to quality management of information. The QMS should enable technology and business processes to align with business requirements and enterprise quality management.</td>
    </tr>
    <tr>
      <td colspan="2">Activities</td>
      <td>Capability Level</td>
    </tr>
    <tr>
      <td>1. Ensure that the I&T control framework and the business and IT processes include a standard, formal and continuous approach to quality management that is aligned with enterprise requirements. Within the I&T control framework and the business and IT processes, identify quality requirements and criteria (e.g., based on legal requirements and requirements from customers).</td>
      <td>3</td>
    </tr>
    <tr>
      <td>2. Define roles, tasks, decision rights and responsibilities for quality management in the organizational structure.</td>
      <td></td>
    </tr>
    <tr>
      <td>3. Obtain input from management and external and internal stakeholders on the definition of quality requirements and quality management criteria.</td>
      <td></td>
    </tr>
    <tr>
      <td>4. Regularly monitor and review the QMS against agreed acceptance criteria. Include feedback from customers, users and management.</td>
      <td>4</td>
    </tr>
    <tr>
      <td>5. Respond to discrepancies in review results to continuously improve the QMS.</td>
      <td>5</td>
    </tr>
    <tr>
      <td>Related Guidance (Standards, Frameworks, Compliance Requirements)</td>
      <td>Detailed Reference</td>
    </tr>
    <tr>
      <td>PMBOK Guide Sixth Edition, 2017</td>
      <td>Part 1: 8.1 Plan quality management</td>
    </tr>
    <tr>
      <td>Management Practice</td>
      <td>Example Metrics</td>
    </tr>
    <tr>
      <td>APO11.02 Focus quality management on customers.</td>
      <td>a. Percent of customer satisfaction<br>b. Percent of customer requirements and expectations communicated throughout the business and IT organization</td>
    </tr>
    <tr>
      <td colspan="2">Focus quality management on customers by determining their requirements and ensuring integration in quality management practices.</td>
    </tr>
    <tr>
      <td colspan="2">Activities</td>
      <td>Capability Level</td>
    </tr>
    <tr>
      <td>1. Focus quality management on customers by determining internal and external customer requirements and ensuring alignment of the I&T standards and practices. Define and communicate roles and responsibilities concerning conflict resolution between the user/customer and the IT organization.</td>
      <td>3</td>
    </tr>
    <tr>
      <td>2. Manage the business needs and expectations for each business process, IT operational service and new solutions. Maintain their quality acceptance criteria.</td>
      <td></td>
    </tr>
    <tr>
      <td>3. Communicate customer requirements and expectations throughout the business and IT organization.</td>
      <td></td>
    </tr>
    <tr>
      <td>4. Periodically obtain customer views on business process and service provisioning and IT solution delivery. Determine the impact on I&T standards and practices and ensure that customer expectations are met and actioned.</td>
      <td>4</td>
    </tr>
    <tr>
      <td>5. Capture quality acceptance criteria for inclusion in SLAs.</td>
      <td></td>
    </tr>
    <tr>
      <td>Related Guidance (Standards, Frameworks, Compliance Requirements)</td>
      <td>Detailed Reference</td>
    </tr>
    <tr>
      <td>No related guidance for this management practice</td>
      <td></td>
    </tr>
    <tr>
      <td>Management Practice</td>
      <td>Example Metrics</td>
    </tr>
    <tr>
      <td>APO11.03 Manage quality standards, practices and procedures and integrate quality management into key processes and solutions.</td>
      <td>a. Number of processes with defined quality requirements<br>b. Number of defects uncovered prior to production<br>c. Number of services with a formal quality management plan<br>d. Number of SLAs that include quality acceptance criteria</td>
    </tr>
    <tr>
      <td colspan="2">Identify and maintain standards, procedures and practices for key processes to guide the enterprise in meeting the intent of the agreed quality management standards (QMS). This activity should align with I&T control framework requirements. Consider certification for key processes, organizational units, products or services.</td>
    </tr>
  </tbody>
</table>

</tr>

<td></td>
</tr>

<td></td>
</tr>

The information gathered should be used by the process owner to improve quality.</td>

Number of processes with a formal quality assessment report<br>d. Percent of projects reviewed that meet target quality goals and objectives<br>e. Number, robustness and timeliness of risk analyses</td>

</tr>

<td>3</td>

<td>4</td>

<tr>

<td></td>
</tr>
<tr>

<td></td>
</tr>
<tr>

<td>2</td>

<td>3</td>

<td></td>

## Page 131

# CHAPTER 4
## COBIT GOVERNANCE AND MANAGEMENT OBJECTIVES—DETAILED GUIDANCE

<table>
  <tr>
    <td colspan="2"><b>Domain: Align, Plan and Organize</b></td>
    <td rowspan="2"><b>Focus Area: COBIT Core Model</b></td>
  </tr>
  <tr>
    <td colspan="2"><b>Management Objective: APO12 – Managed Risk</b></td>
  </tr>
</table>

### Description
Continually identify, assess and reduce I&T-related risk within tolerance levels set by enterprise executive management.

### Purpose
Integrate the management of I&T-related enterprise risk with overall enterprise risk management (ERM) and balance the costs and benefits of managing I&T-related enterprise risk.

The management objective supports the achievement of a set of primary enterprise and alignment goals:

<table>
  <tr>
    <td><b>Enterprise Goals</b></td>
    <td><b>Alignment Goals</b></td>
  </tr>
  <tr>
    <td>
      • EG02 Managed business risk<br>
      • EG06 Business service continuity and availability
    </td>
    <td>
      • AG02 Managed I&T-related risk<br>
      • AG07 Security of information, processing infrastructure and applications, and privacy
    </td>
  </tr>
</table>

### Example Metrics for Enterprise Goals
<table>
  <tr>
    <td>EG02</td>
    <td>a. Percent of critical business objectives and services covered by risk assessment<br>b. Ratio of significant incidents that were not identified in risk assessments vs. total incidents<br>c. Frequency of updating risk profile</td>
  </tr>
  <tr>
    <td>EG06</td>
    <td>a. Number of customer service or business process interruptions causing significant incidents<br>b. Business cost of incidents<br>c. Number of business processing hours lost due to unplanned service interruptions<br>d. Percent of complaints as a function of committed service availability targets</td>
  </tr>
</table>

### Example Metrics for Alignment Goals
<table>
  <tr>
    <td>AG02</td>
    <td>a. Frequency of updating risk profile<br>b. Percent of enterprise risk assessments including I&T-related risk<br>c. Number of significant I&T-related incidents that were not identified in a risk assessment</td>
  </tr>
  <tr>
    <td>AG07</td>
    <td>a. Number of confidentiality incidents causing financial loss, business disruption or public embarrassment<br>b. Number of availability incidents causing financial loss, business disruption or public embarrassment<br>c. Number of integrity incidents causing financial loss, business disruption or public embarrassment</td>
  </tr>
</table>

### A. Component: Process

<table>
  <tr>
    <td><b>Management Practice</b></td>
    <td><b>Example Metrics</b></td>
  </tr>
  <tr>
    <td>
      **APO12.01 Collect data.<br>**Identify and collect relevant data to enable effective I&T-related risk identification, analysis and reporting.
    </td>
    <td>
      a. Number of loss events with key characteristics captured in repositories<br>
      b. Percent of audits, events and trends captured in repositories<br>
      c. Percent of critical systems with known issues
    </td>
  </tr>
</table>

<table>
  <tr>
    <td><b>Activities</b></td>
    <td><b>Capability Level</b></td>
  </tr>
  <tr>
    <td>
      1. Establish and maintain a method for the collection, classification and analysis of I&T risk-related data.<br>
      2. Record relevant and significant I&T risk-related data on the enterprise’s internal and external operating environment.<br>
      3. Adopt or define a risk taxonomy for consistent definitions of risk scenarios and impact and likelihood categories.<br>
      4. Record data on risk events that have caused or may cause business impacts as per the impact categories defined in the risk taxonomy. Capture relevant data from related issues, incidents, problems and investigations.<br>
      5. Survey and analyze the historical I&T risk data and loss experience from externally available data and trends, industry peers through industry-based event logs, databases, and industry agreements for common event disclosure.<br>
      6. For similar classes of events, organize the collected data and highlight contributing factors. Determine common contributing factors across multiple events.<br>
      7. Determine the specific conditions that existed or were absent when risk events occurred and the way the conditions affected event frequency and loss magnitude.<br>
      8. Perform periodic event and risk factor analysis to identify new or emerging risk issues and to gain an understanding of the associated internal and external risk factors.
    </td>
    <td>
      2<br>
      3<br>
      4
    </td>
  </tr>
</table>

<table>
  <tr>
    <td><b>Related Guidance (Standards, Frameworks, Compliance Requirements)</b></td>
    <td><b>Detailed Reference</b></td>
  </tr>
  <tr>
    <td>CMMI Data Management Maturity Model, 2014</td>
    <td>Supporting Processes - Risk Management</td>
  </tr>
  <tr>
    <td>COSO Enterprise Risk Management, June 2017</td>
    <td>8. Performance—Principle 10</td>
  </tr>
  <tr>
    <td>ISO/IEC 27005:2011(E)</td>
    <td>8.2 Risk identification; 12. Information security risk monitoring and review</td>
  </tr>
  <tr>
    <td>National Institute of Standards and Technology Special Publication 800-37, Revision 2 (Draft), May 2018</td>
    <td>3.1 Preparation (Task 7)</td>
  </tr>
</table>

&lt;page_number&gt;131&lt;/page_number&gt;

## Page 132

# COBIT® 2019 FRAMEWORK: GOVERNANCE AND MANAGEMENT OBJECTIVES

## A. Component: Process (cont.)

<table>
  <thead>
    <tr>
      <th>Management Practice</th>
      <th>Example Metrics</th>
    </tr>
  </thead>
  <tbody>
    <tr>
      <td><strong>APO12.02 Analyze risk.</strong><br>Develop a substantiated view on actual I&T risk, in support of risk decisions.</td>
      <td>a. Number of identified I&T risk scenarios<br>b. Time since last update of I&T risk scenarios</td>
    </tr>
    <tr>
      <td colspan="2"><strong>Activities</strong></td>
    </tr>
    <tr>
      <td colspan="2">1. Define the appropriate scope of risk analysis efforts, considering all risk factors and/or the business criticality of assets.</td>
      <td rowspan="8">Capability Level<br>3</td>
    </tr>
    <tr>
      <td colspan="2">2. Build and regularly update I&T risk scenarios; I&T-related loss exposures; and scenarios regarding reputational risk, including compound scenarios of cascading and/or coincidental threat types and events. Develop expectations for specific control activities and capabilities to detect.</td>
    </tr>
    <tr>
      <td colspan="2">3. Estimate the frequency (or likelihood) and magnitude of loss or gain associated with I&T risk scenarios. Take into account all applicable risk factors and evaluate known operational controls.</td>
    </tr>
    <tr>
      <td colspan="2">4. Compare current risk (I&T-related loss exposure) to risk appetite and acceptable risk tolerance. Identify unacceptable or elevated risk.</td>
    </tr>
    <tr>
      <td colspan="2">5. Propose risk responses for risk exceeding risk appetite and tolerance levels.</td>
    </tr>
    <tr>
      <td colspan="2">6. Specify high-level requirements for projects or programs that will implement the selected risk responses. Identify requirements and expectations for appropriate key controls for risk mitigation responses.</td>
    </tr>
    <tr>
      <td colspan="2">7. Validate the risk analysis and business impact analysis (BIA) results before using them in decision making. Confirm that the analysis aligns with enterprise requirements and verify that estimations were properly calibrated and scrutinized for bias.</td>
    </tr>
    <tr>
      <td colspan="2">8. Analyze cost/benefit of potential risk response options such as avoid, reduce/mitigate, transfer/share, and accept and exploit/seize. Confirm the optimal risk response.</td>
    </tr>
    <tr>
      <td colspan="2"><strong>Related Guidance (Standards, Frameworks, Compliance Requirements)</strong></td>
      <td><strong>Detailed Reference</strong></td>
    </tr>
    <tr>
      <td>CMMI Data Management Maturity Model, 2014</td>
      <td>Supporting Processes—Risk Management</td>
    </tr>
    <tr>
      <td>COSO Enterprise Risk Management, June 2017</td>
      <td>8. Performance—Principle 11</td>
    </tr>
    <tr>
      <td>ISF, The Standard of Good Practice for Information Security 2016</td>
      <td>IR2.1 Risk Assessment Scope; IR2.2 Business Impact Assessment</td>
    </tr>
    <tr>
      <td>ISO/IEC 27001:2013/Cor.2:2015(E)</td>
      <td>8.2 Information security risk assessment</td>
    </tr>
    <tr>
      <td>ISO/IEC 27005:2011(E)</td>
      <td>8.3 Risk analysis</td>
    </tr>
    <tr>
      <td>National Institute of Standards and Technology Framework for Improving Critical Infrastructure Cybersecurity v1.1, April 2018</td>
      <td>ID.RA Risk Assessment</td>
    </tr>
    <tr>
      <td>National Institute of Standards and Technology Special Publication 800-37, Revision 2 (Draft), May 2018</td>
      <td>3.6 Authorization (Task 3)</td>
    </tr>
    <tr>
      <td>National Institute of Standards and Technology Special Publication 800-53, Revision 5 (Draft), August 2017</td>
      <td>3.17 Risk assessment (RA-3)</td>
    </tr>
    <tr>
      <td colspan="2"><strong>Management Practice</strong></td>
      <td><strong>Example Metrics</strong></td>
    </tr>
    <tr>
      <td><strong>APO12.03 Maintain a risk profile.</strong><br>Maintain an inventory of known risk and risk attributes, including expected frequency, potential impact and responses. Document related resources, capabilities and current control activities related to risk items.</td>
      <td>a. Completeness of attributes and values in the risk profile<br>b. Percent of key business processes included in the risk profile</td>
    </tr>
    <tr>
      <td colspan="2"><strong>Activities</strong></td>
    </tr>
    <tr>
      <td colspan="2">1. Inventory business processes and document their dependency on I&T service management processes and IT infrastructure resources. Identify supporting personnel, applications, infrastructure, facilities, critical manual records, vendors, suppliers and outsourcers.</td>
      <td rowspan="7">Capability Level<br>2</td>
    </tr>
    <tr>
      <td colspan="2">2. Determine and agree on which I&T services and IT infrastructure resources are essential to sustain the operation of business processes. Analyze dependencies and identify weak links.</td>
    </tr>
    <tr>
      <td colspan="2">3. Aggregate current risk scenarios by category, business line and functional area.</td>
    </tr>
    <tr>
      <td colspan="2">4. Regularly capture all risk profile information and consolidate it into an aggregated risk profile.</td>
    </tr>
    <tr>
      <td colspan="2">5. Capture information on the status of the risk action plan for inclusion in the I&T risk profile of the enterprise.</td>
    </tr>
    <tr>
      <td colspan="2">6. Based on all risk profile data, define a set of risk indicators that allow the quick identification and monitoring of current risk and risk trends.</td>
    </tr>
    <tr>
      <td colspan="2">7. Capture information on I&T risk events that have materialized for inclusion in the IT risk profile of the enterprise.</td>
    </tr>
  </tbody>
</table>

&lt;page_number&gt;132&lt;/page_number&gt;

## Page 133

CHAPTER 4
COBIT GOVERNANCE AND MANAGEMENT OBJECTIVES—DETAILED GUIDANCE

A. Component: Process (cont.)

<table>
<thead>
<tr>
<th>Related Guidance (Standards, Frameworks, Compliance Requirements)</th>
<th>Detailed Reference</th>
</tr>
</thead>
<tbody>
<tr>
<td>CMMI Cybermaturity Platform, 2018</td>
<td>RS.DT Define Organizational Risk Tolerance</td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td>COSO Enterprise Risk Management, June 2017</td>
<td>8. Performance—Principle 12</td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td>National Institute of Standards and Technology Special Publication 800-53, Revision 5 (Draft), August 2017</td>
<td>3.17 Risk assessment (RA-7)</td>
</tr>
</tbody>
</table>

<table>
<thead>
<tr>
<th>Management Practice</th>
<th>Example Metrics</th>
</tr>
</thead>
<tbody>
<tr>
<td><strong>APO12.04 Articulate risk.</strong><br>Communicate information on the current state of I&T-related exposures and opportunities in a timely manner to all required stakeholders for appropriate response.</td>
<td>a. Level of stakeholder satisfaction with provided risk reporting<br>b. Completeness of risk profile reporting (including information in line with stakeholder requirements)<br>c. Use of risk reporting in management decision making</td>
</tr>
</tbody>
</table>

<table>
<thead>
<tr>
<th>Activities</th>
<th>Capability Level</th>
</tr>
</thead>
<tbody>
<tr>
<td>1. Report the results of risk analysis to all affected stakeholders in terms and formats useful to support enterprise decisions. Whenever possible, include probabilities and ranges of loss or gain along with confidence levels, to enable management to balance risk-return.</td>
<td>3</td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td>2. Provide decision makers with an understanding of worst-case and most-probable scenarios, I&T-related loss exposures and significant reputation, legal and regulatory considerations, or any other impact category as per the risk taxonomy.</td>
<td></td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td>3. Report the current risk profile to all stakeholders. Include information on the effectiveness of the risk management process, control effectiveness, gaps, inconsistencies, redundancies, remediation status and their impacts on the risk profile.</td>
<td></td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td>4. On a periodic basis, for areas with relative risk and risk capacity parity, identify I&T-related opportunities that would allow the acceptance of greater risk and enhanced growth and return.</td>
<td></td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td>5. Review the results of objective third-party assessments and internal audit and quality assurance reviews. Include them in the risk profile. Review identified gaps and I&T-related loss exposures to determine the need for additional risk analysis.</td>
<td>4</td>
</tr>
</tbody>
</table>

<table>
<thead>
<tr>
<th>Related Guidance (Standards, Frameworks, Compliance Requirements)</th>
<th>Detailed Reference</th>
</tr>
</thead>
<tbody>
<tr>
<td>CMMI Cybermaturity Platform, 2018</td>
<td>RS.CR Determine Critical Infrastructure Requirements</td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td>COSO Enterprise Risk Management, June 2017</td>
<td>10. Information, Communication, and Reporting—Principle 19</td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td>ISO/IEC 27005:2011(E)</td>
<td>11. Information security risk communication and consultation</td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td>National Institute of Standards and Technology Framework for Improving Critical Infrastructure Cybersecurity v1.1, April 2018</td>
<td>ID.RM Risk Management Strategy</td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td>National Institute of Standards and Technology Special Publication 800-53, Revision 5 (Draft), August 2017</td>
<td>3.15 Program management (PM-32)</td>
</tr>
</tbody>
</table>

<table>
<thead>
<tr>
<th>Management Practice</th>
<th>Example Metrics</th>
</tr>
</thead>
<tbody>
<tr>
<td><strong>APO12.05 Define a risk management action portfolio.</strong><br>Manage opportunities to reduce risk to an acceptable level as a portfolio.</td>
<td>a. Number of significant incidents not identified and included in the risk management portfolio<br>b. Percent of risk management project proposals rejected due to lack of consideration of other related risk</td>
</tr>
</tbody>
</table>

<table>
<thead>
<tr>
<th>Activities</th>
<th>Capability Level</th>
</tr>
</thead>
<tbody>
<tr>
<td>1. Maintain an inventory of control activities that are in place to mitigate risk and that enable risk to be taken in line with the risk appetite and tolerance. Classify control activities and map them to specific I&T risk scenarios and aggregations of I&T risk scenarios.</td>
<td>2</td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td>2. Determine whether each organizational entity monitors risk and accepts accountability for operating within its individual and portfolio tolerance levels.</td>
<td>3</td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td>3. Define a balanced set of project proposals designed to reduce risk and/or projects that enable strategic enterprise opportunities, considering costs, benefits, effect on current risk profile and regulations.</td>
<td></td>
</tr>
</tbody>
</table>

## Page 134

# COBIT® 2019 FRAMEWORK: GOVERNANCE AND MANAGEMENT OBJECTIVES

## A. Component: Process (cont.)

<table>
  <thead>
    <tr>
      <th>Management Practice</th>
      <th>Example Metrics</th>
    </tr>
  </thead>
  <tbody>
    <tr>
      <td><strong>APO12.06 Respond to risk.</strong><br>Respond in a timely manner to materialized risk events with effective measures to limit the magnitude of loss.</td>
      <td>a. Number of measures not reducing residual risk<br>b. Percent of I&T risk action plans executed as designed</td>
    </tr>
    <tr>
      <td colspan="2"><strong>Activities</strong></td>
    </tr>
    <tr>
      <td>1. Prepare, maintain and test plans that document the specific steps to take when a risk event may cause a significant operational or development incident with serious business impact. Ensure that plans include pathways of escalation across the enterprise.</td>
      <td rowspan="5">Capability Level<br>3</td>
    </tr>
    <tr>
      <td>2. Apply the appropriate response plan to minimize the impact when risk incidents occur.</td>
    </tr>
    <tr>
      <td>3. Categorize incidents and compare I&T-related loss exposures against risk tolerance thresholds. Communicate business impacts to decision makers as part of reporting and update the risk profile.</td>
    </tr>
    <tr>
      <td>4. Examine past adverse events/losses and missed opportunities and determine root causes.</td>
    </tr>
    <tr>
      <td>5. Communicate root cause, additional risk response requirements and process improvements to appropriate decision makers. Ensure that the cause, response requirements and process improvement are included in risk governance processes.</td>
    </tr>
    <tr>
      <td colspan="2"><strong>Related Guidance (Standards, Frameworks, Compliance Requirements)</strong></td>
    </tr>
    <tr>
      <td>COSO Enterprise Risk Management, June 2017</td>
      <td>8. Performance—Principle 13</td>
    </tr>
    <tr>
      <td>ISF, The Standard of Good Practice for Information Security 2016</td>
      <td>IR2.9 Risk Treatment</td>
    </tr>
    <tr>
      <td>ISO/IEC 27001:2013/Cor.2:2015(E)</td>
      <td>6.1 Action to address risk and opportunities</td>
    </tr>
    <tr>
      <td>ISO/IEC 27005:2011(E)</td>
      <td>9. Information security risk treatment</td>
    </tr>
    <tr>
      <td>National Institute of Standards and Technology Special Publication 800-37, Revision 2 (Draft), May 2018</td>
      <td>3.6 Authorization (Task 4)</td>
    </tr>
    <tr>
      <td>National Institute of Standards and Technology Special Publication 800-53, Revision 5 (Draft), August 2017</td>
      <td>3.15 Program management (PM-9, PM-31)</td>
    </tr>
  </tbody>
</table>

<td>APO12.06 Respond to risk.</td>
<td>EDM03.03</td>

</tr>

<td>Enterprise risk policy</td>
<td>Defines governance and management of enterprise risk at strategic, tactical and operational levels, pursuant to business objectives. Translates enterprise governance into risk governance principles and policy and elaborates risk management activities.</td>

<td>Fraud risk policy</td>

<td>National Institute of Standards and Technology Special Publication 800- 37, Revision 2 (Draft), May 2018</td>

F. Component: Culture, Ethics and Behavior

<table>

<tr>
<th>Key Culture Elements</th>
<th>Related Guidance</th>
<th>Detailed Reference</th>
</tr>
</thead>
<tbody>
<tr>
<td>To support a transparent and participatory risk culture, senior management should set direction and demonstrate visible and genuine support for incorporation of risk practices throughout the enterprise. Management should encourage open communication and business ownership for I&T-related business risk. Desirable behaviors include aligning policies to the defined risk appetite, reporting risk trends to senior management and risk governing bodies, rewarding effective risk management, and proactively monitoring risk and progress on the risk action plan.</td>
<td>ISF, The Standard of Good Practice for Information Security 2016</td>
<td>IR1.2 Information Risk Assessment</td>
</tr>

## Page 139

# CHAPTER 4
## COBIT GOVERNANCE AND MANAGEMENT OBJECTIVES—DETAILED GUIDANCE

<table>
  <tr>
    <td>
      **Domain:** Align, Plan and Organize<br>
      **Management Objective:** APO13 – Managed Security
    </td>
    <td>
      **Focus Area:** COBIT Core Model
    </td>
  </tr>
</table>

### Description
Define, operate and monitor an information security management system.

### Purpose
Keep the impact and occurrence of information security incidents within the enterprise’s risk appetite levels.

The management objective supports the achievement of a set of primary enterprise and alignment goals:

<table>
  <tr>
    <td>
      **Enterprise Goals**
      <ul>
        <li>EG02 Managed business risk</li>
        <li>EG06 Business service continuity and availability</li>
      </ul>
      **Example Metrics for Enterprise Goals**
      <table>
        <tr>
          <td>EG02</td>
          <td>
            <ul>
              <li>a. Percent of critical business objectives and services covered by risk assessment</li>
              <li>b. Ratio of significant incidents that were not identified in risk assessments vs. total incidents</li>
              <li>c. Frequency of updating risk profile</li>
            </ul>
          </td>
        </tr>
        <tr>
          <td>EG06</td>
          <td>
            <ul>
              <li>a. Number of customer service or business process interruptions causing significant incidents</li>
              <li>b. Business cost of incidents</li>
              <li>c. Number of business processing hours lost due to unplanned service interruptions</li>
              <li>d. Percent of complaints as a function of committed service availability targets</li>
            </ul>
          </td>
        </tr>
      </table>
    </td>
    <td>
      &lt;img&gt;Right arrow icon&lt;/img&gt;
      **Alignment Goals**
      <ul>
        <li>AG07 Security of information, processing infrastructure and applications, and privacy</li>
      </ul>
      **Example Metrics for Alignment Goals**
      <table>
        <tr>
          <td>AG07</td>
          <td>
            <ul>
              <li>a. Number of confidentiality incidents causing financial loss, business disruption or public embarrassment</li>
              <li>b. Number of availability incidents causing financial loss, business disruption or public embarrassment</li>
              <li>c. Number of integrity incidents causing financial loss, business disruption or public embarrassment</li>
            </ul>
          </td>
        </tr>
      </table>
    </td>
  </tr>
</table>

### A. Component: Process

<table>
  <tr>
    <td>
      **Management Practice**
      <br>
      **APO13.01 Establish and maintain an information security management system (ISMS).**
      <br>
      Establish and maintain an information security management system (ISMS) that provides a standard, formal and continuous approach to information security management, enabling secure technology and business processes that are aligned with business requirements.
    </td>
    <td>
      **Example Metrics**
      <ul>
        <li>a. Level of stakeholder satisfaction with the security plan throughout the enterprise</li>
      </ul>
    </td>
  </tr>
  <tr>
    <td>
      **Activities**
      <ol>
        <li>Define the scope and boundaries of the information security management system (ISMS) in terms of the characteristics of the enterprise, the organization, its location, assets and technology. Include details of, and justification for, any exclusions from the scope.</li>
        <li>Define an ISMS in accordance with enterprise policy and the context in which the enterprise operates.</li>
        <li>Align the ISMS with the overall enterprise approach to the management of security.</li>
        <li>Obtain management authorization to implement and operate or change the ISMS.</li>
        <li>Prepare and maintain a statement of applicability that describes the scope of the ISMS.</li>
        <li>Define and communicate Information security management roles and responsibilities.</li>
        <li>Communicate the ISMS approach.</li>
      </ol>
    </td>
    <td>
      **Capability Level**
      <br>
      2
    </td>
  </tr>
</table>

&lt;page_number&gt;139&lt;/page_number&gt;

# COBIT® 2019 FRAMEWORK: GOVERNANCE AND MANAGEMENT OBJECTIVES

## A. Component: Process (cont.)

<table>
<thead>
<tr>
<th>Related Guidance (Standards, Frameworks, Compliance Requirements)</th>

<td>0.01 Information Security Management program</td>

<td>6.6 Information security management</td>
</tr>

<td>ITIL V3, 2011</td>
<td>Service Design, 4.7 Information Security Management</td>
</tr>
<tr>

<td>3.3 Selection (Task 1); 3.4 Implementation (Task 1)</td>

<table>
<thead>
<tr>
<th>Management Practice</th>
<th>Example Metrics</th>
</tr>
</thead>
<tbody>
<tr>
<td><strong>APO13.02 Define and manage an information security and privacy risk treatment plan.</strong><br>Maintain an information security plan that describes how information security risk is to be managed and aligned with enterprise strategy and enterprise architecture. Ensure that recommendations for implementing security improvements are based on approved business cases, implemented as an integral part of services and solutions development, and operated as an integral part of business operation.</td>
<td>a. Percentage of successful security risk scenario simulations<br>b. Number of employees who have successfully completed information security awareness training</td>
</tr>
</tbody>
</table>

<table>
<thead>
<tr>
<th>Activities</th>
<th>Capability Level</th>
</tr>
</thead>
<tbody>
<tr>
<td>1. Formulate and maintain an information security risk treatment plan aligned with strategic objectives and the enterprise architecture. Ensure that the plan identifies the appropriate and optimal management practices and security solutions, with associated resources, responsibilities and priorities for managing identified information security risk.</td>
<td>3</td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td>2. Maintain as part of the enterprise architecture an inventory of solution components that are in place to manage security-related risk.</td>
<td></td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td>3. Develop proposals to implement the information security risk treatment plan, supported by suitable business cases that include consideration of funding and allocation of roles and responsibilities.</td>
<td></td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td>4. Provide input to the design and development of management practices and solutions selected from the information security risk treatment plan.</td>
<td></td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td>5. Implement information security and privacy training and awareness programs.</td>
<td></td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td>6. Integrate the planning, design, implementation and monitoring of information security and privacy procedures and other controls capable of enabling prompt prevention, detection of security events, and response to security incidents.</td>
<td></td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td>7. Define how to measure the effectiveness of the selected management practices. Specify how these measurements are to be used to assess effectiveness to produce comparable and reproducible results.</td>
<td>4</td>
</tr>
</tbody>
</table>

<table>
<thead>
<tr>
<th>Related Guidance (Standards, Frameworks, Compliance Requirements)</th>
<th>Detailed Reference</th>
</tr>
</thead>
<tbody>
<tr>
<td>No related guidance for this management practice</td>
<td></td>
</tr>
</tbody>
</table>

<table>
<thead>
<tr>
<th>Management Practice</th>
<th>Example Metrics</th>
</tr>
</thead>
<tbody>
<tr>
<td><strong>APO13.03 Monitor and review the information security management system (ISMS).</strong><br>Maintain and regularly communicate the need for, and benefits of, continuous improvement in information security. Collect and analyze data about the information security management system (ISMS), and improve its effectiveness. Correct nonconformities to prevent recurrence.</td>
<td>a. Frequency of scheduled security reviews<br>b. Number of findings in regularly scheduled security reviews<br>c. Level of stakeholder satisfaction with the security plan<br>d. Number of security-related incidents caused by failure to adhere to the security plan</td>
</tr>
</tbody>
</table>

E. Component: Policies and Procedures

<table>
<thead>
<tr>
<th>Relevant Policy</th>
<th>Policy Description</th>
<th>Related Guidance</th>
<th>Detailed Reference</th>
</tr>
</thead>
<tbody>
<tr>
<td>Information security and privacy policy</td>
<td>Sets behavioral guidelines to protect corporate information, systems and infrastructure. Given that business requirements regarding security and storage are more dynamic than I&T risk management and privacy, their governance should be handled separately from that of I&T risk and privacy. For operational efficiency, synchronize information security policy with I&T risk and privacy policy.</td>
<td>(1) ISO/IEC 27001:2013/Cor.2:2015(E); (2) ISO/IEC 27002:2013/Cor.2:2015(E); (3) National Institute of Standards and Technology Special Publication 800-53, Revision 5 (Draft), August 2017; (4) HITRUST CSF version 9, September 2017; (5) ISF, The Standard of Good Practice for Information Security 2016</td>
<td>(1) 5.2 Policy; (2) 5. Information security policies; (3) 3.2 Awareness and training (AT-1); (4) 04.01 Information Security Policy; (5) SM1.1 Information Security Policy</td>
</tr>
</tbody>
</table>

F. Component: Culture, Ethics and Behavior

<table>
<thead>
<tr>
<th>Key Culture Elements</th>
<th>Related Guidance</th>
<th>Detailed Reference</th>
</tr>
</thead>
<tbody>
<tr>
<td>Establish a culture of security and privacy awareness that positively influences desirable behavior and actual implementation of security and privacy policy in daily practice. Provide sufficient security and privacy guidance, indicate security and privacy champions (including C-level executives, leaders in HR, and security and/or privacy professionals) and proactively support and communicate security and privacy programs, innovations and challenges.</td>
<td>(1) ISO/IEC 27001:2013/Cor.2:2015(E); (2) Creating a Culture of Security, ISACA, 2011</td>
<td>1) 7.3 Awareness; (2) Framework to achieve an intentional security aware culture (all chapters)</td>
</tr>
</tbody>
</table>

* Configuration management tools
* Security and privacy awareness services

# CHAPTER 4
## COBIT GOVERNANCE AND MANAGEMENT OBJECTIVES—DETAILED GUIDANCE

<table>
  <tr>
    <td colspan="2"><strong>Domain: Align, Plan and Organize</strong></td>
    <td><strong>Focus Area: COBIT Core Model</strong></td>
  </tr>
  <tr>
    <td colspan="2"><strong>Management Objective: APO14 – Managed Data</strong></td>
    <td></td>
  </tr>
  <tr>
    <td colspan="3">
      <table>
        <tr>
          <td><strong>Description</strong></td>
          <td></td>
        </tr>
        <tr>
          <td colspan="2">Achieve and sustain effective management of the enterprise data assets across the data life cycle, from creation through delivery, maintenance and archiving.</td>
        </tr>
        <tr>
          <td><strong>Purpose</strong></td>
          <td></td>
        </tr>
        <tr>
          <td colspan="2">Ensure effective utilization of the critical data assets to achieve enterprise goals and objectives.</td>
        </tr>
        <tr>
          <td colspan="2"><strong>The management objective supports the achievement of a set of primary enterprise and alignment goals:</strong></td>
        </tr>
        <tr>
          <td><strong>Enterprise Goals</strong></td>
          <td><strong>Alignment Goals</strong></td>
        </tr>
        <tr>
          <td>
            • EG04 Quality of financial information<br>
            • EG07 Quality of management information
          </td>
          <td>
            AG10 Quality of I&T management information
          </td>
        </tr>
        <tr>
          <td><strong>Example Metrics for Enterprise Goals</strong></td>
          <td><strong>Example Metrics for Alignment Goals</strong></td>
        </tr>
        <tr>
          <td>
            EG04
            <ul>
              <li>a. Satisfaction survey of key stakeholders regarding the transparency, understanding and accuracy of enterprise financial information</li>
              <li>b. Cost of noncompliance with finance-related regulations</li>
            </ul>
          </td>
          <td>
            AG10
            <ul>
              <li>a. Level of user satisfaction with quality, timeliness and availability of I&T-related management information, taking into account available resources</li>
              <li>b. Ratio and extent of erroneous business decisions in which erroneous or unavailable I&T-related information was a key factor</li>
              <li>c. Percentage of information meeting quality criteria</li>
            </ul>
          </td>
        </tr>
        <tr>
          <td>
            EG07
            <ul>
              <li>a. Degree of board and executive management satisfaction with decision-making information</li>
              <li>b. Number of incidents caused by incorrect business decisions based on inaccurate information</li>
              <li>c. Time to provide information supporting effective business decisions</li>
              <li>d. Timeliness of management information</li>
            </ul>
          </td>
          <td></td>
        </tr>
      </table>
    </td>
  </tr>
</table>

<table>
  <tr>
    <td colspan="2"><strong>A. Component: Process</strong></td>
  </tr>
  <tr>
    <td><strong>Management Practice</strong></td>
    <td><strong>Example Metrics</strong></td>
  </tr>
  <tr>
    <td>
      <strong>APO14.01 Define and communicate the organization’s data management strategy and roles and responsibilities.</strong><br>
      Define how to manage and improve the organization’s data assets, in line with enterprise strategy and objectives. Communicate the data management strategy to all stakeholders. Assign roles and responsibilities to ensure that corporate data are managed as critical assets and the data management strategy is implemented and maintained in an effective and sustainable manner.
    </td>
    <td>
      a. Number of data management breaches in comparison to the defined strategy<br>
      b. Percent of roles and responsibilities identified to support the governance of data management and the interaction between governance and the data management function
    </td>
  </tr>
  <tr>
    <td><strong>Activities</strong></td>
    <td><strong>Capability Level</strong></td>
  </tr>
  <tr>
    <td>
      1. Establish a data management function with responsibility for managing activities that support data management objectives.<br>
      2. Specify roles and responsibilities to support the management of data and the interaction between governance and the data management function.<br>
      3. Ensure that business and technology collaboratively develop the organization’s data management strategy. Make sure that data management objectives, priorities and scope reflect enterprise objectives, are consistent with data management policies and regulation, and are approved by all stakeholders.<br>
      4. Communicate data management objectives, priorities and scope and adjust them as needed, based upon feedback.<br>
      5. Use metrics to assess and monitor the achievement of objectives for data management.<br>
      6. Monitor the sequence plan for implementation of the data management strategy. Update it as needed, based on progress reviews.<br>
      7. Use statistical and other quantitative techniques to evaluate the effectiveness of strategic data management objectives in achieving business objectives. Make modifications as needed, based on metrics.<br>
      8. Ensure that the organization researches innovative business processes and emerging regulatory requirements to ensure that the data management program is compatible with future business needs.<br>
      9. Make contributions to industry best practices for data management strategy development and implementation.
    </td>
    <td>
      2<br>
      3<br>
      4<br>
      5
    </td>
  </tr>
</table>

## A. Component: Process (cont.)

### Management Practice

**APO14.02 Define and maintain a consistent business glossary.**
Create, approve, update and promote consistent business terms and definitions to foster shared data usage across the organization.

#### Example Metrics
a. Level of acceptance and frequency of use of business glossary terms throughout the entire organization
b. Number of synonyms for defined business glossary terminology that are used in new development efforts
c. Level of granularity of defined business glossary terms

### Activities

<table>
<thead>
<tr>
<th>Activities</th>
<th>Capability Level</th>
</tr>
</thead>
<tbody>
<tr>
<td>1. Ensure that standard business terms are readily available and communicated to relevant stakeholders.</td>
<td>2</td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td>2. Ensure that each business term added to the business glossary has a unique name and unique definition.</td>
<td></td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td>3. Use standard industry business terms and definitions, as appropriate, in the business glossary.</td>
<td></td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td>4. Establish, document and follow a process to define, manage, use and maintain the business glossary. For example, new initiatives should apply standard business terms as part of the data requirements definition process to ensure consistency of language. This will help achieve comparability of the content and facilitate data sharing across the organization.</td>
<td>3</td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td>5. Ensure that new development, data integration and data consolidation efforts apply standard business terms as part of the data requirements definition process.</td>
<td></td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td>6. Integrate the business glossary into the organization’s metadata repository, with appropriate access permissions.</td>
<td></td>
</tr>
</tbody>
</table>

### Related Guidance (Standards, Frameworks, Compliance Requirements)

<table>
<thead>
<tr>
<th>Related Guidance (Standards, Frameworks, Compliance Requirements)</th>
<th>Detailed Reference</th>
</tr>
</thead>
<tbody>
<tr>
<td>CMMI Data Management Maturity Model, 2014</td>
<td>Data Governance - Business Glossary</td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td>ISF, The Standard of Good Practice for Information Security 2016</td>
<td>IM1.1 Information Classification and Handling</td>
</tr>
</tbody>
</table>

### Management Practice

**APO14.03 Establish the processes and infrastructure for metadata management.**
Establish the processes and infrastructure for specifying and extending metadata about the organization’s data assets, fostering and supporting data sharing, ensuring compliant use of data, improving responsiveness to business changes and reducing data-related risk.

#### Example Metrics
a. Number of identified inaccuracies in metadata
b. Percent of metadata containing measures and metrics to evaluate the accuracy and adoption of metadata

### Activities

<table>
<thead>
<tr>
<th>Activities</th>
<th>Capability Level</th>
</tr>
</thead>
<tbody>
<tr>
<td>1. Establish and follow a metadata management process.</td>
<td>2</td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td>2. Ensure that metadata documentation captures data interdependencies.</td>
<td></td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td>3. Establish and follow metadata categories, properties and standards.</td>
<td></td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td>4. Develop and use metadata to perform impact analysis on potential data changes.</td>
<td>3</td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td>5. Populate the organization’s metadata repository with additional categories and classifications of metadata according to a phased implementation plan. Link it to architecture layers.</td>
<td></td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td>6. Validate metadata and any changes to metadata against the existing architecture.</td>
<td></td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td>7. Ensure that the organization has developed an integrated metamodel deployed across all platforms.</td>
<td></td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td>8. Ensure that metadata types and data definitions support consistent import, subscription and consumption practices.</td>
<td></td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td>9. Use measures and metrics to evaluate the accuracy and adoption of metadata.</td>
<td>4</td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td>10. Evaluate planned data changes for impact on the metadata repository. Continuously improve metadata capture, change and refinement processes.</td>
<td>5</td>
</tr>
</tbody>
</table>

### Related Guidance (Standards, Frameworks, Compliance Requirements)

<table>
<thead>
<tr>
<th>Related Guidance (Standards, Frameworks, Compliance Requirements)</th>
<th>Detailed Reference</th>
</tr>
</thead>
<tbody>
<tr>
<td>CMMI Data Management Maturity Model, 2014</td>
<td>Data Governance—Metadata Management</td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td>ISO/IEC 27002:2013/Cor.2:2015(E)</td>
<td>8.2 Information classification</td>
</tr>
</tbody>
</table>

## Page 145

CHAPTER 4
COBIT GOVERNANCE AND MANAGEMENT OBJECTIVES—DETAILED GUIDANCE

A. Component: Process (cont.)

<table>
<thead>
<tr>
<th>Management Practice</th>
<th>Example Metrics</th>
</tr>
</thead>
<tbody>
<tr>
<td><strong>APO14.04 Define a data quality strategy.</strong><br>Define an integrated, organizationwide strategy to achieve and maintain the level of data quality (such as complexity, integrity, accuracy, completeness, validity, traceability and timeliness) required to support the business goals and objectives.</td>
<td>a. Number of data quality improvement efforts identified and recorded in a sequence plan<br>b. Percent of stakeholders satisfied with the quality of data</td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td><strong>Activities</strong></td>
<td></td>
<td><strong>Capability Level</strong></td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td>1. Define a data quality strategy in collaboration with business and technology stakeholders, approved by executive management, and managed. The strategy should facilitate moving from the current to the target state. It should also explicitly align with business objectives and the organization’s data management strategy.</td>
<td></td>
<td>3</td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td>2. Ensure that the data quality strategy is followed across the organization and is accompanied by corresponding policies, processes and guidelines.</td>
<td></td>
<td></td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td>3. Anchor the policies, processes and governance contained in the data quality strategy across the data life cycle. Mandate corresponding processes in the system development life cycle methodology.</td>
<td></td>
<td></td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td>4. Develop, monitor and maintain a sequence plan for data quality improvement efforts across the organization.</td>
<td></td>
<td></td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td>5. To evaluate progress, monitor plans to meet the goals and objectives of the data quality strategy.</td>
<td></td>
<td>4</td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td>6. Systematically collect stakeholder reports of data quality issues. Include their expectations for improving data quality in the data quality strategy. Measure and monitor them.</td>
<td></td>
<td></td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td><strong>Related Guidance (Standards, Frameworks, Compliance Requirements)</strong></td>
<td><strong>Detailed Reference</strong></td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td>CMMI Cybermaturity Platform, 2018</td>
<td>DP.DR Safeguard Data at Rest; DP.DT Safeguard Data in Transit; DP.IP Integrity and Data Leak Prevention</td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td>CMMI Data Management Maturity Model, 2014</td>
<td>Data Quality - Data Quality Strategy</td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td><strong>Management Practice</strong></td>
<td><strong>Example Metrics</strong></td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td><strong>APO14.05 Establish data profiling methodologies, processes and tools.</strong><br>Implement standardized data profiling methodologies, processes, practices, tools and templates that can be applied across multiple data repositories and data stores.</td>
<td>a. Number of defined and implemented data templates and their usage percentage<br>b. Number of shared data sets with a defined data profile</td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td><strong>Activities</strong></td>
<td></td>
<td><strong>Capability Level</strong></td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td>1. Define and standardize data profiling methodologies, processes, practices, tools and results templates. Ensure that profiling processes are reusable and leveraged across multiple data stores and shared data repositories.</td>
<td></td>
<td>3</td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td>2. Engage data management to identify core shared data sets that are regularly profiled and monitored.</td>
<td></td>
<td>4</td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td>3. In data profiling efforts, include evaluation of the conformity of data content with its approved metadata and standards.</td>
<td></td>
<td></td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td>4. During a data profiling activity, compare actual issues to the statistically predicted issues, based on historical profiling results.</td>
<td></td>
<td></td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td>5. Ensure that results are centrally stored, systematically monitored and analyzed with respect to statistics and metrics. Provide the resulting insight to data quality improvements over time.</td>
<td></td>
<td></td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td>6. Create real-time or near real-time automated profiling reports for all critical data feeds and repositories.</td>
<td></td>
<td>5</td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td><strong>Related Guidance (Standards, Frameworks, Compliance Requirements)</strong></td>
<td><strong>Detailed Reference</strong></td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td>CMMI Data Management Maturity Model, 2014</td>
<td>Data Quality—Data Profiling</td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td>National Institute of Standards and Technology Special Publication 800-53, Revision 5, August 2017</td>
<td>3.20 System and information integrity (SI-1)</td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td><strong>Management Practice</strong></td>
<td><strong>Example Metrics</strong></td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td><strong>APO14.06 Ensure a data quality assessment approach.</strong><br>Provide a systematic approach to measure and evaluate data quality according to processes and techniques, and against data quality rules.</td>
<td>a. Number of identified issues in data quality assessment results<br>b. Number of data quality assessment results that include recommendations for remediation</td>
</tr>
</tbody>
</table>

A. Component: Process (cont.)

<tr>
<th>Activities</th>
<th>Capability Level</th>
</tr>
</thead>
<tbody>
<tr>
<td>1. Periodically conduct data quality assessments, according to an approved frequency per the data quality assessment policy. Ensure that data governance determines the key set of attributes by subject area for data quality assessments.</td>
<td>4</td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td>2. Include recommendations for remediation, with supporting rationale, in data quality assessment results.</td>
<td></td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td>3. Assess data quality, using established thresholds and targets for each selected quality dimension.</td>
<td></td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td>4. Systematically generate data quality measurement reports, based on criticality of attributes and data volatility.</td>
<td></td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td>5. Continuously review and improve data quality assessment and reporting processes.</td>
<td>5</td>
</tr>
