</tr>
</thead>
<tbody>
<tr>
<td>APO14.07 Define the data cleansing approach.<br/>Define the mechanisms, rules, processes, and methods to validate and correct data according to predefined business rules.</td>
<td>a. Percent of data cleansed correctly<br/>b. Percent of SLAs that include data quality criteria and hold data providers accountable for cleansed data</td>
</tr>
</tbody>
</table>

<table>
<thead>
<tr>
<th>Activities</th>
<th>Capability Level</th>
</tr>
</thead>
<tbody>
<tr>
<td>1. Establish and maintain a data cleansing policy.</td>
<td>2</td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td>2. Maintain data change history through cleansing activities.</td>
<td>3</td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td>3. Establish methods for correcting the data and define those methods within a plan. Methods may include multiple repository comparison, verification against a valid source, logic checks, referential integrity or range tolerance.</td>
<td>4</td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td>4. In service level agreements, include data quality criteria to hold data providers accountable for cleansed data.</td>
<td></td>
</tr>
</tbody>
</table>

<table>
<thead>
<tr>
<th>Related Guidance (Standards, Frameworks, Compliance Requirements)</th>

<td>Data Quality—Data Cleansing</td>
</tr>
</tbody>
</table>

<table>
<thead>
<tr>
<th>Management Practice</th>
<th>Example Metrics</th>
</tr>
</thead>
<tbody>
<tr>
<td>APO14.08 Manage the life cycle of data assets.<br/>Ensure that the organization understands, maps, inventories and controls its data flows through business processes over the data life cycle, from creation or acquisition to retirement.</td>
<td>a. Number of requirements from data consumers that cannot be mapped to a data source<br/>b. Number of shared data sets<br/>c. Time since last compliance check regarding mappings of business processes to data</td>
</tr>
</tbody>
</table>

<table>
<thead>
<tr>
<th>Activities</th>
<th>Capability Level</th>
</tr>
</thead>
<tbody>
<tr>
<td>1. Map and align the requirements of data consumers and producers.</td>
<td>2</td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td>2. Define business process-to-data mappings. Maintain them and periodically review them for compliance.</td>
<td>3</td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td>3. Follow a defined process for collaborative agreements with respect to shared data and data usage within business processes.</td>
<td></td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td>4. Implement data flows and full data-to-process life cycle maps for shared data for each major business process at the organizational level.</td>
<td></td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td>5. Ensure that changes to shared data sets or target data sets for a specific business purpose are managed by data governance structures, with relevant stakeholder engagement.</td>
<td></td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td>6. Use metrics to expand approved shared data reuse and eliminate process redundancy.</td>
<td>4</td>
</tr>
</tbody>
</table>

E. Component: Policies and Procedures

<table>
<thead>
<tr>
<th>Relevant Policy</th>
<th>Policy Description</th>
<th>Related Guidance</th>
<th>Detailed Reference</th>
</tr>
</thead>
<tbody>
<tr>
<td>Data cleansing policy</td>
<td>Outlines management's commitment to data cleansing. Prescribes frequency, guidelines and accountability; documents available methods, solutions and tools.</td>
<td>CMMI Data Management Maturity Model, 2014</td>
<td>Data Cleansing</td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td>Data management policy</td>
<td>Describes the organization's commitment to manage data assets across the data life cycle, from creation through delivery, maintenance and archiving.</td>
<td></td>
<td></td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td>Data quality assessment policy</td>
<td>Describes the organization's data quality assurance assessment philosophy for ensuring the integrity of the data being used to make decisions that impact the organization. Assigns the frequency, guidelines and accountability for data quality assessment. Outlines available methods, solutions and tools.</td>
<td>(1) CMMI Data Management Maturity Model, 2014; (2) National Institute of Standards and Technology Special Publication 800- 53, Revision 5 (Draft), August 2017</td>
<td>(1) Data Quality Assessment; (2) 3.20 System and information integrity (SI-1)</td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td>Privacy policy</td>
<td>Documents the collection, use, disclosure and management of personal data. Personal data can be any data that may be used to identify an individual, including, but not limited to, name, address, date of birth, marital status, contact information, ID issue and expiry date, financial records, credit information, medical history, travel destination, and intent to acquire goods or services. The privacy policy defines how an enterprise collects, stores and releases personal information; how and when the client is informed of specific information that is collected and whether it is kept confidential, shared with partners, or sold to other firms or enterprises. The policy mandates compliance with relevant legislation related to data protection.</td>
<td></td>
<td></td>
</tr>
</tbody>
</table>

F. Component: Culture, Ethics and Behavior

<table>

<tr>
<th>Key Culture Elements</th>
<th>Related Guidance</th>
<th>Detailed Reference</th>
</tr>

<td>Create a culture of shared responsibility for the organization's data assets; acknowledge the potential value of data assets and ensure that roles and responsibilities are clear for governance and management of data assets.</td>
<td>CMMI Data Management Maturity Model, 2014</td>
<td>Data Governance</td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td>Create awareness around data integrity, accuracy, completeness and protection to establish a culture of data quality. Relate data quality to the enterprise's core values. Continuously communicate the impact and risk of data loss. Ensure that employees understand the true cost of failing to implement a data quality culture.</td>
<td>CMMI Data Management Maturity Model, 2014</td>
<td>Data Quality</td>
</tr>

## Page 153

# CHAPTER 4
## COBIT GOVERNANCE AND MANAGEMENT OBJECTIVES—DETAILED GUIDANCE

<table>
  <tr>
    <td><strong>Domain:</strong> Build, Acquire and Implement<br><strong>Management Objective:</strong> BAI01 – Managed Programs</td>
    <td><strong>Focus Area:</strong> COBIT Core Model</td>
  </tr>
</table>

### Description
Manage all programs from the investment portfolio in alignment with enterprise strategy and in a coordinated way, based on a standard program management approach. Initiate, plan, control, and execute programs, and monitor expected value from the program.

### Purpose
Realize desired business value and reduce the risk of unexpected delays, costs and value erosion. To do so, improve communications to and involvement of business and end users, ensure the value and quality of program deliverables and follow up of projects within the programs, and maximize program contribution to the investment portfolio.

The management objective supports the achievement of a set of primary enterprise and alignment goals:

<table>
  <tr>
    <td><strong>Enterprise Goals</strong></td>
    <td><strong>Alignment Goals</strong></td>
  </tr>
  <tr>
    <td>
      • EG01 Portfolio of competitive products and services<br>
      • EG08 Optimization of internal business process functionality<br>
      • EG12 Managed digital transformation programs
    </td>
    <td>
      &lt;img&gt;Right arrow icon&lt;/img&gt;
      <br>
      • AG03 Realized benefits from I&T-enabled investments and services portfolio<br>
      • AG09 Delivering programs on time, on budget and meeting requirements and quality standards
    </td>
  </tr>
  <tr>
    <td><strong>Example Metrics for Enterprise Goals</strong></td>
    <td><strong>Example Metrics for Alignment Goals</strong></td>
  </tr>
  <tr>
    <td>
      EG01
      <ul>
        <li>a. Percent of products and services that meet or exceed targets in revenues and/or market share</li>
        <li>b. Percent of products and services that meet or exceed customer satisfaction targets</li>
        <li>c. Percent of products and services that provide competitive advantage</li>
        <li>d. Time to market for new products and services</li>
      </ul>
    </td>
    <td>
      AG03
      <ul>
        <li>a. Percent of I&T-enabled investments for which claimed benefits in the business case are met or exceeded</li>
        <li>b. Percent of I&T services for which expected benefits (as stated in service level agreements) are realized</li>
      </ul>
    </td>
  </tr>
  <tr>
    <td>
      EG08
      <ul>
        <li>a. Satisfaction levels of board and executive management with business process capabilities</li>
        <li>b. Satisfaction levels of customers with service delivery capabilities</li>
        <li>c. Satisfaction levels of suppliers with supply chain capabilities</li>
      </ul>
    </td>
    <td>
      AG09
      <ul>
        <li>a. Number of programs/projects on time and within budget</li>
        <li>b. Number of programs needing significant rework due to quality defects</li>
        <li>c. Percent of stakeholders satisfied with program/project quality</li>
      </ul>
    </td>
  </tr>
  <tr>
    <td>
      EG12
      <ul>
        <li>a. Number of programs on time and within budget</li>
        <li>b. Percent of stakeholders satisfied with program delivery</li>
        <li>c. Percent of business transformation programs stopped</li>
        <li>d. Percent of business transformation programs with regular reported status updates</li>
      </ul>
    </td>
    <td></td>
  </tr>
</table>

### A. Component: Process

<table>
  <tr>
    <td><strong>Management Practice</strong></td>
    <td><strong>Example Metrics</strong></td>
  </tr>
  <tr>
    <td>
      **BAI01.01 Maintain a standard approach for program management.**<br>
      Maintain a standard approach for program management that enables governance and management review, decision-making and delivery-management activities. These activities should focus consistently on business value and goals (i.e., requirements, risk, costs, schedule and quality targets).
    </td>
    <td>
      a. Percent of successful programs based on the defined standard approach<br>
      b. Percent of stakeholders satisfied with program management
    </td>
  </tr>
  <tr>
    <td><strong>Activities</strong></td>
    <td><strong>Capability Level</strong></td>
  </tr>
  <tr>
    <td>
      1. Maintain and enforce a standard approach to program management, aligned to the enterprise’s specific environment and with good practice based on defined process and use of appropriate technology. Ensure that the approach covers the full life cycle and disciplines to be followed, including the management of scope, resources, risk, cost, quality, time, communication, stakeholder involvement, procurement, change control, integration and benefit realization.<br><br>
      2. Put in place a program office or project management office (PMO) that maintains the standard approach for program and project management across the organization. The PMO supports all programs and projects by creating and maintaining required project documentation templates, providing training and best practices for program/project managers, tracking metrics on the use of best practices for project management, etc. In some cases the PMO may also report on program/project progress to senior management and/or stakeholders, help prioritize projects, and ensure all projects support the overall business objectives of the enterprise.<br><br>
      3. Evaluate lessons learned based on the use of the program management approach and update the approach accordingly.
    </td>
    <td>
      2<br>
      3<br>
      4
    </td>
  </tr>
</table>

&lt;page_number&gt;153&lt;/page_number&gt;

    </tr>
    <tr>
      <td>1. Agree on program sponsorship. Appoint a program board/committee with members who have strategic interest in the program, responsibility for investment decision making, will be significantly impacted by the program and will be required to enable delivery of the change.</td>
      <td></td>
      <td>2</td>
    </tr>
    <tr>
      <td>2. Appoint a dedicated manager for the program, with the commensurate competencies and skills to manage the program effectively and efficiently.</td>
      <td></td>
      <td></td>
    </tr>
    <tr>
      <td>3. Confirm the program mandate with sponsors and stakeholders. Articulate the strategic objectives for the program, potential strategies for delivery, improvement and benefits that are expected, and how the program fits with other initiatives.</td>
      <td></td>
      <td>3</td>
    </tr>
    <tr>
      <td>4. Develop a detailed business case for a program. Involve all key stakeholders to develop and document a complete understanding of the expected enterprise outcomes, how they will be measured, the full scope of initiatives required, the risk involved and the impact on all aspects of the enterprise. Identify and assess alternative courses of action to achieve the desired enterprise outcomes.</td>
      <td></td>
      <td></td>
    </tr>
    <tr>
      <td>5. Develop a benefits realization plan that will be managed throughout the program to ensure that planned benefits always have owners and are achieved, sustained and optimized.</td>
      <td></td>
      <td></td>
    </tr>
    <tr>
      <td>6. Prepare the initial (conceptual) program business case, providing essential decision-making information regarding purpose, contribution to business objectives, expected value created, time frames, etc. Submit it for approval.</td>
      <td></td>
      <td></td>
    </tr>
    <tr>
      <td>Related Guidance (Standards, Frameworks, Compliance Requirements)</td>
      <td>Detailed Reference</td>
      <td></td>
    </tr>
    <tr>
      <td>No related guidance for this management practice</td>
      <td></td>
      <td></td>
    </tr>
    <tr>
      <td>Management Practice</td>
      <td>Example Metrics</td>
      <td></td>
    </tr>
    <tr>
      <td>BAI01.03 Manage stakeholder engagement.</td>
      <td>a. Level of stakeholder satisfaction with involvement<br>b. Percent of stakeholders effectively engaged</td>
      <td></td>
    </tr>
    <tr>
      <td>Activities</td>
      <td></td>
      <td>Capability Level</td>
    </tr>
    <tr>
      <td>1. Plan how stakeholders inside and outside the enterprise will be identified, analyzed, engaged and managed through the life cycle of the projects.</td>
      <td></td>
      <td>3</td>
    </tr>
    <tr>
      <td>2. Identify, engage and manage stakeholders by establishing and maintaining appropriate levels of coordination, communication and liaison to ensure that they are involved in the program.</td>
      <td></td>
      <td></td>
    </tr>
    <tr>
      <td>3. Analyze stakeholder interests and requirements.</td>
      <td></td>
      <td></td>
    </tr>
    <tr>
      <td>4. Follow a defined process for collaborative agreements with respect to shared data and data usage within business processes.</td>
      <td></td>
      <td>4</td>
    </tr>
    <tr>
      <td>Related Guidance (Standards, Frameworks, Compliance Requirements)</td>
      <td>Detailed Reference</td>
      <td></td>
    </tr>
    <tr>
      <td>PMBOK Guide Sixth Edition, 2017</td>
      <td>Part 1: 10. Project communications management</td>
      <td></td>
    </tr>
    <tr>
      <td>Management Practice</td>
      <td>Example Metrics</td>
      <td></td>
    </tr>
    <tr>
      <td>BAI01.04 Develop and maintain the program plan.</td>
      <td>a. Frequency of program status reviews that do not meet value criteria<br>b. Percent of active programs undertaken without valid and updated program value maps</td>
      <td></td>
    </tr>
  </tbody>
</table>

## Page 155

CHAPTER 4
COBIT GOVERNANCE AND MANAGEMENT OBJECTIVES—DETAILED GUIDANCE

A. Component: Process (cont.)

<table>
<thead>
<tr>
<th>Activities</th>
<th>Capability Level</th>
</tr>
</thead>
<tbody>
<tr>
<td>1. Specify funding, cost, schedule and interdependencies of multiple projects.</td>
<td>2</td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td>2. Define and document the program plan covering all projects. Include what is needed to bring about changes to the enterprise; its purpose, mission, vision, values, culture, products and services; business processes; people skills and numbers; relationships with stakeholders, customers, suppliers and others; technology needs; and organizational restructuring required to achieve the program’s expected enterprise outcomes.</td>
<td>3</td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td>3. Ensure that there is effective communication of program plans and progress reports among all projects and with the overall program. Ensure that any changes made to individual plans are reflected in the other enterprise program plans.</td>
<td></td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td>4. Maintain the program plan to ensure that it is up to date and reflects alignment with current strategic objectives, actual progress and material changes to outcomes, benefits, costs and risk. Have the business drive the objectives and prioritize the work throughout to ensure that the program, as designed, will meet enterprise requirements. Review progress of individual projects and adjust the projects as necessary to meet scheduled milestones and releases.</td>
<td></td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td>5. Throughout the program’s economic life, update and maintain the business case and a benefits register to identify and define key benefits arising from undertaking the program.</td>
<td></td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td>6. Prepare a program budget that reflects the full economic life cycle costs and the associated financial and nonfinancial benefits.</td>
<td></td>
</tr>
</tbody>
</table>

<table>
<thead>
<tr>
<th>Related Guidance (Standards, Frameworks, Compliance Requirements)</th>
<th>Detailed Reference</th>
</tr>
</thead>
<tbody>
<tr>
<td>No related guidance for this management practice</td>
<td></td>
</tr>
</tbody>
</table>

<table>
<thead>
<tr>
<th>Management Practice</th>
<th>Example Metrics</th>
</tr>
</thead>
<tbody>
<tr>
<td><strong>BAI01.05 Launch and execute the program.</strong><br>Launch and execute the program to acquire and direct the resources needed to accomplish the goals and benefits of the program as defined in the program plan. In accordance with stage-gate or release review criteria, prepare for stage-gate, iteration or release reviews to report progress and make the case for funding up to the following stage-gate or release review.</td>
<td>a. Percent of stakeholder sign-offs for stage-gate reviews of active programs<br>b. Number of root cause analysis for deviations from the plan and necessary remedial actions addressed</td>
</tr>
</tbody>
</table>

<table>
<thead>
<tr>
<th>Activities</th>
<th>Capability Level</th>
</tr>
</thead>
<tbody>
<tr>
<td>1. Plan, resource and commission the necessary projects required to achieve the program results, based on funding review and approvals at each stage-gate review.</td>
<td>3</td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td>2. Manage each program or project to ensure that decision making and delivery activities are focused on value by achieving benefits for the business and goals in a consistent manner, addressing risk, and achieving stakeholder requirements.</td>
<td></td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td>3. Establish agreed stages of the development process (development checkpoints). At the end of each stage, facilitate formal discussions of approved criteria with the stakeholders. After successful completion of functionality, performance and quality reviews, and before finalizing stage activities, obtain formal approval and sign-off from all stakeholders and the sponsor/business process owner.</td>
<td></td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td>4. Undertake a benefits realization process throughout the program to ensure that planned benefits always have owners and are likely to be achieved, sustained and optimized. Monitor benefits delivery and report against performance targets at the stage-gate or iteration and release reviews. Perform root cause analysis for deviations from the plan and identify and address any necessary remedial actions.</td>
<td>4</td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td>5. Plan audits, quality reviews, phase/stage-gate reviews and reviews of realized benefits.</td>
<td></td>
</tr>
</tbody>
</table>

<table>
<thead>
<tr>
<th>Related Guidance (Standards, Frameworks, Compliance Requirements)</th>
<th>Detailed Reference</th>
</tr>
</thead>
<tbody>
<tr>
<td>No related guidance for this management practice</td>
<td></td>
</tr>

## Page 156

# COBIT® 2019 FRAMEWORK: GOVERNANCE AND MANAGEMENT OBJECTIVES

**A. Component: Process (cont.)**

<table>
  <thead>
    <tr>
      <th>Management Practice</th>
      <th>Example Metrics</th>
    </tr>
  </thead>
  <tbody>
    <tr>
      <td>
        **BAI01.06 Monitor, control and report on the program outcomes.**<br>
        Monitor and control performance against plan throughout the full economic life cycle of the investment, covering solution delivery at the program level and value/outcome at the enterprise level. Report performance to the program steering committee and the sponsors.
      </td>
      <td>
        a. Percent of expected program benefits achieved<br>
        b. Percent of programs for which performance was monitored and timely remedial action taken when required
      </td>
    </tr>
    <tr>
      <td>
        **Activities**
      </td>
      <td>
        <table>
          <thead>
            <tr>
              <th></th>
              <th>Capability Level</th>
            </tr>
          </thead>
          <tbody>
            <tr>
              <td>
                1. Update operational I&T portfolios to reflect changes that result from the program in the relevant I&T service, asset or resource portfolios.
              </td>
              <td>3</td>
            </tr>
            <tr>
              <td>
                2. Monitor and control the performance of the overall program and the projects within the program, including contributions of the business and IT to the projects. Report in a timely, complete and accurate fashion. Reporting may include schedule, funding, functionality, user satisfaction, internal controls and acceptance of accountabilities.
              </td>
              <td>4</td>
            </tr>
            <tr>
              <td>
                3. Monitor and control performance against enterprise and I&T strategies and goals. Report to management on enterprise changes implemented, benefits realized against the benefits realization plan and the adequacy of the benefits realization process.
              </td>
              <td></td>
            </tr>
            <tr>
              <td>
                4. Monitor and control IT services, assets and resources created or changed as a result of the program. Note implementation and in-service dates. Report to management on performance levels, sustained service delivery and contribution to value.
              </td>
              <td></td>
            </tr>
            <tr>
              <td>
                5. Manage program performance against key criteria (e.g., scope, schedule, quality, benefits realization, costs, risk, velocity), identify deviations from the plan and take timely remedial action when required.
              </td>
              <td></td>
            </tr>
            <tr>
              <td>
                6. Monitor individual project performance related to delivery of the expected capabilities, schedule, benefits realization, costs, risk or other metric. Identify potential impacts on program performance and take timely remedial action when required.
              </td>
              <td></td>
            </tr>
            <tr>
              <td>
                7. In accordance with stage-gate, release or iteration review criteria, undertake reviews to report on the progress of the program so that management can make go/no-go or adjustment decisions and approve further funding up to the following stage-gate, release or iteration.
              </td>
              <td></td>
            </tr>
          </tbody>
        </table>
      </td>
    </tr>
    <tr>
      <td>
        **Related Guidance (Standards, Frameworks, Compliance Requirements)**<br>
        No related guidance for this management practice
      </td>
      <td>
        **Detailed Reference**
      </td>
    </tr>
  </tbody>
</table>

**Build, Acquire and Implement**

<table>
  <thead>
    <tr>
      <th>Management Practice</th>
      <th>Example Metrics</th>
    </tr>
  </thead>
  <tbody>
    <tr>
      <td>
        **BAI01.07 Manage program quality.**<br>
        Prepare and execute a quality management plan, processes and practices that align with quality management standards (QMS). Describe the approach to program quality and implementation. The plan should be formally reviewed and agreed on by all parties concerned and incorporated into the integrated program plan.
      </td>
      <td>
        a. Percent of build-to-packages without errors<br>
        b. Percent of program deliverables approved at each gate review
      </td>
    </tr>
    <tr>
      <td>
        **Activities**
      </td>
      <td>
        <table>
          <thead>
            <tr>
              <th></th>
              <th>Capability Level</th>
            </tr>
          </thead>
          <tbody>
            <tr>
              <td>
                1. Identify assurance tasks and practices required to support the accreditation of new or modified systems during program planning, and include them in the integrated plans. Ensure that the tasks provide assurance that internal controls and security/privacy solutions meet the defined requirements.
              </td>
              <td>3</td>
            </tr>
            <tr>
              <td>
                2. To provide quality assurance for the program deliverables, identify ownership and responsibilities, quality review processes, success criteria and performance metrics.
              </td>
              <td></td>
            </tr>
            <tr>
              <td>
                3. Define any requirements for independent validation and verification of the quality of deliverables in the plan.
              </td>
              <td>4</td>
            </tr>
            <tr>
              <td>
                4. Perform quality assurance and control activities in accordance with the quality management plan and QMS.
              </td>
              <td></td>
            </tr>
          </tbody>
        </table>
      </td>
    </tr>
    <tr>
      <td>
        **Related Guidance (Standards, Frameworks, Compliance Requirements)**<br>
        No related guidance for this management practice
      </td>
      <td>
        **Detailed Reference**
      </td>
    </tr>

## Page 157

CHAPTER 4
COBIT GOVERNANCE AND MANAGEMENT OBJECTIVES—DETAILED GUIDANCE

A. Component: Process (cont.)

<table>
<thead>
<tr>
<th>Management Practice</th>
<th>Example Metrics</th>
</tr>
</thead>
<tbody>
<tr>
<td><strong>BAI01.08 Manage program risk.</strong><br>Eliminate or minimize specific risk associated with programs through a systematic process of planning, identifying, analyzing, responding to, monitoring and controlling the areas or events with the potential to cause unwanted change. Define and record any risk faced by program management.</td>
<td>a. Number of programs without a proper risk assessment<br>b. Percent of programs aligned with the enterprise risk management framework</td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td><strong>Activities</strong></td>
<td></td>
<td><strong>Capability Level</strong></td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td>1. Establish a formal risk management approach aligned with the enterprise risk management (ERM) framework. Ensure that the approach includes identifying, analyzing, responding to, mitigating, monitoring and controlling risk.</td>
<td></td>
<td>3</td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td>2. Assign to appropriately skilled personnel the responsibility for executing the enterprise’s risk management process within a program and ensuring that this is incorporated into the solution development practices. Consider allocating this role to an independent team, especially if an objective viewpoint is required or a program is considered critical.</td>
<td></td>
<td></td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td>3. Perform the risk assessment of identifying and quantifying risk continuously throughout the program. Manage and communicate risk appropriately within the program governance structure.</td>
<td></td>
<td></td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td>4. Identify owners for actions to avoid, accept or mitigate risk.</td>
<td></td>
<td></td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td><strong>Related Guidance (Standards, Frameworks, Compliance Requirements)</strong></td>
<td><strong>Detailed Reference</strong></td>
<td></td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td>No related guidance for this management practice</td>
<td></td>
<td></td>
</tr>
</tbody>
</table>

<table>
<thead>
<tr>
<th>Management Practice</th>
<th>Example Metrics</th>
</tr>
</thead>
<tbody>
<tr>
<td><strong>BAI01.09 Close a program.</strong><br>Remove the program from the active investment portfolio when there is agreement that the desired value has been achieved or when it is clear it will not be achieved within the value criteria set for the program.</td>
<td>a. Percent of successfully closed programs that achieved desired value<br>b. Time between program launch and detection of achievability of value</td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td><strong>Activities</strong></td>
<td></td>
<td><strong>Capability Level</strong></td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td>1. Bring the program to an orderly closure, including formal approval, disbanding of the program organization and supporting function, validation of deliverables, and communication of retirement.</td>
<td></td>
<td>3</td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td>2. Review and document lessons learned. Once the program is retired, remove it from the active investment portfolio. Move any resulting capabilities to an operational asset portfolio to ensure that value continues to be created and sustained.</td>
<td></td>
<td>4</td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td>3. Put accountability and processes in place to ensure that the enterprise continues to optimize value from the service, asset or resources. Additional investments may be required at some future time to ensure that this occurs.</td>
<td></td>
<td>5</td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td><strong>Related Guidance (Standards, Frameworks, Compliance Requirements)</strong></td>
<td><strong>Detailed Reference</strong></td>
<td></td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td>National Institute of Standards and Technology Framework for Improving Critical Infrastructure Cybersecurity v1.1, April 2018</td>
<td>RS.IM Improvements</td>
<td></td>
</tr>
</tbody>
</table>

## Page 163

# CHAPTER 4
## COBIT GOVERNANCE AND MANAGEMENT OBJECTIVES—DETAILED GUIDANCE

<table>
  <tr>
    <td style="vertical-align: top;">
      **Domain:** Build, Acquire and Implement<br>
      **Management Objective:** BAI02 – Managed Requirements Definition
    </td>
    <td style="vertical-align: top;">
      **Focus Area:** COBIT Core Model
    </td>
  </tr>
</table>

### Description
Identify solutions and analyze requirements before acquisition or creation to ensure that they align with enterprise strategic requirements covering business processes, applications, information/data, infrastructure and services. Coordinate the review of feasible options with affected stakeholders, including relative costs and benefits, risk analysis, and approval of requirements and proposed solutions.

### Purpose
Create optimal solutions that meet enterprise needs while minimizing risk.

The management objective supports the achievement of a set of primary enterprise and alignment goals:

<table>
  <thead>
    <tr>
      <th>Enterprise Goals</th>
      <th>Alignment Goals</th>
    </tr>
  </thead>
  <tbody>
    <tr>
      <td>
        • EG01 Portfolio of competitive products and services<br>
        • EG08 Optimization of internal business process functionality<br>
        • EG12 Managed digital transformation programs
      </td>
      <td>
        • AG05 Delivery of I&T services in line with business requirements<br>
        • AG06 Agility to turn business requirements into operational solutions<br>
        • AG09 Delivering programs on time, on budget and meeting requirements and quality standards
      </td>
    </tr>
  </tbody>
</table>

### Example Metrics for Enterprise Goals
**EG01**
a. Percent of products and services that meet or exceed targets in revenues and/or market share
b. Percent of products and services that meet or exceed customer satisfaction targets
c. Percent of products and services that provide competitive advantage
d. Time to market for new products and services

**EG08**
a. Satisfaction levels of board and executive management with business process capabilities
b. Satisfaction levels of customers with service delivery capabilities
c. Satisfaction levels of suppliers with supply chain capabilities

**EG12**
a. Number of programs on time and within budget
b. Percent of stakeholders satisfied with program delivery
c. Percent of business transformation programs stopped
d. Percent of business transformation programs with regular reported status updates

### Example Metrics for Alignment Goals
**AG05**
a. Percent of business stakeholders satisfied that I&T service delivery meets agreed service levels
b. Number of business disruptions due to I&T service incidents
c. Percent of users satisfied with the quality of I&T service delivery

**AG06**
a. Level of satisfaction of business executives with I&T responsiveness to new requirements
b. Average time to market for new I&T-related services and applications
c. Average time to turn strategic I&T objectives into agreed and approved initiatives
d. Number of critical business processes supported by up-to-date infrastructure and applications

**AG09**
a. Number of programs/projects on time and within budget
b. Number of programs needing significant rework due to quality defects
c. Percent of stakeholders satisfied with program/project quality

Build, Acquire and Implement

&lt;page_number&gt;163&lt;/page_number&gt;

## Page 164

# COBIT® 2019 FRAMEWORK: GOVERNANCE AND MANAGEMENT OBJECTIVES

## A. Component: Process

<table>
  <thead>
    <tr>
      <th>Management Practice</th>
      <th>Example Metrics</th>
    </tr>
  </thead>
  <tbody>
    <tr>
      <td><strong>BAI02.01 Define and maintain business functional and technical requirements.</strong><br>Based on the business case, identify, prioritize, specify and agree on business information, functional, technical and control requirements covering the scope/understanding of all initiatives required to achieve the expected outcomes of the proposed I&T-enabled business solution.</td>
      <td>a. Percent of requirements reworked due to misalignment with enterprise needs and expectations<br>b. Percent of requirements validated through approaches such as peer review, model validation or operational prototyping</td>
    </tr>
  </tbody>
</table>

<table>
  <thead>
    <tr>
      <th>Activities</th>
      <th>Capability Level</th>
    </tr>
  </thead>
  <tbody>
    <tr>
      <td>1. Ensure that all stakeholder requirements, including relevant acceptance criteria, are considered, captured, prioritized and recorded in a way that is understandable to all stakeholders, recognizing that the requirements may change and will become more detailed as they are implemented.</td>
      <td>2</td>
    </tr>
    <tr>
      <td>2. Express business requirements in terms of how the gap between current and desired business capabilities need to be addressed and how the user (employee, client, etc.) will interact with and use the solution.</td>
      <td></td>
    </tr>
    <tr>
      <td>3. Specify and prioritize information, functional and technical requirements, based on the user experience design and confirmed stakeholder requirements.</td>
      <td></td>
    </tr>
    <tr>
      <td>4. Ensure requirements meet enterprise policies and standards, enterprise architecture, strategic and tactical I&T plans, in-house and outsourced business and IT processes, security requirements, regulatory requirements, people competencies, organizational structure, business case, and enabling technology.</td>
      <td>3</td>
    </tr>
    <tr>
      <td>5. Include information control requirements in the business processes, automated processes and I&T environments to address information risk and to comply with laws, regulations and commercial contracts.</td>
      <td></td>
    </tr>
    <tr>
      <td>6. Confirm acceptance of key aspects of the requirements, including enterprise rules, user experience, information controls, business continuity, legal and regulatory compliance, auditability, ergonomics, operability and usability, safety, confidentiality, and supporting documentation.</td>
      <td></td>
    </tr>
    <tr>
      <td>7. Track and control scope, requirements and changes through the life cycle of the solution as understanding of the solution evolves.</td>
      <td></td>
    </tr>
    <tr>
      <td>8. Define and implement a requirements definition and maintenance procedure and a requirements repository that are appropriate for the size, complexity, objectives and risk of the initiative that the enterprise is considering undertaking.</td>
      <td></td>
    </tr>
    <tr>
      <td>9. Validate all requirements through approaches such as peer review, model validation or operational prototyping.</td>
      <td></td>
    </tr>
  </tbody>
</table>

<table>
  <thead>
    <tr>
      <th>Related Guidance (Standards, Frameworks, Compliance Requirements)</th>
      <th>Detailed Reference</th>
    </tr>
  </thead>
  <tbody>
    <tr>
      <td>ISF, The Standard of Good Practice for Information Security 2016</td>
      <td>SD2.1 Specifications of Requirements</td>
    </tr>
    <tr>
      <td>ISO/IEC 27002:2013/Cor.2:2015(E)</td>
      <td>14.1 Security requirements of information systems</td>
    </tr>
    <tr>
      <td>ITIL V3, 2011</td>
      <td>Service Design, 5.1 Requirements engineering</td>
    </tr>
    <tr>
      <td>PMBOK Guide Sixth Edition, 2017</td>
      <td>Part 1: 5. Project scope management</td>
    </tr>
  </tbody>
</table>

<table>
  <thead>
    <tr>
      <th>Management Practice</th>
      <th>Example Metrics</th>
    </tr>
  </thead>
  <tbody>
    <tr>
      <td><strong>BAI02.02 Perform a feasibility study and formulate alternative solutions.</strong><br>Perform a feasibility study of potential alternative solutions, assess their viability and select the preferred option. If appropriate, implement the selected option as a pilot to determine possible improvements.</td>
      <td>a. Percent of business case objectives met by proposed solution<br>b. Percent of requirements satisfied by proposed solution</td>
    </tr>
  </tbody>
</table>

<table>
  <thead>
    <tr>
      <th>Activities</th>
      <th>Capability Level</th>
    </tr>
  </thead>
  <tbody>
    <tr>
      <td>1. Identify required actions for solution acquisition or development based on the enterprise architecture. Take into account scope and/or time and/or budget limitations.</td>
      <td>2</td>
    </tr>
    <tr>
      <td>2. Review the alternative solutions with all stakeholders. Select the most appropriate one based on feasibility criteria, including risk and cost.</td>
      <td></td>
    </tr>
    <tr>
      <td>3. Translate the preferred course of action into a high-level acquisition/development plan that identifies resources to be used and stages requiring a go/no-go decision.</td>
      <td>3</td>
    </tr>
    <tr>
      <td>4. Define and execute a feasibility study, pilot or basic working solution that clearly and concisely describes the alternative solutions and measures how these would satisfy the business and functional requirements. Include an evaluation of their technological and economic feasibility.</td>
      <td>4</td>
    </tr>
  </tbody>
</table>

<table>
  <thead>
    <tr>
      <th>Related Guidance (Standards, Frameworks, Compliance Requirements)</th>
      <th>Detailed Reference</th>
    </tr>
  </thead>
  <tbody>
    <tr>
      <td>No related guidance for this management practice</td>
      <td></td>
    </tr>
  </tbody>
</table>

A. Component: Process (cont.)

<table>
<thead>
<tr>
<th>Management Practice</th>
<th>Example Metrics</th>
</tr>
</thead>
<tbody>
<tr>
<td><strong>BAI02.03 Manage requirements risk.</strong><br>Identify, document, prioritize and mitigate functional, technical and information processing-related risk associated with the enterprise requirements, assumptions and proposed solution.</td>
<td>a. Percent of requirements risk not covered by an appropriate risk response<br>b. Level of detail of documented requirements risk<br>c. Completeness of estimated probability and impact of listed requirements risk and risk responses</td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td><strong>Activities</strong></td>
<td></td>
<td><strong>Capability Level</strong></td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td>1. Identify quality, functional and technical requirements risk (due to, for example, lack of user involvement, unrealistic expectations, developers adding unnecessary functionality, unrealistic assumptions, etc.).</td>
<td></td>
<td>3</td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td>2. Determine appropriate risk response to requirements risk.</td>
<td></td>
<td></td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td>3. Analyze the identified risk by estimating probability and impact on budget and schedule. Evaluate budgetary impact of appropriate risk response actions.</td>
<td></td>
<td>4</td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td><strong>Related Guidance (Standards, Frameworks, Compliance Requirements)</strong></td>
<td><strong>Detailed Reference</strong></td>
<td></td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td>No related guidance for this management practice</td>
<td></td>
<td></td>
</tr>
</tbody>
</table>

<table>
<thead>
<tr>
<th>Management Practice</th>
<th>Example Metrics</th>
</tr>
</thead>
<tbody>
<tr>
<td><strong>BAI02.04 Obtain approval of requirements and solutions.</strong><br>Coordinate feedback from affected stakeholders. At predetermined key stages, obtain approval and sign-off from the business sponsor or product owner regarding functional and technical requirements, feasibility studies, risk analyses and recommended solutions.</td>
<td>a. Level of stakeholder satisfaction with requirements<br>b. Number of solution exceptions to design noted during stage reviews<br>c. Percent of stakeholders not approving solution in relation to business case</td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td><strong>Activities</strong></td>
<td></td>
<td><strong>Capability Level</strong></td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td>1. Ensure that the business sponsor or product owner makes the final choice of solution, acquisition approach and high-level design, according to the business case. Obtain necessary approvals from affected stakeholders (e.g., business process owner, enterprise architect, operations manager, security, privacy officer).</td>
<td></td>
<td>3</td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td>2. Obtain quality reviews throughout, and at the end of, each key project stage, iteration or release. Assess the results against the original acceptance criteria. Have business sponsors and other stakeholders sign off on each successful quality review.</td>
<td></td>
<td>4</td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td><strong>Related Guidance (Standards, Frameworks, Compliance Requirements)</strong></td>
<td><strong>Detailed Reference</strong></td>
<td></td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td>No related guidance for this management practice</td>
<td></td>
<td></td>
</tr>
</tbody>
</table>

## Page 169

# CHAPTER 4
## COBIT GOVERNANCE AND MANAGEMENT OBJECTIVES—DETAILED GUIDANCE

<table>
  <tr>
    <td style="vertical-align: top;">
      **Domain:** Build, Acquire and Implement<br>
      **Management Objective:** BAI03 – Managed Solutions Identification and Build
    </td>
    <td style="vertical-align: top;">
      **Focus Area:** COBIT Core Model
    </td>
  </tr>
</table>

### Description
Establish and maintain identified products and services (technology, business processes and workflows) in line with enterprise requirements covering design, development, procurement/sourcing and partnering with vendors. Manage configuration, test preparation, testing, requirements management and maintenance of business processes, applications, information/data, infrastructure and services.

### Purpose
Ensure agile and scalable delivery of digital products and services. Establish timely and cost-effective solutions (technology, business processes and workflows) capable of supporting enterprise strategic and operational objectives.

The management objective supports the achievement of a set of primary enterprise and alignment goals:

<table>
  <tr>
    <td style="vertical-align: top; width: 50%;">
      ### Enterprise Goals
      <ul>
        <li>• EG01 Portfolio of competitive products and services</li>
        <li>• EG08 Optimization of internal business process functionality</li>
        <li>• EG12 Managed digital transformation programs</li>
      </ul>
      ### Example Metrics for Enterprise Goals
      <table>
        <tr>
          <td>EG01</td>
          <td>
            <ul>
              <li>a. Percent of products and services that meet or exceed targets in revenues and/or market share</li>
              <li>b. Percent of products and services that meet or exceed customer satisfaction targets</li>
              <li>c. Percent of products and services that provide competitive advantage</li>
              <li>d. Time to market for new products and services</li>
            </ul>
          </td>
        </tr>
        <tr>
          <td>EG08</td>
          <td>
            <ul>
              <li>a. Satisfaction levels of board and executive management with business process capabilities</li>
              <li>b. Satisfaction levels of customers with service delivery capabilities</li>
              <li>c. Satisfaction levels of suppliers with supply chain capabilities</li>
            </ul>
          </td>
        </tr>
        <tr>
          <td>EG12</td>
          <td>
            <ul>
              <li>a. Number of programs on time and within budget</li>
              <li>b. Percent of stakeholders satisfied with program delivery</li>
              <li>c. Percent of business transformation programs stopped</li>
              <li>d. Percent of business transformation programs with regular reported status updates</li>
            </ul>
          </td>
        </tr>
      </table>
    </td>
    <td style="vertical-align: top; width: 50%;">
      ### Alignment Goals
      <ul>
        <li>• AG05 Delivery of I&T services in line with business requirements</li>
        <li>• AG06 Agility to turn business requirements into operational solutions</li>
        <li>• AG09 Delivering programs on time, on budget and meeting requirements and quality standards</li>
      </ul>
      ### Example Metrics for Alignment Goals
      <table>
        <tr>
          <td>AG05</td>
          <td>
            <ul>
              <li>a. Percent of business stakeholders satisfied that I&T service delivery meets agreed service levels</li>
              <li>b. Number of business disruptions due to I&T service incidents</li>
              <li>c. Percent of users satisfied with the quality of I&T service delivery</li>
            </ul>
          </td>
        </tr>
        <tr>
          <td>AG06</td>
          <td>
            <ul>
              <li>a. Level of satisfaction of business executives with I&T responsiveness to new requirements</li>
              <li>b. Average time to market for new I&T-related services and applications</li>
              <li>c. Average time to turn strategic I&T objectives into agreed and approved initiatives</li>
              <li>d. Number of critical business processes supported by up-to-date infrastructure and applications</li>
            </ul>
          </td>
        </tr>
        <tr>
          <td>AG09</td>
          <td>
            <ul>
              <li>a. Number of programs/projects on time and within budget</li>
              <li>b. Number of programs needing significant rework due to quality defects</li>
              <li>c. Percent of stakeholders satisfied with program/project quality</li>
            </ul>
          </td>
        </tr>
      </table>
    </td>
  </tr>
</table>

### A. Component: Process

<table>
  <tr>
    <td style="vertical-align: top;">
      **Management Practice**
      <br>
      **BAI03.01 Design high-level solutions.**<br>
      Develop and document high-level designs for the solution in terms of technology, business processes and workflows. Use agreed and appropriate phased or rapid Agile development techniques. Ensure alignment with the I&T strategy and enterprise architecture. Reassess and update the designs when significant issues occur during detailed design or building phases, or as the solution evolves. Apply a user-centric approach; ensure that stakeholders actively participate in the design and approve each version.
    </td>
    <td style="vertical-align: top;">
      **Example Metrics**
      <ul>
        <li>a. Number of design review deficiencies</li>
        <li>b. Percent of stakeholder participation in the design and approval signoff of each version</li>
      </ul>
    </td>
  </tr>
</table>

&lt;page_number&gt;169&lt;/page_number&gt;

---


## Page 170

# COBIT® 2019 FRAMEWORK: GOVERNANCE AND MANAGEMENT OBJECTIVES

**A. Component: Process (cont.)**

<table>
<thead>
<tr>
<th>Activities</th>
<th>Capability Level</th>
</tr>
</thead>
<tbody>
<tr>
<td>1. Establish a high-level design specification that translates the proposed solution into a high-level design for business processes, supporting services, workflows, applications, infrastructure, and information repositories capable of meeting business and enterprise architecture requirements.</td>
<td rowspan="4">2</td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td>2. Involve appropriately qualified and experienced user experience designers and IT specialists in the design process to make sure that the design provides a solution that optimally uses the proposed I&T capabilities to enhance the business process.</td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td>3. Create a design that complies with the organization’s design standards. Ensure that it maintains a level of detail that is appropriate for the solution and development method and consistent with business, enterprise and I&T strategies, the enterprise architecture, security/privacy plan and applicable laws, regulations and contracts.</td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td>4. After quality assurance approval, submit the final high-level design to the project stakeholders and the sponsor/business process owner for approval based on agreed criteria. This design will evolve throughout the project as understanding grows.</td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td><strong>Related Guidance (Standards, Frameworks, Compliance Requirements)</strong></td>
<td><strong>Detailed Reference</strong></td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td>ISF, The Standard of Good Practice for Information Security 2016</td>
<td>SD2.2 System Design</td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td><strong>Management Practice</strong></td>
<td><strong>Example Metrics</strong></td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td><strong>BAI03.02 Design detailed solution components.</strong><br>Develop, document and elaborate detailed designs progressively. Use agreed and appropriate phased or rapid Agile development techniques, addressing all components (business processes and related automated and manual controls, supporting I&T applications, infrastructure services and technology products, and partners/suppliers). Ensure that the detailed design includes internal and external service level agreements (SLAs) and operational level agreements (OLAs).</td>
<td>a. Number of design review deficiencies<br>b. Number of in-process design changes</td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td><strong>Activities</strong></td>
<td><strong>Capability Level</strong></td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td>1. Design progressively the business process activities and work flows that need to be performed in conjunction with the new application system to meet the enterprise objectives, including the design of the manual control activities.</td>
<td rowspan="10">2</td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td>2. Design the application processing steps. These steps include specification of transaction types and business processing rules, automated controls, data definitions/business objects, use cases, external interfaces, design constraints, and other requirements (e.g., licensing, legal, standards and internationalization/localization).</td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td>3. Classify data inputs and outputs according to enterprise architecture standards. Specify the source data collection design. Document the data inputs (regardless of source) and validation for processing transactions as well as the methods for validation. Design the identified outputs, including data sources.</td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td>4. Design the system/solution interface, including any automated data exchange.</td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td>5. Design data storage, location, retrieval and recoverability.</td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td>6. Design appropriate redundancy, recovery and backup.</td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td>7. Design the interface between the user and the system application so that it is easy to use and self-documenting.</td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td>8. Consider the impact of the solution’s need for infrastructure performance, being sensitive to the number of computing assets, bandwidth intensity and time sensitivity of the information.</td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td>9. Proactively evaluate for design weaknesses (e.g., inconsistencies, lack of clarity, potential flaws) throughout the life cycle. Identify improvements when required.</td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td>10. Provide an ability to audit transactions and identify root causes of processing errors.</td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td><strong>Related Guidance (Standards, Frameworks, Compliance Requirements)</strong></td>
<td><strong>Detailed Reference</strong></td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td>ISF, The Standard of Good Practice for Information Security 2016</td>
<td>SD2.2 System Design</td>
</tr>
</tbody>
</table>

&lt;page_number&gt;170&lt;/page_number&gt;

## Page 171

CHAPTER 4
COBIT GOVERNANCE AND MANAGEMENT OBJECTIVES—DETAILED GUIDANCE

A. Component: Process (cont.)

<table>
<thead>
<tr>
<th>Management Practice</th>
<th>Example Metrics</th>
</tr>
</thead>
<tbody>
<tr>
<td><strong>BAI03.03 Develop solution components.</strong><br>Develop solution components progressively in a separate environment, in accordance with detailed designs following standards and requirements for development and documentation, quality assurance (QA), and approval. Ensure that all control requirements in the business processes, supporting I&T applications and infrastructure services, services and technology products, and partner/vendor services are addressed.</td>
<td>a. Number of solution exceptions to design noted during stage reviews<br>b. Number of detailed designs for business processes, supporting services, applications and infrastructure, and information repositories</td>
</tr>
</tbody>
</table>

<table>
<thead>
<tr>
<th>Activities</th>
<th>Capability Level</th>
</tr>
</thead>
<tbody>
<tr>
<td>1. Within a separate environment, develop the proposed detailed design for business processes, supporting services, applications, infrastructure and information repositories.</td>
<td>2</td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td>2. When third-party providers are involved with the solution development, ensure that maintenance, support, development standards and licensing are addressed and adhered to in contractual obligations.</td>
<td></td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td>3. Track change requests and design, performance and quality reviews. Ensure active participation of all impacted stakeholders.</td>
<td></td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td>4. Document all solution components according to defined standards. Maintain version control over all developed components and associated documentation.</td>
<td></td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td>5. Assess the impact of solution customization and configuration on the performance and efficiency of acquired solutions and on interoperability with existing applications, operating systems and other infrastructure. Adapt business processes as required to leverage the application capability.</td>
<td>3</td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td>6. Ensure that responsibilities for using high-security or restricted-access infrastructure components are clearly defined and understood by those who develop and integrate infrastructure components. Their use should be monitored and evaluated.</td>
<td></td>
</tr>
</tbody>
</table>

<table>
<thead>
<tr>
<th>Related Guidance (Standards, Frameworks, Compliance Requirements)</th>
<th>Detailed Reference</th>
</tr>
</thead>
<tbody>
<tr>
<td>ISF, The Standard of Good Practice for Information Security 2016</td>
<td>SD1.2 System Development Environments</td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td>ISO/IEC 27002:2013/Cor.2:2015(E)</td>
<td>14.2 Security in development and support processes</td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td>ITIL V3, 2011</td>
<td>Service Strategy, 5.5 IT service strategy and application development</td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td>National Institute of Standards and Technology Special Publication 800-53, Revision 5 (Draft), August 2017</td>
<td>3.18 System and services acquisition (SA-3)</td>
</tr>
</tbody>
</table>

<table>
<thead>
<tr>
<th>Management Practice</th>
<th>Example Metrics</th>
</tr>
</thead>
<tbody>
<tr>
<td><strong>BAI03.04 Procure solution components.</strong><br>Procure solution components, based on the acquisition plan, in accordance with requirements and detailed designs, architecture principles and standards, and the enterprise’s overall procurement and contract procedures, QA requirements, and approval standards. Ensure that all legal and contractual requirements are identified and addressed by the vendor.</td>
<td>a. Percent of suppliers certified<br>b. Percent of suppliers engaged in collaborative design</td>
</tr>
</tbody>
</table>

<table>
<thead>
<tr>
<th>Activities</th>
<th>Capability Level</th>
</tr>
</thead>
<tbody>
<tr>
<td>1. Create and maintain a plan for the acquisition of solution components. Consider future flexibility for capacity additions, transition costs, risk and upgrades over the lifetime of the project.</td>
<td>3</td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td>2. Review and approve all acquisition plans. Consider risk, costs, benefits and technical conformance with enterprise architecture standards.</td>
<td></td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td>3. Assess and document the degree to which acquired solutions require adaptation of business process to leverage the benefits of the acquired solution.</td>
<td></td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td>4. Follow required approvals at key decision points during the procurement processes.</td>
<td></td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td>5. Record receipt of all infrastructure and software acquisitions in an asset inventory.</td>
<td></td>
</tr>
</tbody>
</table>

<table>
<thead>
<tr>
<th>Related Guidance (Standards, Frameworks, Compliance Requirements)</th>
<th>Detailed Reference</th>
</tr>
</thead>
<tbody>
<tr>
<td>ISF, The Standard of Good Practice for Information Security 2016</td>
<td>SD2.3 Software Acquisition</td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td>National Institute of Standards and Technology Framework for Improving Critical Infrastructure Cybersecurity v1.1, April 2018</td>
<td>3.4 Buying Decisions</td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td>National Institute of Standards and Technology Special Publication 800-53, Revision 5 (Draft), August 2017</td>
<td>3.18 System and services acquisition (SA-4)</td>
</tr>

## Page 172

# COBIT® 2019 FRAMEWORK: GOVERNANCE AND MANAGEMENT OBJECTIVES

<table>
  <thead>
    <tr>
      <th colspan="3">A. Component: Process (cont.)</th>
    </tr>
    <tr>
      <th>Management Practice</th>
      <th>Example Metrics</th>
    </tr>
  </thead>
  <tbody>
    <tr>
      <td>
        **BAI03.05 Build solutions.**<br>
        Install and configure solutions and integrate with business process activities. During configuration and integration of hardware and infrastructure software, implement control, security, privacy and auditability measures to protect resources and ensure availability and data integrity. Update the product or services catalogue to reflect the new solutions.
      </td>
      <td>
        a. Gap between estimated and final development effort<br>
        b. Number of software problems reported<br>
        c. Number of review errors
      </td>
    </tr>
    <tr>
      <td colspan="2">
        <strong>Activities</strong>
      </td>
      <td rowspan="8" style="vertical-align: top;">
        <table>
          <thead>
            <tr>
              <th>Capability Level</th>
            </tr>
          </thead>
          <tbody>
            <tr>
              <td>2</td>
            </tr>
            <tr>
              <td>3</td>
            </tr>
          </tbody>
        </table>
      </td>
    </tr>
    <tr>
      <td colspan="2">1. Integrate and configure business and IT solution components and information repositories in line with detailed specifications and quality requirements. Consider the role of users, business stakeholders and the process owner in the configuration of business processes.</td>
    </tr>
    <tr>
      <td colspan="2">2. Complete and update business process and operational manuals, where necessary, to account for any customization or special conditions unique to the implementation.</td>
    </tr>
    <tr>
      <td colspan="2">3. Consider all relevant information control requirements in solution component integration and configuration. Include implementation of business controls, where appropriate, into automated application controls such that processing is accurate, complete, timely, authorized and auditable.</td>
    </tr>
    <tr>
      <td colspan="2">4. Implement audit trails during configuration and integration of hardware and infrastructural software to protect resources and ensure availability and integrity.</td>
    </tr>
    <tr>
      <td colspan="2">5. Consider when the effect of cumulative customizations and configurations (including minor changes that were not subjected to formal design specifications) requires a high-level reassessment of the solution and associated functionality.</td>
    </tr>
    <tr>
      <td colspan="2">6. Configure acquired application software to meet business processing requirements.</td>
    </tr>
    <tr>
      <td colspan="2">7. Define product and service catalogues for relevant internal and external target groups, based on business requirements.</td>
    </tr>
    <tr>
      <td colspan="2">8. Ensure the interoperability of solution components with supporting tests, preferably automated.</td>
    </tr>
    <tr>
      <td colspan="2">
        <strong>Related Guidance (Standards, Frameworks, Compliance Requirements)</strong>
      </td>
      <td>
        <strong>Detailed Reference</strong>
      </td>
    </tr>
    <tr>
      <td>HITRUST CSF version 9, September 2017</td>
      <td>10.05 Security in Development & Support Processes</td>
    </tr>
    <tr>
      <td>ISF, The Standard of Good Practice for Information Security 2016</td>
      <td>SD2.4 System Build</td>
    </tr>
    <tr>
      <td colspan="3">
        <strong>A. Component: Process (cont.)</strong>
      </td>
    </tr>
    <tr>
      <td>
        **BAI03.06 Perform quality assurance (QA).**<br>
        Develop, resource and execute a QA plan aligned with the QMS to obtain the quality specified in the requirements definition and in the enterprise's quality policies and procedures.
      </td>
      <td>
        a. Number of reworked solution designs due to misalignment with requirements<br>
        b. Number and robustness of documented monitor activities performed
      </td>
    </tr>
    <tr>
      <td colspan="2">
        <strong>Activities</strong>
      </td>
      <td rowspan="4" style="vertical-align: top;">
        <table>
          <thead>
            <tr>
              <th>Capability Level</th>
            </tr>
          </thead>
          <tbody>
            <tr>
              <td>3</td>
            </tr>
            <tr>
              <td>4</td>
            </tr>
          </tbody>
        </table>
      </td>
    </tr>
    <tr>
      <td colspan="2">1. Define a QA plan and practices include, for example, specification of quality criteria, validation and verification processes, definition of how quality will be reviewed, necessary qualifications of quality reviewers, and roles and responsibilities for the achievement of quality.</td>
    </tr>
    <tr>
      <td colspan="2">2. Frequently monitor the solution quality based on project requirements, enterprise policies, adherence to development methodologies, quality management procedures and acceptance criteria.</td>
    </tr>
    <tr>
      <td colspan="2">3. Employ, as appropriate, code inspection, test-driven development practices, automated testing, continuous integration, walk-throughs and testing of applications. Report on outcomes of the monitoring process and testing to the application software development team and IT management.</td>
    </tr>
    <tr>
      <td colspan="2">4. Monitor all quality exceptions and address all corrective actions. Maintain a record of all reviews, results, exceptions and corrections. Repeat quality reviews, where appropriate, based on the amount of rework and corrective action.</td>
    </tr>
    <tr>
      <td colspan="2">
        <strong>Related Guidance (Standards, Frameworks, Compliance Requirements)</strong>
      </td>
      <td>
        <strong>Detailed Reference</strong>
      </td>
    </tr>
    <tr>
      <td>ISF, The Standard of Good Practice for Information Security 2016</td>
      <td>SD1.3 Quality Assurance</td>
    </tr>
  </tbody>
</table>

&lt;page_number&gt;172&lt;/page_number&gt;

---


## Page 173

CHAPTER 4
COBIT GOVERNANCE AND MANAGEMENT OBJECTIVES—DETAILED GUIDANCE

A. Component: Process (cont.)

<table>
<thead>
<tr>
<th>Management Practice</th>
<th>Example Metrics</th>
</tr>
</thead>
<tbody>
<tr>
<td><strong>BAI03.07 Prepare for solution testing.</strong><br>Establish a test plan and required environments to test the individual and integrated solution components. Include the business processes and supporting services, applications and infrastructure.</td>
<td>a. Number of business users involved in creating a test plan<br>b. Number and robustness of use cases created for testing</td>
</tr>
</tbody>
</table>

<table>
<thead>
<tr>
<th>Activities</th>
<th>Capability Level</th>
</tr>
</thead>
<tbody>
<tr>
<td>1. Create an integrated test plan and practices commensurate with the enterprise environment and strategic technology plans. Ensure that the integrated test plan and practices will enable the creation of suitable testing and simulation environments to help verify that the solution will operate successfully in the live environment and deliver the intended results and that controls are adequate.</td>
<td>2</td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td>2. Create a test environment that supports the full scope of the solution. Ensure that the test environment reflects, as closely as possible, real-world conditions, including the business processes and procedures, range of users, transaction types, and deployment conditions.</td>
<td></td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td>3. Create test procedures that align with the plan and practices and allow evaluation of the operation of the solution in real-world conditions. Ensure that the test procedures evaluate the adequacy of the controls, based on enterprisewide standards that define roles, responsibilities and testing criteria, and are approved by project stakeholders and the sponsor/business process owner.</td>
<td>3</td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td>4. Document and save the test procedures, cases, controls and parameters for future testing of the application.</td>
<td></td>
</tr>
</tbody>
</table>

<table>
<thead>
<tr>
<th>Related Guidance (Standards, Frameworks, Compliance Requirements)</th>
<th>Detailed Reference</th>
</tr>
</thead>
<tbody>
<tr>
<td>CMMI Cybermaturity Platform, 2018</td>
<td>AD.DE Safeguard Development Environment</td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td>National Institute of Standards and Technology Special Publication 800-53, Revision 5 (Draft), August 2017</td>
<td>3.10 Maintenance (MA-2, MA-3)</td>
</tr>
</tbody>
</table>

<table>
<thead>
<tr>
<th>Management Practice</th>
<th>Example Metrics</th>
</tr>
</thead>
<tbody>
<tr>
<td><strong>BAI03.08 Execute solution testing.</strong><br>During development, execute testing continually (including control testing), in accordance with the defined test plan and development practices in the appropriate environment. Engage business process owners and end users in the test team. Identify, log and prioritize errors and issues identified during testing.</td>
<td>a. Number of errors found during testing<br>b. Time and effort to complete tests</td>
</tr>
</tbody>
</table>

<table>
<thead>
<tr>
<th>Activities</th>
<th>Capability Level</th>
</tr>
</thead>
<tbody>
<tr>
<td>1. Undertake testing of solutions and their components in accordance with the testing plan. Include testers independent from the solution team, with representative business process owners and end users. Ensure that testing is conducted only within the development and test environments.</td>
<td>2</td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td>2. Use clearly defined test instructions, as defined in the test plan. Consider the appropriate balance between automated scripted tests and interactive user testing.</td>
<td></td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td>3. Undertake all tests in accordance with the test plan and practices. Include the integration of business processes and IT solution components and of nonfunctional requirements (e.g., security, privacy, interoperability, usability).</td>
<td></td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td>4. Identify, log and classify (e.g., minor, significant and mission-critical) errors during testing. Repeat tests until all significant errors have been resolved. Ensure that an audit trail of test results is maintained.</td>
<td></td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td>5. Record testing outcomes and communicate results of testing to stakeholders in accordance with the test plan.</td>
<td></td>
</tr>
</tbody>
</table>

<table>
<thead>
<tr>
<th>Related Guidance (Standards, Frameworks, Compliance Requirements)</th>
<th>Detailed Reference</th>
</tr>
</thead>
<tbody>
<tr>
<td>CMMI Cybermaturity Platform, 2018</td>
<td>AD.ST Secure Development Testing</td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td>ISF, The Standard of Good Practice for Information Security 2016</td>
<td>SD2.5 System Testing; SD2.6 Security Testing</td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td>National Institute of Standards and Technology Special Publication 800-53, Revision 5 (Draft), August 2017</td>
<td>3.18 System and services acquisition (SA-11)</td>
</tr>
</tbody>
</table>

## Page 174

# COBIT® 2019 FRAMEWORK: GOVERNANCE AND MANAGEMENT OBJECTIVES

**A. Component: Process (cont.)**

<table>
  <thead>
    <tr>
      <th>Management Practice</th>
      <th>Example Metrics</th>
    </tr>
  </thead>
  <tbody>
    <tr>
      <td><strong>BAI03.09 Manage changes to requirements.</strong><br>Track the status of individual requirements (including all rejected requirements) throughout the project life cycle. Manage the approval of changes to requirements.</td>
      <td>a. Number of tracked, approved changes that generate new errors<br>b. Percent of stakeholders satisfied with change management processes</td>
    </tr>
    <tr>
      <td colspan="2"><strong>Activities</strong></td>
    </tr>
    <tr>
      <td colspan="2">1. Assess the impact of all solution change requests on the solution development, the original business case and the budget. Categorize and prioritize them accordingly.</td>
      <td rowspan="3">Capability Level<br>3</td>
    </tr>
    <tr>
      <td colspan="2">2. Track changes to requirements, enabling all stakeholders to monitor, review and approve the changes. Ensure that the outcomes of the change process are fully understood and agreed on by all the stakeholders and the sponsor/business process owner.</td>
    </tr>
    <tr>
      <td colspan="2">3. Apply change requests, maintaining the integrity of integration and configuration of solution components. Assess the impact of any major solution upgrade and classify it according to agreed objective criteria (such as enterprise requirements), based on the outcome of analysis of the risk involved (such as impact on existing systems and processes or security/privacy), cost-benefit justification and other requirements.</td>
    </tr>
    <tr>
      <td colspan="2"><strong>Related Guidance (Standards, Frameworks, Compliance Requirements)</strong></td>
      <td><strong>Detailed Reference</strong></td>
    </tr>
    <tr>
      <td colspan="2">ISF, The Standard of Good Practice for Information Security 2016</td>
      <td>SD2.9 Post-implementation Review</td>
    </tr>
    <tr>
      <td colspan="2"><strong>Management Practice</strong></td>
      <td><strong>Example Metrics</strong></td>
    </tr>
    <tr>
      <td><strong>BAI03.10 Maintain solutions.</strong><br>Develop and execute a plan for the maintenance of solution and infrastructure components. Include periodic reviews against business needs and operational requirements.</td>
      <td>a. Number of demands for maintenance that are not satisfied<br>b. Duration of demands for maintenance that are satisfied and that go unsatisfied</td>
    </tr>
    <tr>
      <td colspan="2"><strong>Activities</strong></td>
    </tr>
    <tr>
      <td colspan="2">1. Develop and execute a plan for the maintenance of solution components. Include periodic reviews against business needs and operational requirements such as patch management, upgrade strategies, risk, privacy, vulnerabilities assessment and security requirements.</td>
      <td rowspan="4">Capability Level<br>2<br>3<br>4</td>
    </tr>
    <tr>
      <td colspan="2">2. Assess the significance of a proposed maintenance activity on current solution design, functionality and/or business processes. Consider risk, user impact and resource availability. Ensure that business process owners understand the effect of designating changes as maintenance.</td>
    </tr>
    <tr>
      <td colspan="2">3. In the event of major changes to existing solutions that result in significant change in current designs and/or functionality and/or business processes, follow the development process used for new systems. For maintenance updates, use the change management process.</td>
    </tr>
    <tr>
      <td colspan="2">4. Ensure that the pattern and volume of maintenance activities are analyzed periodically for abnormal trends that indicate underlying quality or performance problems, cost/benefit of major upgrade, or replacement in lieu of maintenance.</td>
    </tr>
    <tr>
      <td colspan="2"><strong>Related Guidance (Standards, Frameworks, Compliance Requirements)</strong></td>
      <td><strong>Detailed Reference</strong></td>
    </tr>
    <tr>
      <td colspan="2">ISO/IEC 27002:2013/Cor.2:2015(E)</td>
      <td>14.3 Test data</td>
    </tr>
    <tr>
      <td colspan="2"><strong>Management Practice</strong></td>
      <td><strong>Example Metrics</strong></td>
    </tr>
    <tr>
      <td><strong>BAI03.11 Define IT products and services and maintain the service portfolio.</strong><br>Define and agree on new or changed IT products or services and service level options. Document new or changed product and service definitions and service level options to be updated in the products and services portfolio.</td>
      <td>a. Percent of stakeholders signing off on new I&T services<br>b. Percent of new or changed service definitions and service level options documented in the services portfolio.<br>c. Percent of new or changed service definitions and service level options updated in the services portfolio</td>
    </tr>
  </tbody>
</table>

&lt;page_number&gt;174&lt;/page_number&gt;

## Page 175

CHAPTER 4
COBIT GOVERNANCE AND MANAGEMENT OBJECTIVES—DETAILED GUIDANCE

A. Component: Process (cont.)

<table>
<thead>
<tr>
<th>Activities</th>
<th>Capability Level</th>
</tr>
</thead>
<tbody>
<tr>
<td>1. Propose definitions of the new or changed IT products and services to ensure that they are fit for purpose. Document the proposed definitions in the portfolio list of products and services to be developed.</td>
<td rowspan="4">3</td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td>2. Propose new or changed service level options (service times, user satisfaction, availability, performance, capacity, security, privacy, continuity, compliance and usability) to ensure that the IT products and services are fit for use. Document the proposed service options in the portfolio.</td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td>3. Interface with business relationship management and portfolio management to agree on the proposed product and service definitions and service level options.</td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td>4. If product or service change falls within agreed approval authority, build the new or changed IT products and services or service level options. Otherwise, pass the change to portfolio management for investment review.</td>
</tr>
</tbody>
</table>

<table>
<thead>
<tr>
<th>Related Guidance (Standards, Frameworks, Compliance Requirements)</th>
<th>Detailed Reference</th>
</tr>
</thead>
<tbody>
<tr>
<td>No related guidance for this management practice</td>
<td></td>
</tr>
</tbody>
</table>

<table>
<thead>
<tr>
<th>Management Practice</th>
<th>Example Metrics</th>
</tr>
</thead>
<tbody>
<tr>
<td>BAI03.12 Design solutions based on the defined development methodology.<br>Design, develop and implement solutions with the appropriate development methodology (i.e., waterfall, Agile or bimodal I&T), in accordance with the overall strategy and requirements.</td>
<td>a. Percent of solution development projects that apply selected development methodologies<br>b. Percent of processes adapted to the chosen strategy</td>
</tr>
</tbody>
</table>

<table>
<thead>
<tr>
<th>Activities</th>
<th>Capability Level</th>
</tr>
</thead>
<tbody>
<tr>
<td>1. Analyze and assess the impact of choosing a development methodology (i.e., waterfall, Agile, bimodal) on the available resources, architecture requirements, configuration settings and system rigidity.</td>
<td rowspan="4">3</td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td>2. Establish the appropriate development methodology and organizational approach that delivers the proposed solution efficiently and effectively and that is capable of meeting business, architecture and system requirements. Adapt processes as required to the chosen strategy.</td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td>3. Establish the needed project teams as defined by the chosen development methodology. Provide sufficient training.</td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td>4. Consider applying a dual system, if required, in which cross-functional groups (digital factories) focus on developing one product or process using a different technology, operational, or managerial methodology from the rest of the company. Embedding these groups in business units has the advantage of spreading the new culture of agile development and making this digital factory approach the norm.</td>
</tr>
</tbody>
</table>

<table>
<thead>
<tr>
<th>Related Guidance (Standards, Frameworks, Compliance Requirements)</th>
<th>Detailed Reference</th>
</tr>
</thead>
<tbody>
<tr>
<td>ISF, The Standard of Good Practice for Information Security 2016</td>
<td>SD1.1 System Development Methodology</td>
</tr>
</tbody>
</table>

<td>BAI02.04</td>
<td>Approvals of requirements and proposed solutions by sponsor</td>
</tr>
</tbody>
