## Page 181

# CHAPTER 4
## COBIT GOVERNANCE AND MANAGEMENT OBJECTIVES—DETAILED GUIDANCE

<table>
  <tr>
    <td><strong>Domain:</strong> Build, Acquire and Implement<br><strong>Management Objective:</strong> BAI04 – Managed Availability and Capacity</td>
    <td><strong>Focus Area:</strong> COBIT Core Model</td>
  </tr>
</table>

### Description
Balance current and future needs for availability, performance and capacity with cost-effective service provision. Include assessment of current capabilities, forecasting of future needs based on business requirements, analysis of business impacts, and assessment of risk to plan and implement actions to meet the identified requirements.

### Purpose
Maintain service availability, efficient management of resources and optimization of system performance through prediction of future performance and capacity requirements.

The management objective supports the achievement of a set of primary enterprise and alignment goals:

<table>
  <tr>
    <td><strong>Enterprise Goals</strong></td>
    <td><strong>Alignment Goals</strong></td>
  </tr>
  <tr>
    <td>
      • EG01 Portfolio of competitive products and services<br>
      • EG08 Optimization of internal business process functionality
    </td>
    <td>
      AG05 Delivery of I&T services in line with business requirements
    </td>
  </tr>
  <tr>
    <td><strong>Example Metrics for Enterprise Goals</strong></td>
    <td><strong>Example Metrics for Alignment Goals</strong></td>
  </tr>
  <tr>
    <td>
      EG01
      <ul>
        <li>a. Percent of products and services that meet or exceed targets in revenues and/or market share</li>
        <li>b. Percent of products and services that meet or exceed customer satisfaction targets</li>
        <li>c. Percent of products and services that provide competitive advantage</li>
        <li>d. Time to market for new products and services</li>
      </ul>
    </td>
    <td>
      AG05
      <ul>
        <li>a. Percent of business stakeholders satisfied that I&T service delivery meets agreed service levels</li>
        <li>b. Number of business disruptions due to I&T service incidents</li>
        <li>c. Percent of users satisfied with the quality of I&T service delivery</li>
      </ul>
    </td>
  </tr>
  <tr>
    <td>
      EG08
      <ul>
        <li>a. Satisfaction levels of board and executive management with business process capabilities</li>
        <li>b. Satisfaction levels of customers with service delivery capabilities</li>
        <li>c. Satisfaction levels of suppliers with supply chain capabilities</li>
      </ul>
    </td>
    <td></td>
  </tr>
</table>

### A. Component: Process

<table>
  <tr>
    <td><strong>Management Practice</strong></td>
    <td><strong>Example Metrics</strong></td>
  </tr>
  <tr>
    <td>
      **BAI04.01 Assess current availability, performance and capacity and create a baseline.**<br>
      Assess availability, performance and capacity of services and resources to ensure that cost-justifiable capacity and performance are available to support business needs and deliver against service level agreements (SLAs). Create availability, performance and capacity baselines for future comparison.
    </td>
    <td>
      <ul>
        <li>a. Percent of actual capacity usage</li>
        <li>b. Percent of actual availability</li>
        <li>c. Percent of actual performance</li>
      </ul>
    </td>
  </tr>
  <tr>
    <td><strong>Activities</strong></td>
    <td><strong>Capability Level</strong></td>
  </tr>
  <tr>
    <td>
      1. Consider the following (current and forecasted) in the assessment of availability, performance and capacity of services and resources: customer requirements, business priorities, business objectives, budget impact, resource utilization, IT capabilities and industry trends.<br><br>
      2. Identify and follow up on all incidents caused by inadequate performance or capacity.<br><br>
      3. Monitor actual performance and capacity usage against defined thresholds, supported, where necessary, with automated software.<br><br>
      4. Regularly evaluate the current levels of performance for all processing levels (business demand, service capacity and resource capacity) by comparing them against trends and SLAs. Take into account changes in the environment.
    </td>
    <td>
      2<br><br>
      3<br><br>
      4
    </td>
  </tr>
  <tr>
    <td><strong>Related Guidance (Standards, Frameworks, Compliance Requirements)</strong></td>
    <td><strong>Detailed Reference</strong></td>
  </tr>
  <tr>
    <td>CMMI Cybermaturity Platform, 2018</td>
    <td>DP.CP Capacity Planning</td>
  </tr>
  <tr>
    <td>ISF, The Standard of Good Practice for Information Security 2016</td>
    <td>SY2.2 Performance and Capacity Management</td>
  </tr>
  <tr>
    <td>ISO/IEC 20000-1:2011(E)</td>
    <td>6.5 Capacity management</td>
  </tr>
  <tr>
    <td>ITIL V3, 2011</td>
    <td>Service Design, 4.4 Availability Management; 4.5 Capacity Management</td>
  </tr>
  <tr>
    <td>National Institute of Standards and Technology Special Publication 800-53, Revision 5 (Draft), August 2017</td>
    <td>3.14 Planning (PL-10, PL-11)</td>
  </tr>
</table>

&lt;page_number&gt;181&lt;/page_number&gt;

## Page 182

# COBIT® 2019 FRAMEWORK: GOVERNANCE AND MANAGEMENT OBJECTIVES

## A. Component: Process (cont.)

<table>
  <thead>
    <tr>
      <th>Management Practice</th>
      <th>Example Metrics</th>
    </tr>
  </thead>
  <tbody>
    <tr>
      <td><strong>BAI04.02 Assess business impact.</strong><br>Identify important services to the enterprise. Map services and resources to business processes and identify business dependencies. Ensure that the impact of unavailable resources is fully agreed on and accepted by the customer. For vital business functions, ensure that availability requirements can be satisfied per service level agreement (SLA).</td>
      <td>a. Number of scenarios created to assess future availability situations<br>b. Percent of business process owners signing off on analysis results</td>
    </tr>
  </tbody>
</table>

<table>
  <thead>
    <tr>
      <th>Activities</th>
      <th>Capability Level</th>
    </tr>
  </thead>
  <tbody>
    <tr>
      <td>1. Identify only those solutions or services that are critical in the availability and capacity management process.</td>
      <td>2</td>
    </tr>
    <tr>
      <td>2. Map the selected solutions or services to the application(s) and infrastructure (IT and facility) on which they depend to enable a focus on critical resources for availability planning.</td>
      <td>3</td>
    </tr>
    <tr>
      <td>3. Collect data on availability patterns from logs of past failures and performance monitoring. Use modeling tools that help predict failures based on past usage trends and management expectations of new environment or user conditions.</td>
      <td>4</td>
    </tr>
    <tr>
      <td>4. Based on the collected data, create scenarios that describe future availability situations to illustrate a variety of potential capacity levels needed to achieve the availability performance objective.</td>
      <td></td>
    </tr>
    <tr>
      <td>5. Based on the scenarios, determine the likelihood that the availability performance objective will not be achieved.</td>
      <td></td>
    </tr>
    <tr>
      <td>6. Determine the impact of the scenarios on the business performance measures (e.g., revenue, profit, customer services). Engage the business-line, functional (especially finance) and regional leaders to understand their evaluation of impact.</td>
      <td></td>
    </tr>
    <tr>
      <td>7. Ensure that business process owners fully understand and agree to the results of this analysis. From the business owners, obtain a list of unacceptable risk scenarios that require a response to reduce risk to acceptable levels.</td>
      <td></td>
    </tr>
  </tbody>
</table>

<table>
  <thead>
    <tr>
      <th>Related Guidance (Standards, Frameworks, Compliance Requirements)</th>
      <th>Detailed Reference</th>
    </tr>
  </thead>
  <tbody>
    <tr>
      <td>ISO/IEC 20000-1:2011(E)</td>
      <td>6.3 Service continuity and availability management</td>
    </tr>
  </tbody>
</table>

## Management Practice

**BAI04.03 Plan for new or changed service requirements.**
Plan and prioritize availability, performance and capacity implications of changing business needs and service requirements.

<table>
  <thead>
    <tr>
      <th>Example Metrics</th>
    </tr>
  </thead>
  <tbody>
    <tr>
      <td>a. Number of unplanned capacity, performance or availability upgrades<br>b. Percent that management performs comparisons of actual demand on resources against forecasted supply and demand</td>
    </tr>
  </tbody>
</table>

<table>
  <thead>
    <tr>
      <th>Activities</th>
      <th>Capability Level</th>
    </tr>
  </thead>
  <tbody>
    <tr>
      <td>1. Identify availability and capacity implications of changing business needs and improvement opportunities. Use modeling techniques to validate availability, performance and capacity plans.</td>
      <td>3</td>
    </tr>
    <tr>
      <td>2. Review availability and capacity implications of service trend analysis.</td>
      <td>4</td>
    </tr>
    <tr>
      <td>3. Ensure that management performs comparisons of actual demand on resources against forecasted supply and demand to evaluate current forecasting techniques and make improvements where possible.</td>
      <td></td>
    </tr>
    <tr>
      <td>4. Prioritize needed improvements and create cost-justifiable availability and capacity plans.</td>
      <td>5</td>
    </tr>
    <tr>
      <td>5. Adjust the performance and capacity plans and SLAs based on realistic, new, proposed and/or projected business processes and supporting services, applications and infrastructure changes. Also include reviews of actual performance and capacity usage, including workload levels.</td>
      <td></td>
    </tr>
  </tbody>
</table>

<table>
  <thead>
    <tr>
      <th>Related Guidance (Standards, Frameworks, Compliance Requirements)</th>
      <th>Detailed Reference</th>
    </tr>
  </thead>
  <tbody>
    <tr>
      <td>ISO/IEC 20000-1:2011(E)</td>
      <td>5. Design and transition of new changed services</td>
    </tr>
  </tbody>
</table>

## Management Practice

**BAI04.04 Monitor and review availability and capacity.**
Monitor, measure, analyze, report and review availability, performance and capacity. Identify deviations from established baselines. Review trend analysis reports identifying any significant issues and variances. Initiate actions where necessary and ensure that all outstanding issues are addressed.

<table>
  <thead>
    <tr>
      <th>Example Metrics</th>
    </tr>
  </thead>
  <tbody>
    <tr>
      <td>a. Number of events exceeding planned limits for capacity<br>b. Number of transaction peaks exceeding target performance</td>
    </tr>
  </tbody>
</table>

&lt;page_number&gt;182&lt;/page_number&gt;

COBIT GOVERNANCE AND MANAGEMENT OBJECTIVES—DETAILED GUIDANCE

A. Component: Process (cont.)

<table>
<thead>
<tr>
<th>Activities</th>
<th>Capability Level</th>
</tr>
</thead>
<tbody>
<tr>
<td>1. Provide capacity reports to the budgeting processes.</td>
<td>2</td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td>2. Establish a process for gathering data to provide management with monitoring and reporting information for availability, performance and capacity workload of all I&T-related resources.</td>
<td>3</td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td>3. Provide regular reporting of the results in an appropriate form for review by IT and business management and communication to enterprise management.</td>
<td>4</td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td>4. Integrate monitoring and reporting activities in the iterative capacity management activities (monitoring, analysis, tuning and implementations).</td>
<td></td>
</tr>
</tbody>
</table>

<table>
<thead>
<tr>
<th>Related Guidance (Standards, Frameworks, Compliance Requirements)</th>
<th>Detailed Reference</th>
</tr>
</thead>
<tbody>
<tr>
<td>No related guidance for this management practice</td>
<td></td>
</tr>
</tbody>
</table>

<table>
<thead>
<tr>
<th>Management Practice</th>
<th>Example Metrics</th>
</tr>
</thead>
<tbody>
<tr>
<td>BAI04.05 Investigate and address availability, performance and capacity issues.<br/>Address deviations by investigating and resolving identified availability, performance and capacity issues.</td>
<td>a. Number and percentage of unresolved availability, performance and capacity issues<br/>b. Number of availability incidents</td>
</tr>
</tbody>
</table>

<table>
<thead>
<tr>
<th>Activities</th>
<th>Capability Level</th>
</tr>
</thead>
<tbody>
<tr>
<td>1. Obtain guidance from vendor product manuals to ensure an appropriate level of performance availability for peak processing and workloads.</td>
<td>3</td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td>2. Define an escalation procedure for swift resolution in case of emergency capacity and performance problems.</td>
<td></td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td>3. Identify performance and capacity gaps based on monitoring current and forecasted performance. Use the known availability, continuity and recovery specifications to classify resources and allow prioritization.</td>
<td>4</td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td>4. Define corrective actions (e.g., shifting workload, prioritizing tasks or adding resources when performance and capacity issues are identified).</td>
<td>5</td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td>5. Integrate required corrective actions into the appropriate planning and change management processes.</td>
<td></td>
</tr>
</tbody>
</table>

<table>
<thead>
<tr>

<th>Detailed Reference</th>
</tr>
</thead>
<tbody>
<tr>
<td>No related guidance for this management practice</td>
<td></td>
</tr>
</tbody>
</table>

</tr>
</thead>
<tbody>
<tr>
<td>Availability management policy</td>
<td>Informs infrastructure planning in terms of availability, scalability, reliability and potentially resilience. Includes guidelines to identify bandwidth, capacity and availability of services (prior to design and provisioning), establish service level agreements (SLAs), and implement continuous monitoring of circuits, traffic and response times.</td>
<td></td>
<td></td>
</tr>
</tbody>
</table>

## Page 185

CHAPTER 4
COBIT GOVERNANCE AND MANAGEMENT OBJECTIVES—DETAILED GUIDANCE

F. Component: Culture, Ethics and Behavior

<table>
<thead>
<tr>
<th>Key Culture Elements</th>
<th>Related Guidance</th>
<th>Detailed Reference</th>
</tr>
</thead>
<tbody>
<tr>
<td>For enterprises that depend on information, availability and capacity management are critical to successful operations. Establish a culture in which product and service availability and capacity are prioritized (in line with business requirements) and supported by processes and behaviors that not only identify required availability and capacity before design, but also consider them in provisioning. Consistently define smart SLAs; continuously monitor circuits, traffic and response times; perform regular testing for business continuity and disaster recovery of infrastructure.</td>
<td></td>
<td></td>
</tr>
</tbody>
</table>

* Capacity planning tools
* Provisioning services and tools
* Service level monitoring tools

## Page 187

# CHAPTER 4
## COBIT GOVERNANCE AND MANAGEMENT OBJECTIVES—DETAILED GUIDANCE

<table>
  <tr>
    <td style="vertical-align: top;">
      **Domain:** Build, Acquire and Implement<br>
      **Management Objective:** BAI05 – Managed Organizational Change
    </td>
    <td style="vertical-align: top;">
      **Focus Area:** COBIT Core Model
    </td>
  </tr>
</table>

### Description
Maximize the likelihood of successfully implementing sustainable enterprisewide organizational change quickly and with reduced risk. Cover the complete life cycle of the change and all affected stakeholders in the business and IT.

### Purpose
Prepare and commit stakeholders for business change and reduce the risk of failure.

The management objective supports the achievement of a set of primary enterprise and alignment goals:

<table>
  <tr>
    <td style="vertical-align: top;">
      **Enterprise Goals**
      <ul>
        <li>EG01 Portfolio of competitive products and services</li>
        <li>EG05 Customer-oriented service culture</li>
        <li>EG08 Optimization of internal business process functionality</li>
        <li>EG12 Managed digital transformation programs</li>
      </ul>
    </td>
    <td style="vertical-align: top;">
      &lt;img&gt;Right arrow icon&lt;/img&gt;
    </td>
    <td style="vertical-align: top;">
      **Alignment Goals**
      <ul>
        <li>AG03 Realized benefits from I&T-enabled investments and services portfolio</li>
        <li>AG08 Enabling and supporting business processes by integrating applications and technology</li>
        <li>AG09 Delivering programs on time, on budget and meeting requirements and quality standards</li>
      </ul>
    </td>
  </tr>
</table>

<table>
  <tr>
    <td style="vertical-align: top;">
      **Example Metrics for Enterprise Goals**
      <table>
        <tr>
          <td>EG01</td>
          <td>
            a. Percent of products and services that meet or exceed targets in revenues and/or market share<br>
            b. Percent of products and services that meet or exceed customer satisfaction targets<br>
            c. Percent of products and services that provide competitive advantage<br>
            d. Time to market for new products and services
          </td>
        </tr>
        <tr>
          <td>EG05</td>
          <td>
            a. Number of customer service disruptions<br>
            b. Percent of business stakeholders satisfied that customer service delivery meets agreed levels<br>
            c. Number of customer complaints<br>
            d. Trend of customer satisfaction survey results
          </td>
        </tr>
        <tr>
          <td>EG08</td>
          <td>
            a. Satisfaction levels of board and executive management with business process capabilities<br>
            b. Satisfaction levels of customers with service delivery capabilities<br>
            c. Satisfaction levels of suppliers with supply chain capabilities
          </td>
        </tr>
        <tr>
          <td>EG12</td>
          <td>
            a. Number of programs on time and within budget<br>
            b. Percent of stakeholders satisfied with program delivery<br>
            c. Percent of business transformation programs stopped<br>
            d. Percent of business transformation programs with regular reported status updates
          </td>
        </tr>
      </table>
    </td>
    <td style="vertical-align: top;">
      **Example Metrics for Alignment Goals**
      <table>
        <tr>
          <td>AG03</td>
          <td>
            a. Percent of I&T-enabled investments for which claimed benefits in the business case are met or exceeded<br>
            b. Percent of I&T services for which expected benefits (as stated in service level agreements) are realized
          </td>
        </tr>
        <tr>
          <td>AG08</td>
          <td>
            a. Time to execute business services or processes<br>
            b. Number of I&T-enabled business programs delayed or incurring additional cost due to technology-integration issues<br>
            c. Number of business process changes that need to be delayed or reworked because of technology-integration issues<br>
            d. Number of applications or critical infrastructures operating in silos and not integrated
          </td>
        </tr>
        <tr>
          <td>AG09</td>
          <td>
            a. Number of programs/projects on time and within budget<br>
            b. Number of programs needing significant rework due to quality defects<br>
            c. Percent of stakeholders satisfied with program/project quality
          </td>
        </tr>
      </table>
    </td>
  </tr>
</table>

<table>
  <tr>
    <td style="vertical-align: top;">
      **A. Component: Process**
    </td>
    <td style="vertical-align: top;"></td>
  </tr>
  <tr>
    <td style="vertical-align: top;">
      **Management Practice**
    </td>
    <td style="vertical-align: top;">
      **Example Metrics**
    </td>
  </tr>
  <tr>
    <td>
      **BAI05.01 Establish the desire to change.<br>
      Understand the scope and impact of the desired change. Assess stakeholder readiness and willingness to change. Identify actions that will motivate stakeholder acceptance and participation to make the change work successfully.**
    </td>
    <td>
      a. Level of senior management involvement<br>
      b. Level of stakeholder desire for the change
    </td>
  </tr>
</table>

&lt;page_number&gt;187&lt;/page_number&gt;

## Page 188

# COBIT® 2019 FRAMEWORK: GOVERNANCE AND MANAGEMENT OBJECTIVES

## A. Component: Process (cont.)

<table>
<thead>
<tr>
<th>Activities</th>
<th>Capability Level</th>
</tr>
</thead>
<tbody>
<tr>
<td>1. Assess the scope and impact of the envisioned change, the various stakeholders who are affected, the nature of the impact on and involvement required from each stakeholder group, and the current readiness and ability to adopt the change.</td>
<td rowspan="4">2</td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td>2. To establish the desire to change, identify, leverage and communicate current pain points, negative events, risk, customer dissatisfaction and business problems, as well as initial benefits, future opportunities and rewards, and competitive advantages.</td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td>3. Issue key communications from the executive committee or CEO to demonstrate commitment to the change.</td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td>4. Provide visible leadership from senior management to establish direction and to align, motivate and inspire stakeholders to desire the change.</td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td><strong>Related Guidance (Standards, Frameworks, Compliance Requirements)</strong></td>
<td><strong>Detailed Reference</strong></td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td>PROSCI® 3-Phase Change Management Process</td>
<td>Phase 1. Preparing for change—Define your change management strategy</td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td><strong>Management Practice</strong></td>
<td><strong>Example Metrics</strong></td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td>BAI05.02 Form an effective implementation team.<br>Establish an effective implementation team by assembling appropriate members, creating trust, and establishing common goals and effectiveness measures.</td>
<td>a. Number of identified skills or capacity issues in implementation team<br>b. Stakeholder satisfaction ratings of implementation team</td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td><strong>Activities</strong></td>
<td><strong>Capability Level</strong></td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td>1. Identify and assemble an effective core implementation team that includes appropriate members from business and IT with the capacity to spend the required amount of time and contribute knowledge and expertise, experience, credibility, and authority. Consider including external parties such as consultants to provide an independent view or to address skill gaps. Identify potential change agents within different parts of the enterprise with whom the core team can work to support the vision and cascade changes.</td>
<td rowspan="3">3</td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td>2. Create trust within the core implementation team through carefully planned events with effective communication and joint activities.</td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td>3. Develop a common vision and goals that support the enterprise objectives.</td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td><strong>Related Guidance (Standards, Frameworks, Compliance Requirements)</strong></td>
<td><strong>Detailed Reference</strong></td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td>PROSCI® 3-Phase Change Management Process</td>
<td>Phase 1. Preparing for change—Prepare your change management team</td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td><strong>Management Practice</strong></td>
<td><strong>Example Metrics</strong></td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td>BAI05.03 Communicate desired vision.<br>Communicate the desired vision for the change in the language of those affected by it. The communication should be made by senior management and include the rationale for, and benefits of, the change; the impacts of not making the change; and the vision, the road map and the involvement required of the various stakeholders.</td>
<td>a. Number of questions with regards to the change<br>b. Stakeholder feedback on level of understanding of the change</td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td><strong>Activities</strong></td>
<td><strong>Capability Level</strong></td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td>1. Develop a vision communication plan to address the core audience groups, their behavioral profiles and information requirements, communication channels, and principles.</td>
<td rowspan="5">3</td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td>2. Deliver the communication at appropriate levels of the enterprise, in accordance with the plan.</td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td>3. Reinforce the communication through multiple forums and repetition.</td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td>4. Make all levels of leadership accountable for demonstrating the vision.</td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td>5. Check understanding of the desired vision and respond to any issues highlighted by staff.</td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td><strong>Related Guidance (Standards, Frameworks, Compliance Requirements)</strong></td>
<td><strong>Detailed Reference</strong></td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td>No related guidance for this management practice</td>
<td></td>
</tr>
</tbody>
</table>

&lt;page_number&gt;188&lt;/page_number&gt;

## Page 189

CHAPTER 4
COBIT GOVERNANCE AND MANAGEMENT OBJECTIVES—DETAILED GUIDANCE

A. Component: Process (cont.)

<table>
<thead>
<tr>
<th>Management Practice</th>
<th>Example Metrics</th>
</tr>
</thead>
<tbody>
<tr>
<td>BAI05.04 Empower role players and identify short-term wins.<br>Empower those with implementation roles by assigning accountability. Provide training and align organizational structures and HR processes. Identify and communicate short-term wins that are important from a change-enablement perspective.</td>
<td>a. Level of satisfaction of role players operating, using and maintaining the change<br>b. Percent of role players trained<br>c. Percent of role players with appropriate assigned authority<br>d. Role player feedback on level of empowerment<br>e. Role player self-assessment of relevant capabilities</td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td><strong>Activities</strong></td>
<td></td>
<td><strong>Capability Level</strong></td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td>1. Plan the training opportunities staff will need to develop the appropriate skills and attitudes to feel empowered.</td>
<td></td>
<td>2</td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td>2. Identify, prioritize and deliver opportunities for quick wins. These could be related to current known areas of difficulty or external factors that need to be addressed urgently.</td>
<td></td>
<td></td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td>3. Leverage delivered quick wins by communicating the benefits to those impacted to show the vision is on track. Fine-tune the vision, keep leaders on board and build momentum.</td>
<td></td>
<td></td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td>4. Identify organizational structures compatible with the vision; if required, make changes to ensure alignment.</td>
<td></td>
<td>3</td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td>5. Align HR processes and measurement systems (e.g., performance evaluation, compensation decisions, promotion decisions, recruiting and hiring) to support the vision.</td>
<td></td>
<td></td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td>6. Identify and manage leaders who continue to resist needed change.</td>
<td></td>
<td></td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td><strong>Related Guidance (Standards, Frameworks, Compliance Requirements)</strong></td>
<td><strong>Detailed Reference</strong></td>
<td></td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td>No related guidance for this management practice</td>
<td></td>
<td></td>
</tr>
</tbody>
</table>

<table>
<thead>
<tr>
<th>Management Practice</th>
<th>Example Metrics</th>
</tr>
</thead>
<tbody>
<tr>
<td>BAI05.05 Enable operation and use.<br>Plan and implement all technical, operational and usage aspects so all those who are involved in the future state environment can exercise their responsibility.</td>
<td>a. Percent of users appropriately empowered for the change<br>b. Percent of plans developed for operation and use of the change</td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td><strong>Activities</strong></td>
<td></td>
<td><strong>Capability Level</strong></td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td>1. Develop a plan for operation and use of the change. The plan should communicate and build on realized quick wins, address behavioral and cultural aspects of the broader transition, and increase buy-in and engagement. Ensure that the plan covers a holistic view of the change and provides documentation (e.g., procedures), mentoring, training, coaching, knowledge transfer, enhanced immediate post-go-live support and ongoing support.</td>
<td></td>
<td>3</td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td>2. Implement the operation and use plan. Define and track success measures, including hard business measures and perception measures that indicate how people feel about a change. Take remedial action as necessary.</td>
<td></td>
<td>4</td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td><strong>Related Guidance (Standards, Frameworks, Compliance Requirements)</strong></td>
<td><strong>Detailed Reference</strong></td>
<td></td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td>PROSCI® 3-Phase Change Management Process</td>
<td>Phase 2. Managing change</td>
<td></td>
</tr>
</tbody>
</table>

<table>
<thead>
<tr>
<th>Management Practice</th>
<th>Example Metrics</th>
</tr>
</thead>
<tbody>
<tr>
<td>BAI05.06 Embed new approaches.<br>Embed new approaches by tracking implemented changes, assessing the effectiveness of the operation and use plan, and sustaining ongoing awareness through regular communication. Take corrective measures as appropriate (which may include enforcing compliance).</td>
<td>a. Level of satisfaction of users with adoption of the change<br>b. Percent of compliance audits which identified root causes for low adoption<br>c. Number of compliance audits conducted to identify root causes for low adoption and recommended corrective action</td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td><strong>Activities</strong></td>
<td></td>
<td><strong>Capability Level</strong></td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td>1. Make process owners accountable for normal day-to-day operations.</td>
<td></td>
<td>2</td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td>2. Celebrate successes and implement reward and recognition programs to reinforce the change.</td>
<td></td>
<td>3</td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td>3. Provide ongoing awareness through regular communication of the change and its adoption.</td>
<td></td>
<td></td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td>4. Use performance measurement systems to identify root causes for low adoption. Take corrective action.</td>
<td></td>
<td>4</td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td>5. Conduct compliance audits to identify root causes for low adoption. Recommend corrective action.</td>
<td></td>
<td></td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td><strong>Related Guidance (Standards, Frameworks, Compliance Requirements)</strong></td>
<td><strong>Detailed Reference</strong></td>
<td></td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td>PROSCI® 3-Phase Change Management Process</td>
<td>Phase 3. Reinforcing change</td>
<td></td>
</tr>
</tbody>
</table>

# COBIT® 2019 FRAMEWORK: GOVERNANCE AND MANAGEMENT OBJECTIVES

## A. Component: Process (cont.)

<table>
<thead>
<tr>
<th>Management Practice</th>
<th>Example Metrics</th>
</tr>
</thead>
<tbody>
<tr>
<td><strong>BAI05.07 Sustain changes.</strong><br>Sustain changes through effective training of new staff, ongoing communication campaigns, continued commitment of top management, monitoring of adoption and sharing of lessons learned across the enterprise.</td>
<td>a. Number of trainings and knowledge transfers performed<br>b. Percent of top management engagement towards reinforcing the change</td>
</tr>
</tbody>
</table>

<table>
<thead>
<tr>
<th>Activities</th>
<th>Capability Level</th>
</tr>
</thead>
<tbody>
<tr>
<td>1. Sustain and reinforce the change through regular communication that demonstrates top management commitment.</td>
<td>2</td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td>2. Provide mentoring, training, coaching and knowledge transfer to new staff to sustain the change.</td>
<td>3</td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td>3. Perform periodic reviews of the operation and use of the change. Identify improvements.</td>
<td>4</td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td>4. Capture lessons learned relating to implementation of the change. Share knowledge across the enterprise.</td>
<td>5</td>
</tr>
</tbody>
</table>

<table>
<thead>
<tr>
<th>Related Guidance (Standards, Frameworks, Compliance Requirements)</th>
<th>Detailed Reference</th>
</tr>
</thead>
<tbody>
<tr>
<td>PROSCI® 3-Phase Change Management Process</td>
<td>Phase 3. Reinforcing change</td>
</tr>
</tbody>
</table>

<table>
<thead>
<tr>
<th>Key Culture Elements</th>
<th>Related Guidance</th>
<th>Detailed Reference</th>
</tr>
</thead>
<tbody>
<tr>
<td>Realizing value from I&T-enabled investments requires more than delivering I&T solutions and services. It also requires changes to business processes, skills and competencies, culture and behavior, etc., all of which must be included in the business case for the investment. Leadership must create a culture of continuous change through flexibility, openness and confidence and establish appropriate change management support and communication.</td>
<td></td>
<td></td>
</tr>

</table>

## Page 193

# CHAPTER 4
## COBIT GOVERNANCE AND MANAGEMENT OBJECTIVES—DETAILED GUIDANCE

<table>
  <tr>
    <td><strong>Domain:</strong> Build, Acquire and Implement<br><strong>Management Objective:</strong> BAI06 – Managed IT Changes</td>
    <td><strong>Focus Area:</strong> COBIT Core Model</td>
  </tr>
</table>

### Description
Manage all changes in a controlled manner, including standard changes and emergency maintenance relating to business processes, applications and infrastructure. This includes change standards and procedures, impact assessment, prioritization and authorization, emergency changes, tracking, reporting, closure, and documentation.

### Purpose
Enable fast and reliable delivery of change to the business. Mitigate the risk of negatively impacting the stability or integrity of the changed environment.

The management objective supports the achievement of a set of primary enterprise and alignment goals:

<table>
  <tr>
    <td><strong>Enterprise Goals</strong></td>
    <td><strong>Alignment Goals</strong></td>
  </tr>
  <tr>
    <td>EG01 Portfolio of competitive products and services</td>
    <td>AG06 Agility to turn business requirements into operational solutions</td>
  </tr>
  <tr>
    <td><strong>Example Metrics for Enterprise Goals</strong></td>
    <td><strong>Example Metrics for Alignment Goals</strong></td>
  </tr>
  <tr>
    <td>EG01
      <ol type="a">
        <li>Percent of products and services that meet or exceed targets in revenues and/or market share</li>
        <li>Percent of products and services that meet or exceed customer satisfaction targets</li>
        <li>Percent of products and services that provide competitive advantage</li>
        <li>Time to market for new products and services</li>
      </ol>
    </td>
    <td>AG06
      <ol type="a">
        <li>Level of satisfaction of business executives with I&T responsiveness to new requirements</li>
        <li>Average time to market for new I&T-related services and applications</li>
        <li>Average time to turn strategic I&T objectives into agreed and approved initiatives</li>
        <li>Number of critical business processes supported by up-to-date infrastructure and applications</li>
      </ol>
    </td>
  </tr>
</table>

### A. Component: Process

<table>
  <tr>
    <td><strong>Management Practice</strong></td>
    <td><strong>Example Metrics</strong></td>
  </tr>
  <tr>
    <td><strong>BAI06.01 Evaluate, prioritize and authorize change requests.</strong><br>Evaluate all requests for change to determine the impact on business processes and I&T services, and to assess whether change will adversely affect the operational environment and introduce unacceptable risk. Ensure that changes are logged, prioritized, categorized, assessed, authorized, planned and scheduled.</td>
    <td>a. Amount of rework caused by failed changes<br>b. Percent of unsuccessful changes due to inadequate impact assessments</td>
  </tr>
  <tr>
    <td><strong>Activities</strong></td>
    <td><strong>Capability Level</strong></td>
  </tr>
  <tr>
    <td>1. Use formal change requests to enable business process owners and IT to request changes to business process, infrastructure, systems or applications. Make sure that all such changes arise only through the change request management process.<br><br>2. Categorize all requested changes (e.g., business process, infrastructure, operating systems, networks, application systems, purchased/packaged application software) and relate affected configuration items.<br><br>3. Prioritize all requested changes based on the business and technical requirements; resources required; and the legal, regulatory and contractual reasons for the requested change.<br><br>4. Formally approve each change by business process owners, service managers and IT technical stakeholders, as appropriate. Changes that are low-risk and relatively frequent should be pre-approved as standard changes.<br><br>5. Plan and schedule all approved changes.<br><br>6. Plan and evaluate all requests in a structured fashion. Include an impact analysis on business process, infrastructure, systems and applications, business continuity plans (BCPs) and service providers to ensure that all affected components have been identified. Assess the likelihood of adversely affecting the operational environment and the risk of implementing the change. Consider security, privacy, legal, contractual and compliance implications of the requested change. Consider also inter-dependencies among changes. Involve business process owners in the assessment process, as appropriate.<br><br>7. Consider the impact of contracted services providers (e.g., of outsourced business processing, infrastructure, application development and shared services) on the change management process. Include integration of organizational change management processes with change management processes of service providers and the impact on contractual terms and SLAs.</td>
    <td>2</td>
  </tr>
  <tr>
    <td><strong>Related Guidance (Standards, Frameworks, Compliance Requirements)</strong></td>
    <td><strong>Detailed Reference</strong></td>
  </tr>
  <tr>
    <td>ISF, The Standard of Good Practice for Information Security 2016</td>
    <td>SY2.4 Change Management</td>
  </tr>
  <tr>
    <td>ISO/IEC 20000-1:2011(E)</td>
    <td>9.2 Change management</td>
  </tr>
  <tr>
    <td>ITIL V3, 2011</td>
    <td>Service Transition, 4.2 Change Management</td>
  </tr>
  <tr>
    <td>PMBOK Guide Sixth Edition, 2017</td>
    <td>Part 1: 4.6 Perform Integrated Change Control</td>
  </tr>
</table>

&lt;page_number&gt;193&lt;/page_number&gt;

## Page 194

# COBIT® 2019 FRAMEWORK: GOVERNANCE AND MANAGEMENT OBJECTIVES

## A. Component: Process (cont.)

<table>
  <thead>
    <tr>
      <th>Management Practice</th>
      <th>Example Metrics</th>
    </tr>
  </thead>
  <tbody>
    <tr>
      <td>
        **BAI06.02 Manage emergency changes.**<br>
        Carefully manage emergency changes to minimize further incidents. Ensure the emergency change is controlled and takes place securely. Verify that emergency changes are appropriately assessed and authorized after the change.
      </td>
      <td>
        a. Number of emergency changes not authorized after the incident<br>
        b. Percent of total changes that are emergency fixes
      </td>
    </tr>
  </tbody>
</table>

<table>
  <thead>
    <tr>
      <th>Activities</th>
      <th>Capability Level</th>
    </tr>
  </thead>
  <tbody>
    <tr>
      <td>1. Define what constitutes an emergency change.</td>
      <td>2</td>
    </tr>
    <tr>
      <td>2. Ensure that a documented procedure exists to declare, assess, approve preliminarily, authorize after the change and record an emergency change.</td>
      <td></td>
    </tr>
    <tr>
      <td>3. Verify that all emergency access arrangements for changes are appropriately authorized, documented and revoked after the change has been applied.</td>
      <td>3</td>
    </tr>
    <tr>
      <td>4. Monitor all emergency changes and conduct post-implementation reviews involving all concerned parties. The review should consider and initiate corrective actions based on root causes such as problems with business process, application system development and maintenance, development and test environments, documentation and manuals, and data integrity.</td>
      <td>4</td>
    </tr>
  </tbody>
</table>

<table>
  <thead>
    <tr>
      <th>Related Guidance (Standards, Frameworks, Compliance Requirements)</th>
      <th>Detailed Reference</th>
    </tr>
  </thead>
  <tbody>
    <tr>
      <td>No related guidance for this management practice</td>
      <td></td>
    </tr>
  </tbody>
</table>

<table>
  <thead>
    <tr>
      <th>Management Practice</th>
      <th>Example Metrics</th>
    </tr>
  </thead>
  <tbody>
    <tr>
      <td>
        **BAI06.03 Track and report change status.**<br>
        Maintain a tracking and reporting system to document rejected changes and communicate the status of approved, in-process and complete changes. Make certain that approved changes are implemented as planned.
      </td>
      <td>
        a. Number and age of backlogged change requests<br>
        b. Percent of change request status reported to stakeholders in a timely manner
      </td>
    </tr>
  </tbody>
</table>

<table>
  <thead>
    <tr>
      <th>Activities</th>
      <th>Capability Level</th>
    </tr>
  </thead>
  <tbody>
    <tr>
      <td>1. Categorize change requests in the tracking process (e.g., rejected, approved but not yet initiated, approved and in process, and closed).</td>
      <td>4</td>
    </tr>
    <tr>
      <td>2. Implement change status reports with performance metrics to enable management review and monitoring of both the detailed status of changes and the overall state (e.g., aged analysis of change requests). Ensure that status reports form an audit trail so changes can subsequently be tracked from inception to eventual disposition.</td>
      <td></td>
    </tr>
    <tr>
      <td>3. Monitor open changes to ensure that all approved changes are closed in a timely fashion, depending on priority.</td>
      <td></td>
    </tr>
    <tr>
      <td>4. Maintain a tracking and reporting system for all change requests.</td>
      <td></td>
    </tr>
  </tbody>
</table>

<table>
  <thead>
    <tr>
      <th>Related Guidance (Standards, Frameworks, Compliance Requirements)</th>
      <th>Detailed Reference</th>
    </tr>
  </thead>
  <tbody>
    <tr>
      <td>CMMI Cybermaturity Platform, 2018</td>
      <td>IP.CC Apply Change Control</td>
    </tr>
  </tbody>
</table>

<table>
  <thead>
    <tr>
      <th>Management Practice</th>
      <th>Example Metrics</th>
    </tr>
  </thead>
  <tbody>
    <tr>
      <td>
        **BAI06.04 Close and document the changes.**<br>
        Whenever changes are implemented, update the solution, user documentation and procedures affected by the change.
      </td>
      <td>
        a. Number of review errors found in the documentation<br>
        b. Percent of user documentation and procedures updates performed in a timely manner
      </td>
    </tr>
  </tbody>
</table>

<table>
  <thead>
    <tr>
      <th>Activities</th>
      <th>Capability Level</th>
    </tr>
  </thead>
  <tbody>
    <tr>
      <td>1. Include changes in the documentation within the management procedure. Examples of documentation include business and IT operational procedures, business continuity and disaster recovery documentation, configuration information, application documentation, help screens, and training materials.</td>
      <td>2</td>
    </tr>
    <tr>
      <td>2. Define an appropriate retention period for change documentation and pre- and post-change system and user documentation.</td>
      <td>3</td>
    </tr>
    <tr>
      <td>3. Subject documentation to the same level of review as the actual change.</td>
      <td></td>
    </tr>
  </tbody>
</table>

<table>
  <thead>
    <tr>
      <th>Related Guidance (Standards, Frameworks, Compliance Requirements)</th>
      <th>Detailed Reference</th>
    </tr>
  </thead>

## E. Component: Policies and Procedures

<table>
<thead>
<tr>
<th>Relevant Policy</th>
<th>Policy Description</th>
<th>Related Guidance</th>
<th>Detailed Reference</th>
</tr>
</thead>
<tbody>
<tr>
<td>IT change management policy</td>
<td>Communicates management intent that all changes to enterprise IT are managed and implemented so as to minimize risk and impact to stakeholders. Covers in-scope assets and standard change management process.</td>
<td></td>
<td></td>
</tr>
</tbody>
</table>

## F. Component: Culture, Ethics and Behavior

<table>
<thead>
<tr>
<th>Key Culture Elements</th>
<th>Related Guidance</th>
<th>Detailed Reference</th>
</tr>
</thead>
<tbody>
<tr>
<td>Leaders must create a culture of continuous improvement in IT solutions and services, recognizing that improvement requires them to understand the impact of technology change on the enterprise, its inherent risk and associated mitigation, as well as its cost. Leaders must balance the impact of change against its expected benefits and contribution to I&T strategy and enterprise objectives.</td>
<td></td>
<td></td>
</tr>
</tbody>
</table>

## G. Component: Services, Infrastructure and Applications

* Configuration management tools
* IT change management tools

## Page 197

# CHAPTER 4
## COBIT GOVERNANCE AND MANAGEMENT OBJECTIVES—DETAILED GUIDANCE

<table>
  <tr>
    <td>Domain: Build, Acquire and Implement<br>Management Objective: BAI07 – Managed IT Change Acceptance and Transitioning</td>
    <td>Focus Area: COBIT Core Model</td>
  </tr>
</table>

### Description
Formally accept and make operational new solutions. Include implementation planning, system and data conversion, acceptance testing, communication, release preparation, promotion to production of new or changed business processes and I&T services, early production support, and a post-implementation review.

### Purpose
Implement solutions safely and in line with the agreed expectations and outcomes.

The management objective supports the achievement of a set of primary enterprise and alignment goals:

<table>
  <tr>
    <td>Enterprise Goals</td>
    <td>Alignment Goals</td>
  </tr>
  <tr>
    <td>EG01 Portfolio of competitive products and services</td>
    <td>AG06 Agility to turn business requirements into operational solutions</td>
  </tr>
  <tr>
    <td>Example Metrics for Enterprise Goals</td>
    <td>Example Metrics for Alignment Goals</td>
  </tr>
  <tr>
    <td>EG01
      <ul>
        <li>a. Percent of products and services that meet or exceed targets in revenues and/or market share</li>
        <li>b. Percent of products and services that meet or exceed customer satisfaction targets</li>
        <li>c. Percent of products and services that provide competitive advantage</li>
        <li>d. Time to market for new products and services</li>
      </ul>
    </td>
    <td>AG06
      <ul>
        <li>a. Level of satisfaction of business executives with I&T responsiveness to new requirements</li>
        <li>b. Average time to market for new I&T-related services and applications</li>
        <li>c. Average time to turn strategic I&T objectives into agreed and approved initiatives</li>
        <li>d. Number of critical business processes supported by up-to-date infrastructure and applications</li>
      </ul>
    </td>
  </tr>
</table>

### A. Component: Process

<table>
  <tr>
    <td>Management Practice</td>
    <td>Example Metrics</td>
  </tr>
  <tr>
    <td>BAI07.01 Establish an implementation plan.<br>Establish an implementation plan that covers system and data conversion, acceptance testing criteria, communication, training, release preparation, promotion to production, early production support, a fallback/back-up plan, and a post-implementation review. Obtain approval from relevant parties.</td>
    <td>
      <ul>
        <li>a. Number and category of stakeholders signing off on the implementation plan</li>
        <li>b. Number of implementation plans that are robust and contain all required components</li>
      </ul>
    </td>
  </tr>
  <tr>
    <td>Activities</td>
    <td>Capability Level</td>
  </tr>
  <tr>
    <td>
      <ol>
        <li>Create an implementation plan that reflects the broad implementation strategy, the sequence of implementation steps, resource requirements, inter-dependencies, criteria for management acceptance of the production implementation, installation verification requirements, transition strategy for production support, and update of business continuity plans.</li>
        <li>From external solution providers, obtain commitment to their involvement in each step of the implementation.</li>
        <li>Identify and document the fallback and recovery processes.</li>
        <li>Confirm that all implementation plans are approved by technical and business stakeholders and reviewed by internal audit, as appropriate.</li>
        <li>Formally review the technical and business risk associated with implementation. Ensure that the key risk is considered and addressed in the planning process.</li>
      </ol>
    </td>
    <td>2</td>
  </tr>
  <tr>
    <td>Related Guidance (Standards, Frameworks, Compliance Requirements)</td>
    <td>Detailed Reference</td>
  </tr>
  <tr>
    <td>ITIL V3, 2011</td>
    <td>Service Transition, 4.1 Transition Planning and Support</td>
  </tr>
</table>

&lt;page_number&gt;197&lt;/page_number&gt;

## Page 198

# COBIT® 2019 FRAMEWORK: GOVERNANCE AND MANAGEMENT OBJECTIVES

## A. Component: Process (cont.)

<table>
  <thead>
    <tr>
      <th>Management Practice</th>
      <th>Example Metrics</th>
    </tr>
  </thead>
  <tbody>
    <tr>
      <td><strong>BAI07.02 Plan business process, system and data conversion.</strong><br>Prepare for business process, I&T service data and infrastructure migration as part of the enterprise's development methods. Include audit trails and a recovery plan should the migration fail.</td>
      <td>a. Percent of successful conversion<br>b. Percent of necessary adjustments made to procedures (including revised roles and responsibilities and control procedures)</td>
    </tr>
  </tbody>
</table>

<table>
  <thead>
    <tr>
      <th>Activities</th>
      <th>Capability Level</th>
    </tr>
  </thead>
  <tbody>
    <tr>
      <td>1. Define a business process, I&T service data and infrastructure migration plan. In developing the plan, consider, for example, hardware, networks, operating systems, software, transaction data, master files, backups and archives, interfaces with other systems (both internal and external), possible compliance requirements, business procedures, and system documentation.</td>
      <td>2</td>
    </tr>
    <tr>
      <td>2. In the business process conversion plan, consider all necessary adjustments to procedures, including revised roles and responsibilities and control procedures.</td>
      <td></td>
    </tr>
    <tr>
      <td>3. Confirm that the data conversion plan does not require changes in data values unless absolutely necessary for business reasons. Document changes made to data values, and secure approval from the business process data owner.</td>
      <td></td>
    </tr>
    <tr>
      <td>4. Plan retention of backup and archived data to conform to business needs and regulatory or compliance requirements.</td>
      <td></td>
    </tr>
    <tr>
      <td>5. Rehearse and test the conversion before attempting a live conversion.</td>
      <td></td>
    </tr>
    <tr>
      <td>6. Coordinate and verify the timing and completeness of the conversion cutover so there is a smooth, continuous transition with no loss of transaction data. Where necessary, in the absence of any other alternative, freeze live operations.</td>
      <td></td>
    </tr>
    <tr>
      <td>7. Plan to back up all systems and data taken at the point prior to conversion. Maintain audit trails to enable the conversion to be retraced. Ensure that there is a recovery plan that covers rollback of migration and fallback to previous processing should the migration fail.</td>
      <td></td>
    </tr>
    <tr>
      <td>8. In the data conversion plan, incorporate methods for collecting, converting and verifying data to be converted, and identifying and resolving any errors found during conversion. Include comparing the original and converted data for completeness and integrity.</td>
      <td>3</td>
    </tr>
    <tr>
      <td>9. Consider the risk of conversion problems, business continuity planning and fallback procedures in the business process, data and infrastructure migration plan where there are risk management, business needs or regulatory/compliance requirements.</td>
      <td></td>
    </tr>
  </tbody>
</table>

<table>
  <thead>
    <tr>
      <th>Related Guidance (Standards, Frameworks, Compliance Requirements)</th>
      <th>Detailed Reference</th>
    </tr>
  </thead>
  <tbody>
    <tr>
      <td>ITIL V3, 2011</td>
      <td>Service Transition, 4.1 Transition Planning and Support</td>
    </tr>
  </tbody>
</table>

<table>
  <thead>
    <tr>
      <th>Management Practice</th>
      <th>Example Metrics</th>
    </tr>
  </thead>
  <tbody>
    <tr>
      <td><strong>BAI07.03 Plan acceptance tests.</strong><br>Establish a test plan based on enterprisewide standards that define roles, responsibilities, and entry and exit criteria. Ensure that the plan is approved by relevant parties.</td>
      <td>a. Percent of stakeholders satisfied with the completeness of testing process<br>b. Number of documented test plans that include all testing phases and robust testing scenarios and are appropriate to the operational requirements and environment</td>
    </tr>
  </tbody>
</table>

&lt;page_number&gt;198&lt;/page_number&gt;

## Page 199

CHAPTER 4
COBIT GOVERNANCE AND MANAGEMENT OBJECTIVES—DETAILED GUIDANCE

A. Component: Process (cont.)

<table>
<thead>
<tr>
<th>Activities</th>
<th>Capability Level</th>
</tr>
</thead>
<tbody>
<tr>
<td>1. Develop and document the test plan, which aligns to the program, project quality plan and relevant organizational standards. Communicate and consult with appropriate business process owners and IT stakeholders.</td>
<td>2</td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td>2. Ensure that the test plan reflects an assessment of risk from the project and that all functional and technical requirements are tested. Based on assessment of the risk of system failure and faults on implementation, include in the plan requirements for performance, stress, usability, pilot, security testing and privacy.</td>
<td></td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td>3. Ensure that the test plan addresses the potential need for internal or external accreditation of outcomes of the test process (e.g., financial or regulatory requirements).</td>
<td></td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td>4. Ensure that the test plan identifies necessary resources to execute testing and evaluate the results. Examples of resources may be construction of test environments and use of staff time for the test group, including potential temporary replacement of test staff in the production or development environments. Ensure that stakeholders are consulted on the resource implications of the test plan.</td>
<td></td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td>5. Ensure that the test plan identifies testing phases appropriate to the operational requirements and environment. Examples of such testing phases include unit test, system test, integration test, user acceptance test, performance test, stress test, data conversion test, security test, privacy test, operational readiness test, and backup and recovery tests.</td>
<td></td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td>6. Confirm that the test plan considers test preparation (including site preparation), training requirements, installation or an update of a defined test environment, planning/performing/documenting/retaining test cases, error and problem handling, correction and escalation, and formal approval.</td>
<td></td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td>7. Confirm that all test plans are approved by stakeholders, including business process owners and IT, as appropriate. Stakeholders may include application development managers, project managers and business process end users.</td>
<td></td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td>8. Ensure that the test plan establishes clear criteria for measuring the success of undertaking each testing phase. Consult the business process owners and IT stakeholders in defining the success criteria. Determine that the plan establishes remediation procedures when the success criteria are not met. For example, if there is a significant failure in a testing phase, the plan should provide guidance on whether to proceed to the next phase, stop testing or postpone implementation.</td>
<td>3</td>
</tr>
</tbody>
</table>

<table>
<thead>
<tr>
<th>Related Guidance (Standards, Frameworks, Compliance Requirements)</th>
<th>Detailed Reference</th>
</tr>
</thead>
<tbody>
<tr>
<td>No related guidance for this management practice</td>
<td></td>
</tr>
</tbody>
</table>

<table>
<thead>
<tr>
<th>Management Practice</th>
<th>Example Metrics</th>
</tr>
</thead>
<tbody>
<tr>
<td>BAI07.04 Establish a test environment.<br>Define and establish a secure test environment representative of the planned business process and IT operations environment in terms of performance, capacity, security, internal controls, operational practices, data quality, privacy requirements and workloads.</td>
<td>a. Level of comparability between test environment and future business and operational landscape<br>b. Level of sanitized test data (and/or databases) that are representative of the production environment</td>
</tr>
</tbody>
</table>

<table>
<thead>
<tr>
<th>Activities</th>
<th>Capability Level</th>
</tr>
</thead>
<tbody>
<tr>
<td>1. Create a database of test data that are representative of the production environment. Sanitize data used in the test environment from the production environment according to business needs and organizational standards. For example, consider whether compliance or regulatory requirements oblige the use of sanitized data.</td>
<td>2</td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td>2. Protect sensitive test data and results against disclosure, including access, retention, storage and destruction. Consider the effect of interaction of organizational systems with those of third parties.</td>
<td>3</td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td>3. Put in place a process to enable proper retention or disposal of test results, media and other associated documentation that will enable adequate review and subsequent analysis or efficient retesting as required by the test plan. Consider the effect of regulatory or compliance requirements.</td>
<td></td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td>4. Ensure that the test environment is representative of the future business and operational landscape. Include business process procedures and roles, likely workload stress, operating systems, necessary application software, database management systems, and network and computing infrastructure found in the production environment.</td>
<td></td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td>5. Ensure that the test environment is secure and incapable of interacting with production systems.</td>
<td></td>
</tr>
</tbody>
</table>

<table>
<thead>
<tr>
<th>Related Guidance (Standards, Frameworks, Compliance Requirements)</th>
<th>Detailed Reference</th>
</tr>
</thead>
<tbody>
<tr>
<td>No related guidance for this management practice</td>
<td></td>
</tr>
</tbody>
</table>

## Page 200

# COBIT® 2019 FRAMEWORK: GOVERNANCE AND MANAGEMENT OBJECTIVES

<table>
  <thead>
    <tr>
      <th colspan="3">A. Component: Process (cont.)</th>
    </tr>
  </thead>
  <tbody>
    <tr>
      <td><strong>Management Practice</strong></td>
      <td><strong>Example Metrics</strong></td>
      <td></td>
    </tr>
    <tr>
      <td><strong>BAI07.05 Perform acceptance tests.</strong><br>Test changes independently, in accordance with the defined test plan, prior to migration to the live operational environment.</td>
      <td>a. Number of identified gaps between acceptance test results and the defined success criteria<br>b. Number of successful acceptance tests</td>
      <td></td>
    </tr>
    <tr>
      <td><strong>Activities</strong></td>
      <td></td>
      <td><strong>Capability Level</strong></td>
    </tr>
    <tr>
      <td>1. Review the categorized log of errors found in the testing process by the development team. Verify that all errors have been remediated or formally accepted.</td>
      <td></td>
      <td>2</td>
    </tr>
    <tr>
      <td>2. Evaluate the final acceptance against the success criteria and interpret the final acceptance testing results. Present them in a form that is understandable to business process owners and IT, so an informed review and evaluation can take place.</td>
      <td></td>
      <td>3</td>
    </tr>
    <tr>
      <td>3. Approve the acceptance, with formal sign-off by the business process owners, third parties (as appropriate) and IT stakeholders prior to promotion.</td>
      <td></td>
      <td></td>
    </tr>
    <tr>
      <td>4. Ensure that testing of changes is undertaken in accordance with the testing plan. Ensure that the testing is designed and conducted by a test group that is independent from the development team. Consider the extent to which business process owners and end users are involved in the test group. Ensure that testing is conducted only within the test environment.</td>
      <td></td>
      <td></td>
    </tr>
    <tr>
      <td>5. Ensure that the tests and anticipated outcomes are in accordance with the defined success criteria set out in the testing plan.</td>
      <td></td>
      <td></td>
    </tr>
    <tr>
      <td>6. Consider using clearly defined test instructions (scripts) to implement the tests. Ensure that the independent test group assesses and approves each test script to confirm that it adequately addresses test success criteria set out in the test plan. Consider using scripts to verify the extent to which the system meets security and privacy requirements.</td>
      <td></td>
      <td></td>
    </tr>
    <tr>
      <td>7. Consider the appropriate balance between automated scripted tests and interactive user testing.</td>
      <td></td>
      <td></td>
    </tr>
    <tr>
      <td>8. Undertake tests of security in accordance with the test plan. Measure the extent of security weaknesses or loopholes. Consider the effect of security incidents since construction of the test plan. Consider the effect on access and boundary controls. Consider privacy.</td>
      <td></td>
      <td></td>
    </tr>
    <tr>
      <td>9. Undertake tests of system and application performance in accordance with the test plan. Consider a range of performance metrics (e.g., end-user response times and database management system update performance).</td>
      <td></td>
      <td></td>
    </tr>
    <tr>
      <td>10. When undertaking testing, ensure that the fallback and rollback elements of the test plan have been addressed.</td>
      <td></td>
      <td></td>
    </tr>
    <tr>
      <td>11. Identify, log and classify (e.g., minor, significant, mission-critical) errors during testing. Ensure that an audit trail of test results is available. In accordance with the test plan, communicate results of testing to stakeholders to facilitate bug fixing and further quality enhancement.</td>
      <td></td>
      <td></td>
    </tr>
    <tr>
      <td><strong>Related Guidance (Standards, Frameworks, Compliance Requirements)</strong></td>
      <td><strong>Detailed Reference</strong></td>
      <td></td>
    </tr>
    <tr>
      <td>ITIL V3, 2011</td>
      <td>Service Transition, 4.5 Service Validation and Testing</td>
      <td></td>
    </tr>
    <tr>
      <td><strong>Management Practice</strong></td>
      <td><strong>Example Metrics</strong></td>
      <td></td>
    </tr>
    <tr>
      <td><strong>BAI07.06 Promote to production and manage releases.</strong><br>Promote the accepted solution to the business and operations. Where appropriate, run the solution as a pilot implementation or in parallel with the old solution for a defined period and compare behavior and results. If significant problems occur, revert to the original environment based on the fallback/back-up plan. Manage releases of solution components.</td>
      <td>a. Number and percent of releases not ready for release on schedule<br>b. Percent of stakeholder satisfaction with the implemented solution</td>
      <td></td>
    </tr>
    <tr>
      <td><strong>Activities</strong></td>
      <td></td>
      <td><strong>Capability Level</strong></td>
    </tr>
    <tr>
      <td>1. Prepare for transfer of business procedures and supporting services, applications and infrastructure from testing to the production environment in accordance with organizational change management standards.</td>
      <td></td>
      <td>2</td>
    </tr>
    <tr>
      <td>2. Determine the extent of pilot implementation or parallel processing of the old and new systems in line with the implementation plan.</td>
      <td></td>
      <td></td>
    </tr>
    <tr>
      <td>3. Promptly update relevant business process and system documentation, configuration information and contingency plan documents, as appropriate.</td>
      <td></td>
      <td></td>
    </tr>
    <tr>
      <td>4. Ensure that all media libraries are updated promptly with the version of the solution component being transferred from testing to the production environment. Archive the existing version and its supporting documentation. Ensure that promotion to production of systems, application software and infrastructure is under configuration control.</td>
      <td></td>
      <td></td>
    </tr>
    <tr>
      <td>5. Where distribution of solution components is conducted electronically, control automated distribution to ensure that users are notified, and distribution occurs only to authorized and correctly identified destinations. In the release process, include backup procedures to enable the distribution of changes to be reviewed in the event of a malfunction or error.</td>
      <td></td>
      <td></td>
    </tr>
    <tr>
      <td>6. Where distribution takes physical form, keep a formal log of what items have been distributed, to whom, where they have been implemented, and when each has been updated.</td>
      <td></td>
      <td></td>
    </tr>
  </tbody>
</table>

## Page 201

CHAPTER 4
COBIT GOVERNANCE AND MANAGEMENT OBJECTIVES—DETAILED GUIDANCE

A. Component: Process (cont.)

<table>
<thead>
<tr>
<th>Related Guidance (Standards, Frameworks, Compliance Requirements)</th>
<th>Detailed Reference</th>
</tr>
</thead>
<tbody>
<tr>
<td>ISO/IEC 20000-1:2011(E)</td>
<td>9.3 Release and deployment management</td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td>ITIL V3 2011</td>
<td>Service Transition, 4.4 Release and Deployment Management</td>
</tr>
</tbody>
</table>

<table>
<thead>
<tr>
<th>Management Practice</th>
<th>Example Metrics</th>
</tr>
</thead>
<tbody>
<tr>
<td>BAI07.07 Provide early production support.<br>For an agreed period of time, provide early support to users and I&T operations to resolve issues and help stabilize the new solution.</td>
<td>a. Number of additional I&T system resources provided for support<br>b. Number of additional staff resources provided for support</td>
</tr>
</tbody>
</table>

<table>
<thead>
<tr>
<th>Activities</th>
<th>Capability Level</th>
</tr>
</thead>
<tbody>
<tr>
<td>1. Provide additional resources, as required, to end users and support personnel until the release has stabilized.</td>
<td rowspan="2">3</td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td>2. Provide additional I&T systems resources, as required, until the release is in a stable operational environment.</td>
</tr>
</tbody>
</table>

<table>
<thead>
<tr>
<th>Related Guidance (Standards, Frameworks, Compliance Requirements)</th>
<th>Detailed Reference</th>
</tr>
</thead>
<tbody>
<tr>
<td>No related guidance for this management practice</td>
<td></td>
</tr>
</tbody>
</table>

<table>
<thead>
<tr>
<th>Management Practice</th>
<th>Example Metrics</th>
</tr>
</thead>
<tbody>
<tr>
<td>BAI07.08 Perform a post-implementation review.<br>Conduct a post-implementation review to confirm outcome and results, identify lessons learned, and develop an action plan. Evaluate actual performance and outcomes of the new or changed service against expected performance and outcomes anticipated by the user or customer.</td>
<td>a. Number and percent of root cause analyses completed<br>b. Number or percent of releases that fail to stabilize within an acceptable period<br>c. Percent of releases causing downtime</td>
</tr>
</tbody>
</table>

<table>
<thead>
<tr>
<th>Activities</th>
<th>Capability Level</th>
</tr>
</thead>
<tbody>
<tr>
<td>1. Establish procedures to ensure that post-implementation reviews identify, assess and report on the extent to which the following events have occurred: enterprise requirements have been met; expected benefits have been realized; the system is considered usable; internal and external stakeholder expectations are met; unexpected impacts on the enterprise have occurred; key risk is mitigated; and the change management, installation and accreditation processes were performed effectively and efficiently.</td>
<td>3</td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td>2. Consult business process owners and IT technical management in the choice of metrics for measurement of success and achievement of requirements and benefits.</td>
<td>4</td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td>3. Conduct the post-implementation review in accordance with the organizational change management process. Engage business process owners and third parties, as appropriate.</td>
<td></td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td>4. Consider requirements for post-implementation review arising from outside business and IT (e.g., internal audit, ERM, compliance).</td>
<td></td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td>5. Agree on and implement an action plan to address issues identified in the post-implementation review. Engage business process owners and IT technical management in the development of the action plan.</td>
<td>5</td>
</tr>
</tbody>
</table>

<table>
<thead>
<tr>
<th>Related Guidance (Standards, Frameworks, Compliance Requirements)</th>
<th>Detailed Reference</th>
</tr>
</thead>
<tbody>
<tr>
<td>ITIL V3, 2011</td>
<td>Service Transition, 4.6 Change Evaluation</td>
</tr>
</tbody>
</table>
