## Page 205

# CHAPTER 4
## COBIT GOVERNANCE AND MANAGEMENT OBJECTIVES—DETAILED GUIDANCE

<table>
  <tr>
    <td style="vertical-align: top;">
      **Domain:** Build, Acquire and Implement<br>
      **Management Objective:** BAI08 – Managed Knowledge
    </td>
    <td style="vertical-align: top;">
      **Focus Area:** COBIT Core Model
    </td>
  </tr>
</table>

### Description
Maintain the availability of relevant, current, validated and reliable knowledge and management information to support all process activities and to facilitate decision making related to the governance and management of enterprise I&T. Plan for the identification, gathering, organizing, maintaining, use and retirement of knowledge.

### Purpose
Provide the knowledge and information required to support all staff in the governance and management of enterprise I&T and allow for informed decision making.

The management objective supports the achievement of a set of primary enterprise and alignment goals:

<table>
  <tr>
    <td style="vertical-align: top; width: 50%;">
      ### Enterprise Goals
      *   EG01 Portfolio of competitive products and services
      *   EG10 Staff skills, motivation and productivity
      *   EG13 Product and business innovation
    </td>
    <td style="vertical-align: top; width: 50%; border-left: 2px solid black; padding-left: 20px;">
      ### Alignment Goals
      *   AG12 Competent and motivated staff with mutual understanding of technology and business
      *   AG13 Knowledge, expertise and initiatives for business innovation
    </td>
  </tr>
  <tr>
    <td style="vertical-align: top;">
      ### Example Metrics for Enterprise Goals
      EG01
      *   a. Percent of products and services that meet or exceed targets in revenues and/or market share
      *   b. Percent of products and services that meet or exceed customer satisfaction targets
      *   c. Percent of products and services that provide competitive advantage
      *   d. Time to market for new products and services
      EG10
      *   a. Staff productivity compared to benchmarks
      *   b. Level of stakeholder satisfaction with staff expertise and skills
      *   c. Percent of staff whose skills are insufficient for competency in their role
      *   d. Percent of satisfied staff
      EG13
      *   a. Level of awareness and understanding of business innovation opportunities
      *   b. Stakeholder satisfaction with levels of product and innovation expertise and ideas
      *   c. Number of approved product and service initiatives resulting from innovative ideas
    </td>
    <td style="vertical-align: top;">
      ### Example Metrics for Alignment Goals
      AG12
      *   a. Percent of I&T-savvy business people (i.e., those having the required knowledge and understanding of I&T to guide, direct, innovate and see I&T opportunities in their domain of business expertise)
      *   b. Percent of business-savvy I&T people (i.e., those having the required knowledge and understanding of relevant business domains to guide, direct, innovate and see I&T opportunities for the business domain)
      *   c. Number or percentage of business people with technology management experience
      AG13
      *   a. Level of business executive awareness and understanding of I&T innovation possibilities
      *   b. Number of approved initiatives resulting from innovative I&T ideas
      *   c. Number of innovation champions recognized/awarded
    </td>
  </tr>
</table>

Build, Acquire and Implement
&lt;page_number&gt;205&lt;/page_number&gt;

---


## Page 206

# COBIT® 2019 FRAMEWORK: GOVERNANCE AND MANAGEMENT OBJECTIVES

<table>
  <thead>
    <tr>
      <th colspan="3">A. Component: Process</th>
    </tr>
  </thead>
  <tbody>
    <tr>
      <td><strong>Management Practice</strong></td>
      <td><strong>Example Metrics</strong></td>
      <td></td>
    </tr>
    <tr>
      <td><strong>BAI08.01 Identify and classify sources of information for governance and management of I&amp;T.</strong><br>Identify, validate and classify diverse sources of internal and external information required to enable governance and management of I&amp;T, including strategy documents, incident reports and configuration information that progresses from development to operations before going live.</td>
      <td>a. Percent of categorized information validated<br>b. Percent of appropriateness of content types, artifacts, and structured and unstructured information</td>
      <td></td>
    </tr>
    <tr>
      <td><strong>Activities</strong></td>
      <td></td>
      <td><strong>Capability Level</strong></td>
    </tr>
    <tr>
      <td>1. Identify potential knowledge users, including owners of information who may need to contribute and approve knowledge. Obtain knowledge requirements and sources of information from identified users.</td>
      <td></td>
      <td>2</td>
    </tr>
    <tr>
      <td>2. Consider content types (procedures, processes, structures, concepts, policies, rules, facts, classifications), artefacts (documents, records, video, voice), and structured and unstructured information (experts, social media, email, voice mail, Rich Site Summary (RSS) feeds).</td>
      <td></td>
      <td></td>
    </tr>
    <tr>
      <td>3. Classify sources of information based on a content classification scheme (e.g., information architecture model). Map sources of information to the classification scheme.</td>
      <td></td>
      <td>3</td>
    </tr>
    <tr>
      <td>4. Collect, collate and validate information sources based on information validation criteria (e.g., understandability, relevance, importance, integrity, accuracy, consistency, confidentiality, currency and reliability).</td>
      <td></td>
      <td>4</td>
    </tr>
    <tr>
      <td><strong>Related Guidance (Standards, Frameworks, Compliance Requirements)</strong></td>
      <td><strong>Detailed Reference</strong></td>
      <td></td>
    </tr>
    <tr>
      <td>No related guidance for this management practice</td>
      <td></td>
      <td></td>
    </tr>
    <tr>
      <td><strong>Management Practice</strong></td>
      <td><strong>Example Metrics</strong></td>
      <td></td>
    </tr>
    <tr>
      <td><strong>BAI08.02 Organize and contextualize information into knowledge.</strong><br>Organize information based on classification criteria. Identify and create meaningful relationships among information elements and enable use of information. Identify owners, and leverage and implement enterprise-defined information levels of access to management information and knowledge resources.</td>
      <td>a. Number of relationships identified among sources of information (tagging)<br>b. Percent of stakeholder satisfaction with the organization and contextualization of information into knowledge</td>
      <td></td>
    </tr>
    <tr>
      <td><strong>Activities</strong></td>
      <td></td>
      <td><strong>Capability Level</strong></td>
    </tr>
    <tr>
      <td>1. Identify shared attributes and match sources of information, creating relationships among information sets (information tagging).</td>
      <td></td>
      <td>3</td>
    </tr>
    <tr>
      <td>2. Create views to related data sets, considering stakeholder and organizational requirements.</td>
      <td></td>
      <td></td>
    </tr>
    <tr>
      <td>3. Devise and implement a scheme to manage unstructured knowledge not available through formal sources (e.g., expert knowledge).</td>
      <td></td>
      <td></td>
    </tr>
    <tr>
      <td>4. Publish and make knowledge accessible to relevant stakeholders, based on roles and access mechanisms.</td>
      <td></td>
      <td></td>
    </tr>
    <tr>
      <td><strong>Related Guidance (Standards, Frameworks, Compliance Requirements)</strong></td>
      <td><strong>Detailed Reference</strong></td>
      <td></td>
    </tr>
    <tr>
      <td>COSO Enterprise Risk Management, June 2017</td>
      <td>10. Information, Communication, and Reporting - Principle 18</td>
      <td></td>
    </tr>
    <tr>
      <td><strong>Management Practice</strong></td>
      <td><strong>Example Metrics</strong></td>
      <td></td>
    </tr>
    <tr>
      <td><strong>BAI08.03 Use and share knowledge.</strong><br>Propagate available knowledge resources to relevant stakeholders and communicate how these resources can be used to address different needs (e.g., problem solving, learning, strategic planning and decision making).</td>
      <td>a. Percent of available knowledge actually used<br>b. Percent of knowledge user satisfaction</td>
      <td></td>
    </tr>
    <tr>
      <td><strong>Activities</strong></td>
      <td></td>
      <td><strong>Capability Level</strong></td>
    </tr>
    <tr>
      <td>1. Set management expectations and demonstrate appropriate attitude regarding the usefulness of knowledge and the need to share knowledge related to the governance and management of enterprise I&amp;T.</td>
      <td></td>
      <td>2</td>
    </tr>
    <tr>
      <td>2. Identify potential knowledge users by knowledge classification.</td>
      <td></td>
      <td></td>
    </tr>
    <tr>
      <td>3. Transfer knowledge to knowledge users, based on a needs gap analysis and effective learning techniques. Create an environment, tools and artifacts that support the sharing and transfer of knowledge. Ensure appropriate access controls are in place, in line with defined knowledge classification.</td>
      <td></td>
      <td>3</td>
    </tr>
    <tr>
      <td>4. Measure the use of knowledge tools and elements and evaluate the impact on governance processes.</td>
      <td></td>
      <td>4</td>
    </tr>
    <tr>
      <td>5. Improve information and knowledge for governance processes that show knowledge gaps.</td>
      <td></td>
      <td>5</td>
    </tr>
  </tbody>
</table>

&lt;page_number&gt;206&lt;/page_number&gt;

## Page 209

# CHAPTER 4
## COBIT GOVERNANCE AND MANAGEMENT OBJECTIVES—DETAILED GUIDANCE

<table>
  <tr>
    <td><strong>Domain:</strong> Build, Acquire and Implement<br><strong>Management Objective:</strong> BAI09 – Managed Assets</td>
    <td><strong>Focus Area:</strong> COBIT Core Model</td>
  </tr>
</table>

### Description
Manage I&T assets through their life cycle to make sure that their use delivers value at optimal cost, they remain operational (fit for purpose), and they are accounted for and physically protected. Ensure that those assets that are critical to support service capability are reliable and available. Manage software licenses to ensure that the optimal number are acquired, retained and deployed in relation to required business usage, and the software installed is in compliance with license agreements.

### Purpose
Account for all I&T assets and optimize the value provided by their use.

The management objective supports the achievement of a set of primary enterprise and alignment goals:

<table>
  <tr>
    <td><strong>Enterprise Goals</strong></td>
    <td><strong>Alignment Goals</strong></td>
  </tr>
  <tr>
    <td>
      • EG04 Quality of financial information<br>
      • EG07 Quality of management information<br>
      • EG09 Optimization of business process costs
    </td>
    <td>
      AG04 Quality of technology-related financial information
    </td>
  </tr>
  <tr>
    <td><strong>Example Metrics for Enterprise Goals</strong></td>
    <td><strong>Example Metrics for Alignment Goals</strong></td>
  </tr>
  <tr>
    <td>
      EG04
      <ul>
        <li>a. Satisfaction survey of key stakeholders regarding the transparency, understanding and accuracy of enterprise financial information</li>
        <li>b. Cost of noncompliance with finance-related regulations</li>
      </ul>
    </td>
    <td>
      AG04
      <ul>
        <li>a. Satisfaction of key stakeholders regarding the level of transparency, understanding and accuracy of I&T financial information</li>
        <li>b. Percent of I&T services with defined and approved operational costs and expected benefits</li>
      </ul>
    </td>
  </tr>
  <tr>
    <td>
      EG07
      <ul>
        <li>a. Degree of board and executive management satisfaction with decision-making information</li>
        <li>b. Number of incidents caused by incorrect business decisions based on inaccurate information</li>
        <li>c. Time to provide information supporting effective business decisions</li>
        <li>d. Timeliness of management information</li>
      </ul>
    </td>
    <td></td>
  </tr>
  <tr>
    <td>
      EG09
      <ul>
        <li>a. Ratio of cost vs. achieved service levels</li>
        <li>b. Satisfaction levels of board and executive management with business processing costs</li>
      </ul>
    </td>
    <td></td>
  </tr>
</table>

### A. Component: Process

<table>
  <tr>
    <td><strong>Management Practice</strong></td>
    <td><strong>Example Metrics</strong></td>
  </tr>
  <tr>
    <td>
      **BAI09.01 Identify and record current assets.**<br>
      Maintain an up-to-date, accurate record of all I&T assets that are required to deliver services and that are owned or controlled by the organization with an expectation of future benefit (including resources with economic value, such as hardware or software). Ensure alignment with configuration management and financial management.
    </td>
    <td>
      <ul>
        <li>a. Percent of assets accurately recorded in asset register</li>
        <li>b. Percent of assets that are fit for purpose</li>
        <li>c. Percent of assets inventoried and kept current</li>
      </ul>
    </td>
  </tr>
</table>

<table>
  <tr>
    <td><strong>Activities</strong></td>
    <td><strong>Capability Level</strong></td>
  </tr>
  <tr>
    <td>
      1. Identify all owned assets in an asset register that records current status. Assets are reported on the balance sheet; they are bought or created to increase the value of a firm or benefit the enterprise’s operations (e.g., hardware and software). Identify all owned assets and maintain alignment with the change management and configuration management processes, the configuration management system, and the financial accounting records.<br><br>
      2. Identify legal, regulatory or contractual requirements that need to be addressed when managing the asset.<br><br>
      3. Verify that the assets are fit for purpose (i.e., in a useful condition).<br><br>
      4. Ensure accounting for all assets.<br><br>
      5. Verify the existence of all owned assets by performing regular physical and logical inventory checks and reconciliation. Include the use of software discovery tools.<br><br>
      6. Determine on a regular basis whether each asset continues to provide value. If so, estimate the expected useful life for delivering value.
    </td>
    <td>
      2<br><br>
      3<br><br>
      4
    </td>
  </tr>
</table>

&lt;page_number&gt;209&lt;/page_number&gt;

## Page 210

# COBIT® 2019 FRAMEWORK: GOVERNANCE AND MANAGEMENT OBJECTIVES

## A. Component: Process (cont.)

<table>
<thead>
<tr>
<th>Related Guidance (Standards, Frameworks, Compliance Requirements)</th>
<th>Detailed Reference</th>
</tr>
</thead>
<tbody>
<tr>
<td>CMMI Cybermaturity Platform, 2018</td>
<td>RI.AD Asset Discovery & Identification</td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td>ISF, The Standard of Good Practice for Information Security 2016</td>
<td>BA1.1 Business Application Register</td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td>ISO/IEC 27002:2013/Cor.2:2015(E)</td>
<td>8.1 Responsibility for assets</td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td>National Institute of Standards and Technology Special Publication 800-53, Revision 5 (Draft), August 2017</td>
<td>3.13 Physical and environmental protection (PE-9)</td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td>The CIS Critical Security Controls for Effective Cyber Defense Version 6.1, August 2016</td>
<td>CSC 1: Inventory of Authorized and Unauthorized Devices;<br>CSC 2: Inventory of Authorized and Unauthorized Software</td>
</tr>
</tbody>
</table>

<table>
<thead>
<tr>
<th>Management Practice</th>
<th>Example Metrics</th>
</tr>
</thead>
<tbody>
<tr>
<td><strong>BAI09.02 Manage critical assets.</strong><br>Identify assets that are critical in providing service capability. Maximize their reliability and availability to support business needs.</td>
<td>a. Number of critical assets<br>b. Average downtime per critical asset<br>c. Number of incident trends identified</td>
</tr>
</tbody>
</table>

<table>
<thead>
<tr>
<th>Activities</th>
<th>Capability Level</th>
</tr>
</thead>
<tbody>
<tr>
<td>1. Identify assets that are critical in providing service capability by referencing requirements in service definitions, SLAs and the configuration management system.</td>
<td>2</td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td>2. On a regular basis, consider the risk of failure or need for replacement of each critical asset.</td>
<td></td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td>3. Communicate to affected customers and users the expected impact (e.g., performance restrictions) of maintenance activities.</td>
<td></td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td>4. Incorporate planned downtime in an overall production schedule. Schedule the maintenance activities to minimize the adverse impact on business processes.</td>
<td>3</td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td>5. Maintain the resilience of critical assets by applying regular preventive maintenance. Monitor performance and, if required, provide alternative and/or additional assets to minimize the likelihood of failure.</td>
<td></td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td>6. Establish a preventive maintenance plan for all hardware, considering cost/benefit analysis, vendor recommendations, risk of outage, qualified personnel and other relevant factors.</td>
<td></td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td>7. Establish maintenance agreements involving third-party access to organizational I&T facilities for on-site and off-site activities (e.g., outsourcing). Establish formal service contracts containing or referring to all necessary security and privacy conditions, including access authorization procedures, to ensure compliance with the organizational security/privacy policies and standards.</td>
<td></td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td>8. Ensure that remote access services and user profiles (or other means used for maintenance or diagnosis) are active only when required.</td>
<td></td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td>9. Monitor performance of critical assets by examining incident trends. Where necessary, take action to repair or replace.</td>
<td>4</td>
</tr>
</tbody>
</table>

<table>
<thead>
<tr>
<th>Related Guidance (Standards, Frameworks, Compliance Requirements)</th>
<th>Detailed Reference</th>
</tr>
</thead>
<tbody>
<tr>
<td>National Institute of Standards and Technology Framework for Improving Critical Infrastructure Cybersecurity v1.1, April 2018</td>
<td>ID.AM Asset Management</td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td>National Institute of Standards and Technology Special Publication 800-53, Revision 5 (Draft), August 2017</td>
<td>3.13 Physical and environmental protection (PE-20)</td>
</tr>
</tbody>
</table>

<table>
<thead>
<tr>
<th>Management Practice</th>
<th>Example Metrics</th>
</tr>
</thead>
<tbody>
<tr>
<td><strong>BAI09.03 Manage the asset life cycle.</strong><br>Manage assets from procurement to disposal. Ensure that assets are utilized as effectively and efficiently as possible and are accounted for and physically protected until appropriately retired.</td>
<td>a. Percent of assets managed from procurement to disposal<br>b. Utilization percentage per asset<br>c. Percent of assets deployed following the standard implementation life cycle</td>
</tr>
</tbody>
</table>

&lt;page_number&gt;210&lt;/page_number&gt;

## Page 211

CHAPTER 4
COBIT GOVERNANCE AND MANAGEMENT OBJECTIVES—DETAILED GUIDANCE

A. Component: Process (cont.)

<table>
<thead>
<tr>
<th>Activities</th>
<th>Capability Level</th>
</tr>
</thead>
<tbody>
<tr>
<td>1. Procure all assets based on approved requests and in accordance with the enterprise procurement policies and practices.</td>
<td>2</td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td>2. Source, receive, verify, test and record all assets in a controlled manner, including physical labeling as required.</td>
<td></td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td>3. Approve payments and complete the process with suppliers according to agreed contract conditions.</td>
<td></td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td>4. Deploy assets following the standard implementation life cycle, including change management and acceptance testing.</td>
<td>3</td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td>5. Allocate assets to users, with acceptance of responsibilities and sign-off, as appropriate.</td>
<td></td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td>6. Whenever possible, reallocate assets when they are no longer required due to a change of user role, redundancy within a service, or retirement of a service.</td>
<td></td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td>7. Plan, authorize and implement retirement-related activities, retaining appropriate records to meet ongoing business and regulatory needs.</td>
<td></td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td>8. Dispose of assets securely, considering, for example, the permanent deletion of any recorded data on media devices and potential damage to the environment.</td>
<td></td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td>9. Dispose of assets responsibly when they serve no useful purpose due to retirement of all related services, obsolete technology or lack of users with regard to environmental impact.</td>
<td>4</td>
</tr>
</tbody>
</table>

<table>
<thead>
<tr>
<th>Related Guidance (Standards, Frameworks, Compliance Requirements)</th>
<th>Detailed Reference</th>
</tr>
</thead>
<tbody>
<tr>
<td>CMMI Cybermaturity Platform, 2018</td>
<td>DP.ML Manage Asset Lifecycle</td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td>ISF, The Standard of Good Practice for Information Security 2016</td>
<td>IM2.1 Document Management; PA1.1 Hardware Life Cycle Management</td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td>ITIL V3, 2011</td>
<td>Service Transition, 4.3 Service Asset and Configuration Management</td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td>National Institute of Standards and Technology Framework for Improving Critical Infrastructure Cybersecurity v1.1, April 2018</td>
<td>PR.MA Maintenance</td>
</tr>
</tbody>
</table>

<table>
<thead>
<tr>
<th>Management Practice</th>
<th>Example Metrics</th>
</tr>
</thead>
<tbody>
<tr>
<td>BAI09.04 Optimize asset value.<br>Regularly review the overall asset base to identify ways to optimize value in alignment with business needs.</td>
<td>a. Benchmark costs<br>b. Number of assets not utilized</td>
</tr>
</tbody>
</table>

<table>
<thead>
<tr>
<th>Activities</th>
<th>Capability Level</th>
</tr>
</thead>
<tbody>
<tr>
<td>1. On a regular basis, review the overall asset base, considering whether it is aligned with business requirements.</td>
<td>3</td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td>2. Assess maintenance costs, consider reasonableness, and identify lower-cost options. Include, where necessary, replacement with new alternatives.</td>
<td>4</td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td>3. Review warranties and consider value-for-money and replacement strategies to determine lowest-cost options.</td>
<td>5</td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td>4. Use capacity and utilization statistics to identify underutilized or redundant assets that could be considered for disposal or replacement to reduce costs.</td>
<td></td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td>5. Review the overall base to identify opportunities for standardization, single sourcing, and other strategies that may lower procurement, support and maintenance costs.</td>
<td></td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td>6. Review the overall state to identify opportunities to leverage emerging technologies or alternative sourcing strategies to reduce costs or increase value-for-money.</td>
<td></td>
</tr>
</tbody>
</table>

<table>
<thead>
<tr>
<th>Related Guidance (Standards, Frameworks, Compliance Requirements)</th>
<th>Detailed Reference</th>
</tr>
</thead>
<tbody>
<tr>
<td>No related guidance for this management practice</td>
<td></td>
</tr>
</tbody>
</table>

<table>
<thead>
<tr>
<th>Management Practice</th>
<th>Example Metrics</th>
</tr>
</thead>
<tbody>
<tr>
<td>BAI09.05 Manage licenses.<br>Manage software licenses to maintain the optimal number of licenses and support business requirements. Ensure that the number of licenses owned is sufficient to cover the installed software in use.</td>
<td>a. Percent of used licenses against purchased licenses<br>b. Percent of licenses still being paid for but not being used<br>c. Percent of products and licenses that should be upgraded to achieve better value</td>
</tr>
</tbody>

&lt;page_number&gt;211&lt;/page_number&gt;

## Page 212

# COBIT® 2019 FRAMEWORK: GOVERNANCE AND MANAGEMENT OBJECTIVES

**A. Component: Process (cont.)**

<table>
<thead>
<tr>
<th>Activities</th>
<th>Capability Level</th>
</tr>
</thead>
<tbody>
<tr>
<td>1. Maintain a register of all purchased software licenses and associated license agreements.</td>
<td>2</td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td>2. On a regular basis, conduct an audit to identify all instances of installed licensed software.</td>
<td>3</td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td>3. Compare the number of installed software instances with the number of licenses owned. Ensure that the license compliance measurement method is compliant with the license and contractual requirements.</td>
<td>4</td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td>4. When instances are lower than the number owned, decide whether there is a need to retain or terminate licenses, considering the potential to save on unnecessary maintenance, training and other costs.</td>
<td></td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td>5. When instances are higher than the number owned, consider first the opportunity to uninstall instances that are no longer required or justified, and then, if necessary, purchase additional licenses to comply with the license agreement.</td>
<td></td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td>6. On a regular basis, consider whether better value can be obtained by upgrading products and associated licenses.</td>
<td>5</td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td><strong>Related Guidance (Standards, Frameworks, Compliance Requirements)</strong></td>
<td><strong>Detailed Reference</strong></td>
</tr>

<td>No related guidance for this management practice</td>
<td></td>
</tr>

</table>

<td>Asset management policy</td>

<td></td>
<td></td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td>Intellectual property (IP) policy</td>
<td>Addresses risk related to use, ownership, sale and distribution of the outputs of I&T-related creative endeavors by employees (e.g., software development). Mandates appropriate documentation, level of detail, etc., from inception of work.</td>
<td></td>

</tr>

## Page 215

# CHAPTER 4
## COBIT GOVERNANCE AND MANAGEMENT OBJECTIVES—DETAILED GUIDANCE

<table>
  <tr>
    <td><strong>Domain:</strong> Build, Acquire and Implement<br><strong>Management Objective:</strong> BAI10 – Managed Configuration</td>
    <td><strong>Focus Area:</strong> COBIT Core Model</td>
  </tr>
</table>

### Description
Define and maintain descriptions and relationships among key resources and capabilities required to deliver I&T-enabled services. Include collecting configuration information, establishing baselines, verifying and auditing configuration information, and updating the configuration repository.

### Purpose
Provide sufficient information about service assets to enable the service to be effectively managed. Assess the impact of changes and deal with service incidents.

The management objective supports the achievement of a set of primary enterprise and alignment goals:

<table>
  <tr>
    <td><strong>Enterprise Goals</strong></td>
    <td><strong>Alignment Goals</strong></td>
  </tr>
  <tr>
    <td>
      • EG02 Managed business risk<br>
      • EG06 Business service continuity and availability
    </td>
    <td>
      AG07 Security of information, processing infrastructure and applications, and privacy
    </td>
  </tr>
  <tr>
    <td><strong>Example Metrics for Enterprise Goals</strong></td>
    <td><strong>Example Metrics for Alignment Goals</strong></td>
  </tr>
  <tr>
    <td>
      EG02
      <ul>
        <li>a. Percent of critical business objectives and services covered by risk assessment</li>
        <li>b. Ratio of significant incidents that were not identified in risk assessments vs. total incidents</li>
        <li>c. Frequency of updating risk profile</li>
      </ul>
    </td>
    <td>
      AG07
      <ul>
        <li>a. Number of confidentiality incidents causing financial loss, business disruption or public embarrassment</li>
        <li>b. Number of availability incidents causing financial loss, business disruption or public embarrassment</li>
        <li>c. Number of integrity incidents causing financial loss, business disruption or public embarrassment</li>
      </ul>
    </td>
  </tr>
  <tr>
    <td>
      EG06
      <ul>
        <li>a. Number of customer service or business process interruptions causing significant incidents</li>
        <li>b. Business cost of incidents</li>
        <li>c. Number of business processing hours lost due to unplanned service interruptions</li>
        <li>d. Percent of complaints as a function of committed service availability targets</li>
      </ul>
    </td>
    <td></td>
  </tr>
</table>

### A. Component: Process

<table>
  <tr>
    <td><strong>Management Practice</strong></td>
    <td><strong>Example Metrics</strong></td>
  </tr>
  <tr>
    <td>
      **BAI10.01 Establish and maintain a configuration model.**<br>
      Establish and maintain a logical model of the services, assets, infrastructure and recording of configuration items (CIs), including the relationships among them. Include the CIs considered necessary to manage services effectively and to provide a single, reliable description of the assets in a service.
    </td>
    <td>
      <ul>
        <li>a. Number of stakeholders signing off on the configuration model</li>
        <li>b. Percent of accuracy of relationships of configuration items</li>
      </ul>
    </td>
  </tr>
  <tr>
    <td><strong>Activities</strong></td>
    <td><strong>Capability Level</strong></td>
  </tr>
  <tr>
    <td>
      1. Define and agree on the scope and level of detail for configuration management (i.e., which services, assets and infrastructure configurable items to include).
    </td>
    <td rowspan="2">3</td>
  </tr>
  <tr>
    <td>
      2. Establish and maintain a logical model for configuration management, including information on CI types, attributes, relationship types, relationship attributes and status codes.
    </td>
  </tr>
  <tr>
    <td><strong>Related Guidance (Standards, Frameworks, Compliance Requirements)</strong></td>
    <td><strong>Detailed Reference</strong></td>
  </tr>
  <tr>
    <td>CMMI Data Management Maturity Model, 2014</td>
    <td>Supporting Processes - Configuration Management</td>
  </tr>
  <tr>
    <td>ISF, The Standard of Good Practice for Information Security 2016</td>
    <td>SY1 System Configuration</td>
  </tr>
  <tr>
    <td>ISO/IEC 20000-1:2011(E)</td>
    <td>9.1 Configuration management</td>
  </tr>
  <tr>
    <td>ITIL V3, 2011</td>
    <td>Service Transition, 4.3 Service Asset and Configuration Management</td>
  </tr>
  <tr>
    <td>National Institute of Standards and Technology Special Publication 800-53, Revision 5 (Draft), August 2017</td>
    <td>3.5 Configuration management (CM-6)</td>
  </tr>
</table>

&lt;page_number&gt;215&lt;/page_number&gt;

# COBIT® 2019 FRAMEWORK: GOVERNANCE AND MANAGEMENT OBJECTIVES

## A. Component: Process (cont.)

<table>
  <thead>
    <tr>
      <th>Management Practice</th>
      <th>Example Metrics</th>
    </tr>
  </thead>
  <tbody>
    <tr>
      <td><strong>BAI10.02 Establish and maintain a configuration repository and baseline.</strong><br>Establish and maintain a configuration management repository and create controlled configuration baselines.</td>
      <td>a. Number of configuration items (CIs) listed in the repository<br>b. Percent of accuracy of configuration baselines of a service, application or infrastructure</td>
    </tr>
  </tbody>
</table>

### Activities

<table>
  <thead>
    <tr>
      <th></th>
      <th>Capability Level</th>
    </tr>
  </thead>
  <tbody>
    <tr>
      <td>1. Identify and classify CIs and populate the repository.</td>
      <td>2</td>
    </tr>
    <tr>
      <td>2. Create, review and formally agree on configuration baselines of a service, application or infrastructure.</td>
      <td>3</td>
    </tr>
  </tbody>
</table>

### Related Guidance (Standards, Frameworks, Compliance Requirements)

<table>
  <thead>
    <tr>
      <th></th>
      <th>Detailed Reference</th>
    </tr>
  </thead>
  <tbody>
    <tr>
      <td>CMMI Cybermaturity Platform, 2018</td>
      <td>IP.CB Apply Configuration Baselines</td>
    </tr>
    <tr>
      <td>National Institute of Standards and Technology Special Publication 800-37, Revision 2 (Draft), May 2018</td>
      <td>3.4 Implementation (Task 2)</td>
    </tr>
    <tr>
      <td>National Institute of Standards and Technology Special Publication 800-53, Revision 5 (Draft), August 2017</td>
      <td>3.19 System and service acquisition (SA-10)</td>
    </tr>
  </tbody>
</table>

### Management Practice

**BAI10.03 Maintain and control configuration items.**
Maintain an up-to-date repository of configuration items (CIs) by populating any configuration changes.

### Activities

<table>
  <thead>
    <tr>
      <th></th>
      <th>Capability Level</th>
    </tr>
  </thead>
  <tbody>
    <tr>
      <td>1. Regularly identify all changes to CIs.</td>
      <td>2</td>
    </tr>
    <tr>
      <td>2. To ensure completeness and accuracy, review proposed changes to CIs against the baseline.</td>
      <td></td>
    </tr>
    <tr>
      <td>3. Update configuration details for approved changes to CIs.</td>
      <td></td>
    </tr>
    <tr>
      <td>4. Create, review and formally agree on changes to configuration baselines whenever needed.</td>
      <td>3</td>
    </tr>
  </tbody>
</table>

### Related Guidance (Standards, Frameworks, Compliance Requirements)

<table>
  <thead>
    <tr>
      <th></th>
      <th>Detailed Reference</th>
    </tr>
  </thead>
  <tbody>
    <tr>
      <td>National Institute of Standards and Technology Special Publication 800-53, Revision 5 (Draft), August 2017</td>
      <td>3.5 Configuration management (CM-2)</td>
    </tr>
  </tbody>
</table>

### Management Practice

**BAI10.04 Produce status and configuration reports.**
Define and produce configuration reports on status changes of configuration items.

### Activities

<table>
  <thead>
    <tr>
      <th></th>
      <th>Capability Level</th>
    </tr>
  </thead>
  <tbody>
    <tr>
      <td>1. Identify status changes of CIs and report against the baseline.</td>
      <td>2</td>
    </tr>
    <tr>
      <td>2. Match all configuration changes with approved requests for change to identify any unauthorized changes. Report unauthorized changes to change management.</td>
      <td>3</td>
    </tr>
    <tr>
      <td>3. Identify reporting requirements from all stakeholders, including content, frequency and media. Produce reports according to the identified requirements.</td>
      <td></td>
    </tr>
  </tbody>
</table>

### Related Guidance (Standards, Frameworks, Compliance Requirements)

<table>
  <thead>
    <tr>
      <th></th>
      <th>Detailed Reference</th>
    </tr>
  </thead>
  <tbody>
    <tr>
      <td>National Institute of Standards and Technology Special Publication 800-53, Revision 5 (Draft), August 2017</td>
      <td>3.5 Configuration management (CM-3)</td>
    </tr>
  </tbody>
</table>

### Management Practice

**BAI10.05 Verify and review integrity of the configuration repository.**
Periodically review the configuration repository and verify completeness and correctness against the desired target.

### Example Metrics

<table>
  <thead>
    <tr>
      <th></th>
      <th>Example Metrics</th>
    </tr>
  </thead>
  <tbody>
    <tr>
      <td></td>
      <td>a. Number of deviations between the configuration repository and live configuration<br>b. Number of discrepancies relating to incomplete or missing configuration information</td>
    </tr>
  </tbody>
</table>

&lt;page_number&gt;216&lt;/page_number&gt;

## Page 217

CHAPTER 4
COBIT GOVERNANCE AND MANAGEMENT OBJECTIVES—DETAILED GUIDANCE

A. Component: Process (cont.)

<table>
<thead>
<tr>
<th>Activities</th>
<th>Capability Level</th>
</tr>
</thead>
<tbody>
<tr>
<td>1. Periodically verify live configuration items against the configuration repository by comparing physical and logical configurations and using appropriate discovery tools, as required.</td>
<td>4</td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td>2. Report and review all deviations for approved corrections or action to remove any unauthorized assets.</td>
<td></td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td>3. Periodically verify that all physical configuration items, as defined in the repository, physically exist. Report any deviations to management.</td>
<td></td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td>4. Set and periodically review the target for completeness of the configuration repository based on business need.</td>
<td></td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td>5. Periodically compare the degree of completeness and accuracy against targets and take remedial action, as necessary, to improve the quality of the repository data.</td>
<td>5</td>
</tr>
</tbody>
</table>

      <td>Configuration management policy</td>
      <td>Communicates guidance for establishing and using a comprehensive configuration repository, including all technology components, associated configuration definitions and interdependencies with other technology components. Helps ensure that system and software changes are minimally disruptive to services. Ensures that changes are coordinated among applicable groups, so conflicts or duplication of effort do not occur.</td>
      <td></td>
      <td></td>
    </tr>
  </tbody>
</table>

## Page 221

# CHAPTER 4
## COBIT GOVERNANCE AND MANAGEMENT OBJECTIVES—DETAILED GUIDANCE

<table>
  <tr>
    <td style="vertical-align: top;">
      **Domain:** Build, Acquire and Implement<br>
      **Management objective:** BAI11 – Managed Projects
    </td>
    <td style="vertical-align: top;">
      **Focus Area:** COBIT Core Model
    </td>
  </tr>
</table>

### Description
Manage all projects that are initiated within the enterprise in alignment with enterprise strategy and in a coordinated way based on the standard project management approach. Initiate, plan, control and execute projects, and close with a post-implementation review.

### Purpose
Realize defined project outcomes and reduce the risk of unexpected delays, costs and value erosion by improving communications to and involvement of business and end users. Ensure the value and quality of project deliverables and maximize their contribution to the defined programs and investment portfolio.

The management objective supports the achievement of a set of primary enterprise and alignment goals:

<table>
  <thead>
    <tr>
      <th>Enterprise Goals</th>
      <th>Alignment Goals</th>
    </tr>
  </thead>
  <tbody>
    <tr>
      <td>
        • EG01 Portfolio of competitive products and services<br>
        • EG08 Optimization of internal business process functionality<br>
        • EG12 Managed digital transformation programs
      </td>
      <td>
        • AG03 Realized benefits from I&T-enabled investments and services portfolio<br>
        • AG06 Agility to turn business requirements into operational solutions<br>
        • AG09 Delivering programs on time, on budget and meeting requirements and quality standards
      </td>
    </tr>
  </tbody>
</table>

<table>
  <thead>
    <tr>
      <th>Example Metrics for Enterprise Goals</th>
      <th>Example Metrics for Alignment Goals</th>
    </tr>
  </thead>
  <tbody>
    <tr>
      <td>
        EG01
        <ul>
          <li>a. Percent of products and services that meet or exceed targets in revenues and/or market share</li>
          <li>b. Percent of products and services that meet or exceed customer satisfaction targets</li>
          <li>c. Percent of products and services that provide competitive advantage</li>
          <li>d. Time to market for new products and services</li>
        </ul>
      </td>
      <td>
        AG03
        <ul>
          <li>a. Percent of I&T-enabled investments for which claimed benefits in the business case are met or exceeded</li>
          <li>b. Percent of I&T services for which expected benefits (as stated in service level agreements) are realized</li>
        </ul>
      </td>
    </tr>
    <tr>
      <td>
        EG08
        <ul>
          <li>a. Satisfaction levels of board and executive management with business process capabilities</li>
          <li>b. Satisfaction levels of customers with service delivery capabilities</li>
          <li>c. Satisfaction levels of suppliers with supply chain capabilities</li>
        </ul>
      </td>
      <td>
        AG06
        <ul>
          <li>a. Level of satisfaction of business executives with I&T responsiveness to new requirements</li>
          <li>b. Average time to market for new I&T-related services and applications</li>
          <li>c. Average time to turn strategic I&T objectives into agreed and approved initiatives</li>
          <li>d. Number of critical business processes supported by up-to-date infrastructure and applications</li>
        </ul>
      </td>
    </tr>
    <tr>
      <td>
        EG12
        <ul>
          <li>a. Number of programs on time and within budget</li>
          <li>b. Percent of stakeholders satisfied with program delivery</li>
          <li>c. Percent of business transformation programs stopped</li>
          <li>d. Percent of business transformation programs with regular reported status updates</li>
        </ul>
      </td>
      <td>
        AG09
        <ul>
          <li>a. Number of programs/projects on time and within budget</li>
          <li>b. Number of programs needing significant rework due to quality defects</li>
          <li>c. Percent of stakeholders satisfied with program/project quality</li>
        </ul>
      </td>
    </tr>
  </tbody>
</table>

&lt;page_number&gt;221&lt;/page_number&gt;

## Page 222

# COBIT® 2019 FRAMEWORK: GOVERNANCE AND MANAGEMENT OBJECTIVES

<table>
  <thead>
    <tr>
      <th colspan="3">A. Component: Process</th>
    </tr>
  </thead>
  <tbody>
    <tr>
      <td><strong>Management Practice</strong></td>
      <td><strong>Example Metrics</strong></td>
      <td></td>
    </tr>
    <tr>
      <td><strong>BAI11.01 Maintain a standard approach for project management.</strong><br>Maintain a standard approach for project management that enables governance and management review, decision-making and delivery-management activities. These activities should focus consistently on business value and goals (i.e., requirements, risk, costs, schedule and quality targets).</td>
      <td>a. Percent of successful projects based on the defined standard approach<br>b. Number of updates to project management approach, good practices, tools and templates</td>
      <td></td>
    </tr>
    <tr>
      <td><strong>Activities</strong></td>
      <td></td>
      <td><strong>Capability Level</strong></td>
    </tr>
    <tr>
      <td>1. Maintain and enforce a standard approach to project management aligned to the enterprise's specific environment and with good practice based on defined process and use of appropriate technology. Ensure that the approach covers the full life cycle and disciplines to be followed, including the management of scope, resources, risk, cost, quality, time, communication, stakeholder involvement, procurement, change control, integration and benefit realization.</td>
      <td></td>
      <td>2</td>
    </tr>
    <tr>
      <td>2. Provide appropriate project management training and consider certification for project managers.</td>
      <td></td>
      <td></td>
    </tr>
    <tr>
      <td>3. Put in place a project management office (PMO) that maintains the standard approach for program and project management across the organization. The PMO supports all projects by creating and maintaining required project documentation templates, providing training and best practices for project managers, tracking metrics on the use of best practices for project management, etc. In some cases, the PMO may also report on project progress to senior management and/or stakeholders, help prioritize projects, and ensure all projects support the overall business objectives of the enterprise.</td>
      <td></td>
      <td>3</td>
    </tr>
    <tr>
      <td>4. Evaluate lessons learned on the use of the project management approach. Update the good practices, tools and templates accordingly.</td>
      <td></td>
      <td>4</td>
    </tr>
    <tr>
      <td><strong>Related Guidance (Standards, Frameworks, Compliance Requirements)</strong></td>
      <td><strong>Detailed Reference</strong></td>
      <td></td>
    </tr>
    <tr>
      <td>National Institute of Standards and Technology Special Publication 800-53, Revision 5 (Draft), August 2017</td>
      <td>3.15 Program management (PM-2)</td>
      <td></td>
    </tr>
    <tr>
      <td><strong>Management Practice</strong></td>
      <td><strong>Example Metrics</strong></td>
      <td></td>
    </tr>
    <tr>
      <td><strong>BAI11.02 Start up and initiate a project.</strong><br>Define and document the nature and scope of the project to confirm and develop a common understanding of project scope among stakeholders. The definition should be formally approved by the project sponsors.</td>
      <td>a. Percent of stakeholders approving enterprise need, scope, planned outcome and level of project risk<br>b. Percent of projects in which stakeholders received a clear written statement defining the nature, scope and benefit of the project</td>
      <td></td>
    </tr>
    <tr>
      <td><strong>Activities</strong></td>
      <td></td>
      <td><strong>Capability Level</strong></td>
    </tr>
    <tr>
      <td>1. To create a common understanding of project scope among stakeholders, provide them a clear written statement defining the nature, scope and deliverables of every project.</td>
      <td></td>
      <td>2</td>
    </tr>
    <tr>
      <td>2. Ensure that each project has one or more sponsors with sufficient authority to manage execution of the project within the overall program.</td>
      <td></td>
      <td></td>
    </tr>
    <tr>
      <td>3. Ensure that key stakeholders and sponsors within the enterprise (business and IT) agree on and accept the requirements for the project, including definition of project success (acceptance) criteria and key performance indicators (KPIs).</td>
      <td></td>
      <td></td>
    </tr>
    <tr>
      <td>4. Appoint a dedicated manager for the project. Ensure that the individual has the required understanding of technology and business and the commensurate competencies and skills to manage the project effectively and efficiently.</td>
      <td></td>
      <td></td>
    </tr>
    <tr>
      <td>5. Ensure that the project definition describes the requirements for a project communication plan that identifies internal and external project communications.</td>
      <td></td>
      <td></td>
    </tr>
    <tr>
      <td>6. With the approval of stakeholders, maintain the project definition throughout the project, reflecting changing requirements.</td>
      <td></td>
      <td></td>
    </tr>
    <tr>
      <td>7. To track the execution of a project, put in place mechanisms such as regular reporting and stage-gate, release or phase reviews, to occur in a timely manner and with appropriate approval.</td>
      <td></td>
      <td></td>
    </tr>
    <tr>
      <td><strong>Related Guidance (Standards, Frameworks, Compliance Requirements)</strong></td>
      <td><strong>Detailed Reference</strong></td>
      <td></td>
    </tr>
    <tr>
      <td>PMBOK Guide Sixth Edition, 2017</td>
      <td>Part 1: 4.1 Develop project charter; Part 1: 6. Project schedule management</td>
      <td></td>
    </tr>
  </tbody>
</table>

CHAPTER 4
COBIT GOVERNANCE AND MANAGEMENT OBJECTIVES—DETAILED GUIDANCE

A. Component: Process (cont.)

<table>
<thead>
<tr>
<th>Management Practice</th>
<th>Example Metrics</th>
</tr>
</thead>
<tbody>
<tr>
<td><strong>BAI11.03 Manage stakeholder engagement.</strong><br>Manage stakeholder engagement to ensure an active exchange of accurate, consistent and timely information that reaches all relevant stakeholders. This includes planning, identifying and engaging stakeholders and managing their expectations.</td>
<td>a. Level of stakeholder satisfaction with involvement<br>b. Percent of stakeholders effectively engaged</td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td><strong>Activities</strong></td>
<td></td>
<td><strong>Capability Level</strong></td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td>1. Plan how stakeholders inside and outside the enterprise will be identified, analyzed, engaged and managed through the life cycle of the project.</td>
<td></td>
<td>3</td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td>2. Identify, engage and manage stakeholders by establishing and maintaining appropriate levels of co-ordination, communication and liaison to ensure they are involved in the project.</td>
<td></td>
<td></td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td>3. Analyze stakeholder interests, requirements and engagement. Take remedial actions as required.</td>
<td></td>
<td>4</td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td><strong>Related Guidance (Standards, Frameworks, Compliance Requirements)</strong></td>
<td><strong>Detailed Reference</strong></td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td>PMBOK Guide Sixth Edition, 2017</td>
<td>Part 1: 13. Project stakeholder management<br>Part 1: 10. Project communications management</td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td><strong>Management Practice</strong></td>
<td><strong>Example Metrics</strong></td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td><strong>BAI11.04 Develop and maintain the project plan.</strong><br>Establish and maintain a formal, approved, integrated project plan (covering business and IT resources) to guide project execution and control throughout the life of the project. The scope of projects should be clearly defined and tied to building or enhancing business capability.</td>
<td>a. Percent of active projects undertaken without valid and updated project value maps<br>b. Percent of milestone or task completion vs. plan</td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td><strong>Activities</strong></td>
<td></td>
<td><strong>Capability Level</strong></td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td>1. Develop a project plan that provides information to enable management to control project progress progressively. The plan should include details of project deliverables and acceptance criteria, required internal and external resources and responsibilities, clear work breakdown structures and work packages, estimates of resources required, milestones/release plan/phases, key dependencies, budget and costs, and identification of a critical path.</td>
<td></td>
<td>2</td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td>2. Maintain the project plan and any dependent plans (e.g., risk plan, quality plan, benefits realization plan). Ensure that the plans are up to date and reflect actual progress and approved material changes.</td>
<td></td>
<td></td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td>3. Ensure that there is effective communication of project plans and progress reports. Ensure that any changes made to individual plans are reflected in other plans.</td>
<td></td>
<td></td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td>4. Determine the activities, interdependencies and required collaboration and communication within the project and among multiple projects within a program.</td>
<td></td>
<td></td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td>5. Ensure that each milestone is accompanied by a significant deliverable requiring review and sign-off.</td>
<td></td>
<td></td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td>6. Establish a project baseline (e.g., cost, schedule, scope, quality) that is appropriately reviewed, approved and incorporated into the integrated project plan.</td>
<td></td>
<td></td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td><strong>Related Guidance (Standards, Frameworks, Compliance Requirements)</strong></td>
<td><strong>Detailed Reference</strong></td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td>PMBOK Guide Sixth Edition, 2017</td>
<td>Part 1: 4.2 Develop project management plan</td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td><strong>Management Practice</strong></td>
<td><strong>Example Metrics</strong></td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td><strong>BAI11.05 Manage project quality.</strong><br>Prepare and execute a quality management plan, processes and practices that align with quality management standards (QMS). Describe the approach to project quality and implementation. The plan should be formally reviewed and agreed on by all parties concerned and incorporated into the integrated project plans.</td>
<td>a. Percent of build-to-products without errors<br>b. Number of cancelled projects</td>
</tr>
</tbody>
</table>

## Page 224

# COBIT® 2019 FRAMEWORK: GOVERNANCE AND MANAGEMENT OBJECTIVES

**A. Component: Process (cont.)**

<table>
<thead>
<tr>
<th>Activities</th>
<th>Capability Level</th>
</tr>
</thead>
<tbody>
<tr>
<td>1. To provide quality assurance for the project deliverables, identify ownership and responsibilities, quality review processes, success criteria and performance metrics.</td>
<td>2</td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td>2. Identify assurance tasks and practices required to support the accreditation of new or modified systems during project planning. Include them in the integrated plans. Ensure that the tasks provide assurance that internal controls and security and privacy solutions meet the defined requirements.</td>
<td>3</td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td>3. Define any requirements for independent validation and verification of the quality of deliverables in the plan.</td>
<td></td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td>4. Perform quality assurance and control activities in accordance with the quality management plan and QMS.</td>
<td></td>
</tr>
</tbody>
</table>

<table>
<thead>
<tr>
<th>Related Guidance (Standards, Frameworks, Compliance Requirements)</th>
<th>Detailed Reference</th>
</tr>
</thead>
<tbody>
<tr>
<td>PMBOK Guide Sixth Edition, 2017</td>
<td>Part 1: 8. Project quality management</td>
</tr>
</tbody>
</table>

<table>
<thead>
<tr>
<th>Management Practice</th>
<th>Example Metrics</th>
</tr>
</thead>
<tbody>
<tr>
<td>BAI11.06 Manage project risk.<br>Eliminate or minimize specific risk associated with projects through a systematic process of planning, identifying, analyzing, responding to, monitoring and controlling the areas or events with potential to cause unwanted change. Define and record any risk faced by project management.</td>
<td>a. Number of identified delays and issues<br>b. Number of projects with a formal project risk management approach aligned with the ERM framework</td>
</tr>
</tbody>
</table>

<table>
<thead>
<tr>
<th>Activities</th>
<th>Capability Level</th>
</tr>
</thead>
<tbody>
<tr>
<td>1. Establish a formal project risk management approach aligned with the ERM framework. Ensure that the approach includes identifying, analyzing, responding to, mitigating, monitoring and controlling risk.</td>
<td>2</td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td>2. Assign to appropriately skilled personnel the responsibility for executing the enterprise’s project risk management process within a project and ensure that this is incorporated into the solution development practices. Consider allocating this role to an independent team, especially if an objective viewpoint is required or a project is considered critical.</td>
<td></td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td>3. Identify owners for actions to avoid, accept or mitigate risk.</td>
<td></td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td>4. Perform the project risk assessment of identifying and quantifying risk continuously throughout the project. Manage and communicate risk appropriately within the project governance structure.</td>
<td>3</td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td>5. Reassess project risk periodically, including at initiation of each major project phase and as part of major change request assessments.</td>
<td></td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td>6. Maintain and review a project risk register of all potential project risk and a risk mitigation log of all project issues and their resolution. Analyze the log periodically for trends and recurring problems to ensure that root causes are corrected.</td>
<td></td>
</tr>
</tbody>
</table>

<table>
<thead>
<tr>
<th>Related Guidance (Standards, Frameworks, Compliance Requirements)</th>
<th>Detailed Reference</th>
</tr>
</thead>
<tbody>
<tr>
<td>National Institute of Standards and Technology Special Publication 800-53, Revision 5 (Draft), August 2017</td>
<td>3.15 Program management (PM-4)</td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td>PMBOK Guide Sixth Edition, 2017</td>
<td>Part 1: 11. Project risk management</td>
</tr>
</tbody>
</table>

<table>
<thead>
<tr>
<th>Management Practice</th>
<th>Example Metrics</th>
</tr>
</thead>
<tbody>
<tr>
<td>BAI11.07 Monitor and control projects.<br>Measure project performance against key project performance criteria such as schedule, quality, cost and risk. Identify any deviations from expected targets. Assess the impact of deviations on the project and overall program and report results to key stakeholders.</td>
<td>a. Percent of activities aligned to scope and expected outcomes<br>b. Percent of deviations from plan addressed<br>c. Frequency of project status reviews</td>
</tr>
</tbody>
</table>

&lt;page_number&gt;224&lt;/page_number&gt;

## Page 225

CHAPTER 4
COBIT GOVERNANCE AND MANAGEMENT OBJECTIVES—DETAILED GUIDANCE

A. Component: Process (cont.)

<table>
<thead>
<tr>
<th>Activities</th>
<th>Capability Level</th>
</tr>
</thead>
<tbody>
<tr>
<td>1. Establish and use a set of project criteria including, but not limited to, scope, expected business benefit, schedule, quality, cost and level of risk.</td>
<td>2</td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td>2. Report to identified key stakeholders project progress within the project, deviations from established key project performance criteria (such as, but not limited to, the expected business benefits), and potential positive and negative effects on the project.</td>
<td></td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td>3. Document and submit any necessary changes to the project’s key stakeholders for their approval before adoption. Communicate revised criteria to project managers for use in future performance reports.</td>
<td></td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td>4. For the deliverables produced in each iteration, release or project phase, gain approval and sign-off from designated managers and users in the affected business and IT functions.</td>
<td></td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td>5. Base the approval process on clearly defined acceptance criteria agreed on by key stakeholders before work commences on the project phase or iteration deliverable.</td>
<td>3</td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td>6. Assess the project at agreed major stage-gates, releases or iterations. Make formal go/no-go decisions based on predetermined critical success criteria.</td>
<td></td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td>7. Establish and operate a change control system for the project so that all changes to the project baseline (e.g., scope, expected business benefits, schedule, quality, cost, risk level) are appropriately reviewed, approved and incorporated into the integrated project plan in line with the program and project governance framework.</td>
<td></td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td>8. Measure project performance against key project performance criteria. Analyze deviations from established key project performance criteria for cause and assess positive and negative effects on the project.</td>
<td>4</td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td>9. Monitor changes to the project and review existing key project performance criteria to determine whether they still represent valid measures of progress.</td>
<td></td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td>10. Recommend and monitor remedial action, when required, in line with the project governance framework.</td>
<td></td>
</tr>
</tbody>
</table>

<table>
<thead>
<tr>
<th>Related Guidance (Standards, Frameworks, Compliance Requirements)</th>
<th>Detailed Reference</th>
</tr>
</thead>
<tbody>
<tr>
<td>PMBOK Guide Sixth Edition, 2017</td>
<td>Part 1: 4.5 Monitor and control project work</td>
</tr>
</tbody>
</table>

<table>
<thead>
<tr>
<th>Management Practice</th>
<th>Example Metrics</th>
</tr>
</thead>
<tbody>
<tr>
<td>BAI11.08 Manage project resources and work packages.<br>Manage project work packages by placing formal requirements on authorizing and accepting work packages and assigning and coordinating appropriate business and IT resources.</td>
<td>a. Number of resource issues (e.g., skills, capacity)<br>b. Number of clearly defined roles, responsibilities and prerogatives of project manager, assigned staff and other involved parties</td>
</tr>
</tbody>
</table>

<table>
<thead>
<tr>
<th>Activities</th>
<th>Capability Level</th>
</tr>
</thead>
<tbody>
<tr>
<td>1. Identify business and IT resource needs for the project and clearly map appropriate roles and responsibilities, with escalation and decision-making authorities agreed and understood.</td>
<td>2</td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td>2. Identify required skills and time requirements for all individuals involved in the project phases in relation to defined roles. Staff the roles based on available skills information (e.g., IT skills matrix).</td>
<td></td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td>3. Utilize experienced project management and team leader resources with skills appropriate to the size, complexity and risk of the project.</td>
<td></td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td>4. Consider and clearly define the roles and responsibilities of other involved parties, including finance, legal, procurement, HR, internal audit and compliance.</td>
<td></td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td>5. Clearly define and agree on the responsibility for procurement and management of third-party products and services, and manage the relationships.</td>
<td></td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td>6. Identify and authorize the execution of the work according to the project plan.</td>
<td></td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td>7. Identify project plan gaps and provide feedback to the project manager to remediate.</td>
<td></td>
</tr>
</tbody>
</table>

<table>
<thead>
<tr>
<th>Related Guidance (Standards, Frameworks, Compliance Requirements)</th>
<th>Detailed Reference</th>
</tr>
</thead>
<tbody>
<tr>
<td>PMBOK Guide Sixth Edition, 2017</td>
<td>Part 1: 4.3 Direct and manage project work</td>
</tr>
</tbody>
</table>

Build, Acquire and Implement

# COBIT® 2019 FRAMEWORK: GOVERNANCE AND MANAGEMENT OBJECTIVES

## A. Component: Process (cont.)

<table>
<thead>
<tr>
<th>Management Practice</th>
<th>Example Metrics</th>
</tr>
</thead>
<tbody>
<tr>
<td><strong>BAI11.09 Close a project or iteration.</strong><br>At the end of each project, release or iteration, require the project stakeholders to ascertain whether the project, release or iterations delivered the required results in terms of capabilities and contributed as expected to program benefits. Identify and communicate any outstanding activities required to achieve planned results of the project and/or benefits of the program. Identify and document lessons learned for future projects, releases, iterations and programs.</td>
<td>a. Level of stakeholder satisfaction expressed at project closure review<br>b. Percent of outcomes with first-time acceptance</td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td><strong>Activities</strong></td>
<td></td>
<td><strong>CAPABILITY LEVEL</strong></td>

<table>
<thead>
<tr>
<th></th>
<th></th>

</tr>
</thead>
<tbody>
<tr>
<td>1. Obtain stakeholder acceptance of project deliverables and transfer ownership.</td>
<td>2</td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td></td>
<td></td>

</tr>
<tr>

<td>2. Define and apply key steps for project closure, including post-implementation reviews that assess whether a project attained desired results.</td>
<td></th>
<td>3</td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td></td>
<td></td>

</tr>
<tr>

<td>3. Plan and execute post-implementation reviews to determine whether projects delivered expected results. Improve the project management and system development process methodology.</td>
<td></th>
<td></td> 
</tr>
<tr>
<td></td>
<td></td>

</tr>
<tr>

<td>4. Identify, assign, communicate and track any uncompleted activities required to ensure the project delivered the required results in terms of capabilities and the results contributed as expected to the program benefits.</td>
<td></th>
<td></td> 
</tr>
<tr>
<td></td>
<td></td>

</tr>
<tr>

<td>5. Regularly, and upon completion of the project, collect lessons learned from the project participants. Review them and the key activities that led to delivered benefits and value. Analyze the data and make recommendations for improving the current project and the project management method for future projects.</td>
<td></th>
<td>4</td> 
</tr>
</tbody>
</table>
</td>
</tr>

<td>Program/project management policy</td>
<td>Guides management of risk related to programs and projects. Details management position and expectation regarding program and project management. Treats accountability, goals and objectives regarding performance, budget, risk analysis, reporting and mitigation of adverse events during program/project execution.</td>

<tr>
<th>Key Culture Elements</th>
<th>Related Guidance</th>
<th>Detailed Reference</th>
</tr>
</thead>
<tbody>
<tr>
<td>Establish an enterprisewide project management culture that ensures consistent and optimal implementation of project management across the enterprise, taking into account organizational structure and business environment. Ensure that all initiatives are translated into projects (or changes, where minor in scope); ensure that no ad hoc actions occur outside the scope of project management.</td>
<td></td>
<td></td>
</tr>

</table>

## Page 231

# CHAPTER 4
## COBIT GOVERNANCE AND MANAGEMENT OBJECTIVES—DETAILED GUIDANCE

<table>
  <tr>
    <td>
      **Domain:** Deliver, Service and Support<br>
      **Management Objective:** DSS01 - Managed Operations
    </td>
    <td style="text-align: right;">
      **Focus Area:** COBIT Core Model
    </td>

    <tr>
      <td colspan="2">
        <h3>Description</h3>
        Coordinate and execute the activities and operational procedures required to deliver internal and outsourced I&T services. Include the execution of predefined standard operating procedures and the required monitoring activities.
      </td>
    </tr>
    <tr>
      <td colspan="2">
        <h3>Purpose</h3>
        Deliver I&T operational product and service outcomes as planned.
      </td>

      <tr>
        <td colspan="1" style="vertical-align: top;">
          <p>The management objective supports the achievement of a set of primary enterprise and alignment goals:</p>
          <table>
            <tr>
              <td>
                <h3>Enterprise Goals</h3>
                <ul>
                  <li>EG01 Portfolio of competitive products and services</li>
                  <li><b>EG08</b> Optimization of internal business process functionality</li>
                </ul>
              </td>
              <td style="text-align: center; vertical-align: top;">➡️</td>
              <table>
                <tr>
                  <td>
                    <h3>Alignment Goals</h3>
                    <ul>
                      <li>AG05 Delivery of I&T services in line with business requirements</li>
                    </ul>
                  </td>
                </tr>
                <tr style="height: 10px;"></tr>
                <table>
                  <tr>
                    <td>
                      <h3>Example Metrics for Enterprise Goals</h3>
                      <ul>
                        <li>EG01
                          <ol type="a">
                            <li>a. Percent of products and services that meet or exceed targets in revenues and/or market share</li>
                            <li>b. Percent of products &amp; services that meet or exceeds customer satisfaction targets</li>
                            <!-- <li>c. Percent of products that provide competitive advantage</li> -->
                            <li>c. Time to market for new products and services</li>
                          </ol>
                        </li>
                        <li>EG08
                          <ol type="a">
                            <li>a. Satisfaction levels of board and executive management with business process capabilities</li>
                            <li>b. Satisfaction levels of customers with service delivery capabilities</li>
                          </ol>
                        </li>
                      </ul>
                    </td>
                    <td style="text-align: center; vertical-align: top;">➡️</td>
                    <table>
                      <tr>
                        <td>
                          <h3>Example Metrics for Alignment Goals</h3>
                          <ul>
                            <li>AG05
                              <ol type="a">
                                <li>a. Percent of business stakeholders satisfied that I&amp;T service delivery meets agreed service levels</li>
                                <li>b. Number of business disruptions due to I&amp;T incident</li>
                                <!-- <li>c. Percent of users satisfied with the quality of I&amp;T services</li> -->
                              </ol>
                            </li>
                          </ul>
                        </td>
                      </tr>
                    </table>
                  </td>
                </table>
              </td>
            </tr>
          </table>
        </td>
      </tr>
    </tr>
  </tr>
</table>

### A. Component: Process

<table>
  <tr>
    <td>
      <h3>Management Practice</h3>
      <p><b>DSS01.01 Perform operational procedures.</b><br>Maintain and perform operational procedures and operational tasks reliably and consistently.</p>
    </td>
    <td style="text-align: right;">
      <h3>Example Metrics</h3>
      <ul>
        <li>a. Number of incidents caused by operational problems</li>
        <li>b. Number of nonstandard operational procedures executed</li>
      </ul>
    </td>
  </tr>
  <tr>
    <td>
      <h3>Activities</h3>
      <ol>
        <li>Develop and maintain operational procedures and related activities to support all delivered services.</li>
        <li>Maintain a schedule of operational activities and perform the activities.</li>
        <!-- <li>Verify that all data expected for processing are received and processed completely, accurately and in a timely manner. Deliver output in accordance with enterprise requirements. Support restart and reprocessing needs. Ensure that users are receiving the right outputs in a secure and timely manner.</li> -->
        <li>Manage the performance and throughput of the scheduled activities.</li>
        <li>Monitor incidents and problems dealing with operational procedures and take appropriate action to improve reliability of operational tasks performed.</li>
      </ol>
    </td>
    <td style="text-align: right;">
      <h3>Capability Level</h3>
      <ul>
        <li>2</li>
        <li></li>
        <ul>
          <li>3</li>
        </ul>
        <ul style="list-style-type: none;">
          <li>4</li>
        ul>
        <ul><li>5</li></ul>
      </ul>
    </td>
  </tr>
  <tr>
    <td>
      <h3>Related Guidance (Standards, Frameworks, Compliance Requirements)</h3>
      <ul>
        <li>CMMI Cybermaturity Platform, 2018</li>
        <li>HITRUST CSF version 9, September 2017</li>
        <!-- <li>ISO/IEC 27002:2013/Cor.2:2005(E)</li> -->
        <li>ITIL V3, 2011</li>
        <li>National Institute of Standards and Technology Special Publication 800-53, Revision 5 (Draft), August 2017</li>
      </ul>
    </td>
    <td style="text-align: right;">
      <h3>Detailed Reference</h3>
      <ul>
        <li>TP.SE Safeguard Operational Environment</li>
        <li><u>09.01 Document Operating Procedures</u></li>
        <ul>
          <li>12.1 Operational procedures and responsibilities</li>
        </ul>
        <ul style="list-style-type: none;">
          <li>Service Operation, 4.1 Event Management</li>
        ul>
        <ul><li>3.13 Physical and environmental protection (PE-13, PE-14, PE-15)</li></ul>
      </ul>
    </td>
  </tr>
</table>

## Page 232

# COBIT® 2019 FRAMEWORK: GOVERNANCE AND MANAGEMENT OBJECTIVES

<table>
  <thead>
    <tr>
      <th colspan="3">A. Component: Process (cont.)</th>
    </tr>
  </thead>
  <tbody>
    <tr>
      <td><strong>Management Practice</strong></td>
      <td><strong>Example Metrics</strong></td>
      <td></td>
    </tr>
    <tr>
      <td><strong>DSS01.02 Manage outsourced I&amp;T services.</strong><br>Manage the operation of outsourced I&amp;T services to maintain the protection of enterprise information and reliability of service delivery.</td>
      <td>a. Number of specific/smart KPIs included in outsourcing contracts<br>b. Frequency of failure by outsourcing partner to meet KPIs</td>
      <td></td>
    </tr>
    <tr>
      <td><strong>Activities</strong></td>
      <td></td>
      <td><strong>Capability Level</strong></td>
    </tr>
    <tr>
      <td>1. Ensure that the enterprise's requirements for security of information processes adhere to contracts and SLAs with third parties hosting or providing services.</td>
      <td></td>
      <td>3</td>
    </tr>
    <tr>
      <td>2. Ensure that the enterprise's operational business and IT processing requirements and priorities for service delivery adhere to contracts and SLAs with third parties hosting or providing services.</td>
      <td></td>
      <td></td>
    </tr>
    <tr>
      <td>3. Integrate critical internal IT management processes with those of outsourced service providers. This should cover, for example, performance and capacity planning, change management, configuration management, service request and incident management, problem management, security management, business continuity, and the monitoring of process performance and reporting.</td>
      <td></td>
      <td></td>
    </tr>
    <tr>
      <td>4. Plan for independent audit and assurance of the operational environments of outsourced providers to confirm that agreed requirements are being adequately addressed.</td>
      <td></td>
      <td>4</td>
    </tr>
    <tr>
      <td><strong>Related Guidance (Standards, Frameworks, Compliance Requirements)</strong></td>
      <td><strong>Detailed Reference</strong></td>
      <td></td>
    </tr>
    <tr>
      <td>ISF, The Standard of Good Practice for Information Security 2016</td>
      <td>SC1.2 Outsourcing</td>
      <td></td>
    </tr>
    <tr>
      <td>ISO/IEC 20000-1:2011(E)</td>
      <td>4.2 Governance of processes operated by other parties</td>
      <td></td>
    </tr>
    <tr>
      <td><strong>Management Practice</strong></td>
      <td><strong>Example Metrics</strong></td>
      <td></td>
    </tr>
    <tr>
      <td><strong>DSS01.03 Monitor I&amp;T infrastructure.</strong><br>Monitor the I&amp;T infrastructure and related events. Store sufficient chronological information in operations logs to reconstruct and review time sequences of operations and other activities surrounding or supporting operations.</td>
      <td>a. Percent of critical operational event types covered by automatic detection systems<br>b. Percent of infrastructure assets monitored based on service criticality and the relationship between configuration items and services that depend on them</td>
      <td></td>
    </tr>
    <tr>
      <td><strong>Activities</strong></td>
      <td></td>
      <td><strong>Capability Level</strong></td>
    </tr>
    <tr>
      <td>1. Log events. Identify the level of information to be recorded, based on a consideration of risk and performance.</td>
      <td></td>
      <td>2</td>
    </tr>
    <tr>
      <td>2. Identify and maintain a list of infrastructure assets that need to be monitored, based on service criticality and the relationship between configuration items and services that depend on them.</td>
      <td></td>
      <td>3</td>
    </tr>
    <tr>
      <td>3. Define and implement rules that identify and record threshold breaches and event conditions. Find a balance between generating spurious minor events and significant events so event logs are not overloaded with unnecessary information.</td>
      <td></td>
      <td></td>
    </tr>
    <tr>
      <td>4. Produce event logs and retain them for an appropriate period to assist in future investigations.</td>
      <td></td>
      <td></td>
    </tr>
    <tr>
      <td>5. Ensure that incident tickets are created in a timely manner when monitoring identified deviations from defined thresholds.</td>
      <td></td>
      <td></td>
    </tr>
    <tr>
      <td>6. Establish procedures for monitoring event logs. Conduct regular reviews.</td>
      <td></td>
      <td>4</td>
    </tr>
    <tr>
      <td><strong>Related Guidance (Standards, Frameworks, Compliance Requirements)</strong></td>
      <td><strong>Detailed Reference</strong></td>
      <td></td>
    </tr>
    <tr>
      <td>National Institute of Standards and Technology Special Publication 800-53, Revision 5 (Draft), August 2017</td>
      <td>3.10 Maintenance (MA-2, MA-3)</td>
      <td></td>
    </tr>
    <tr>
      <td><strong>Management Practice</strong></td>
      <td><strong>Example Metrics</strong></td>
      <td></td>
    </tr>
    <tr>
      <td><strong>DSS01.04 Manage the environment.</strong><br>Maintain measures for protection against environmental factors. Install specialized equipment and devices to monitor and control the environment.</td>
      <td>a. Number of people trained to respond to environmental alarm procedures<br>b. Number of risk scenarios defined for environmental threats</td>
      <td></td>
    </tr>
  </tbody>
</table>
