## Page 233

CHAPTER 4
COBIT GOVERNANCE AND MANAGEMENT OBJECTIVES—DETAILED GUIDANCE

A. Component: Process (cont.)

<table>
<thead>
<tr>
<th>Activities</th>
<th>Capability Level</th>
</tr>
</thead>
<tbody>
<tr>
<td>1. Identify natural and man-made disasters that might occur in the area where the IT facilities are located. Assess the potential effect on the IT facilities.</td>
<td>2</td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td>2. Identify how I&T equipment, including mobile and off-site equipment, is protected against environmental threats. Ensure that the policy limits or excludes eating, drinking and smoking in sensitive areas, and prohibits storage of stationery and other supplies that pose a fire hazard within computer rooms.</td>
<td></td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td>3. Keep the IT sites and server rooms clean and in a safe condition at all times (i.e., no mess, no paper or cardboard boxes, no filled dustbins, no flammable chemicals or materials).</td>
<td></td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td>4. Situate and construct IT facilities to minimize and mitigate susceptibility to environmental threats (e.g., theft, air, fire, smoke, water, vibration, terror, vandalism, chemicals, explosives). Consider specific security zones and/or fireproof cells (e.g., locating production and development environments/servers away from each other).</td>
<td>3</td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td>5. Compare measures and contingency plans against insurance policy requirements and report results. Address points of noncompliance in a timely manner.</td>
<td></td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td>6. Respond to environmental alarms and other notifications. Document and test procedures, which should include prioritization of alarms and contact with local emergency response authorities. Train personnel in these procedures.</td>
<td></td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td>7. Regularly monitor and maintain devices that proactively detect environmental threats (e.g., fire, water, smoke, humidity).</td>
<td>4</td>
</tr>
</tbody>
</table>

<table>
<thead>
<tr>
<th>Related Guidance (Standards, Frameworks, Compliance Requirements)</th>
<th>Detailed Reference</th>
</tr>
</thead>
<tbody>
<tr>
<td>National Institute of Standards and Technology Special Publication 800-37, Revision 2 (Draft), May 2018</td>
<td>2.1 System and system elements; 3.2 Categorization (Task 5, 6)</td>
</tr>
</tbody>
</table>

<table>
<thead>
<tr>
<th>Management Practice</th>
<th>Example Metrics</th>
</tr>
</thead>
<tbody>
<tr>
<td>DSS01.05 Manage facilities.<br>Manage facilities, including power and communications equipment, in line with laws and regulations, technical and business requirements, vendor specifications, and health and safety guidelines.</td>
<td>a. Time since last test of uninterruptible power supply<br>b. Number of people trained on health and safety guidelines</td>
</tr>
</tbody>
</table>

<table>
<thead>
<tr>
<th>Activities</th>
<th>Capability Level</th>
</tr>
</thead>
<tbody>
<tr>
<td>1. Examine the IT facilities’ requirement for protection against power fluctuations and outages, in conjunction with other business continuity planning requirements. Procure suitable uninterruptible supply equipment (e.g., batteries, generators) to support business continuity planning.</td>
<td>2</td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td>2. Regularly test the uninterruptible power supply’s mechanisms. Ensure that power can be switched to the supply without any significant effect on business operations.</td>
<td></td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td>3. Ensure that the facilities housing the I&T systems have more than one source for dependent utilities (e.g., power, telecommunications, water, gas). Separate the physical entrance of each utility.</td>
<td></td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td>4. Confirm that cabling external to the IT site is located underground or has suitable alternative protection. Determine that cabling within the IT site is contained within secured conduits, and access to wiring cabinets is restricted to authorized personnel. Properly protect cabling against damage caused by fire, smoke, water, interception and interference.</td>
<td></td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td>5. Ensure that cabling and physical patching (data and phone) are structured and organized. Cabling and conduit structures should be documented (e.g., blueprint building plan and wiring diagrams).</td>
<td></td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td>6. On regular basis, educate personnel on health and safety laws, regulations, and relevant guidelines. Educate personnel on fire and rescue drills to ensure knowledge and actions taken in case of fire or similar incidents.</td>
<td></td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td>7. Ensure that IT sites and equipment are maintained according to the supplier’s recommended service intervals and specifications. Ensure that maintenance is carried out only by authorized personnel.</td>
<td>3</td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td>8. Analyze the facilities housing’s high-availability systems for redundancy and fail-over cabling requirements (external and internal).</td>
<td></td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td>9. Ensure that IT sites and facilities are in ongoing compliance with relevant health and safety laws, regulations, guidelines, and vendor specifications.</td>
<td></td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td>10. Record, monitor, manage and resolve facilities incidents in line with the I&T incident management process. Make available reports on facilities incidents for which disclosure is required by laws and regulations.</td>
<td>4</td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td>11. Analyze physical alterations to IT sites or premises to reassess the environmental risk (e.g., fire or water damage). Report results of this analysis to business continuity and facilities management.</td>
<td></td>
</tr>
</tbody>
</table>

<table>
<thead>
<tr>
<th>Related Guidance (Standards, Frameworks, Compliance Requirements)</th>
<th>Detailed Reference</th>
</tr>
</thead>
<tbody>
<tr>
<td>No related guidance for this management practice</td>
<td></td>
</tr>
</tbody>
</table>

## Page 237

# CHAPTER 4
## COBIT GOVERNANCE AND MANAGEMENT OBJECTIVES—DETAILED GUIDANCE

<table>
  <tr>
    <td><strong>Domain:</strong> Deliver, Service and Support<br><strong>Management Objective:</strong> DSS02 - Managed Service Requests and Incidents</td>
    <td><strong>Focus Area:</strong> COBIT Core Model</td>
  </tr>
</table>

### Description
Provide timely and effective response to user requests and resolution of all types of incidents. Restore normal service; record and fulfil user requests; and record, investigate, diagnose, escalate and resolve incidents.

### Purpose
Achieve increased productivity and minimize disruptions through quick resolution of user queries and incidents. Assess the impact of changes and deal with service incidents. Resolve user requests and restore service in response to incidents.

The management objective supports the achievement of a set of primary enterprise and alignment goals:

<table>
  <tr>
    <td><strong>Enterprise Goals</strong></td>
    <td><strong>Alignment Goals</strong></td>
  </tr>
  <tr>
    <td>
      • EG01 Portfolio of competitive products and services<br>
      • EG08 Optimization of internal business process functionality
    </td>
    <td>
      AG05 Delivery of I&T services in line with business requirements
    </td>
  </tr>
  <tr>
    <td><strong>Example Metrics for Enterprise Goals</strong></td>
    <td><strong>Example Metrics for Alignment Goals</strong></td>
  </tr>
  <tr>
    <td>
      EG01
      <ul>
        <li>a. Percent of products and services that meet or exceed targets in revenues and/or market share</li>
        <li>b. Percent of products and services that meet or exceed customer satisfaction targets</li>
        <li>c. Percent of products and services that provide competitive advantage</li>
        <li>d. Time to market for new products and services</li>
      </ul>
    </td>
    <td>
      AG05
      <ul>
        <li>a. Percent of business stakeholders satisfied that I&T service delivery meets agreed service levels</li>
        <li>b. Number of business disruptions due to I&T service incidents</li>
        <li>c. Percent of users satisfied with the quality of I&T service delivery</li>
      </ul>
    </td>
  </tr>
  <tr>
    <td>
      EG08
      <ul>
        <li>a. Satisfaction levels of board and executive management with business process capabilities</li>
        <li>b. Satisfaction levels of customers with service delivery capabilities</li>
        <li>c. Satisfaction levels of suppliers with supply chain capabilities</li>
      </ul>
    </td>
    <td></td>
  </tr>
</table>

### A. Component: Process

<table>
  <tr>
    <td><strong>Management Practice</strong></td>
    <td><strong>Example Metrics</strong></td>
  </tr>
  <tr>
    <td>
      **DSS02.01 Define classification schemes for incidents and service requests.**<br>
      Define classification schemes and models for incidents and service requests.
    </td>
    <td>
      a. Total number of service requests and incidents per priority level<br>
      b. Total number of incidents escalated
    </td>
  </tr>
  <tr>
    <td><strong>Activities</strong></td>
    <td><strong>Capability Level</strong></td>
  </tr>
  <tr>
    <td>
      1. Define incident and service request classification and prioritization schemes, and criteria for problem registration. Use this information to ensure consistent approaches for handling and informing users about problems and conducting trend analysis.<br><br>
      2. Define incident models for known errors to enable efficient and effective resolution.<br><br>
      3. Define service request models according to service request type to enable self-help and efficient service for standard requests.<br><br>
      4. Define incident escalation rules and procedures, especially for major incidents and security incidents.<br><br>
      5. Define knowledge sources on incidents and requests and describe how to use them.
    </td>
    <td>3</td>
  </tr>
  <tr>
    <td><strong>Related Guidance (Standards, Frameworks, Compliance Requirements)</strong></td>
    <td><strong>Detailed Reference</strong></td>
  </tr>
  <tr>
    <td>CMMI Cybermaturity Platform, 2018</td>
    <td>IA.IP Implement Incident Investigation Processes</td>
  </tr>
  <tr>
    <td>HITRUST CSF version 9, September 2017</td>
    <td>11.01 Reporting Information Security Incidents and Weaknesses</td>
  </tr>
  <tr>
    <td>ISF, The Standard of Good Practice for Information Security 2016</td>
    <td>TM2 Security Incident Management</td>
  </tr>
  <tr>
    <td>ISO/IEC 20000-1:2011(E)</td>
    <td>8.1 Incident and service request management</td>
  </tr>
  <tr>
    <td>ISO/IEC 27002:2013/Cor.2:2015(E)</td>
    <td>16. Information security incident management</td>
  </tr>
</table>

&lt;page_number&gt;237&lt;/page_number&gt;

## Page 238

# COBIT® 2019 FRAMEWORK: GOVERNANCE AND MANAGEMENT OBJECTIVES

## A. Component: Process (cont.)

<table>
  <thead>
    <tr>
      <th>Management Practice</th>
      <th>Example Metrics</th>
    </tr>
  </thead>
  <tbody>
    <tr>
      <td><strong>DSS02.02 Record, classify and prioritize requests and incidents.</strong><br>Identify, record and classify service requests and incidents and assign a priority according to business criticality and service agreements.</td>
      <td>a. Number of types and categories defined for recording service requests and incidents<br>b. Number of service requests and incidents that are not categorized</td>
    </tr>
    <tr>
      <td colspan="2"><strong>Activities</strong></td>
    </tr>
    <tr>
      <td colspan="2">1. Log all service requests and incidents, recording all relevant information, so they can be handled effectively and a full historical record can be maintained.</td>
    </tr>
    <tr>
      <td colspan="2">2. To enable trend analysis, classify service requests and incidents by identifying type and category.</td>
    </tr>
    <tr>
      <td colspan="2">3. Prioritize service requests and incidents based on the SLA service definition of business impact and urgency.</td>
    </tr>
    <tr>
      <td colspan="2"><strong>Related Guidance (Standards, Frameworks, Compliance Requirements)</strong></td>
    </tr>
    <tr>
      <td colspan="2">No related guidance for this management practice</td>
    </tr>
    <tr>
      <td colspan="2"><strong>Management Practice</strong></td>
    </tr>
    <tr>
      <td><strong>DSS02.03 Verify, approve and fulfill service requests.</strong><br>Select the appropriate request procedures and verify that the service requests fulfill defined request criteria. Obtain approval, if required, and fulfill the requests.</td>
      <td>a. Mean elapsed time for handling each type of service request<br>b. Percent of service requests that fulfill defined request criteria</td>
    </tr>
    <tr>
      <td colspan="2"><strong>Activities</strong></td>
    </tr>
    <tr>
      <td colspan="2">1. Verify entitlement for service requests using, where possible, a predefined process flow and standard changes.</td>
    </tr>
    <tr>
      <td colspan="2">2. Obtain financial and functional approval or sign-off, if required, or predefined approvals for agreed standard changes.</td>
    </tr>
    <tr>
      <td colspan="2">3. Fulfill the requests by performing the selected request procedure. Where possible, use self-help automated menus and predefined request models for frequently requested items.</td>
    </tr>
    <tr>
      <td colspan="2"><strong>Related Guidance (Standards, Frameworks, Compliance Requirements)</strong></td>
    </tr>
    <tr>
      <td colspan="2">ITIL V3, 2011</td>
    </tr>
    <tr>
      <td colspan="2"><strong>Detailed Reference</strong></td>
    </tr>
    <tr>
      <td colspan="2">Service Operation, 4.3 Request Fulfilment</td>
    </tr>
    <tr>
      <td colspan="2"><strong>Management Practice</strong></td>
    </tr>
    <tr>
      <td><strong>DSS02.04 Investigate, diagnose and allocate incidents.</strong><br>Identify and record incident symptoms, determine possible causes, and allocate for resolution.</td>
      <td>a. Number of identified and recorded incident symptoms<br>b. Number of correctly determined symptom causes<br>c. Number of duplicate problems in the reference log</td>
    </tr>
    <tr>
      <td colspan="2"><strong>Activities</strong></td>
    </tr>
    <tr>
      <td colspan="2">1. Identify and describe relevant symptoms to establish the most probable causes of the incidents. Reference available knowledge resources (including known errors and problems) to identify possible incident resolutions (temporary workarounds and/or permanent solutions).</td>
    </tr>
    <tr>
      <td colspan="2">2. If a related problem or known error does not already exist and if the incident satisfies agreed criteria for problem registration, log a new problem.</td>
    </tr>
    <tr>
      <td colspan="2">3. Assign incidents to specialist functions if deeper expertise is needed. Engage the appropriate level of management, where and if needed.</td>
    </tr>
    <tr>
      <td colspan="2"><strong>Related Guidance (Standards, Frameworks, Compliance Requirements)</strong></td>
    </tr>
    <tr>
      <td colspan="2">No related guidance for this management practice</td>
    </tr>
    <tr>
      <td colspan="2"><strong>Management Practice</strong></td>
    </tr>
    <tr>
      <td><strong>DSS02.05 Resolve and recover from incidents.</strong><br>Document, apply and test the identified solutions or workarounds. Perform recovery actions to restore the I&T-related service.</td>
      <td>a. Percent of incidents resolved within agreed SLA<br>b. Percent of stakeholder satisfaction with resolution and recovery from incident</td>
    </tr>
    <tr>
      <td colspan="2"><strong>Activities</strong></td>
    </tr>
    <tr>
      <td colspan="2">1. Select and apply the most appropriate incident resolutions (temporary workaround and/or permanent solution).</td>
    </tr>
    <tr>
      <td colspan="2">2. Record whether workarounds were used for incident resolution.</td>
    </tr>
    <tr>
      <td colspan="2">3. Perform recovery actions, if required.</td>
    </tr>
    <tr>
      <td colspan="2">4. Document incident resolution and assess if the resolution can be used as a future knowledge source.</td>
    </tr>
  </tbody>
</table>

&lt;page_number&gt;238&lt;/page_number&gt;

<td>4</td>

<td>5</td>

## Page 243

# CHAPTER 4
## COBIT GOVERNANCE AND MANAGEMENT OBJECTIVES—DETAILED GUIDANCE

<table>
  <tr>
    <td><strong>Domain:</strong> Deliver, Service and Support<br><strong>Management Objective:</strong> DSS03 - Managed Problems</td>
    <td><strong>Focus Area:</strong> COBIT Core Model</td>
  </tr>
</table>

### Description
Identify and classify problems and their root causes. Provide timely resolution to prevent recurring incidents. Provide recommendations for improvements.

### Purpose
Increase availability, improve service levels, reduce costs, improve customer convenience and satisfaction by reducing the number of operational problems, and identify root causes as part of problem resolution.

The management objective supports the achievement of a set of primary enterprise and alignment goals:

<table>
  <tr>
    <td><strong>Enterprise Goals</strong></td>
    <td><strong>Alignment Goals</strong></td>
  </tr>
  <tr>
    <td>
      • EG01 Portfolio of competitive products and services<br>
      • EG08 Optimization of internal business process functionality
    </td>
    <td>
      AG05 Delivery of I&T services in line with business requirements
    </td>
  </tr>
  <tr>
    <td><strong>Example Metrics for Enterprise Goals</strong></td>
    <td><strong>Example Metrics for Alignment Goals</strong></td>
  </tr>
  <tr>
    <td>
      EG01
      <ul>
        <li>a. Percent of products and services that meet or exceed targets in revenues and/or market share</li>
        <li>b. Percent of products and services that meet or exceed customer satisfaction targets</li>
        <li>c. Percent of products and services that provide competitive advantage</li>
        <li>d. Time to market for new products and services</li>
      </ul>
    </td>
    <td>
      AG05
      <ul>
        <li>a. Percent of business stakeholders satisfied that I&T service delivery meets agreed service levels</li>
        <li>b. Number of business disruptions due to I&T service incidents</li>
        <li>c. Percent of users satisfied with the quality of I&T service delivery</li>
      </ul>
    </td>
  </tr>
  <tr>
    <td>
      EG08
      <ul>
        <li>a. Satisfaction levels of board and executive management with business process capabilities</li>
        <li>b. Satisfaction levels of customers with service delivery capabilities</li>
        <li>c. Satisfaction levels of suppliers with supply chain capabilities</li>
      </ul>
    </td>
    <td></td>
  </tr>
</table>

### A. Component: Process

<table>
  <tr>
    <td><strong>Management Practice</strong></td>
    <td><strong>Example Metrics</strong></td>
    <td><strong>Capability Level</strong></td>
  </tr>
  <tr>
    <td>
      **DSS03.01 Identify and classify problems.**<br>
      Define and implement criteria and procedures to identify and report problems. Include problem classification, categorization and prioritization.
    </td>
    <td>
      <ul>
        <li>a. Percent of major incidents for which problems were logged</li>
        <li>b. Percent of incidents solved in accordance with agreed SLAs</li>
        <li>c. Percent of problems appropriately identified, including classification, categorization and prioritization</li>
      </ul>
    </td>
    <td rowspan="7">2</td>
  </tr>
  <tr>
    <td><strong>Activities</strong></td>
    <td></td>
  </tr>
  <tr>
    <td colspan="2">1. Identify problems through the correlation of incident reports, error logs and other problem identification resources.</td>
  </tr>
  <tr>
    <td colspan="2">2. Handle all problems formally with access to all relevant data. Include information from the IT change management system and IT configuration/asset and incident details.</td>
  </tr>
  <tr>
    <td colspan="2">3. Define appropriate support groups to assist with problem identification, root cause analysis and solution determination to support problem management. Determine support groups based on predefined categories, such as hardware, network, software, applications and support software.</td>
  </tr>
  <tr>
    <td colspan="2">4. Define priority levels through consultation with the business to ensure that problem identification and root cause analysis are handled in a timely manner according to the agreed SLAs. Base priority levels on business impact and urgency.</td>
  </tr>
  <tr>
    <td colspan="2">5. Report the status of identified problems to the service desk so customers and IT management can be kept informed.</td>
  </tr>
  <tr>
    <td colspan="2">6. Maintain a single problem management catalog to register and report problems identified. Use the catalog to establish audit trails of the problem management processes, including the status of each problem (i.e., open, reopen, in progress or closed).</td>
  </tr>
  <tr>
    <td><strong>Related Guidance (Standards, Frameworks, Compliance Requirements)</strong></td>
    <td><strong>Detailed Reference</strong></td>
    <td></td>
  </tr>
  <tr>
    <td>ISO/IEC 20000-1:2011(E)</td>
    <td>8.2 Problem management</td>
    <td></td>
  </tr>
</table>

&lt;page_number&gt;243&lt;/page_number&gt;

---


## Page 244

# COBIT® 2019 FRAMEWORK: GOVERNANCE AND MANAGEMENT OBJECTIVES

## A. Component: Process (cont.)

<table>
  <thead>
    <tr>
      <th>Management Practice</th>
      <th>Example Metrics</th>
    </tr>
  </thead>
  <tbody>
    <tr>
      <td><strong>DSS03.02 Investigate and diagnose problems.</strong><br>Investigate and diagnose problems using relevant subject matter experts to assess and analyze root causes.</td>
      <td>a. Number of identified problems classified as known errors<br>b. Percent of problems investigated and diagnosed throughout their life cycle</td>
    </tr>
  </tbody>
</table>

### Activities

<table>
  <thead>
    <tr>
      <th></th>
      <th>Capability Level</th>
    </tr>
  </thead>
  <tbody>
    <tr>
      <td>1. Identify problems that may be known errors by comparing incident data with the database of known and suspected errors (e.g., those communicated by external vendors). Classify problems as known errors.</td>
      <td>3</td>
    </tr>
    <tr>
      <td>2. Associate the affected configuration items to the established/known error.</td>
      <td></td>
    </tr>
    <tr>
      <td>3. Produce reports to communicate the progress in resolving problems and to monitor the continuing impact of problems not solved. Monitor the status of the problem-handling process throughout its life cycle, including input from IT change and configuration management.</td>
      <td></td>
    </tr>
  </tbody>
</table>

### Related Guidance (Standards, Frameworks, Compliance Requirements)

No related guidance for this management practice

<table>
  <thead>
    <tr>
      <th>Management Practice</th>
      <th>Example Metrics</th>
    </tr>
  </thead>
  <tbody>
    <tr>
      <td><strong>DSS03.03 Raise known errors.</strong><br>As soon as root causes of problems are identified, create known-error records, document appropriate workarounds and identify potential solutions.</td>
      <td>a. Number of problems with satisfactory resolution that addressed root causes<br>b. Percent of stakeholder satisfaction with identification of root causes, creation of known-error records and appropriate workarounds, and identification of potential solutions</td>
    </tr>
  </tbody>
</table>

### Activities

<table>
  <thead>
    <tr>
      <th></th>
      <th>Capability Level</th>
    </tr>
  </thead>
  <tbody>
    <tr>
      <td>1. As soon as the root causes of problems are identified, create known-error records and develop a suitable workaround.</td>
      <td>2</td>
    </tr>
    <tr>
      <td>2. Identify, evaluate, prioritize and process (via IT change management) solutions to known errors, based on a cost/benefit business case and business impact and urgency.</td>
      <td>3</td>
    </tr>
  </tbody>
</table>

### Related Guidance (Standards, Frameworks, Compliance Requirements)

No related guidance for this management practice

<table>
  <thead>
    <tr>
      <th>Management Practice</th>
      <th>Example Metrics</th>
    </tr>
  </thead>
  <tbody>
    <tr>
      <td><strong>DSS03.04 Resolve and close problems.</strong><br>Identify and initiate sustainable solutions addressing the root cause. Raise change requests via the established change management process, if required, to resolve errors. Ensure that the personnel affected are aware of the actions taken and the plans developed to prevent future incidents from occurring.</td>
      <td>a. Decrease in number of recurring incidents caused by unresolved problems<br>b. Percent of workarounds defined for open problems</td>
    </tr>
  </tbody>
</table>

### Activities

<table>
  <thead>
    <tr>
      <th></th>
      <th>Capability Level</th>
    </tr>
  </thead>
  <tbody>
    <tr>
      <td>1. Close problem records either after confirmation for successful elimination of the known error or after agreement with the business on how to alternatively handle the problem.</td>
      <td>2</td>
    </tr>
    <tr>
      <td>2. Inform the service desk of the schedule for problem closure (e.g., the schedule for fixing the known errors, the possible workaround or the fact that the problem will remain until the change is implemented) and the consequences of the approach taken. Keep affected users and customers informed as appropriate.</td>
      <td></td>
    </tr>
    <tr>
      <td>3. Throughout the resolution process, obtain regular reports from IT change management on progress in resolving problems and errors.</td>
      <td>3</td>
    </tr>
    <tr>
      <td>4. Monitor the continuing impact of problems and known errors on services.</td>
      <td>4</td>
    </tr>
    <tr>
      <td>5. Review and confirm the success of resolutions of major problems.</td>
      <td></td>
    </tr>
    <tr>
      <td>6. Make sure the knowledge learned from the review is incorporated into a service review meeting with the business customer.</td>
      <td>5</td>
    </tr>
  </tbody>
</table>

### Related Guidance (Standards, Frameworks, Compliance Requirements)

No related guidance for this management practice

## Page 245

# CHAPTER 4
## COBIT GOVERNANCE AND MANAGEMENT OBJECTIVES—DETAILED GUIDANCE

**A. Component: Process (cont.)**

<table>
<thead>
<tr>
<th>Management Practice</th>
<th>Example Metrics</th>
</tr>
</thead>
<tbody>
<tr>
<td><strong>DSS03.05 Perform proactive problem management.</strong><br>Collect and analyze operational data (especially incident and change records) to identify emerging trends that may indicate problems. Log problem records to enable assessment.</td>
<td>a. Percent of problems logged as part of the proactive problem management activity<br>b. Percent of key stakeholder satisfaction with the communication of problem information related to IT changes and incidents</td>
</tr>
</tbody>
</table>

<table>
<thead>
<tr>
<th>Activities</th>
<th>Capability Level</th>
</tr>
</thead>
<tbody>
<tr>
<td>1. Capture problem information related to I&T changes and incidents and communicate it to key stakeholders. Communicate via reports and periodic meetings among incident, problem, change and configuration management process owners to consider recent problems and potential corrective actions.</td>
<td>3</td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td>2. Ensure that process owners and managers from incident, problem, change and configuration management meet regularly to discuss known problems and future planned changes.</td>
<td></td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td>3. Identify and initiate sustainable solutions (permanent fixes) addressing the root cause. Raise change requests via the established change management processes.</td>
<td></td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td>4. To enable the enterprise to monitor the total costs of problems, capture change efforts resulting from problem management process activities (e.g., fixes to problems and known errors) and report on them.</td>
<td>4</td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td>5. Produce reports to monitor problem resolution against the business requirements and SLAs. Ensure the proper escalation of problems, such as escalating to a higher management level according to agreed criteria, contacting external vendors, or referring to the change advisory board to increase the priority of an urgent request for change (RFC) to implement a temporary workaround.</td>
<td></td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td>6. To optimize the use of resources and reduce workarounds, track problem trends.</td>
<td></td>
</tr>
</tbody>
</table>

## Page 249

# CHAPTER 4
## COBIT GOVERNANCE AND MANAGEMENT OBJECTIVES—DETAILED GUIDANCE

<table>
  <tr>
    <td style="vertical-align: top;">
      **Domain:** Deliver, Service and Support<br>
      **Management Objective:** DSS04 - Managed Continuity
    </td>
    <td style="vertical-align: top;">
      **Focus Area:** COBIT Core Model
    </td>
  </tr>
</table>

### Description
Establish and maintain a plan to enable the business and IT organizations to respond to incidents and quickly adapt to disruptions. This will enable continued operations of critical business processes and required I&T services and maintain availability of resources, assets and information at a level acceptable to the enterprise.

### Purpose
Adapt rapidly, continue business operations and maintain availability of resources and information at a level acceptable to the enterprise in the event of a significant disruption (e.g., threats, opportunities, demands).

The management objective supports the achievement of a set of primary enterprise and alignment goals:

<table>
  <tr>
    <td style="vertical-align: top; width: 50%;">
      ### Enterprise Goals
      <ul>
        <li>• EG01 Portfolio of competitive products and services</li>
        <li>• EG02 Managed business risk</li>
        <li>• EG06 Business service continuity and availability</li>
        <li>• EG08 Optimization of internal business process functionality</li>
      </ul>
      ### Example Metrics for Enterprise Goals
      <table>
        <tr>
          <td>EG01</td>
          <td>
            <ul>
              <li>a. Percent of products and services that meet or exceed targets in revenues and/or market share</li>
              <li>b. Percent of products and services that meet or exceed customer satisfaction targets</li>
              <li>c. Percent of products and services that provide competitive advantage</li>
              <li>d. Time to market for new products and services</li>
            </ul>
          </td>
        </tr>
        <tr>
          <td>EG02</td>
          <td>
            <ul>
              <li>a. Percent of critical business objectives and services covered by risk assessment</li>
              <li>b. Ratio of significant incidents that were not identified in risk assessments vs. total incidents</li>
              <li>c. Frequency of updating risk profile</li>
            </ul>
          </td>
        </tr>
        <tr>
          <td>EG06</td>
          <td>
            <ul>
              <li>a. Number of customer service or business process interruptions causing significant incidents</li>
              <li>b. Business cost of incidents</li>
              <li>c. Number of business processing hours lost due to unplanned service interruptions</li>
              <li>d. Percent of complaints as a function of committed service availability targets</li>
            </ul>
          </td>
        </tr>
        <tr>
          <td>EG08</td>
          <td>
            <ul>
              <li>a. Satisfaction levels of board and executive management with business process capabilities</li>
              <li>b. Satisfaction levels of customers with service delivery capabilities</li>
              <li>c. Satisfaction levels of suppliers with supply chain capabilities</li>
            </ul>
          </td>
        </tr>
      </table>
    </td>
    <td style="vertical-align: top; width: 50%;">
      ### Alignment Goals
      <ul>
        <li>• AG05 Delivery of I&T services in line with business requirements</li>
        <li>• AG07 Security of information, processing infrastructure and applications, and privacy</li>
      </ul>
      ### Example Metrics for Alignment Goals
      <table>
        <tr>
          <td>AG05</td>
          <td>
            <ul>
              <li>a. Percent of business stakeholders satisfied that I&T service delivery meets agreed service levels</li>
              <li>b. Number of business disruptions due to I&T service incidents</li>
              <li>c. Percent of users satisfied with the quality of I&T service delivery</li>
            </ul>
          </td>
        </tr>
        <tr>
          <td>AG07</td>
          <td>
            <ul>
              <li>a. Number of confidentiality incidents causing financial loss, business disruption or public embarrassment</li>
              <li>b. Number of availability incidents causing financial loss, business disruption or public embarrassment</li>
              <li>c. Number of integrity incidents causing financial loss, business disruption or public embarrassment</li>
            </ul>
          </td>
        </tr>
      </table>
    </td>
  </tr>
</table>

Deliver, Service and Support

## Page 250

# COBIT® 2019 FRAMEWORK: GOVERNANCE AND MANAGEMENT OBJECTIVES

<table>
  <thead>
    <tr>
      <th colspan="3">A. Component: Process</th>
    </tr>
  </thead>
  <tbody>
    <tr>
      <td><strong>Management Practice</strong></td>
      <td><strong>Example Metrics</strong></td>
      <td></td>
    </tr>
    <tr>
      <td><strong>DSS04.01 Define the business continuity policy, objectives and scope.</strong><br>Define business continuity policy and scope, aligned with enterprise and stakeholder objectives, to improve business resilience.</td>
      <td>a. Percent of business continuity objectives and scope reworked due to misidentified processes and activities<br>b. Percent of key stakeholders participating, defining and agreeing on continuity policy and scope</td>
      <td></td>
    </tr>
    <tr>
      <td><strong>Activities</strong></td>
      <td></td>
      <td><strong>Capability Level</strong></td>
    </tr>
    <tr>
      <td>1. Identify internal and outsourced business processes and service activities that are critical to the enterprise operations or necessary to meet legal and/or contractual obligations.<br>2. Identify key stakeholders and roles and responsibilities for defining and agreeing on continuity policy and scope.<br>3. Define and document the agreed minimum policy objectives and scope for business resilience.<br>4. Identify essential supporting business processes and related I&amp;T services.</td>
      <td></td>
      <td>2</td>
    </tr>
    <tr>
      <td><strong>Related Guidance (Standards, Frameworks, Compliance Requirements)</strong></td>
      <td><strong>Detailed Reference</strong></td>
      <td></td>
    </tr>
    <tr>
      <td>HITRUST CSF version 9, September 2017</td>
      <td>12.01 Information Security Aspects of Business Continuity Management</td>
      <td></td>
    </tr>
    <tr>
      <td>ISF, The Standard of Good Practice for Information Security 2016</td>
      <td>BC1.1 Business Continuity Strategy; BC1.2 Business Continuity Programme</td>
      <td></td>
    </tr>
    <tr>
      <td>ISO/IEC 27002:2013/Cor.2:2015(E)</td>
      <td>17. Information security aspects of business continuity management</td>
      <td></td>
    </tr>
    <tr>
      <td>National Institute of Standards and Technology Special Publication 800-53, Revision 5 (Draft), August 2017</td>
      <td>3.6 Contingency planning (CP-1)</td>
      <td></td>
    </tr>
    <tr>
      <td><strong>Management Practice</strong></td>
      <td><strong>Example Metrics</strong></td>
      <td></td>
    </tr>
    <tr>
      <td><strong>DSS04.02 Maintain business resilience.</strong><br>Evaluate business resilience options and choose a cost-effective and viable strategy that will ensure enterprise continuity, disaster recovery and incident response in the face of a disaster or other major incident or disruption.</td>
      <td>a. Total downtime resulting from major incident or disruption<br>b. Percent of key stakeholders involved in business impact analyses evaluating the impact over time of a disruption to critical business functions and the effect that a disruption would have on them</td>
      <td></td>
    </tr>
    <tr>
      <td><strong>Activities</strong></td>
      <td></td>
      <td><strong>Capability Level</strong></td>
    </tr>
    <tr>
      <td>1. Identify potential scenarios likely to give rise to events that could cause significant disruptive incidents.<br>2. Conduct a business impact analysis to evaluate the impact over time of a disruption to critical business functions and the effect that a disruption would have on them.<br>3. Establish the minimum time required to recover a business process and supporting I&amp;T, based on an acceptable length of business interruption and maximum tolerable outage.<br>4. Determine the conditions and owners of key decisions that will cause the continuity plans to be invoked.<br>5. Assess the likelihood of threats that could cause loss of business continuity. Identify measures that will reduce the likelihood and impact through improved prevention and increased resilience.<br>6. Analyze continuity requirements to identify possible strategic business and technical options.<br>7. Identify resource requirements and costs for each strategic technical option and make strategic recommendations.<br>8. Obtain executive business approval for selected strategic options.</td>
      <td></td>
      <td>2<br>3</td>
    </tr>
    <tr>
      <td><strong>Related Guidance (Standards, Frameworks, Compliance Requirements)</strong></td>
      <td><strong>Detailed Reference</strong></td>
      <td></td>
    </tr>
    <tr>
      <td>ISF, The Standard of Good Practice for Information Security 2016</td>
      <td>BC1.3 Resilient Technical Environments</td>
      <td></td>
    </tr>
    <tr>
      <td>ITIL V3, 2011</td>
      <td>Service Design, 4.6 IT Continuity Management</td>
      <td></td>
    </tr>
    <tr>
      <td>National Institute of Standards and Technology Special Publication 800-53, Revision 5 (Draft), August 2017</td>
      <td>3.6 Contingency planning (CP-2)</td>
      <td></td>
    </tr>
  </tbody>
</table>

## Page 251

CHAPTER 4
COBIT GOVERNANCE AND MANAGEMENT OBJECTIVES—DETAILED GUIDANCE

A. Component: Process (cont.)

<table>
<thead>
<tr>
<th>Management Practice</th>
<th>Example Metrics</th>
</tr>
</thead>
<tbody>
<tr>
<td><strong>DSS04.03 Develop and implement a business continuity response.</strong><br>Develop a business continuity plan (BCP) and disaster recovery plan (DRP) based on the strategy. Document all procedures necessary for the enterprise to continue critical activities in the event of an incident.</td>
<td>a. Number of critical business systems not covered by the plan<br>b. Percent of key stakeholders involved in developing BCPs and DRPs</td>
</tr>
</tbody>
</table>

<table>
<thead>
<tr>
<th>Activities</th>
<th>Capability Level</th>
</tr>
</thead>
<tbody>
<tr>
<td>1. Define the incident response actions and communications to be taken in the event of disruption. Define related roles and responsibilities, including accountability for policy and implementation.</td>
<td>2</td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td>2. Ensure that key suppliers and outsource partners have effective continuity plans in place. Obtain audited evidence as required.</td>
<td></td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td>3. Define the conditions and recovery procedures that would enable resumption of business processing. Include updating and reconciliation of information databases to preserve information integrity.</td>
<td></td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td>4. Develop and maintain operational BCPs and DRPs that contain the procedures to be followed to enable continued operation of critical business processes and/or temporary processing arrangements. Include links to plans of outsourced service providers.</td>
<td></td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td>5. Define and document the resources required to support the continuity and recovery procedures, considering people, facilities and IT infrastructure.</td>
<td></td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td>6. Define and document the information backup requirements required to support the plans. Include plans and paper documents as well as data files. Consider the need for security and off-site storage.</td>
<td></td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td>7. Determine required skills for individuals involved in executing the plan and procedures.</td>
<td></td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td>8. Distribute the plans and supporting documentation securely to appropriately authorized interested parties. Make sure the plans and documentation are accessible under all disaster scenarios.</td>
<td>3</td>
</tr>
</tbody>
</table>

<table>
<thead>
<tr>
<th>Related Guidance (Standards, Frameworks, Compliance Requirements)</th>
<th>Detailed Reference</th>
</tr>
</thead>
<tbody>
<tr>
<td>ISF, The Standard of Good Practice for Information Security 2016</td>
<td>BC1.4 Crisis Management; BC2.1 Business Continuity Planning</td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td>National Institute of Standards and Technology Special Publication 800-53, Revision 5 (Draft), August 2017</td>
<td>3.6 Contingency planning (CP-6, CP-9, CP-10)</td>
</tr>
</tbody>
</table>

<table>
<thead>
<tr>
<th>Management Practice</th>
<th>Example Metrics</th>
</tr>
</thead>
<tbody>
<tr>
<td><strong>DSS04.04 Exercise, test and review the business continuity plan (BCP) and disaster response plan (DRP).</strong><br>Test continuity on a regular basis to exercise plans against predetermined outcomes, uphold business resilience and allow innovative solutions to be developed.</td>
<td>a. Frequency of tests<br>b. Number of exercises and tests that achieved recovery objectives</td>
</tr>
</tbody>
</table>

<table>
<thead>
<tr>
<th>Activities</th>
<th>Capability Level</th>
</tr>
</thead>
<tbody>
<tr>
<td>1. Define objectives for exercising and testing the business, technical, logistical, administrative, procedural and operational systems of the plan to verify completeness of the BCP and DRP in meeting business risk.</td>
<td>2</td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td>2. Define and agree on stakeholder exercises that are realistic and validate continuity procedures. Include roles and responsibilities and data retention arrangements that cause minimum disruption to business processes.</td>
<td></td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td>3. Assign roles and responsibilities for performing continuity plan exercises and tests.</td>
<td></td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td>4. Schedule exercises and test activities as defined in the continuity plans.</td>
<td>3</td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td>5. Conduct a post-exercise debriefing and analysis to consider the achievement.</td>
<td>4</td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td>6. Based on the results of the review, develop recommendations for improving the current continuity plans.</td>
<td>5</td>
</tr>
</tbody>
</table>

<table>
<thead>
<tr>
<th>Related Guidance (Standards, Frameworks, Compliance Requirements)</th>
<th>Detailed Reference</th>
</tr>
</thead>
<tbody>
<tr>
<td>CMMI Cybermaturity Platform, 2018</td>
<td>PP.RS Develop and Maintain Response Plans; PP.RP Develop and Maintain Recovery Plans</td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td>ISF, The Standard of Good Practice for Information Security 2016</td>
<td>BC2.3 Business Continuity Testing</td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td>The CIS Critical Security Controls for Effective Cyber Defense Version 6.1, August 2016</td>
<td>CSC 20: Penetration Tests and Red Team Exercises</td>
</tr>
</tbody>
</table>

## Page 252

# COBIT® 2019 FRAMEWORK: GOVERNANCE AND MANAGEMENT OBJECTIVES

<table>
  <thead>
    <tr>
      <th colspan="3">A. Component: Process (cont.)</th>
    </tr>
  </thead>
  <tbody>
    <tr>
      <td><strong>Management Practice</strong></td>
      <td><strong>Example Metrics</strong></td>
      <td></td>
    </tr>
    <tr>
      <td><strong>DSS04.05 Review, maintain and improve the continuity plans.</strong><br>Conduct a management review of the continuity capability at regular intervals to ensure its continued suitability, adequacy and effectiveness. Manage changes to the plans in accordance with the change control process to ensure that continuity plans are kept up to date and continually reflect actual business requirements.</td>
      <td>a. Percent of agreed improvements to the plan that have been reflected in the plan<br>b. Percent of continuity plans and business impact assessments that are up to date</td>
      <td></td>
    </tr>
    <tr>
      <td><strong>Activities</strong></td>
      <td></td>
      <td><strong>Capability Level</strong></td>
    </tr>
    <tr>
      <td>1. On a regular basis, review the continuity plans and capability against any assumptions made and current business operational and strategic objectives.</td>
      <td></td>
      <td>3</td>
    </tr>
    <tr>
      <td>2. On a regular basis, review the continuity plans to consider the impact of new or major changes to enterprise organization, business processes, outsourcing arrangements, technologies, infrastructure, operating systems and application systems.</td>
      <td></td>
      <td></td>
    </tr>
    <tr>
      <td>3. Consider whether a revised business impact assessment may be required, depending on the nature of the change.</td>
      <td></td>
      <td></td>
    </tr>
    <tr>
      <td>4. Recommend changes in policy, plans, procedures, infrastructure, and roles and responsibilities. Communicate them as appropriate for management approval and processing via the IT change management process.</td>
      <td></td>
      <td></td>
    </tr>
    <tr>
      <td><strong>Related Guidance (Standards, Frameworks, Compliance Requirements)</strong></td>
      <td><strong>Detailed Reference</strong></td>
      <td></td>
    </tr>
    <tr>
      <td>No related guidance for this management practice</td>
      <td></td>
      <td></td>
    </tr>
    <tr>
      <td><strong>Management Practice</strong></td>
      <td><strong>Example Metrics</strong></td>
      <td></td>
    </tr>
    <tr>
      <td><strong>DSS04.06 Conduct continuity plan training.</strong><br>Provide all concerned internal and external parties with regular training sessions regarding procedures and their roles and responsibilities in case of disruption.</td>
      <td>a. Percent of internal and external stakeholders who received training<br>b. Percent of relevant internal and external parties whose skills and competencies are current</td>
      <td></td>
    </tr>
    <tr>
      <td><strong>Activities</strong></td>
      <td></td>
      <td><strong>Capability Level</strong></td>
    </tr>
    <tr>
      <td>1. Roll out BCP and DRP awareness and training.</td>
      <td></td>
      <td>2</td>
    </tr>
    <tr>
      <td>2. Define and maintain training requirements and plans for those performing continuity planning, impact assessments, risk assessments, media communication and incident response. Ensure that the training plans consider frequency of training and training delivery mechanisms.</td>
      <td></td>
      <td>3</td>
    </tr>
    <tr>
      <td>3. Develop competencies based on practical training, including participation in exercises and tests.</td>
      <td></td>
      <td></td>
    </tr>
    <tr>
      <td>4. Based on the exercise and test results, monitor skills and competencies.</td>
      <td></td>
      <td>4</td>
    </tr>
    <tr>
      <td><strong>Related Guidance (Standards, Frameworks, Compliance Requirements)</strong></td>
      <td><strong>Detailed Reference</strong></td>
      <td></td>
    </tr>
    <tr>
      <td>National Institute of Standards and Technology Special Publication 800-53, Revision 5 (Draft), August 2017</td>
      <td>3.6 Contingency planning (CP-4)</td>
      <td></td>
    </tr>
    <tr>
      <td><strong>Management Practice</strong></td>
      <td><strong>Example Metrics</strong></td>
      <td></td>
    </tr>
    <tr>
      <td><strong>DSS04.07 Manage backup arrangements.</strong><br>Maintain availability of business-critical information.</td>
      <td>a. Percent of backup media transferred and stored securely<br>b. Percent of successful and timely restoration from backup or alternate media copies</td>
      <td></td>
    </tr>
    <tr>
      <td><strong>Activities</strong></td>
      <td></td>
      <td><strong>Capability Level</strong></td>
    </tr>
    <tr>
      <td>1. Back up systems, applications, data and documentation according to a defined schedule. Consider frequency (monthly, weekly, daily, etc.), mode of backup (e.g., disk mirroring for real-time backups vs. DVD-ROM for long-term retention), type of backup (e.g., full vs. incremental), and type of media. Consider also automated online backups, data types (e.g., voice, optical), creation of logs, critical end-user computing data (e.g., spreadsheets), physical and logical location of data sources, security and access rights, and encryption.</td>
      <td></td>
      <td>2</td>
    </tr>
    <tr>
      <td>2. Define requirements for on-site and off-site storage of backup data that meet the business requirements. Consider the accessibility required to back up data.</td>
      <td></td>
      <td></td>
    </tr>
    <tr>
      <td>3. Periodically test and refresh archived and backup data.</td>
      <td></td>
      <td></td>
    </tr>
    <tr>
      <td>4. Ensure that systems, applications, data and documentation maintained or processed by third parties are adequately backed up or otherwise secured. Consider requiring return of backups from third parties. Consider escrow or deposit arrangements.</td>
      <td></td>
      <td></td>
    </tr>
  </tbody>
</table>

## Page 255

CHAPTER 4
COBIT GOVERNANCE AND MANAGEMENT OBJECTIVES—DETAILED GUIDANCE

E. Component: Policies and Procedures

<table>
<thead>
<tr>
<th>Relevant Policy</th>
<th>Policy Description</th>
<th>Related Guidance</th>
<th>Detailed Reference</th>
</tr>
</thead>
<tbody>
<tr>
<td>Business continuity policy</td>
<td>Outlines management’s commitment to the business impact assessment (BIA), business contingency plan (including trusted recovery), recovery requirements for critical systems, defined thresholds and triggers for contingencies, escalation plan, data recovery plan, training and testing.</td>
<td></td>
<td></td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td>Crisis management policy</td>
<td>Sets guidelines and sequence of crisis response in key areas of risk. Along with I&T security, network management, and data security and privacy, crisis management is one of the operational-level policies that should be considered for complete I&T risk management.</td>
<td></td>
<td></td>
</tr>
</tbody>
</table>

F. Component: Culture, Ethics and Behavior

<table>
<thead>
<tr>
<th>Key Culture Elements</th>
<th>Related Guidance</th>
<th>Detailed Reference</th>
</tr>
</thead>
<tbody>
<tr>
<td>Embed the need for business resilience in the enterprise culture. Regularly and frequently update employees about core values, desired behaviors and strategic objectives to maintain the enterprise’s composure and image in every situation. Regularly test business continuity procedures and disaster recovery.</td>
<td></td>
<td></td>
</tr>
</tbody>
</table>

G. Component: Services, Infrastructure and Applications

*   External hosting services
*   Incident monitoring tools
*   Remote storage facility services

## Page 257

# CHAPTER 4
## COBIT GOVERNANCE AND MANAGEMENT OBJECTIVES—DETAILED GUIDANCE

<table>
  <tr>
    <td>
      **Domain:** Deliver, Service and Support<br>
      **Management Objective:** DSS05 - Managed Security Services
    </td>
    <td style="text-align: right;">
      **Focus Area:** COBIT Core Model
    </td>

    <tr>
      <td colspan="2">
        <h3>Description</h3>
        Protect enterprise information to maintain the level of information security risk acceptable to the enterprise in accordance with the security policy. Establish and maintain information security roles and access privileges. Perform security monitoring.
      </td>
    </tr>
    <tr>
      <td colspan="2">
        <h3>Purpose</h3>
        Minimize the business impact of operational information security vulnerabilities and incidents.
      </td>

      <tr>
        <td colspan="1" style="vertical-align: top;">
          <p>The management objective supports the achievement of a set of primary enterprise and alignment goals:</p>
          <table>
            <thead>
              <tr>
                <th>Enterprise Goals</th>
                <th></th>
              </tr>
            </thead>
            <tbody>
              <tr><td>• EG02</td><td>Managed business risk</td></tr>
              <tr style="background-color:#D3D3D3;"><td>• EG6</td><td style="color:white;">Business service continuity and availability</td></tr>

              <tr>
                <td colspan="2"><strong>Example Metrics for Enterprise Goals</strong></td>
              </tr>
              <tr>
                  <td>EG02</td>
                  <td>
                    a. Percent of critical business objectives and services covered by risk assessment<br>
                    b. Ratio of significant incidents that were not identified in risk assessments vs. total incidents<br>
                    c. Frequency of updating risk profile
                  </td>
              </tr>
              <tr>
                  <td>EG6</td>
                  <td>
                    a. Number of customer service or business process interruptions causing significant incidents<br>
                    b. Business cost of incidents<br>
                    c. Number of business processing hours lost due to unplanned service interruptions<br>
                    d. Percent of complaints as a function of committed service availability targets
                  </td>
              </tr>
            </tbody>
          </table>
        </td>
        <td style="vertical-align: top; padding-left: 20px;">
          <p><strong>Alignment Goals</strong></p>
          <ul>
            <li>• AG02 Managed I&T-related risk</li>
            <li style="background-color:#D3D3D3;"><li>• AG7 Security of information, processing infrastructure and applications, and privacy</li></ul>
          <p><strong>Example Metrics for Alignment Goals</strong></p>
          <table>
            <thead>
              <tr>
                <th>Alignment Goals</th>
                <th></th>
              </tr>
            </thead>
            <tbody>
              <tr><td>• AG02</td><td>
                a. Frequency of updating risk profile<br>
                b. Percent of enterprise risk assessments including I&T-related risk<br>
                c. Number of significant I&T-related incidents that were not identified in a risk assessment
              </td></tr>
              <tr><td>• AG07</td><td>
                a. Number of confidentiality incidents causing financial loss, business disruption or public embarrassment<br>
                b. Number of availability incidents causing financial loss or business disruption<br>
                c. Number of integrity incidents causing financial loss<br>
                d. Number of availability or integrity incidents causing public embarrassment
              </td></tr>
            </tbody>
          </table>
        </td>
      </tr>
    </table>

    <br>

    <h3>A. Component: Process</h3>

    <table>
      <thead>
        <tr>
          <th>Management Practice</th>
          <th style="width: 50%; vertical-align: top;">Example Metrics</th>
          <!-- This table has two columns: Management Practice and Example Metrics -->
        </tr>
      </thead>
      <tbody>
        <tr>
          <td>
            <strong>DSS05.01 Protect against malicious software.</strong><br>
            Implement and maintain preventive, detective and corrective measures (especially up-to-date security patches and virus control) across the enterprise to protect information systems and technology from malicious software (e.g., ransomware, malware, viruses, worms, spyware, spam).
          </td>
          <td>
            a. Number of successful malicious software attacks<br>
            b. Percent of employees failing tests on malicious attacks (e.g., test of phishing email)
          </td>
        </tr>
      </tbody>
    </table>

    <table>
      <thead>
        <tr>
          <th>Activities</th>
          <th style="width: 50%; vertical-align: top;"></th>
          <td style="text-align: center;">Capability Level</td>
        </tr>
      </thead>
      <tbody>
        <tr>
          <td>
            1. Install and activate malicious software protection tools on all processing facilities, with malicious software definition files that are updated as required (automatically or semi-automatically).
          </td>
          <td></td>
          <th>2</th>
        </tr>
        <tr>
          <td>
            2. Filter incoming traffic, such as email and downloads, to protect against unsolicited information (e.g., spyware, phishing emails).
          </td>
          <td></td>
          <th></th>
        </tr>
        <tr>
          <td>
            3. Communicate malicious software awareness and enforce prevention procedures and responsibilities. Conduct periodic training about malware in email and Internet usage. Train users to not open, but report, suspicious emails and to not install shared or unapproved software.
          </td>
          <td></td>
          <th>3</th>
        </tr>
        <tr>
          <td>
            4. Distribute all protection software centrally (version and patch-level) using centralized configuration and IT change management.
          </td><td></td>
        </tr>

        <tr>
            <td>
                5. Regularly review and evaluate information on new potential threats (e.g., reviewing vendors’ products and services security advisories).
            </td>
            <td></td>
            <th>4</th>
        </tr>
      </tbody>
    </table>

    <table>
      <thead>
        <tr>

## Page 258

# COBIT® 2019 FRAMEWORK: GOVERNANCE AND MANAGEMENT OBJECTIVES

## A. Component: Process (cont.)

<table>
  <thead>
    <tr>
      <th>Management Practice</th>
      <th>Example Metrics</th>
    </tr>
  </thead>
  <tbody>
    <tr>
      <td><strong>DSS05.02 Manage network and connectivity security.</strong><br>Use security measures and related management procedures to protect information over all methods of connectivity.</td>
      <td>a. Number of firewall breaches<br>b. Number of vulnerabilities discovered<br>c. Percent of time network and systems not available due to security incident</td>
    </tr>
  </tbody>
</table>

### Activities

<table>
  <thead>
    <tr>
      <th>Activities</th>
      <th>Capability Level</th>
    </tr>
  </thead>
  <tbody>
    <tr>
      <td>1. Allow only authorized devices to have access to corporate information and the enterprise network. Configure these devices to force password entry.</td>
      <td>2</td>
    </tr>
    <tr>
      <td>2. Implement network filtering mechanisms, such as firewalls and intrusion detection software. Enforce appropriate policies to control inbound and outbound traffic.</td>
      <td></td>
    </tr>
    <tr>
      <td>3. Apply approved security protocols to network connectivity.</td>
      <td></td>
    </tr>
    <tr>
      <td>4. Configure network equipment in a secure manner.</td>
      <td></td>
    </tr>
    <tr>
      <td>5. Encrypt information in transit according to its classification.</td>
      <td>3</td>
    </tr>
    <tr>
      <td>6. Based on risk assessments and business requirements, establish and maintain a policy for security of connectivity.</td>
      <td></td>
    </tr>
    <tr>
      <td>7. Establish trusted mechanisms to support the secure transmission and receipt of information.</td>
      <td></td>
    </tr>
    <tr>
      <td>8. Carry out periodic penetration testing to determine adequacy of network protection.</td>
      <td>4</td>
    </tr>
    <tr>
      <td>9. Carry out periodic testing of system security to determine adequacy of system protection.</td>
      <td></td>
    </tr>
  </tbody>
</table>

### Related Guidance (Standards, Frameworks, Compliance Requirements)

<table>
  <thead>
    <tr>
      <th>Related Guidance (Standards, Frameworks, Compliance Requirements)</th>
      <th>Detailed Reference</th>
    </tr>
  </thead>
  <tbody>
    <tr>
      <td>CMMI Cybermaturity Platform, 2018</td>
      <td>AC.MI Manage Network Integrity & Segregation; CM.MN Monitor Networks; AC.CP Manage Communication Protections</td>
    </tr>
    <tr>
      <td>HITRUST CSF version 9, September 2017</td>
      <td>01.04 Network Access Control</td>
    </tr>
    <tr>
      <td>ISF, The Standard of Good Practice for Information Security 2016</td>
      <td>PA2.3 Mobile Device Connectivity; NC1.1 Network Device Configuration</td>
    </tr>
    <tr>
      <td>ISO/IEC 27002:2013/Cor.2:2015(E)</td>
      <td>13.1 Network security management</td>
    </tr>
    <tr>
      <td>National Institute of Standards and Technology Special Publication 800-53, Revision 5 (Draft), August 2017</td>
      <td>3.20 System and information integrity (SI-8)</td>
    </tr>
    <tr>
      <td>The CIS Critical Security Controls for Effective Cyber Defense Version 6.1, August 2016</td>
      <td>CSC 9: Limitation and Control of Network Ports, Protocols, and Services; CSC 11: Secure Configurations for Network Devices such as Firewalls, Routers, and Switches</td>
    </tr>
  </tbody>
</table>

### Management Practice

**DSS05.03 Manage endpoint security.**
Ensure that endpoints (e.g., laptop, desktop, server, and other mobile and network devices or software) are secured at a level that is equal to or greater than the defined security requirements for the information processed, stored or transmitted.

### Activities

<table>
  <thead>
    <tr>
      <th>Activities</th>
      <th>Capability Level</th>
    </tr>
  </thead>
  <tbody>
    <tr>
      <td>1. Configure operating systems in a secure manner.</td>
      <td>2</td>
    </tr>
    <tr>
      <td>2. Implement device lockdown mechanisms.</td>
      <td></td>
    </tr>
    <tr>
      <td>3. Manage remote access and control (e.g., mobile devices, teleworking).</td>
      <td></td>
    </tr>
    <tr>
      <td>4. Manage network configuration in a secure manner.</td>
      <td></td>
    </tr>
    <tr>
      <td>5. Implement network traffic filtering on endpoint devices.</td>
      <td></td>
    </tr>
    <tr>
      <td>6. Protect system integrity.</td>
      <td></td>
    </tr>
    <tr>
      <td>7. Provide physical protection of endpoint devices.</td>
      <td></td>
    </tr>
    <tr>
      <td>8. Dispose of endpoint devices securely.</td>
      <td></td>
    </tr>
    <tr>
      <td>9. Manage malicious access through email and web browsers. For example, block certain websites and deactivate click-through on links for smartphones.</td>
      <td></td>
    </tr>
    <tr>
      <td>10. Encrypt information in storage according to its classification.</td>
      <td>3</td>
    </tr>
  </tbody>
</table>

&lt;page_number&gt;258&lt;/page_number&gt;

## Page 259

CHAPTER 4
COBIT GOVERNANCE AND MANAGEMENT OBJECTIVES—DETAILED GUIDANCE

A. Component: Process (cont.)

<table>
<thead>
<tr>
<th>Related Guidance (Standards, Frameworks, Compliance Requirements)</th>
<th>Detailed Reference</th>
</tr>
</thead>
<tbody>
<tr>
<td>CMMI Cybermaturity Platform, 2018</td>
<td>IP.MM Apply Mobile Device Management; TP.MP Apply Media Protection; DP.DP Detect Mobile Code and Browser Protection</td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td>ISF, The Standard of Good Practice for Information Security 2016</td>
<td>PM1.3 Remote Working; PA2.1 Mobile Device Configuration; PA2.4 Employee-owned Devices; PA2.5 Portable Storage Devices; NC1.6 Remote Maintenance</td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td>National Institute of Standards and Technology Special Publication 800-53, Revision 5 (Draft), August 2017</td>
<td>3.4 Assessment, authorization and monitoring (CA-8, CA-9); 3.19 System and communications protection (SC-10)</td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td>The CIS Critical Security Controls for Effective Cyber Defense Version 6.1, August 2016</td>
<td>CSC 3: Secure Configurations for Hardware and Software on Mobile Devices, Laptops, Workstations, and Servers; CSC 7: Email and Web Browser Protections</td>
</tr>
</tbody>
</table>

<table>
<thead>
<tr>
<th>Management Practice</th>
<th>Example Metrics</th>
</tr>
</thead>
<tbody>
<tr>
<td><strong>DSS05.04 Manage user identity and logical access.</strong><br>Ensure that all users have information access rights in accordance with business requirements. Coordinate with business units that manage their own access rights within business processes.</td>
<td>a. Average time between change and update of accounts<br>b. Number of accounts (vs. number of authorized users/staff)<br>c. Number of incidents relating to unauthorized access to information</td>
</tr>
</tbody>
</table>

<table>
<thead>
<tr>
<th>Activities</th>
<th>Capability Level</th>
</tr>
</thead>
<tbody>
<tr>
<td>1. Maintain user access rights in accordance with business function, process requirements and security policies. Align the management of identities and access rights to the defined roles and responsibilities, based on least-privilege, need-to-have and need-to-know principles.</td>
<td>2</td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td>2. Administer all changes to access rights (creation, modifications and deletions) in a timely manner based only on approved and documented transactions authorized by designated management individuals.</td>
<td>3</td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td>3. Segregate, reduce to the minimum number necessary and actively manage privileged user accounts. Ensure monitoring on all activity on these accounts.</td>
<td></td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td>4. Uniquely identify all information processing activities by functional roles. Coordinate with business units to ensure that all roles are consistently defined, including roles that are defined by the business itself within business process applications.</td>
<td></td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td>5. Authenticate all access to information assets based on the individual’s role or business rules. Coordinate with business units that manage authentication within applications used in business processes to ensure that authentication controls have been properly administered.</td>
<td></td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td>6. Ensure that all users (internal, external and temporary) and their activity on IT systems (business application, IT infrastructure, system operations, development and maintenance) are uniquely identifiable.</td>
<td></td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td>7. Maintain an audit trail of access to information depending upon its sensitivity and regulatory requirements.</td>
<td>4</td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td>8. Perform regular management review of all accounts and related privileges.</td>
<td></td>
</tr>
</tbody>
</table>

<table>
<thead>
<tr>
<th>Related Guidance (Standards, Frameworks, Compliance Requirements)</th>
<th>Detailed Reference</th>
</tr>
</thead>
<tbody>
<tr>
<td>HITRUST CSF version 9, September 2017</td>
<td>10.03 Cryptographic Controls</td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td>ISF, The Standard of Good Practice for Information Security 2016</td>
<td>PM1.1 Employment Life Cycle; SA1 Access Management</td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td>ISO/IEC 27002:2013/Cor.2:2015(E)</td>
<td>7.3 Termination and change of employment; 9. Access control</td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td>ITIL V3, 2011</td>
<td>Service Operation, 4.5 Access Management</td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td>National Institute of Standards and Technology Special Publication 800-53, Revision 5 (Draft), August 2017</td>
<td>3.1 Access control (AC-11, AC-12); 3.11 Media protection (MP-2, MP-4, MP-7); 3.13 Physical and environmental protection (PE-2, PE-3, PE-6)</td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td>The CIS Critical Security Controls for Effective Cyber Defense Version 6.1, August 2016</td>
<td>CSC 1: Inventory of Authorized and Unauthorized Devices; CSC 2: Inventory of Authorized and Unauthorized Software; CSC 5: Controlled Use of Administrative Privileges; CSC 16: Account Monitoring and Control</td>
</tr>
</tbody>
</table>

## Page 260

# COBIT® 2019 FRAMEWORK: GOVERNANCE AND MANAGEMENT OBJECTIVES

**A. Component: Process (cont.)**

<table>
<thead>
<tr>
<th>Management Practice</th>
<th>Example Metrics</th>
</tr>
</thead>
<tbody>
<tr>
<td><strong>DSS05.05 Manage physical access to I&amp;T assets.</strong><br>Define and implement procedures (including emergency procedures) to grant, limit and revoke access to premises, buildings and areas, according to business need. Access to premises, buildings and areas should be justified, authorized, logged and monitored. This requirement applies to all persons entering the premises, including staff, temporary staff, clients, vendors, visitors or any other third party.</td>
<td>a. Average rating for physical security assessments<br>b. Number of physical information security-related incidents</td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td><strong>Activities</strong></td>
<td></td>
<td><strong>Capability Level</strong></td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td>1. Log and monitor all entry points to IT sites. Register all visitors, including contractors and vendors, to the site.</td>
<td></td>
<td>2</td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td>2. Ensure all personnel display properly approved identification at all times.</td>
<td></td>
<td></td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td>3. Require visitors to be escorted at all times while on-site.</td>
<td></td>
<td></td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td>4. Restrict and monitor access to sensitive IT sites by establishing perimeter restrictions, such as fences, walls and security devices on interior and exterior doors.</td>
<td></td>
<td></td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td>5. Manage requests to allow appropriately authorized access to the computing facilities.</td>
<td></td>
<td>3</td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td>6. Ensure that access profiles remain current. Base access to IT sites (server rooms, buildings, areas or zones) on job function and responsibilities.</td>
<td></td>
<td></td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td>7. Conduct regular physical information security awareness training.</td>
<td></td>
<td></td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td><strong>Related Guidance (Standards, Frameworks, Compliance Requirements)</strong></td>
<td><strong>Detailed Reference</strong></td>
<td></td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td>CMMI Cybermaturity Platform, 2018</td>
<td>AC.MA Manage Access; ID.DI Determine Impacts</td>
<td></td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td>HITRUST CSF version 9, September 2017</td>
<td>01.01 Business Requirement for Access Control; 01.02 Authorized Access to Information Systems; 02.0 Human Resources Security</td>
<td></td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td>ISF, The Standard of Good Practice for Information Security 2016</td>
<td>NC1.2 Physical Network Management</td>
<td></td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td>ISO/IEC 27002:2013/Cor.2:2015(E)</td>
<td>11. Physical and environmental security</td>
<td></td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td><strong>Management Practice</strong></td>
<td><strong>Example Metrics</strong></td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td><strong>DSS05.06 Manage sensitive documents and output devices.</strong><br>Establish appropriate physical safeguards, accounting practices and inventory management regarding sensitive I&amp;T assets, such as special forms, negotiable instruments, special-purpose printers or security tokens.</td>
<td>a. Number of stolen output devices<br>b. Percent of sensitive documents and output devices identified in inventory</td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td><strong>Activities</strong></td>
<td></td>
<td><strong>Capability Level</strong></td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td>1. Establish procedures to govern the receipt, use, removal and disposal of sensitive documents and output devices into, within, and outside of the enterprise.</td>
<td></td>
<td>2</td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td>2. Ensure cryptographic controls are in place to protect sensitive electronically stored information.</td>
<td></td>
<td></td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td>3. Assign access privileges to sensitive documents and output devices based on the least-privilege principle, balancing risk and business requirements.</td>
<td></td>
<td>3</td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td>4. Establish an inventory of sensitive documents and output devices, and conduct regular reconciliations.</td>
<td></td>
<td></td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td>5. Establish appropriate physical safeguards over sensitive documents.</td>
<td></td>
<td></td>
</tr>
</tbody>
</table>

<tbody>
<tr>
<td>DSS05.07 Manage vulnerabilities and monitor the infrastructure for security-related events.<br>Using a portfolio of tools and technologies (e.g., intrusion detection tools), manage vulnerabilities and monitor the infrastructure for unauthorized access. Ensure that security tools, technologies and detection are integrated with general event monitoring and incident management.</td>
<td>a. Number of vulnerability tests carried out on perimeter devices<br>b. Number of vulnerabilities discovered during testing<br>c. Time taken to remediate any vulnerabilities<br>d. Percent of tickets created in a timely manner when monitoring systems identify potential security incidents</td>
</tr>
</tbody>
</table>

<table>
<thead>
<tr>
<th>Activities</th>
<th>Capability Level</th>
</tr>
</thead>
<tbody>
<tr>
<td>1. Continually use a portfolio of supported technologies, services and assets (e.g., vulnerability scanners, fuzzers and sniffers, protocol analyzers) to identify information security vulnerabilities.</td>
<td>2</td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td>2. Define and communicate risk scenarios, so they can be easily recognized, and the likelihood and impact understood.</td>
<td></td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td>3. Regularly review the event logs for potential incidents.</td>
<td></td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td>4. Ensure that security--related incident tickets are created in a timely manner when monitoring identifies potential incidents.</td>
<td></td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td>5. Log security-related events and retain records for appropriate period.</td>
<td>3</td>
</tr>
</tbody>
</table>

## Page 265

# CHAPTER 4
## COBIT GOVERNANCE AND MANAGEMENT OBJECTIVES—DETAILED GUIDANCE

<table>
  <tr>
    <td>
      **Domain:** Deliver, Service and Support<br>
      **Management Objective:** DSS06 - Managed Business Process Controls
    </td>
    <td>
      **Focus Area:** COBIT Core Model
    </td>
  </tr>
</table>

### Description
Define and maintain appropriate business process controls to ensure that information related to and processed by in-house or outsourced business processes satisfies all relevant information control requirements. Identify the relevant information control requirements. Manage and operate adequate input, throughput and output controls (application controls) to ensure that information and information processing satisfy these requirements.

### Purpose
Maintain information integrity and the security of information assets handled within business processes in the enterprise or its outsourced operation.

The management objective supports the achievement of a set of primary enterprise and alignment goals:

<table>
  <tr>
    <td>
      **Enterprise Goals**
    </td>
    <td>
      <table>
        <tr>
          <td>
            • EG01 Portfolio of competitive products and services<br>
            • EG05 Customer-oriented service culture<br>
            • EG08 Optimization of internal business process functionality<br>
            • EG12 Managed digital transformation programs
          </td>
          <td>
            <br>
            <br>
            <br>
            <br>
          </td>
        </tr>
      </table>
    </td>
    <td>
      <table>
        <tr>
          <td>
            <strong>Alignment Goals</strong>
          </td>
          <td>
            AG08 Enabling and supporting business processes by integrating applications and technology
          </td>
        </tr>
      </table>
    </td>
  </tr>
  <tr>
    <td>
      **Example Metrics for Enterprise Goals**
    </td>
    <td>
      <table>
        <tr>
          <td>
            EG01
          </td>
          <td>
            a. Percent of products and services that meet or exceed targets in revenues and/or market share<br>
            b. Percent of products and services that meet or exceed customer satisfaction targets<br>
            c. Percent of products and services that provide competitive advantage<br>
            d. Time to market for new products and services
          </td>
        </tr>
        <tr>
          <td>
            EG05
          </td>
          <td>
            a. Number of customer service disruptions<br>
            b. Percent of business stakeholders satisfied that customer service delivery meets agreed levels<br>
            c. Number of customer complaints<br>
            d. Trend of customer satisfaction survey results
          </td>
        </tr>
        <tr>
          <td>
            EG08
          </td>
          <td>
            a. Satisfaction levels of board and executive management with business process capabilities<br>
            b. Satisfaction levels of customers with service delivery capabilities<br>
            c. Satisfaction levels of suppliers with supply chain capabilities
          </td>
        </tr>
        <tr>
          <td>
            EG12
          </td>
          <td>
            a. Number of programs on time and within budget<br>
            b. Percent of stakeholders satisfied with program delivery<br>
            c. Percent of business transformation programs stopped<br>
            d. Percent of business transformation programs with regular reported status updates
          </td>
        </tr>
      </table>
    </td>
    <td>
      **Example Metrics for Alignment Goals**
    </td>
  </tr>
  <tr>
    <td>
      <br>
      <br>
      <br>
      <br>
    </td>
    <td>
      <table>
        <tr>
          <td>
            AG08
          </td>
          <td>
            a. Time to execute business services or processes<br>
            b. Number of I&T-enabled business programs delayed or incurring additional cost due to technology-integration issues<br>
            c. Number of business process changes that need to be delayed or reworked because of technology-integration issues<br>
            d. Number of applications or critical infrastructures operating in silos and not integrated
          </td>
        </tr>
      </table>
    </td>
    <td>
      <br>
      <br>
      <br>
      <br>
    </td>
  </tr>
</table>

### A. Component: Process

<table>
  <tr>
    <td>
      **Management Practice**
    </td>
    <td>
      **Example Metrics**
    </td>
  </tr>
  <tr>
    <td>
      **DSS06.01 Align control activities embedded in business processes with enterprise objectives.<br><br>Continually assess and monitor the execution of business process activities and related controls (based on enterprise risk), to ensure that processing controls align with business needs.**
    </td>
    <td>
      a. Percent of completed inventory of critical processes and key controls<br>
      b. Percent of processing controls aligned with business needs
    </td>
  </tr>
</table>
