## Page 266

# COBIT® 2019 FRAMEWORK: GOVERNANCE AND MANAGEMENT OBJECTIVES

## A. Component: Process (cont.)

<table>
<thead>
<tr>
<th>Activities</th>
<th>Capability Level</th>
</tr>
</thead>
<tbody>
<tr>
<td>1. Identify and document the necessary control activities for key business processes to satisfy control requirements for strategic, operational, reporting and compliance objectives.</td>
<td>2</td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td>2. Prioritize control activities based on the inherent risk to the business. Identify key controls.</td>
<td></td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td>3. Ensure ownership of key control activities.</td>
<td></td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td>4. Implement automated controls.</td>
<td>3</td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td>5. Continually monitor control activities on an end-to-end basis to identify opportunities for improvement.</td>
<td>4</td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td>6. Continually improve the design and operation of business process controls.</td>
<td>5</td>
</tr>
</tbody>
</table>

<table>
<thead>
<tr>
<th>Related Guidance (Standards, Frameworks, Compliance Requirements)</th>
<th>Detailed Reference</th>
</tr>
</thead>
<tbody>
<tr>
<td>National Institute of Standards and Technology Special Publication 800-37, Revision 2 (Draft), May 2018</td>
<td>3.1 Preparation (Task 10, 11)</td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td>The CIS Critical Security Controls for Effective Cyber Defense Version 6.1, August 2016</td>
<td>CSC 14: Controlled Access Based on the Need to Know</td>
</tr>
</tbody>
</table>

<table>
<thead>
<tr>
<th>Management Practice</th>
<th>Example Metrics</th>
</tr>
</thead>
<tbody>
<tr>
<td>DSS06.02 Control the processing of information.<br>Operate the execution of the business process activities and related controls, based on enterprise risk. Ensure that information processing is valid, complete, accurate, timely and secure (i.e., reflects legitimate and authorized business use).</td>
<td>a. Number of incidents and audit report findings indicating failure of key controls<br>b. Percent of coverage of key controls within test plans</td>
</tr>
</tbody>
</table>

<table>
<thead>
<tr>
<th>Activities</th>
<th>Capability Level</th>
</tr>
</thead>
<tbody>
<tr>
<td>1. Authenticate the originator of transactions and verify that the individual has the authority to originate the transaction.</td>
<td>2</td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td>2. Ensure adequate segregation of duties regarding the origination and approval of transactions.</td>
<td></td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td>3. Verify that transactions are accurate, complete and valid. Controls may include sequence, limit, range, validity, reasonableness, table look-ups, existence, key verification, check digit, completeness, duplicate and logical relationship checks, and time edits. Validation criteria and parameters should be subject to periodic reviews and confirmations. Validate input data and edit or, where applicable, send back for correction as close to the point of origination as possible.</td>
<td>3</td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td>4. Without compromising original transaction authorization levels, correct and resubmit data that were erroneously input. Where appropriate for reconstruction, retain original source documents for the appropriate amount of time.</td>
<td></td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td>5. Maintain the integrity and validity of data throughout the processing cycle. Ensure that detection of erroneous transactions does not disrupt processing of valid transactions.</td>
<td></td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td>6. Handle output in an authorized manner, deliver it to the appropriate recipient and protect the information during transmission. Verify the accuracy and completeness of the output.</td>
<td></td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td>7. Maintain the integrity of data during unexpected interruptions in business processing. Confirm data integrity after processing failures.</td>
<td></td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td>8. Before passing transaction data between internal applications and business/operational functions (inside or outside the enterprise), check for proper addressing, authenticity of origin and integrity of content. Maintain authenticity and integrity during transmission or transport.</td>
<td></td>
</tr>
</tbody>
</table>

<table>
<thead>
<tr>
<th>Related Guidance (Standards, Frameworks, Compliance Requirements)</th>
<th>Detailed Reference</th>
</tr>
</thead>
<tbody>
<tr>
<td>HITRUST CSF version 9, September 2017</td>
<td>13.01 Openness and Transparency; 13.02 Individual Choice and Participation</td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td>ISF, The Standard of Good Practice for Information Security 2016</td>
<td>BA1.4 Information Validation</td>
</tr>
</tbody>
</table>

## Page 267

CHAPTER 4
COBIT GOVERNANCE AND MANAGEMENT OBJECTIVES—DETAILED GUIDANCE

A. Component: Process (cont.)

<table>
<thead>
<tr>
<th>Management Practice</th>
<th>Example Metrics</th>
</tr>
</thead>
<tbody>
<tr>
<td><strong>DSS06.03 Manage roles, responsibilities, access privileges and levels of authority.</strong><br>Manage business roles, responsibilities, levels of authority and segregation of duties needed to support the business process objectives. Authorize access to all information assets related to business information processes, including those under the custody of the business, IT and third parties. This ensures that the business knows where the data are and who is handling data on its behalf.</td>
<td>a. Number of incidents and audit findings due to access or separation-of-duties violations<br>b. Percent of business process roles with assigned access rights and levels of authority<br>c. Percent of business process roles with clear separation of duties</td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td><strong>Activities</strong></td>
<td></td>
<td><strong>Capability Level</strong></td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td>1. Allocate roles and responsibilities based on approved job descriptions and business process activities.</td>
<td></td>
<td>2</td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td>2. Allocate levels of authority for approval of transactions, transaction limits and any other decisions relating to the business process, based on approved job roles.</td>
<td></td>
<td></td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td>3. Allocate roles for sensitive activities so there is a clear segregation of duties.</td>
<td></td>
<td></td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td>4. Allocate access rights and privileges based on the minimum that is required to perform job activities, based on pre-defined job roles. Remove or revise access rights immediately if the job role changes or a staff member leaves the business process area. Periodically review to ensure that the access is appropriate for the current threats, risk, technology and business need.</td>
<td></td>
<td>3</td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td>5. On a regular basis, provide awareness and training regarding roles and responsibilities so that everyone understands their responsibilities; the importance of controls; and the security, integrity, confidentiality and privacy of company information in all its forms.</td>
<td></td>
<td></td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td>6. Ensure administrative privileges are sufficiently and effectively secured, tracked and controlled to prevent misuse.</td>
<td></td>
<td></td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td>7. Periodically review access control definitions, logs and exception reports. Ensure that all access privileges are valid and aligned with current staff members and their allocated roles.</td>
<td></td>
<td>4</td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td><strong>Related Guidance (Standards, Frameworks, Compliance Requirements)</strong></td>
<td><strong>Detailed Reference</strong></td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td>HITRUST CSF version 9, September 2017</td>
<td>13.04 Collection, Use and Disclosure</td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td>ISO/IEC 27002:2013/Cor.2:2015(E)</td>
<td>7. Human resource security</td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td>The CIS Critical Security Controls for Effective Cyber Defense Version 6.1, August 2016</td>
<td>CSC 5: Controlled Use of Administrative Privileges</td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td><strong>Management Practice</strong></td>
<td><strong>Example Metrics</strong></td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td><strong>DSS06.04 Manage errors and exceptions.</strong><br>Manage business process exceptions and errors and facilitate remediation, executing defined corrective actions and escalating as necessary. This treatment of exceptions and errors provides assurance of the accuracy and integrity of the business information process.</td>
<td>a. Frequency of processing inefficiencies due to incomplete data entry<br>b. Number of errors detected in a timely manner<br>c. Number of data processing errors that were efficiently remediated</td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td><strong>Activities</strong></td>
<td></td>
<td><strong>Capability Level</strong></td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td>1. Review errors, exceptions and deviations.</td>
<td></td>
<td>2</td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td>2. Follow up, correct, approve and resubmit source documents and transactions.</td>
<td></td>
<td></td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td>3. Maintain evidence of remedial actions.</td>
<td></td>
<td></td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td>4. Define and maintain procedures to assign ownership for errors and exceptions, correct errors, override errors and handle out-of-balance conditions.</td>
<td></td>
<td>3</td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td>5. Report relevant business information process errors in a timely manner to perform root cause and trending analysis.</td>
<td></td>
<td>4</td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td><strong>Related Guidance (Standards, Frameworks, Compliance Requirements)</strong></td>
<td><strong>Detailed Reference</strong></td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td>No related guidance for this management practice</td>
<td></td>
</tr>
</tbody>
</table>

## Page 268

# COBIT® 2019 FRAMEWORK: GOVERNANCE AND MANAGEMENT OBJECTIVES

<table>
  <thead>
    <tr>
      <th colspan="3">A. Component: Process (cont.)</th>
    </tr>
    <tr>
      <th>Management Practice</th>
      <th>Example Metrics</th>
      <th></th>
    </tr>
  </thead>
  <tbody>
    <tr>
      <td>
        **DSS06.05 Ensure traceability and accountability for information events.**<br>
        Ensure that business information can be traced to an originating business event and associated with accountable parties. This discoverability provides assurance that business information is reliable and has been processed in accordance with defined objectives.
      </td>
      <td>
        a. Number of incidents in which transaction history cannot be recovered<br>
        b. Percent of completeness of traceable transaction log
      </td>
      <td></td>
    </tr>
    <tr>
      <td>
        **Activities**
      </td>
      <td></td>
      <td>Capability Level</td>
    </tr>
    <tr>
      <td>
        1. Capture source information, supporting evidence and the record of transactions.<br>
        2. Define retention requirements, based on business requirements, to meet operational, financial reporting and compliance needs.<br>
        3. Dispose of source information, supporting evidence and the record of transactions in accordance with the retention policy.
      </td>
      <td></td>
      <td>2<br>3</td>
    </tr>
    <tr>
      <td>
        **Related Guidance (Standards, Frameworks, Compliance Requirements)**
      </td>
      <td>
        No related guidance for this management practice
      </td>
      <td></td>
    </tr>
    <tr>
      <td>
        **Management Practice**
      </td>
      <td>
        **Example Metrics**
      </td>
      <td></td>
    </tr>
    <tr>
      <td>
        **DSS06.06 Secure information assets.**<br>
        Secure information assets accessible by the business through approved methods, including information in electronic form (e.g., portable media devices, user applications and storage devices, or other methods that create new assets in any form), information in physical form (e.g., source documents or output reports) and information during transit. This benefits the business by providing end-to-end safeguarding of information.
      </td>
      <td>
        a. Cases of sensitive transaction data delivered to wrong recipient<br>
        b. Frequency of compromised integrity of critical data
      </td>
      <td></td>
    </tr>
    <tr>
      <td>
        **Activities**
      </td>
      <td></td>
      <td>Capability Level</td>
    </tr>
    <tr>
      <td>
        1. Restrict use, distribution and physical access of information according to its classification.<br>
        2. Provide acceptable use awareness and training.<br>
        3. Apply data classification and acceptable use and security policies and procedures to protect information assets under the control of the business.<br>
        4. Identify and implement processes, tools and techniques to reasonably verify compliance.<br>
        5. Report to business and other stakeholders on violations and deviations.
      </td>
      <td></td>
      <td>2<br>3<br>4</td>
    </tr>
    <tr>
      <td>
        **Related Guidance (Standards, Frameworks, Compliance Requirements)**
      </td>
      <td>
        CMMI Cybermaturity Platform, 2018<br><br>
        The CIS Critical Security Controls for Effective Cyber Defense Version 6.1, August 2016
      </td>
      <td>
        AC.MP Manage Access Permissions<br><br>
        CSC 18: Application Software Security
      </td>
    </tr>
  </tbody>
</table>

<th>Relevant Policy</th>
<th>Policy Description</th>
<th>Related Guidance</th>
<th>Detailed Reference</th>
</tr>
</thead>
<tbody>
<tr>
<td>Business controls guidance</td>
<td>Defines business process controls to ensure proper control and reduce risk of fraud and errors. Identifies manual controls to protect documents (e.g., source, input, processing and output documents); identifies supervisory controls to review the flow of documents and ensure correct processing. Includes I&T general controls (e.g., physical security, access and authentication, and change management) and application controls (e.g., edit checking, system configuration and security settings).</td>
<td></td>
<td></td>
</tr>
</tbody>
</table>

<table>
<thead>
<tr>
<th>Key Culture Elements</th>
<th>Related Guidance</th>
<th>Detailed Reference</th>
</tr>
</thead>
<tbody>
<tr>
<td>Create a culture that embraces the need for sound controls in business processes, building them into applications in development or requiring them in applications bought or accessed as a service. Encourage all employees to have a controls consciousness to protect all assets of the organization (e.g., paper records and facilities).</td>
<td></td>
<td></td>
</tr>
</tbody>
</table>

* Automated application controls
* Event log auditing tools

## Page 273

# CHAPTER 4
## COBIT GOVERNANCE AND MANAGEMENT OBJECTIVES—DETAILED GUIDANCE

<table>
  <tr>
    <td>
      **Domain:** Monitor, Evaluate and Assess<br>
      **Management Objective:** MEA01 – Managed Performance and Conformance Monitoring
    </td>
    <td>
      **Focus Area:** COBIT Core Model
    </td>
  </tr>
</table>

### Description
Collect, validate and evaluate enterprise and alignment goals and metrics. Monitor that processes and practices are performing against agreed performance and conformance goals and metrics. Provide reporting that is systematic and timely.

### Purpose
Provide transparency of performance and conformance and drive achievement of goals.

The management objective supports the achievement of a set of primary enterprise and alignment goals:

<table>
  <tr>
    <td>
      **Enterprise Goals**
    </td>
    <td>
      &lt;img&gt;Right arrow icon&lt;/img&gt;
    </td>
    <td>
      **Alignment Goals**
    </td>
  </tr>
  <tr>
    <td>
      • EG01 Portfolio of competitive products and services<br>
      • EG04 Quality of financial information<br>
      • EG07 Quality of management information<br>
      • EG08 Optimization of internal business process functionality
    </td>
    <td>
    </td>
    <td>
      • AG05 Delivery of I&T services in line with business requirements<br>
      • AG10 Quality of I&T management information
    </td>
  </tr>
  <tr>
    <td>
      **Example Metrics for Enterprise Goals**
    </td>
    <td>
    </td>
    <td>
      **Example Metrics for Alignment Goals**
    </td>
  </tr>
  <tr>
    <td>
      EG01
      <ul>
        <li>a. Percent of products and services that meet or exceed targets in revenues and/or market share</li>
        <li>b. Percent of products and services that meet or exceed customer satisfaction targets</li>
        <li>c. Percent of products and services that provide competitive advantage</li>
        <li>d. Time to market for new products and services</li>
      </ul>
    </td>
    <td>
    </td>
    <td>
      AG05
      <ul>
        <li>a. Percent of business stakeholders satisfied that I&T service delivery meets agreed service levels</li>
        <li>b. Number of business disruptions due to I&T service incidents</li>
        <li>c. Percent of users satisfied with the quality of I&T service delivery</li>
      </ul>
    </td>
  </tr>
  <tr>
    <td>
      EG04
      <ul>
        <li>a. Satisfaction survey of key stakeholders regarding the transparency, understanding and accuracy of enterprise financial information</li>
        <li>b. Cost of noncompliance with finance-related regulations</li>
      </ul>
    </td>
    <td>
    </td>
    <td>
      AG10
      <ul>
        <li>a. Level of user satisfaction with quality, timeliness and availability of I&T-related management information, taking into account available resources</li>
        <li>b. Ratio and extent of erroneous business decisions in which erroneous or unavailable I&T-related information was a key factor</li>
        <li>c. Percentage of information meeting quality criteria</li>
      </ul>
    </td>
  </tr>
  <tr>
    <td>
      EG07
      <ul>
        <li>a. Degree of board and executive management satisfaction with decision-making information</li>
        <li>b. Number of incidents caused by incorrect business decisions based on inaccurate information</li>
        <li>c. Time to provide information supporting effective business decisions</li>
        <li>d. Timeliness of management information</li>
      </ul>
    </td>
    <td>
    </td>
    <td>
    </td>
  </tr>
  <tr>
    <td>
      EG08
      <ul>
        <li>a. Satisfaction levels of board and executive management with business process capabilities</li>
        <li>b. Satisfaction levels of customers with service delivery capabilities</li>
        <li>c. Satisfaction levels of suppliers with supply chain capabilities</li>
      </ul>
    </td>
    <td>
    </td>
    <td>
    </td>
  </tr>
</table>

### A. Component: Process

<table>
  <tr>
    <td>
      **Management Practice**
    </td>
    <td>
      **Example Metrics**
    </td>
  </tr>
  <tr>
    <td>
      **MEA01.01 Establish a monitoring approach.<br>
      Engage with stakeholders to establish and maintain a monitoring approach to define the objectives, scope and method for measuring business solution and service delivery and contribution to enterprise objectives. Integrate this approach with the corporate performance management system.
    </td>
    <td>
      a. Percent of processes with defined goals and metrics<br>
      b. Percent of integration of monitoring approach within corporate performance management system
    </td>
  </tr>
</table>

Monitor, Evaluate and Assess

&lt;page_number&gt;273&lt;/page_number&gt;

## Page 274

# COBIT® 2019 FRAMEWORK: GOVERNANCE AND MANAGEMENT OBJECTIVES

## A. Component: Process (cont.)

<table>
<thead>
<tr>
<th>Activities</th>
<th>Capability Level</th>
</tr>
</thead>
<tbody>
<tr>
<td>1. Identify stakeholders (e.g., management, process owners and users).</td>
<td>2</td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td>2. Engage with stakeholders and communicate the enterprise requirements and objectives for monitoring, aggregating and reporting, using common definitions (e.g., business glossary, metadata and taxonomy), baselining and benchmarking.</td>
<td></td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td>3. Align and continually maintain the monitoring and evaluation approach with the enterprise approach and the tools to be used for data gathering and enterprise reporting (e.g., business intelligence applications).</td>
<td></td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td>4. Agree on the types of goals and metrics (e.g., conformance, performance, value, risk), taxonomy (classification and relationships between goals and metrics) and data (evidence) retention.</td>
<td></td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td>5. Request, prioritize and allocate resources for monitoring, consider appropriateness, efficiency, effectiveness and confidentiality.</td>
<td></td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td>6. Periodically validate the approach used and identify new or changed stakeholders, requirements and resources.</td>
<td>3</td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td>7. Agree on a life cycle management and change control process for monitoring and reporting. Include improvement opportunities for reporting, metrics, approach, baselining and benchmarking.</td>
<td></td>
</tr>
</tbody>
</table>

<table>
<thead>
<tr>
<th>Related Guidance (Standards, Frameworks, Compliance Requirements)</th>
<th>Detailed Reference</th>
</tr>
</thead>
<tbody>
<tr>
<td>CMMI Data Management Maturity Model, 2014</td>
<td>Supporting Processes - Measurement and Analysis</td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td>SF, The Standard of Good Practice for Information Security 2016</td>
<td>SI2 Security Performance</td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td>ISO/IEC 27001:2013/Cor.2:2015(E)</td>
<td>9.1 Monitoring, measurement, analysis and evaluation</td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td>ISO/IEC 27004:2016(E)</td>
<td>6. Characteristics; 7. Types of measures; 8. Processes</td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td>ISO/IEC 38500:2015(E)</td>
<td>5.5 Principle 4: Performance; 5.6 Principle 5: Conformance</td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td>National Institute of Standards and Technology Special Publication 800-37, Revision 2 (Draft), May 2018</td>
<td>3.1 Preparation (Task 13); 3.3 Selection (Task 2); 3.7 Monitoring (Task 1)</td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td>National Institute of Standards and Technology Special Publication 800-53, Revision 5 (Draft), August 2017</td>
<td>3.4 Assessment, authorization and monitoring (CA-2, CA-7); 3.20 System and information integrity (SI-4)</td>
</tr>
</tbody>
</table>

<table>
<thead>
<tr>
<th>Management Practice</th>
<th>Example Metrics</th>
</tr>
</thead>
<tbody>
<tr>
<td>MEA01.02 Set performance and conformance targets.<br/>Work with stakeholders to define, periodically review, update and approve performance and conformance targets within the performance measurement system.</td>
<td>a. Percent of goals and metrics approved by stakeholders<br/>b. Percent of processes with effectiveness of goals and metrics reviewed and improved</td>
</tr>
</tbody>
</table>

<table>
<thead>
<tr>
<th>Activities</th>
<th>Capability Level</th>
</tr>
</thead>
<tbody>
<tr>
<td>1. Define the goals and metrics. Periodically review them with stakeholders to identify any significant missing items and define reasonableness of targets and tolerances.</td>
<td>2</td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td>2. Evaluate whether the goals and metrics are adequate, that is, specific, measurable, achievable, relevant and time-bound (SMART).</td>
<td></td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td>3. Communicate proposed changes to performance and conformance targets and tolerances (relating to metrics) with key due diligence stakeholders (e.g., legal, audit, HR, ethics, compliance, finance).</td>
<td></td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td>4. Publish changed targets and tolerances to users of this information.</td>
<td></td>
</tr>
</tbody>
</table>

<table>
<thead>
<tr>
<th>Related Guidance (Standards, Frameworks, Compliance Requirements)</th>
<th>Detailed Reference</th>
</tr>
</thead>
<tbody>
<tr>
<td>CMMI Data Management Maturity Model, 2014</td>
<td>Supporting Processes - Process Management</td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td>National Institute of Standards and Technology Special Publication 800-53, Revisionv5 (Draft), August 2017</td>
<td>3.4 Assessment, authorization and monitoring (CA-5)</td>
</tr>
</tbody>
</table>

<table>
<thead>
<tr>
<th>Management Practice</th>
<th>Example Metrics</th>
</tr>
</thead>
<tbody>
<tr>
<td>MEA01.03 Collect and process performance and conformance data.<br/>Collect and process timely and accurate data aligned with enterprise approaches.</td>
<td>a. Percent of critical processes monitored<br/>b. Percent of controls environment that is monitored, benchmarked and improved to meet organizational objectives</td>
</tr>
</tbody>
</table>

<footer>Monitor, Evaluate and Assess</footer>
&lt;page_number&gt;274&lt;/page_number&gt;

## Page 275

CHAPTER 4
COBIT GOVERNANCE AND MANAGEMENT OBJECTIVES—DETAILED GUIDANCE

A. Component: Process (cont.)

<table>
<thead>
<tr>
<th>Activities</th>
<th>Capability Level</th>
</tr>
</thead>
<tbody>
<tr>
<td>1. Collect data from defined processes (automated, where possible).</td>
<td>2</td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td>2. Assess efficiency (effort in relation to insight provided) and appropriateness (usefulness and meaning) of collected data and validate the data's integrity (accuracy and completeness).</td>
<td></td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td>3. Aggregate data to support measurement of agreed metrics.</td>
<td></td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td>4. Align aggregated data to the enterprise reporting approach and objectives.</td>
<td>3</td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td>5. Use suitable tools and systems for the processing and analysis of data.</td>
<td>4</td>
</tr>
</tbody>
</table>

<table>
<thead>
<tr>
<th>Related Guidance (Standards, Frameworks, Compliance Requirements)</th>
<th>Detailed Reference</th>
</tr>
</thead>
<tbody>
<tr>
<td>National Institute of Standards and Technology Special Publication 800-53, Revision 5 (Draft), August 2017</td>
<td>3.20 System and information integrity (SI-2)</td>
</tr>
</tbody>
</table>

<table>
<thead>
<tr>
<th>Management Practice</th>
<th>Example Metrics</th>
</tr>
</thead>
<tbody>
<tr>
<td>MEA01.04 Analyze and report performance.<br>Periodically review and report performance against targets. Use a method that provides a succinct all-around view of I&T performance and fits within the enterprise monitoring system.</td>
<td>a. Percent of goals and metrics aligned to enterprise monitoring system<br>b. Percent of performance reports delivered as scheduled<br>c. Percent of processes with assured output meeting targets within tolerances</td>
</tr>
</tbody>
</table>

<table>
<thead>
<tr>
<th>Activities</th>
<th>Capability Level</th>
</tr>
</thead>
<tbody>
<tr>
<td>1. Design process performance reports that are concise, easy to understand, and tailored to various management needs and audiences. Facilitate effective, timely decision making (e.g., scorecards, traffic light reports). Ensure that the cause and effect between goals and metrics are communicated in an understandable manner.</td>
<td>3</td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td>2. Distribute reports to the relevant stakeholders.</td>
<td></td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td>3. Analyze the cause of deviations against targets, initiate remedial actions, assign responsibilities for remediation, and follow up. At appropriate times, review all deviations and search for root causes, where necessary. Document the issues for further guidance if the problem recurs. Document results.</td>
<td>4</td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td>4. Where feasible, integrate performance and compliance into individual staff members' performance objectives and link achievement of performance targets to the organizational reward compensation system.</td>
<td></td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td>5. Compare the performance values to internal targets and benchmarks and, where possible, to external benchmarks (industry and key competitors).</td>
<td></td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td>6. Analyze trends in performance and compliance and take appropriate action.</td>
<td></td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td>7. Recommend changes to the goals and metrics, where appropriate.</td>
<td>5</td>
</tr>
</tbody>
</table>

<table>
<thead>
<tr>
<th>Related Guidance (Standards, Frameworks, Compliance Requirements)</th>
<th>Detailed Reference</th>
</tr>
</thead>
<tbody>
<tr>
<td>CMMI Data Management Maturity Model, 2014</td>
<td>Supporting Processes - Measurement and Analysis</td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td>National Institute of Standards and Technology Special Publication 800-53, Revision 5 (Draft), August 2017</td>
<td>3.3 Audit and accountability (AU-6)</td>
</tr>
</tbody>
</table>

<table>
<thead>
<tr>
<th>Management Practice</th>
<th>Example Metrics</th>
</tr>
</thead>
<tbody>
<tr>
<td>MEA01.05 Ensure the implementation of corrective actions.<br>Assist stakeholders in identifying, initiating and tracking corrective actions to address anomalies.</td>
<td>a. Number of recurring anomalies<br>b. Number of corrective actions implemented</td>
</tr>
</tbody>
</table>

<table>
<thead>
<tr>
<th>Activities</th>
<th>Capability Level</th>
</tr>
</thead>
<tbody>
<tr>
<td>1. Review management responses, options and recommendations to address issues and major deviations.</td>
<td>2</td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td>2. Ensure that the assignment of responsibility for corrective action is maintained.</td>
<td></td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td>3. Track the results of actions committed.</td>
<td></td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td>4. Report the results to the stakeholders.</td>
<td></td>
</tr>
</tbody>
</table>

<table>
<thead>
<tr>
<th>Related Guidance (Standards, Frameworks, Compliance Requirements)</th>

## Page 278

# COBIT® 2019 FRAMEWORK: GOVERNANCE AND MANAGEMENT OBJECTIVES

## E. Component: Policies and Procedures

<table>
<thead>
<tr>
<th>Relevant Policy</th>
<th>Policy Description</th>
<th>Related Guidance</th>
<th>Detailed Reference</th>
</tr>
</thead>
<tbody>
<tr>
<td>Self-assessment policy</td>
<td>Provides guidance for management's responsibilities in assessing operations as part of the continuous improvement program. Often used to report internally to executives or board on current capabilities, progress and improvement, based on business requirements. Assessments may be used during or after a process improvement program (i.e., to assess progress after completing an improvement).</td>
<td></td>
<td></td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td>Whistle-blower policy</td>
<td>Encourages employees to raise concerns and questions in full confidence. Ensures employees that they will receive a response and be able to escalate concerns if they are not satisfied with the response. Assures that employees are protected when they raise issues and should not fear reprisal.</td>
<td></td>
<td></td>
</tr>
</tbody>
</table>

## F. Component: Culture, Ethics and Behavior

<table>

<tr>
<th>Key Culture Elements</th>
<th>Related Guidance</th>
<th>Detailed Reference</th>
</tr>
</thead>
<tbody>
<tr>
<td>To achieve the organization's goals and optimize performance, promote a culture of continuous improvement of business and I&T processes.</td>
<td></td>
<td></td>
</tr>
</tbody>
</table>

*   Self-assessment tools

## Page 279

# CHAPTER 4
## COBIT GOVERNANCE AND MANAGEMENT OBJECTIVES—DETAILED GUIDANCE

<table>
  <tr>
    <td><strong>Domain:</strong> Monitor, Evaluate and Assess<br><strong>Management Objective:</strong> MEA02 – Managed System of Internal Control</td>
    <td><strong>Focus Area:</strong> COBIT Core Model</td>
  </tr>
</table>

### Description
Continuously monitor and evaluate the control environment, including self-assessments and self-awareness. Enable management to identify control deficiencies and inefficiencies and to initiate improvement actions. Plan, organize and maintain standards for internal control assessment and process control effectiveness.

### Purpose
Obtain transparency for key stakeholders on the adequacy of the system of internal controls and thus provide trust in operations, confidence in the achievement of enterprise objectives and an adequate understanding of residual risk.

The management objective supports the achievement of a set of primary enterprise and alignment goals:

<table>
  <tr>
    <td><strong>Enterprise Goals</strong></td>
    <td><strong>Alignment Goals</strong></td>
  </tr>
  <tr>
    <td>
      • EG03 Compliance with external laws and regulations<br>
      • EG11 Compliance with internal policies
    </td>
    <td>
      AG11 I&T compliance with internal policies
    </td>
  </tr>
  <tr>
    <td><strong>Example Metrics for Enterprise Goals</strong></td>
    <td><strong>Example Metrics for Alignment Goals</strong></td>
  </tr>
  <tr>
    <td>
      EG03
      <ul>
        <li>a. Cost of regulatory noncompliance, including settlements and fines</li>
        <li>b. Number of regulatory noncompliance issues causing public comment or negative publicity</li>
        <li>c. Number of noncompliance matters noted by regulators</li>
        <li>d. Number of regulatory noncompliance issues relating to contractual agreements with business partners</li>
      </ul>
    </td>
    <td>
      AG11
      <ul>
        <li>a. Number of incidents related to noncompliance with I&T-related policies</li>
        <li>b. Number of exceptions to internal policies</li>
        <li>c. Frequency of policy review and update</li>
      </ul>
    </td>
  </tr>
  <tr>
    <td>
      EG11
      <ul>
        <li>a. Number of incidents related to noncompliance to policy</li>
        <li>b. Percent of stakeholders who understand policies</li>
        <li>c. Percent of policies supported by effective standards and working practices</li>
      </ul>
    </td>
    <td></td>
  </tr>
</table>

### A. Component: Process

<table>
  <tr>
    <td><strong>Management Practice</strong></td>
    <td><strong>Example Metrics</strong></td>
  </tr>
  <tr>
    <td>
      MEA02.01 Monitor internal controls.<br>
      Continuously monitor, benchmark and improve the I&T control environment and control framework to meet organizational objectives.
    </td>
    <td>
      a. Number of major internal control breaches<br>
      b. Percent of controls environment and framework continuously monitored, benchmarked and improved to meet organizational objectives
    </td>
  </tr>
  <tr>
    <td><strong>Activities</strong></td>
    <td><strong>Capability Level</strong></td>
  </tr>
  <tr>
    <td>
      1. Identify the boundaries of the internal control system. For example, consider how organizational internal controls take into account outsourced and/or offshore development or production activities.<br><br>
      2. Assess the status of external service providers’ internal controls. Confirm that service providers comply with legal and regulatory requirements and contractual obligations.<br><br>
      3. Perform internal control monitoring and evaluation activities based on organizational governance standards and industry-accepted frameworks and practices. Also include monitoring and evaluation of the efficiency and effectiveness of managerial supervisory activities.<br><br>
      4. Ensure that control exceptions are promptly reported, followed up and analyzed, and appropriate corrective actions are prioritized and implemented according to the risk management profile (e.g., classify certain exceptions as a key risk and others as a non-key risk).<br><br>
      5. Consider independent evaluations of the internal control system (e.g., by internal audit or peers).<br><br>
      6. Maintain the internal control system, considering ongoing changes in business and I&T risk, the organizational control environment, and relevant business and I&T processes. If gaps exist, evaluate and recommend changes.<br><br>
      7. Regularly evaluate the performance of the control framework, benchmarking against industry accepted standards and good practices. Consider formal adoption of a continuous improvement approach to internal control monitoring.
    </td>
    <td>
      3<br><br>
      4<br><br>
      5
    </td>
  </tr>
</table>

Monitor, Evaluate and Assess

&lt;page_number&gt;279&lt;/page_number&gt;

## Page 280

# COBIT® 2019 FRAMEWORK: GOVERNANCE AND MANAGEMENT OBJECTIVES

## A. Component: Process (cont.)

<table>
<thead>
<tr>
<th>Related Guidance (Standards, Frameworks, Compliance Requirements)</th>
<th>Detailed Reference</th>
</tr>
</thead>
<tbody>
<tr>
<td>HITRUST CSF version 9, September 2017</td>
<td>09.10 Monitoring</td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td>ISO/IEC 38502:2017(E)</td>
<td>5.5 Governance and internal control</td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td>National Institute of Standards and Technology Special Publication 800-53, Revision 5 (Draft), August 2017</td>
<td>3.3 Audit and accountability (AU-2)</td>
</tr>
</tbody>
</table>

<table>
<thead>
<tr>
<th>Management Practice</th>
<th>Example Metrics</th>
</tr>
</thead>
<tbody>
<tr>
<td><strong>MEA02.02 Review effectiveness of business process controls.</strong><br>Review the operation of controls, including monitoring and test evidence, to ensure that controls within business processes operate effectively. Include activities to maintain evidence of the effective operation of controls through mechanisms such as periodic testing, continuous monitoring, independent assessments, command and control centers, and network operation centers. This evidence assures the enterprise that controls meet requirements related to business, regulatory and social responsibilities.</td>
<td>a. Number of weaknesses identified by external qualification and certification reports<br>b. Number of controls being monitored and tested to ensure that controls within business processes operate effectively</td>
</tr>
</tbody>
</table>

<table>
<thead>
<tr>
<th>Activities</th>
<th>Capability Level</th>
</tr>
</thead>
<tbody>
<tr>
<td>1. Understand and prioritize risk to organizational objectives.</td>
<td>3</td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td>2. Identify key controls and develop a strategy suitable for validating controls.</td>
<td></td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td>3. Identify information that will indicate whether the internal control environment is operating effectively.</td>
<td></td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td>4. Maintain evidence of control effectiveness.</td>
<td>4</td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td>5. Develop and implement cost-effective procedures to obtain this information in line with applicable information quality criteria.</td>
<td></td>
</tr>
</tbody>
</table>

<table>
<thead>
<tr>
<th>Related Guidance (Standards, Frameworks, Compliance Requirements)</th>
<th>Detailed Reference</th>
</tr>
</thead>
<tbody>
<tr>
<td>No related guidance for this management practice</td>
<td></td>
</tr>
</tbody>
</table>

<table>
<thead>
<tr>
<th>Management Practice</th>
<th>Example Metrics</th>
</tr>
</thead>
<tbody>
<tr>
<td><strong>MEA02.03 Perform control self-assessments.</strong><br>Encourage management and process owners to improve controls proactively through a continuing program of self-assessment that evaluates the completeness and effectiveness of management's control over processes, policies and contracts.</td>
<td>a. Number of self-assessments performed<br>b. Number of identified gaps in self-assessments vs. industry standards or good practices</td>
</tr>
</tbody>
</table>

<table>
<thead>
<tr>
<th>Activities</th>
<th>Capability Level</th>
</tr>
</thead>
<tbody>
<tr>
<td>1. Define an agreed, consistent approach for performing control self-assessments and coordinating with internal and external auditors.</td>
<td>3</td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td>2. Maintain evaluation plans, and scope and identify evaluation criteria for conducting self-assessments. Plan the communication of results of the self-assessment process to business, IT and general management and the board. Consider internal audit standards in the design of self-assessments.</td>
<td></td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td>3. Determine the frequency of periodic self-assessments, considering the overall effectiveness and efficiency of ongoing monitoring.</td>
<td></td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td>4. Assign responsibility for self-assessment to appropriate individuals to ensure objectivity and competence.</td>
<td></td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td>5. Provide for independent reviews to ensure objectivity of the self-assessment and enable the sharing of internal control good practices from other enterprises.</td>
<td></td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td>6. Compare the results of the self-assessments against industry standards and good practices.</td>
<td>4</td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td>7. Summarize and report outcomes of self-assessments and benchmarking for remedial actions.</td>
<td>5</td>
</tr>
</tbody>
</table>

<table>
<thead>
<tr>
<th>Related Guidance (Standards, Frameworks, Compliance Requirements)</th>
<th>Detailed Reference</th>
</tr>
</thead>
<tbody>
<tr>
<td>ISO/IEC 27001:2013/Cor.2:2015(E)</td>
<td>9.3 Management review</td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td>National Institute of Standards and Technology Special Publication 800-37, Revision 2 (Draft), May 2018</td>
<td>3.7 Monitoring (Task 2)</td>
</tr>
</tbody>
</table>

## Page 281

CHAPTER 4

A. Component: Process (cont.)

<table>
<thead>
<tr>
<th>Management Practice</th>
<th>Example Metrics</th>
</tr>
</thead>
<tbody>
<tr>
<td><strong>MEA02.04 Identify and report control deficiencies.</strong><br>Identify control deficiencies and analyze and identify their underlying root causes. Escalate control deficiencies and report to stakeholders.</td>
<td>a. Time between internal control deficiency occurrence and reporting<br>b. Time between exception identification and agreed actions addressed<br>c. Percent of implementation of remedial actions arising from control assessments</td>
</tr>
</tbody>
</table>

<table>
<thead>
<tr>
<th>Activities</th>
<th>Capability Level</th>
</tr>
</thead>
<tbody>
<tr>
<td>1. Communicate procedures for escalation of control exceptions, root cause analysis, and reporting to process owners and I&T stakeholders.</td>
<td>3</td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td>2. Consider related enterprise risk to establish thresholds for escalation of control exceptions and breakdowns.</td>
<td></td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td>3. Identify, report and log control exceptions. Assign responsibility for resolving them and reporting on the status.</td>
<td></td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td>4. Decide which control exceptions should be communicated to the individual responsible for the function and which exceptions should be escalated. Inform affected process owners and stakeholders.</td>
<td></td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td>5. Follow up on all exceptions to ensure that agreed-on actions have been addressed.</td>
<td>4</td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td>6. Identify, initiate, track and implement remedial actions arising from control assessments and reporting.</td>
<td>5</td>
</tr>
</tbody>
</table>

CHAPTER 4
COBIT GOVERNANCE AND MANAGEMENT OBJECTIVES—DETAILED GUIDANCE

E. Component: Policies and Procedures

<table>

<tr>
<th>Relevant Policy</th>
<th>Policy Description</th>
<th>Related Guidance</th>
<th>Detailed Reference</th>
</tr>
</thead>
<tbody>
<tr>
<td>Internal control policy</td>
<td>Communicates management's internal control objectives. Establishes standards for the design and operation of the enterprise system of internal controls to reduce exposure to all risk. Provides guidance for continuously monitoring and evaluating the control environment, including self-awareness and self-assessments.</td>
<td></td>
<td></td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td>Internal control self-assessment guidance</td>
<td>Recommends continuous monitoring of internal controls to identify deficiencies and gaps in effectiveness, determine their root causes, and initiate plans of action and corrective milestones for reporting to stakeholders.</td>
<td></td>
<td></td>
</tr>
</tbody>
</table>

<tr>
<th>Key Culture Elements</th>

<td></td>
<td></td>
</tr>

## Page 285

CHAPTER 4
COBIT GOVERNANCE AND MANAGEMENT OBJECTIVES—DETAILED GUIDANCE

<table>
  <tr>
    <td colspan="2">Domain: Monitor, Evaluate and Assess<br>Management Objective: MEA03 – Managed Compliance With External Requirements</td>
    <td>Focus Area: COBIT Core Model</td>
  </tr>
  <tr>
    <td colspan="3">
      **Description**<br>
      Evaluate that I&T processes and I&T-supported business processes are compliant with laws, regulations and contractual requirements. Obtain assurance that the requirements have been identified and complied with; integrate IT compliance with overall enterprise compliance.
    </td>
  </tr>
  <tr>
    <td colspan="3">
      **Purpose**<br>
      Ensure that the enterprise is compliant with all applicable external requirements.
    </td>
  </tr>
  <tr>
    <td colspan="3">
      The management objective supports the achievement of a set of primary enterprise and alignment goals:
    </td>
  </tr>
  <tr>
    <td>
      **Enterprise Goals**
    </td>
    <td>
      <table>
        <tr>
          <td>EG03</td>
          <td>Compliance with external laws and regulations</td>
        </tr>
      </table>
    </td>
    <td>
      <table>
        <tr>
          <td>
            **Alignment Goals**
          </td>
        </tr>
        <tr>
          <td>AG01</td>
          <td>I&T compliance and support for business compliance with external laws and regulations</td>
        </tr>
      </table>
    </td>
  </tr>
  <tr>
    <td>
      **Example Metrics for Enterprise Goals**
    </td>
    <td>
      EG03
      <ul>
        <li>a. Cost of regulatory noncompliance, including settlements and fines</li>
        <li>b. Number of regulatory noncompliance issues causing public comment or negative publicity</li>
        <li>c. Number of noncompliance matters noted by regulators</li>
        <li>d. Number of regulatory noncompliance issues relating to contractual agreements with business partners</li>
      </ul>
    </td>
    <td>
      **Example Metrics for Alignment Goals**
    </td>
  </tr>
  <tr>
    <td>
      AG01
      <ul>
        <li>a. Cost of IT noncompliance, including settlements and fines, and the impact of reputational loss</li>
        <li>b. Number of IT-related noncompliance issues reported to the board, or causing public comment or embarrassment</li>
        <li>c. Number of noncompliance issues relating to contractual agreements with IT service providers</li>
      </ul>
    </td>
    <td></td>
    <td></td>
  </tr>
</table>

A. Component: Process

<table>
  <tr>
    <td>
      **Management Practice**
    </td>
    <td>
      **Example Metrics**
    </td>
  </tr>
  <tr>
    <td>
      **MEA03.01 Identify external compliance requirements.**<br>
      On a continuous basis, monitor changes in local and international laws, regulations and other external requirements and identify mandates for compliance from an I&T perspective.
    </td>
    <td>
      a. Frequency of compliance requirements reviews<br>
      b. Percent of satisfaction of key stakeholders in regulatory review compliance process
    </td>
  </tr>
  <tr>
    <td>
      **Activities**
    </td>
    <td>
      **Capability Level**
    </td>
  </tr>
  <tr>
    <td>
      1. Assign responsibility for identifying and monitoring any changes of legal, regulatory and other external contractual requirements relevant to the use of IT resources and the processing of information within the business and IT operations of the enterprise.<br><br>
      2. Identify and assess all potential compliance requirements and the impact on I&T activities in areas such as data flow, privacy, internal controls, financial reporting, industry-specific regulations, intellectual property, health and safety.<br><br>
      3. Assess the impact of I&T-related legal and regulatory requirements on third-party contracts related to IT operations, service providers and business trading partners.<br><br>
      4. Define the consequences of noncompliance.<br><br>
      5. Obtain independent counsel, where appropriate, on changes to applicable laws, regulations and standards.<br><br>
      6. Maintain an up-to-date log of all relevant legal, regulatory and contractual requirements; their impact and required actions.<br><br>
      7. Maintain a harmonized and integrated overall register of external compliance requirements for the enterprise.
    </td>
    <td>
      2<br><br>
      3
    </td>
  </tr>
  <tr>
    <td>
      **Related Guidance (Standards, Frameworks, Compliance Requirements)**
    </td>
    <td>
      **Detailed Reference**
    </td>
  </tr>
  <tr>
    <td>
      CMMI Cybermaturity Platform, 2018
    </td>
    <td>
      BC.RR Determine Legal / Regulatory Requirements
    </td>
  </tr>
  <tr>
    <td>
      HITRUST CSF version 9, September 2017
    </td>
    <td>
      06.01 Compliance with Legal Requirements
    </td>
  </tr>
  <tr>
    <td>
      ISF, The Standard of Good Practice for Information Security 2016
    </td>
    <td>
      SM2.3 Legal and Regulatory Compliance
    </td>
  </tr>
</table>

Monitor, Evaluate and Assess

# COBIT® 2019 FRAMEWORK: GOVERNANCE AND MANAGEMENT OBJECTIVES

**A. Component: Process (cont.)**

<table>
  <thead>
    <tr>
      <th>Management Practice</th>
      <th>Example Metrics</th>
    </tr>
  </thead>
  <tbody>
    <tr>
      <td><strong>MEA03.02 Optimize response to external requirements.</strong><br>Review and adjust policies, principles, standards, procedures and methodologies to ensure that legal, regulatory and contractual requirements are addressed and communicated. Consider adopting and adapting industry standards, codes of good practice, and good practice guidance.</td>
      <td>a. Average time between identifying external compliance issues and resolution<br>b. Percent of satisfaction of relevant personnel with communication of new and changed regulatory compliance requirements</td>
    </tr>
  </tbody>
</table>

<table>
  <thead>
    <tr>
      <th>Activities</th>
      <th>Capability Level</th>
    </tr>
  </thead>
  <tbody>
    <tr>
      <td>1. Regularly review and adjust policies, principles, standards, procedures and methodologies for their effectiveness in ensuring necessary compliance and addressing enterprise risk. Use internal and external experts, as required.</td>
      <td>3</td>
    </tr>
    <tr>
      <td>2. Communicate new and changed requirements to all relevant personnel.</td>
      <td></td>
    </tr>
  </tbody>
</table>

<table>
  <thead>
    <tr>
      <th>Related Guidance (Standards, Frameworks, Compliance Requirements)</th>
      <th>Detailed Reference</th>
    </tr>
  </thead>
  <tbody>
    <tr>
      <td>King IV Report on Corporate Governance for South Africa, 2016</td>
      <td>Part 5.4: Governance functional areas - Principle 13</td>
    </tr>
  </tbody>
</table>

<table>
  <thead>
    <tr>
      <th>Management Practice</th>
      <th>Example Metrics</th>
    </tr>
  </thead>
  <tbody>
    <tr>
      <td><strong>MEA03.03 Confirm external compliance.</strong><br>Confirm compliance of policies, principles, standards, procedures and methodologies with legal, regulatory and contractual requirements.</td>
      <td>a. Number of critical noncompliance issues identified per year<br>b. Percent of process owners signing off, confirming compliance</td>
    </tr>
  </tbody>
</table>

<table>
  <thead>
    <tr>
      <th>Activities</th>
      <th>Capability Level</th>
    </tr>
  </thead>
  <tbody>
    <tr>
      <td>1. Regularly evaluate organizational policies, standards, procedures and methodologies in all functions of the enterprise to ensure compliance with relevant legal and regulatory requirements in relation to the processing of information.</td>
      <td>3</td>
    </tr>
    <tr>
      <td>2. Address compliance gaps in policies, standards and procedures on a timely basis.</td>
      <td></td>
    </tr>
    <tr>
      <td>3. Periodically evaluate business and IT processes and activities to ensure adherence to applicable legal, regulatory and contractual requirements.</td>
      <td></td>
    </tr>
    <tr>
      <td>4. Regularly review for recurring patterns of compliance failures and assess lessons learned.</td>
      <td>4</td>
    </tr>
    <tr>
      <td>5. Based on review and lessons learned, improve policies, standards, procedures, methodologies, and associated processes and activities.</td>
      <td>5</td>
    </tr>
  </tbody>
</table>

<table>
  <thead>
    <tr>
      <th>Related Guidance (Standards, Frameworks, Compliance Requirements)</th>
      <th>Detailed Reference</th>
    </tr>
  </thead>
  <tbody>
    <tr>
      <td>No related guidance for this management practice</td>
      <td></td>
    </tr>
  </tbody>
</table>

<table>
  <thead>
    <tr>
      <th>Management Practice</th>
      <th>Example Metrics</th>
    </tr>
  </thead>
  <tbody>
    <tr>
      <td><strong>MEA03.04 Obtain assurance of external compliance.</strong><br>Obtain and report assurance of compliance and adherence with policies, principles, standards, procedures and methodologies. Confirm that corrective actions to address compliance gaps are closed in a timely manner.</td>
      <td>a. Number of compliance reports obtained<br>b. Percent of service provider compliance based on independent reviews<br>c. Time between identification of compliance gap and corrective action<br>d. Number of corrective action reports addressing compliance gaps closed in a timely manner</td>
    </tr>
  </tbody>
</table>

<table>
  <thead>
    <tr>
      <th>Activities</th>
      <th>Capability Level</th>
    </tr>
  </thead>
  <tbody>
    <tr>
      <td>1. Obtain regular confirmation of compliance with internal policies from business and IT process owners and unit heads.</td>
      <td>2</td>
    </tr>
    <tr>
      <td>2. Perform regular (and, where appropriate, independent) internal and external reviews to assess levels of compliance.</td>
      <td></td>
    </tr>
    <tr>
      <td>3. If required, obtain assertions from third-party I&T service providers on levels of their compliance with applicable laws and regulations.</td>
      <td></td>
    </tr>
    <tr>
      <td>4. If required, obtain assertions from business partners on levels of their compliance with applicable laws and regulations as they relate to intercompany electronic transactions.</td>
      <td></td>
    </tr>
    <tr>
      <td>5. Integrate reporting on legal, regulatory and contractual requirements at an enterprisewide level, involving all business units.</td>
      <td>3</td>
    </tr>
    <tr>
      <td>6. Monitor and report on noncompliance issues and, where necessary, investigate the root cause.</td>
      <td>4</td>
    </tr>
  </tbody>
</table>

<table>
  <thead>
    <tr>
      <th>Related Guidance (Standards, Frameworks, Compliance Requirements)</th>
      <th>Detailed Reference</th>
    </tr>
  </thead>
  <tbody>

## Page 289

# CHAPTER 4
## COBIT GOVERNANCE AND MANAGEMENT OBJECTIVES—DETAILED GUIDANCE

<table>
  <tr>
    <td>
      **Domain:** Monitor, Evaluate and Assess<br>
      **Management Objective:** MEA04 – Managed Assurance
    </td>
    <td>
      **Focus Area:** COBIT Core Model
    </td>
  </tr>
</table>

### Description
Plan, scope and execute assurance initiatives to comply with internal requirements, laws, regulations and strategic objectives. Enable management to deliver adequate and sustainable assurance in the enterprise by performing independent assurance reviews and activities.

### Purpose
Enable the organization to design and develop efficient and effective assurance initiatives, providing guidance on planning, scoping, executing and following up on assurance reviews, using a road map based on well-accepted assurance approaches.

The management objective supports the achievement of a set of primary enterprise and alignment goals:

<table>
  <tr>
    <td>
      **Enterprise Goals**
      <ul>
        <li>• EG03 Compliance with external laws and regulations</li>
        <li>• EG11 Compliance with internal policies</li>
      </ul>
      **Example Metrics for Enterprise Goals**
      <ul>
        <li>EG03
          <ol>
            <li>a. Cost of regulatory noncompliance, including settlements and fines</li>
            <li>b. Number of regulatory noncompliance issues causing public comment or negative publicity</li>
            <li>c. Number of noncompliance matters noted by regulators</li>
            <li>d. Number of regulatory noncompliance issues relating to contractual agreements with business partners</li>
          </ol>
        </li>
        <li>EG11
          <ol>
            <li>a. Number of incidents related to noncompliance to policy</li>
            <li>b. Percent of stakeholders who understand policies</li>
            <li>c. Percent of policies supported by effective standards and working practices</li>
          </ol>
        </li>
      </ul>
    </td>
    <td>
      &lt;img&gt;Right arrow icon&lt;/img&gt;
      **Alignment Goals**
      <ul>
        <li>AG11 I&T compliance with internal policies</li>
      </ul>
      **Example Metrics for Alignment Goals**
      <ul>
        <li>AG11
          <ol>
            <li>a. Number of incidents related to noncompliance with I&T-related policies</li>
            <li>b. Number of exceptions to internal policies</li>
            <li>c. Frequency of policy review and update</li>
          </ol>
        </li>
      </ul>
    </td>
  </tr>
</table>

### A. Component: Process

<table>
  <tr>
    <td>
      **Management Practice**
      <ul>
        <li>MEA04.01 Ensure that assurance providers are independent and qualified.<br>
          Ensure that the entities performing assurance are independent from the function, groups or organizations in scope. The entities performing assurance should demonstrate an appropriate attitude and appearance, competence in the skills and knowledge necessary to perform assurance, and adherence to codes of ethics and professional standards.
        </li>
      </ul>
      **Activities**
      <ol>
        <li>Establish adherence to applicable codes of ethics and standards (e.g., Code of Professional Ethics of ISACA) and (industry-and geography-specific) assurance standards (e.g., IT Audit and Assurance Standards of ISACA and the International Auditing and Assurance Standards Board’s [IAASB’s] International Framework for Assurance Engagements [IAASB Assurance Framework]).</li>
        <li>Establish independence of assurance providers.</li>
        <li>Establish competency and qualification of assurance providers.</li>
      </ol>
      **Related Guidance (Standards, Frameworks, Compliance Requirements)**
      <ul>
        <li>HITRUST CSF version 9, September 2017</li>
      </ul>
    </td>
    <td>
      **Example Metrics**
      <ul>
        <li>a. Percent of processes receiving independent review</li>
        <li>b. Percent of qualifications and competencies met by service providers</li>
      </ul>
      **Capability Level**
      <ul>
        <li>2</li>
      </ul>
      **Detailed Reference**
      <ul>
        <li>06.03 Information System Audit Considerations</li>
      </ul>
    </td>
  </tr>
</table>

Monitor, Evaluate and Assess
&lt;page_number&gt;289&lt;/page_number&gt;

## Page 290

# COBIT® 2019 FRAMEWORK: GOVERNANCE AND MANAGEMENT OBJECTIVES

## A. Component: Process (cont.)

<table>
  <thead>
    <tr>
      <th>Management Practice</th>
      <th>Example Metrics</th>
    </tr>
  </thead>
  <tbody>
    <tr>
      <td>
        **MEA04.02 Develop risk-based planning of assurance initiatives.**<br>
        Determine assurance objectives based on assessments of the internal and external environment and context, the risk of not achieving enterprise goals, and the opportunities associated achievement of the same goals.
      </td>
      <td>
        a. Percent of assurance initiatives following approved assurance program and plan standards<br>
        b. Percent of assurance plan initiatives based on risk
      </td>
    </tr>
    <tr>
      <td colspan="2">
        <strong>Activities</strong>
      </td>
    </tr>
    <tr>
      <td colspan="2">1. Understand the enterprise strategy and priorities.</td>
    </tr>
    <tr>
      <td colspan="2">2. Understand the internal context of the enterprise. This understanding will help the assurance professional to better assess the enterprise goals and the relative importance of enterprise and alignment goals, as well as the most important threats to these goals. In turn, this will assist in defining a better and more relevant scope for the assurance engagement.</td>
    </tr>
    <tr>
      <td colspan="2">3. Understand the external context of the enterprise. This understanding will help the assurance professional to better understand the enterprise goals and the relative importance of enterprise and alignment goals, as well as the most important threats to these goals. In turn, this will assist in defining a better and more relevant scope for the assurance engagement.</td>
    </tr>
    <tr>
      <td colspan="2">4. Develop an overall yearly plan for assurance initiatives containing the consolidated assurance objectives.</td>
    </tr>
    <tr>
      <td colspan="2"><strong>Related Guidance (Standards, Frameworks, Compliance Requirements)</strong></td>
    </tr>
    <tr>
      <td colspan="2">King IV Report on Corporate Governance for South Africa, 2016</td>
    </tr>
    <tr>
      <td colspan="2"><strong>Detailed Reference</strong></td>
    </tr>
    <tr>
      <td colspan="2">Part 5.4: Governance functional areas—Principle 15</td>
    </tr>
    <tr>
      <td colspan="2">
        <strong>Management Practice</strong>
      </td>
    </tr>
    <tr>
      <td>
        **MEA04.03 Determine the objectives of the assurance initiative.**<br>
        Define and agree with all stakeholders on the objectives of the assurance initiative.
      </td>
      <td>
        a. Percent of objectives achieved through the assurance initiative<br>
        b. Percent of stakeholder satisfaction with the assurance initiative's objectives
      </td>
    </tr>
    <tr>
      <td colspan="2">
        <strong>Activities</strong>
      </td>
    </tr>
    <tr>
      <td colspan="2">1. Define the assurance objective of the assurance initiative by identifying the stakeholders of the assurance initiative and their interests.</td>
    </tr>
    <tr>
      <td colspan="2">2. Agree on the high-level objectives and the organizational boundaries of the assurance engagement.</td>
    </tr>
    <tr>
      <td colspan="2">3. Consider the use of the COBIT Goals Cascade and its different levels to express the assurance objective.</td>
    </tr>
    <tr>
      <td colspan="2">4. Ensure that the objectives of the assurance engagement consider all three value objective components: delivering benefits that support strategic objectives, optimizing the risk that strategic objectives are not achieved and optimizing resource levels required to achieve the strategic objectives.</td>
    </tr>
    <tr>
      <td colspan="2">
        <strong>Related Guidance (Standards, Frameworks, Compliance Requirements)</strong>
      </td>
    </tr>
    <tr>
      <td colspan="2">CMMI Data Management Maturity Model, 2014</td>
    </tr>
    <tr>
      <td colspan="2"><strong>Detailed Reference</strong></td>
    </tr>
    <tr>
      <td colspan="2">Supporting Processes - Process Quality Assurance</td>
    </tr>
    <tr>
      <td colspan="2">
        <strong>Management Practice</strong>
      </td>
    </tr>
    <tr>
      <td>
        **MEA04.04 Define the scope of the assurance initiative.**<br>
        Define and agree with all stakeholders on the scope of the assurance initiative, based on the assurance objectives.
      </td>
      <td>
        a. Number of engagement plans, based on the scope, that consider information to be collected and stakeholders to be interviewed<br>
        b. Percent of stakeholder satisfaction with the scope of the assurance initiative, based on the assurance objectives
      </td>
    </tr>
    <tr>
      <td colspan="2">
        <strong>Activities</strong>
      </td>
    </tr>
    <tr>
      <td colspan="2">1. Define all governance components in scope of the review, that is, the principles, policies and frameworks; processes; organizational structures; culture, ethics and behavior; information; services, infrastructure and applications; people, skills and competences</td>
    </tr>
    <tr>
      <td colspan="2">2. Based on the scope definition, define an engagement plan, considering information to be collected and stakeholders to be interviewed.</td>
    </tr>
    <tr>
      <td colspan="2">3. Confirm and refine the scope based on an understanding of the enterprise architecture.</td>
    </tr>
    <tr>
      <td colspan="2">4. Refine the scope of the assurance engagement, based on available resources.</td>
    </tr>
    <tr>
      <td colspan="2">
        <strong>Related Guidance (Standards, Frameworks, Compliance Requirements)</strong>
      </td>
    </tr>
    <tr>
      <td colspan="2">CMMI Cybermaturity Platform, 2018</td>
    </tr>
    <tr>
      <td colspan="2"><strong>Detailed Reference</strong></td>
    </tr>
    <tr>
      <td colspan="2">TP.LA Apply Logging and Audit Processes</td>
    </tr>
  </tbody>
</table>

<footer>Monitor, Evaluate and Assess</footer>
&lt;page_number&gt;290&lt;/page_number&gt;

## Page 291

CHAPTER 4
COBIT GOVERNANCE AND MANAGEMENT OBJECTIVES—DETAILED GUIDANCE

A. Component: Process (cont.)

<table>
<thead>
<tr>
<th>Management Practice</th>
<th>Example Metrics</th>
</tr>
</thead>
<tbody>
<tr>
<td><strong>MEA04.05 Define the work program for the assurance initiative.</strong><br>Define a detailed work program for the assurance initiative, structured according to the management objectives and governance components in scope.</td>
<td>a. Percent of management controls identified as weak without defined practices to reduce residual risk<br>b. Number of controls reviewed<br>c. Percent of stakeholder satisfaction with the work program for the assurance initiative</td>
</tr>
</tbody>
</table>

<table>
<thead>
<tr>
<th>Activities</th>
<th>Capability Level</th>
</tr>
</thead>
<tbody>
<tr>
<td>1. Define detailed steps for collecting and evaluating information from management controls within scope. Focus on assessing the definition and application of good practices, related to control design, and achievement of control objectives, related to control effectiveness.</td>
<td>2</td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td>2. Understand the context of the management objectives and the supporting management controls that are put in place. Understand how these management controls contribute to the achievement of the alignment goals and enterprise goals.</td>
<td></td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td>3. Understand all stakeholders and their interests.</td>
<td></td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td>4. Agree on the expected good practices for the management controls.</td>
<td>3</td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td>5. Should a management control be weak, define practices to identify residual risk (in preparation for reporting).</td>
<td></td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td>6. Understand the life cycle stage of the management controls and agree on expected values.</td>
<td></td>
</tr>
</tbody>
</table>

<table>
<thead>
<tr>
<th>Related Guidance (Standards, Frameworks, Compliance Requirements)</th>
<th>Detailed Reference</th>
</tr>
</thead>
<tbody>
<tr>
<td>No related guidance for this management practice</td>
<td></td>
</tr>
</tbody>
</table>

<table>
<thead>
<tr>
<th>Management Practice</th>
<th>Example Metrics</th>
</tr>
</thead>
<tbody>
<tr>
<td><strong>MEA04.06 Execute the assurance initiative, focusing on design effectiveness.</strong><br>Execute the planned assurance initiative. Validate and confirm the design of the internal controls in place. Additionally, and specifically in internal audit assignments, consider the cost-effectiveness of the governance component design.</td>
<td>a. Percent of assurance initiatives that consider cost effectiveness of design<br>b. Percent of stakeholder satisfaction with the design of the assurance initiative</td>
</tr>
</tbody>
</table>

<table>
<thead>
<tr>
<th>Activities</th>
<th>Capability Level</th>
</tr>
</thead>
<tbody>
<tr>
<td>1. Refine the understanding of the IT assurance subject.</td>
<td>2</td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td>2. Refine the scope of the IT assurance subject.</td>
<td></td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td>3. Observe/inspect and review the management control approach. Validate the design with the control owner for completeness, relevancy, timeliness and measurability.</td>
<td>3</td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td>4. Ask the control owner whether the responsibilities for the governance component and overall accountability have been assigned. Confirm the response. Test whether accountability and responsibilities are understood and accepted. Verify that the right skills and the necessary resources are available.</td>
<td></td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td>5. Reconsider the balance of prevention vs. detection and correction types of management control activities.</td>
<td></td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td>6. Consider the effort spent in maintaining the management controls and the associated cost/effectiveness.</td>
<td></td>
</tr>
</tbody>
</table>

<table>
<thead>
<tr>
<th>Related Guidance (Standards, Frameworks, Compliance Requirements)</th>
<th>Detailed Reference</th>
</tr>
</thead>
<tbody>
<tr>
<td>ISF, The Standard of Good Practice for Information Security 2016</td>
<td>SI1 Security Audit</td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td>ISO/IEC 27001:2013/Cor.2:2015(E)</td>
<td>9.2 Internal audit</td>
</tr>
</tbody>
</table>

<table>
<thead>
<tr>
<th>Management Practice</th>
<th>Example Metrics</th>
</tr>
</thead>
<tbody>
<tr>
<td><strong>MEA04.07 Execute the assurance initiative, focusing on operating effectiveness.</strong><br>Execute the planned assurance initiative. Test whether the internal controls in place are appropriate and sufficient. Test the outcome of the key management objectives in scope of the assurance initiative.</td>
<td>a. Percent of assurance initiatives that test the outcome of key, in-scope management objectives<br>b. Percent of stakeholder satisfaction with the execution of the assurance initiative</td>
</tr>
</tbody>
</table>

Monitor, Evaluate and Assess

&lt;page_number&gt;291&lt;/page_number&gt;

## Page 292

# COBIT® 2019 FRAMEWORK: GOVERNANCE AND MANAGEMENT OBJECTIVES

**A. Component: Process (cont.)**

<table>
<thead>
<tr>
<th>Activities</th>
<th>Capability Level</th>
</tr>
</thead>
<tbody>
<tr>
<td>1. Assess whether the expected outcomes for each of the management controls in scope are achieved. That is, assess the effectiveness of the management control (control effectiveness).</td>
<td>3</td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td>2. Ensure that the assurance professional tests the outcome or effectiveness of the management control by looking for direct and indirect evidence of the impact on the management controls goals. This implies the direct and indirect substantiation of measurable contribution of the management goals to the alignment goals, thereby recording direct and indirect evidence of actually achieving the expected outcomes.</td>
<td></td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td>3. Determine whether the assurance professional obtains direct or indirect evidence for selected items/periods by applying a selection of testing techniques to ensure that the management control under review is working effectively. Ensure that the assurance professional also performs a limited review of the adequacy of the management control results and determines the level of substantive testing and additional work needed to provide assurance that the management control performance is adequate.</td>
<td></td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td>4. Investigate whether a management control can be made more efficient and if its design can be more effective by optimizing steps or looking for synergies with other management controls.</td>
<td></td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td><strong>Related Guidance (Standards, Frameworks, Compliance Requirements)</strong></td>
<td><strong>Detailed Reference</strong></td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td>ISF, The Standard of Good Practice for Information Security 2016</td>
<td>SI1 Security Audit</td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td>SO/IEC 27001:2013/Cor.2:2015(E)</td>
<td>9.2 Internal audit</td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td><strong>Management Practice</strong></td>
<td><strong>Example Metrics</strong></td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td><strong>MEA04.08 Report and follow up on the assurance initiative.</strong><br>Provide positive assurance opinions, where appropriate, and recommendations for improvement relating to identified operational performance, external compliance and internal control weaknesses.</td>
<td>a. Stakeholder acceptance of the assurance report<br>b. Stakeholder acceptance of recommendations for improvement relating to identified operational performance, external compliance and internal control weaknesses</td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td><strong>Activities</strong></td>
<td><strong>Capability Level</strong></td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td>1. Document the impact of control weaknesses.</td>
<td>2</td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td>2. Communicate with management during execution of the initiative so there is a clear understanding of the work performed and agreement on and acceptance of the preliminary findings and recommendations.</td>
<td></td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td>3. Provide management with a report (aligned with the terms of reference, scope and agreed reporting standards) that supports the results of the initiative and enables a clear focus on key issues and important actions.</td>
<td>3</td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td>4. Supervise the assurance activities and make sure the work done is complete, meets objectives and is of an acceptable quality. Revise the approach or detailed steps if quality gaps occur.</td>
<td>4</td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td><strong>Related Guidance (Standards, Frameworks, Compliance Requirements)</strong></td>
<td><strong>Detailed Reference</strong></td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td>No related guidance for this management practice</td>
<td></td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td><strong>Management Practice</strong></td>
<td><strong>Example Metrics</strong></td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td><strong>MEA04.09 Follow up on recommendations and actions.</strong><br>Agree on, follow up and implement the identified recommendations for improvement.</td>
<td>a. Number of recurring weaknesses<br>b. Number of identified weaknesses resolved</td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td><strong>Activities</strong></td>
<td><strong>Capability Level</strong></td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td>1. Agree on and implement internally, within the organization, the necessary actions that need to be taken to resolve identified weaknesses and gaps.</td>
<td>2</td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td>2. Follow up, within the organization, to determine whether corrective actions were taken and internal control weaknesses were resolved.</td>
<td></td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td><strong>Related Guidance (Standards, Frameworks, Compliance Requirements)</strong></td>
<td><strong>Detailed Reference</strong></td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td>No related guidance for this management practice</td>
<td></td>
</tr>
</tbody>
</table>

<footer>Monitor, Evaluate and Assess</footer>
&lt;page_number&gt;292&lt;/page_number&gt;

## Page 295

CHAPTER 4
COBIT GOVERNANCE AND MANAGEMENT OBJECTIVES—DETAILED GUIDANCE

D. Component: People, Skills and Competencies

<table>
<thead>
<tr>
<th>Skill</th>
<th>Related Guidance (Standards, Frameworks, Compliance Requirements)</th>
<th>Detailed Reference</th>
</tr>
</thead>
<tbody>
<tr>
<td>A number of core principles, described by the Institute of Internal Auditors®, support the effectiveness and efficiency of the (internal) audit function. These principles include, among others, the importance of independence, effective communication skills, proactiveness, etc.</td>
<td>Core Principles for the Professional Practice of Internal Auditing, The Institute of Internal Auditors</td>
<td>cfr. IIA website—Standards & Guidance - Core Principles</td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td>Risk management</td>
<td>e-Competence Framework (e-CF)—A common European Framework for ICT Professionals in all industry sectors—Part 1: Framework, 2016</td>
<td>E. Manage—E.3. Risk Management</td>
</tr>
</tbody>
</table>

E. Component: Policies and Procedures

<table>
<thead>
<tr>
<th>Relevant Policy</th>
<th>Policy Description</th>
<th>Related Guidance</th>
<th>Detailed Reference</th>
</tr>
</thead>
<tbody>
<tr>
<td>Assurance guide</td>
<td>Provides guidance on performing assurance activities. Enables efficient and effective development of I&T assurance initiatives, including planning, scoping and executing assurance reviews, based on well-accepted assurance approaches. Provides assurance steps to test the control design, test the outcome of the operational effectiveness of the control, and document control weaknesses and their impact.</td>
<td></td>
<td></td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td>Internal audit charter</td>
<td>Provides independence to undertake audit reviews and report findings and recommendations directly to top management. The internal audit function should be a separate entity reporting either to the chief executive officer or chief operating officer. With respect to I&T, the charter should stipulate that the function is responsible for reviewing both general and application controls to determine whether the controls have been designed in accordance with management direction, established standards and procedures, and known legal requirements, and whether the controls are operating effectively to provide reliability and security over the data being processed (i.e., confidentiality, integrity and availability). The charter should stipulate that the internal audit function is responsible for reviewing the design, development and implementation of new systems or major modifications of existing systems.</td>
<td></td>
<td></td>
</tr>
</tbody>
</table>

Monitor, Evaluate and Assess
&lt;page_number&gt;295&lt;/page_number&gt;

---


## Page 296

# COBIT® 2019 FRAMEWORK: GOVERNANCE AND MANAGEMENT OBJECTIVES

## F. Component: Culture, Ethics and Behavior

<table>
<thead>
<tr>
<th>Key Culture Elements</th>
<th>Related Guidance</th>
<th>Detailed Reference</th>
</tr>
</thead>
<tbody>
<tr>
<td>Create a culture that embraces internal audit and assurance findings and recommendations, based on root cause analysis. Leaders must ensure that internal audit and assurance are involved in strategic initiatives and recognize the need for (and value of) audit and assurance reports.</td>
<td></td>
<td></td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td>Ensure an ethical culture of internal auditing through an appropriate code of ethics.</td>
<td>Code of Ethics, The Institute of Internal Auditors</td>
<td>cfr. IIA website—Standards & Guidance—Code of Ethics</td>
</tr>
</tbody>
</table>

## G. Component: Services, Infrastructure and Applications

* Assurance engagement tools
* Event log auditing tools
* Third-party assurance provisioning services

Monitor, Evaluate and Assess

&lt;page_number&gt;296&lt;/page_number&gt;

CHAPTER 5
APPENDICES

# Appendices

## 5.1 Appendix A: Goals Cascade—Mapping Tables

The mapping tables in Appendix A inform the goals cascade. The first table maps alignment goals to enterprise goals; the second table maps governance and management objectives to alignment goals. The “P” in the table refers to primary and the “S” refers to secondary.
